title: Module build failed when bundling `swc-loader` and `babel-loader`
body:
I am working on a Webpack5 tool that can build itself. I am able to build the tool the first time but when I try to rebuild it a second time I get the error `Cannot find module: swc-loader`. The same issue exists when swapping `swc-loader` with `babel-loader`. My hunch is that this is related to webpack's `resolveLoader`

Here is a [GitHub repository][1] containing the tool.

Below are the steps to reproduce the issue: 

1. Bundle the `src` directory by running `node src/index.js`
2. Bundle the `src` directory again by running `node dist/index.js`

The goal is to be able to run step 2 over and over again.

 


  [1]: https://github.com/woodpav/webpack5-self-bundler
author: woodpav

title: Watching symlinks doesn't work in dev mode
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

Watching symlinks doesn't work after multiple changes in development mode.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

**webpack.config.js**

```
module.exports = {
  entry: './main.js',
  watchOptions: { followSymlinks: true, },
  mode: "development",
  output: {
    path: __dirname,
    filename: 'bundle.js',
  },
  resolve: { symlinks: false }
};
```

**main.js**

Symlink to ./data/123.js

**data/123.js**

```js
console.log("A");
```

Run

```
webpack --watch
```

```
echo 'console.log("B")' > main.js
echo 'console.log("C")' > main.js
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

I expected bundle.js to contain `console.log("C")`, but it contained `console.log("B")`.

**Other relevant information:**

webpack version:  5.65.0
Node.js version: 17.3.0
Operating System: Ubuntu 20.04
Additional tools:

---

Strangely, this example works fine in production mode.

title: Treeshaking failing to remove an export that's only dynamically imported
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**


I found a bug with treeshaking, and will link to [this repo](https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/tree/main/frontend) to explain it.

The `RxFunctionList` file defines 2 named exported variables - [one small](https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/blob/main/frontend/src/RxFunctionList.tsx#L27-L31) (a graphQL fragment) and [one heavy](https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/blob/main/frontend/src/RxFunctionList.tsx#L12-L25) (the entire list of rxjs exports).
Another file, `App`, [statically imports the light export](https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/blob/main/frontend/src/App.tsx#L4) from `RxFunctionList`, and [dynamically imports the heavy export](https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/blob/main/frontend/src/App.tsx#L6-L9).

**Goal: for webpack's treeshaking to remove the heavy export from the main bundle, as we only statically import the light variable from `RxFunctionList`.** We want the main bundle to be free of any of the rxjs exports.

Test case | Static import only | Dynamic import only | BUG: Static AND dynamic import 
-|-|-|-
Result | treeshaking removes rxjs exports as expected | the bundle does not contain rxjs exports (they are code split) | **BUG: treeshaking fails, bundle includes rxjs exports**
Main bundle size (before minification) | 589kb | 588kb | 802kb
Webpack bundle analzyer | ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5571242/148147279-b1542245-c744-48e1-b476-84b6d9e5271f.png) | ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5571242/148147747-29cf3b9a-4cd7-49b7-b7b8-625ee725041b.png) | ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5571242/148147007-8f5c7401-8574-48b8-8e51-c4ed1b0d2e81.png)
Branch code | [static import](https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/blob/static-import-only/frontend/src/App.tsx#L4) | [dynamic import](https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/blob/dynamic-import-only/frontend/src/App.tsx#L6-L9) | [static import](https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/blob/main/frontend/src/App.tsx#L4) and [dynamic import](https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/blob/main/frontend/src/App.tsx#L6-L9)



## Steps to reproduce

1. make sure your node version is 17+
2. `git clone` [this repo](https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/issues/1)
3. `cd frontend`
4. `yarn install`
5. `yarn build`
6. `webpack-bundle-analyzer` should open, and the main bundle should be 800+ kb (before minification). 
7. for further proof the bundle contains rxjs, open `dist/main.bundle.js` and see that it includes rx functions like `bindnodecallback`, `dematerialize`


## Additional information

I found [this discussion](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/15052) of a similar topic and [made sure to enable Terser in my webpack config](https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/blob/main/frontend/webpack.prod.config.js#L13). As explained in the grid above, I am getting treeshaking to work properly when only statically importing from a file - but it fails to treeshake exports that are dynamically imported, despite the fact they are never statically imported.


**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.65
Node.js version: 17
Operating System: Mac OS 
Additional tools: yarn, react, typescript

## Are you willing to work on this yourself

Potentially
author: ditkin

title: Dynamic import returns undefined when using variable query string
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
Dynamic import returns undefined when using variable query string, but when using normal string it works as expected

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
bring your own svg (or use this https://freesvg.org/nicubunu-potato) and use the following code:

```
import React, { useState, useEffect, useRef } from 'react';

function useDynamicSVGImport(name) {
  const ImportedIconRef = useRef();
  const [loading, setLoading] = useState(false);
  const [error, setError] = useState();

  useEffect(() => {
    setLoading(true);
    const importIcon = async () => {
      try {
        ImportedIconRef.current = (
          await import( '../icons/' + name + '.svg')
        ).ReactComponent;
      } catch (err) {
        setError(err);
        throw err;
      } finally {
        setLoading(false);
      }
    };
    importIcon();
  }, [name]);

  return { error, loading, SvgIcon: ImportedIconRef.current };
}

const Icon = ({ name, ...rest }) => {
  const { loading, SvgIcon } = useDynamicSVGImport(name);

  if (loading) {
    return null;
  }

  if (SvgIcon) {
    return <SvgIcon {...rest} />;
  }
  return null;
};


<Icon name="icon" />
```

replacing import with `await import( '../icons/icon.svg')` works

**What is the expected behavior?**
it should return the svg


**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.64.4 (using CRA)
Node.js version: 14.5.0
Operating System: Windows
Additional tools: react scripts 5.0.0

title: `asset/source` option to change line endings from CRLF to LF
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
When using `type: 'asset/source'`, I get a string which contains `\r\n`. I want the string to be consistent on all platforms regardless of if the file on the file system was CRLF or not. The feature request is to add a way of changing all `\r\n` to `\n` as part of the imported string.

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Imported string should have LF line endings

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
I am using the imported string in `useState` in react. The `\r\n` is causing an unnecessary re-render because the code changes it to `\n` and my `useEffect`s get called twice, with basically the same string (but with different line endings).

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
### Breaking change
Always replace CRLF with LF

### Option
Maybe an option to process the text before, or an option to set the line ending.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
maybe, depending on how many changes it needs

title: I want to get env parameter by using webpack config as function
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

I can't get env parameter when i using webpack config as function

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

 I'm building a web server with using webpack and webpack-hot-middleware.

When i put `webpack-hot-middleware/client` to every entry like this, my production build bundle file includes `webpack-hot-middleware` package codes

So, I want to put `webpack-hot-middleware/client` to entry array when **only development enviroment** 

Like this

[Referring to this pages to seperate env files](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/mode/)

**webpack.config.js**
```javascript
module.exports = (env, argv) => {
  const config = {};
  const mode = argv.mode || 'development';

  const entry = {
        test: ['./src/script/test.ts'],
    };

  if(mode === 'development'){
    Object.keys(entry).forEach((key) => (entry[key].push('webpack-hot-middleware/client'))
  }

  ...
}
```

**app.js (server.js)**
```javascript
const webpackHotMiddleware = require('webpack-hot-middleware');
const webpackDevMiddleware = require('webpack-dev-middleware');
const webpack = require('webpack');

const webpackConfig = require('./webpack.config');
const compiler = webpack(webpackConfig());

if (node_env === 'local') {
    app.use(webpackDevMiddleware(compiler));
    app.use(webpackHotMiddleware(compiler, { path: '/__webpack_hmr' }));
}
```

**package.json**
```json
"scripts": {
  "start": "webpack --mode=development & npm run app.js"
}
```

Todo pass env, argv parameter to webpack.config.json, i change webpack.config.json Object to function
So i have to pass config like this (using function)
```javascript
const compiler = webpack(webpackConfig());
```

But, I Can't pass parameter to webpack config (Because no parameter in webpackConfig function) and `env`, `argv` parameters are undefined

So, as a temporary measure, Optional Chaining has been used.

```javascript
const mode = argv?.mode || 'development';
```

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

I want to be able to receive the env parameter values by using callback function.

Like this

```javascript
const compiler = webpack(((env, argv) => webpackConfig(env, argv))());
```

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: add a module "magic comment" to force federated import
body:
## Feature request
Add a "magic comment" (see here https://webpack.js.org/api/module-methods/#magic-comments) to help Webpack understand imported module is federated.

**What is the expected behavior?**
today, it seems like federated module have to be imported explicitly, so that the transpiled import code would look like this:
(wrapper by `React.lazy()`)
```javascript
react__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0___default().lazy(() => __webpack_require__.e(/*! import() */ "webpack_container_remote_<module name>").then(__webpack_require__.t.bind(__webpack_require__, /*! <module name> */ "webpack/container/remote/<module name>", 23)))
```
instead of
```javascript
react__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0___default().lazy(() => __webpack_require__("./src/blocks lazy recursive")(widgetName));
```
When adding the "magic comment" `/* webpackFederatedModule: true */` inside an `import` statement, Webpack will compile it as if the module was recognized by the `ModuleFederationPlugin` (resulting in the first example above, rather than the second).

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
Allow dynamically importing modules using module federation for use-cases such as dynamic dashboard/plugin systems.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
again - magic comment would be best (least amount of footprint in the code).
a different import function would also work.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
I wish. Maybe with some guidance from a senior contributor.

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When using dynamic imports with expressions together with `ContextReplacementPlugin` in order to limit the possible file matches, some module ids which should deterministic, are actually dependent on the project's location.
Meaning two identical projects (code, dependencies, configuration etc..) built on the same machine have different build outputs based on their location on the filesystem.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

- use dynamic imports with expessions
  - ie: **import(`../i18n/${locale}.json`)**)
- configure Webpack to use `ContextReplacementPlugin` to limit which JSON files should be bundled
  - ie: `new webpack.ContextReplacementPlugin(/i18n/, regExpForLocales)`
- build the project for production
- duplicate the project at another location on the filesystem at a different path root level
  - ie: `/home/project` vs `/home/work/projects`
- build the project for production at the new location
- diff both output bundles, observe that both bundles are different, invalidating the project build's reproducibility 

```bash
$ diff -u context-a/dist/main.js folder-b/context-b/dist/main.js
```
```diff
--- context-a/dist/main.js	2022-01-10 14:52:49.395750091 +0100
+++ folder-b/context-b/dist/main.js	2022-01-10 14:52:52.143773687 +0100
@@ -4,7 +4,7 @@
     t,
     o,
     n = {
-      595: (e, r, t) => {
+      174: (e, r, t) => {
         var o = { "./a.json": [359, 359], "./b.json": [376, 376] };
         function n(e) {
           if (!t.o(o, e))
@@ -16,7 +16,7 @@
             n = r[0];
           return t.e(r[1]).then(() => t.t(n, 19));
         }
-        (n.keys = () => Object.keys(o)), (n.id = 595), (e.exports = n);
+        (n.keys = () => Object.keys(o)), (n.id = 174), (e.exports = n);
       },
     },
     i = {};

```

[Minimal reproduction repository](https://github.com/ziir/webpack-repro-dynamic-import-context-replacement-deterministic-module-id)

- both bundles should be identical irrelevant of their location on the filesystem

title: With output.environment.module, packaged dependency gets incorrect reference to external dependency
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
I'm building a react component library, so I declare react and react-dom as `externals`. I'm using webpack to build our library as a module, with `experiments.outputModule = true`, `output.environment.module = true`, and `output.library.type = 'module'`.

I'm using a library that also declares `react` as an external dependency.

When I do my build:

- In the compiled code, we end up getting a reference to `react` as `external_react_`
- my code's calls to `React.createContext` get compiled correctly as `external_react_.createContext()`
- my dependency's calls to `React.createContext` get compiled **incorrectly** as `external_react_["default"].createContext()`

This results in a hard crash in consumers of my library, because `external_react_["default"]` is `undefined` and then `external_react_["default"].createContext()` throws.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

title: Webpack 5 and Module Fed.: Duplication of lib code when using multiple named entry points and eager: true
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

When having several named entry points and sharing a lib with ``eager:true``, the lib code is duplicated to all resulting bundles.

Example for entry points:

```javascript
  entry: {
    main: "./shell/main", 
    styles: "./shell/styles.css",
    polyfills: "./shell/polyfills"
  },
```

Example for shared:

```javascript
      shared: {
        "rxjs": {
          eager: true,
          singleton: true,
        },
      }
```

**What is the current behavior?**

The shared lib is duplicated to all bundles (main and polyfills in the example shown).

**What is the expected behavior?**

The shared lib should not be duplicated but only put into one bundle


**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  Every version since webpack 5
Node.js version: 16.13.2 LTS
Operating System: windows
Additional tools: no

title: Worker not bundling imported files
body:
<!-- Bug reports and Feature requests must use other templates, or will be closed -->

<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

I am trying to load a Worker in the following way:

```ts
const url = new URL('./path/to/worker.ts', import.meta.url);
const worker = new Worker(url);
```

In the worker file I am trying to do an import:

```ts
import { someFunction } from './functions';

someFunction();
```

If I don't provide `{ type: 'module' }` to the Worker constructor, the import doesn't get bundled with the worker and the following error is displayed in the console:

> Uncaught SyntaxError: Cannot use import statement outside a module

 If I do provide `{ type: 'module' }` to the Worker constructor then it request the file without the extension: `http://localhost:8080/functions` which then returns a `404` due to the fact that a file without the extension doesn't exist.

I would expect this to be bundled similar to the way that it is bundled when creating a webpage that uses imports as well where everything is in one file. Is there something I am doing wrong or is this a missing Webpack feature?

Full reduced test case: https://github.com/OliverJAsh/tree-shaking-test/tree/class

Contents inlined below.

`webpack.config.js`:
```js
const pathHelpers = require("path");

const config = {
    mode: "production",
    entry: pathHelpers.join(__dirname, "./src/index.js"),
    output: {
        path: pathHelpers.join(__dirname, "./target"),
        filename: "webpack.js",
    },
};

module.exports = config;

```

`src/index.js`:
```js
import * as M from "./test";

console.log(M.foo);

```

`src/test.js`:
```js
function Iso() {}
Iso.prototype.reverse = function() {
    return new Iso();
};

export const iso = /*#__PURE__*/ new Iso();

export const foo = 1;

```

`package.json`:
```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "webpack": "^5.66.0",
    "webpack-cli": "^4.9.1"
  }
}

```


**What is the expected behavior?**

When I run `webpack`, the `Iso` class and class instance `iso` should be removed because of tree shaking. [It works in Rollup](https://rollupjs.org/repl/?version=2.63.0&), even without the `#__PURE__` annotation.

Actual behaviour: it is not removed:

```js
(()=>{"use strict";function e(){}e.prototype.reverse=function(){return new e},console.log(1)})();
```

title: Duplicate modules are found in both entrypoint chunks and async chunks
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
Under certain circumstances, modules in entrypoint chunks will be bundled into other async chunks.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Here is the reproducible test repo: https://github.com/Mensu/webpack-reproduce-issue-15173

To reproduce the issue, simply run `npm install` and `npm run build`. After the build is finished, search for `"moduleAB"` in the `dist` folder, and find out module `src/moduleAB.js` was bundled into chunk `dist/entryA.js`, chunk `dist/entryB.js` (entrypoint chunks, which is expected) and chunk `dist/commonAsync.js` (async chunks, which is unexpected)

Module `src/moduleAB.js` is statically imported by entrypoint modules `src/entryA.js` and `src/entryB.js`, therefore is available in entrypoint chunks `dist/entryA.js` and `dist/entryB.js`. Since `dist/commonAsync.js` is an async chunk, the factory of module `src/moduleAB.js` must have been available in the entrypoint chunks when we are to load `dist/commonAsync.js`, therefore it seems unnecessary to duplicately bundle `src/moduleAB.js` into `dist/commonAsync.js`.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Module `src/moduleAB.js` was only bundled into entrypoint chunks `dist/entryA.js` and `dist/entryB.js`, not into `dist/commonAsync.js`.

I kind of believe the problem lies here `availableModules.has(module) || availableModules.plus.has(m)`
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/v5.66.0/lib/buildChunkGraph.js#L906-L909

The first condition checks whether `availableModules` has `module`, but when it comes to the second condition, it checks whether `availableModules.plus` has `m` instead of `module`, which is inconsistent with the first condition. I'm not sure whether checking against `m` is intended for some reason I'm missing, or it's just a typo. Changing the condition to `availableModules.plus.has(module)` may fix this issue, but I don't know whether the change will break other codes or not.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**

body:

**What is the expected behavior?**
When building esm modules, where is no way to build them fully natively. Webpack generate code with static imports of dependencies, but also wrap them and source in its own modules which use __webpack_require__ instead of real imports.


**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
I wanted to build node application. I used tsc but I need to have some transformations with ts code (I am going to write loader for them). I use nodejs 16 and esm modules and want to see esm imports in output. So, I had them with tsc and partially have them with Webpack. Also I need this for my web app where I want to build sources (from ts with assets) to esm output.

Additional problem is that I can't build code without bundling, that I want to do for esm.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
I wanted to options for:
* disable bundling (for esm only or not): I want to see dist folder like after tsc but with assets and loader transformations.
* disable wrapping in __webpack_require__ for esm modules

Both of this points are because this manipulation are not needed for esm modules at all and brake all import/export idea.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
no, sorry.
author: tsapko3628

title: Lazy Compilation and HMR fails when dependency contains static `import` statements
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

When using `experiments.lazyCompilation`, a simple `module.hot.accept('./dep')` will fail (when `'./dep'` changes) with a message like:

```
Error: Aborted because ./simple-entry.js!lazy-compilation-proxy is not accepted
```

If the containing file contains any `import` (as opposed to `import()`) statements.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

See this reproduction repository: https://github.com/tmeasday/webpack-lazy-compilation-repro/tree/main

From the readme:

>>> 
0. Vary import technique in `simple-entry.js`
1. Run `yarn webpack serve`
2. Load http://localhost:8080
3. Edit `load.js`, notice HMR fails.
>>>

The simplest version of this looks like:

```js
import './load'; // note it works if this is import('./load')

module.hot.accept('./load'); // <- this has no effect if `load.js` is edited.
```

Note it also fails if you `import('./load')` but the file contains other static `import` statements, eg.

```js
import './null'; // <- this file is not associated with the HMR

import('./load');

module.hot.accept('./load'); // <- this has no effect if `load.js` is edited.
```

It also fails with a `import()` with a `'eager'` loading mode.

**What is the expected behavior?**

I would expect the HMR behaviour to not vary given the import style, or different to when lazy compilation is disabled (try running with `NO_LAZY=1`).

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.66.0
Node.js version: 14.18.1
Operating System: MacOS 12.0.1
Additional tools: `webpack-dev-server@4.7.3`

title: Migrate project webpack 4 to 5 : ProvidePlugin issue ?
body:
Hi,

I am migrating a web project from webpack 4 to webpack 5.

In console, i have the following error :

`rivets is not defined`

In webpack 4, without ProvidePlugin, the development output is : 

`eval("(function () {\n  var $document,\n      Cart,\n      CartJS,\n      FORMAT_MONEY_WARNING,\n`

In webpack 4, when i add ProvidePlugin : 

```
new webpack.ProvidePlugin({
  $: 'jquery',
  jQuery: 'jquery',
  rivets: 'rivets',
});
```
Everything is working and development output is now with the wrapper  :

`eval("/* WEBPACK VAR INJECTION */(function(jQuery, rivets) {(function () {\n  var $document,\n      Cart,\n      CartJS,\n      FORMAT_MONEY_WARNING,\n`

But now in webpack 5, the output is always without the wrapper `(function(jQuery, rivets) {` (even with ProvidePlugin) :

`eval("(function() {\n  var $document, Cart, CartJS, FORMAT_MONEY_WARNING`

Am i missing something ? 

Thanks.
Best regards.

title: Webpack removes parentheses inside Typescript decorators
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
I have created a repository that you can download and reproduce the issue for yourself:  https://github.com/etiennenoel/webpack-or-ts-loader-parentheses-issue

Here's an explanation:

In file `src/greeter.ts`, I have the following class and a decorator that accepts an anonymous function:

```
const test = (condition: any) => {
    return condition;
}

export class Greeter {
    @test((o: { allo: any; target: any; }) => (o.allo && o.target) || (o.allo) )
    name: string;

    constructor(name: string) {
        this.name = name;
    }

    greet(): void {
        console.log(`Hi, ${this.name}!`);
    }
}
```

If you run `npm run build` and look in the generated file `dist/main.bundle.js`, you can see that the parentheses in the condition are removed:

**Expecting to see the following parentheses**
```
...
(o.allo && o.target) || (o.allo)
...
```

**Actual result**
```
...
e.allo&&e.target||e.allo
...
```

I believe it's a bug in webpack or in ts-loader (I'm not sure how to test who's at fault here) since when I run `tsc`, I obtain the proper output:

```
...
(o.allo && o.target) || (o.allo);
...
```

title: Incorrect cache invalidation using module with side effects and re-exporting from empty file
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Cache get invalidated on the second run when using a module with side effects (like `console.log`) and re-exporting from an empty file.

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
The cache pack gets invalid on the second run. On the third run and more cache hits.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

https://github.com/7rulnik/webpack-repro-empty-export-cache-invalidation

There is side effect https://github.com/7rulnik/webpack-repro-empty-export-cache-invalidation/blob/main/convert/typescript/es.object.assign.js#L1
And there re-export from empty file https://github.com/7rulnik/webpack-repro-empty-export-cache-invalidation/blob/main/convert/typescript/index.js#L2

Run `yarn webpack` twice. You will find a log like this:

```
[webpack.cache.PackFileCacheStrategy] Pack got invalid because of write to:
ResolverCachePlugin|normal|dependencyType=|esm|path=|/Users/v7rulnik/projects/webpack-cache-test|request=|./index.js
```


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

A cache should not get invalidated on the second run.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**

You can come to the question *«Why re-export from an empty file? Just delete it»*. 
Repro is based on the real case of Storybook. Storybook imports `@storybook/addon-docs/dist/esm/frameworks/react/config.js`.

Source code of addon-docs looks like this
https://github.com/storybookjs/storybook/blob/6657698254b26df6aa7c4143eae8593bbfe5c208/addons/docs/src/lib/convert/typescript/index.ts. But `types.ts` contains only types https://github.com/storybookjs/storybook/blob/6657698254b26df6aa7c4143eae8593bbfe5c208/addons/docs/src/lib/convert/typescript/types.ts — this is why we get empty file. You can check published file https://unpkg.com/browse/@storybook/addon-docs@6.4.13/dist/esm/lib/convert/typescript/types.js And in this case, side effects come from `core-js` https://unpkg.com/browse/@storybook/addon-docs@6.4.13/dist/esm/lib/convert/typescript/convert.js. See https://unpkg.com/core-js@3.20.3/modules/es.array.map.js.


Also, the bug doesn't reproduce when `usedExports` is set to false.

----------------

webpack version:  5.66.0
Node.js version: 14.17.6
Operating System: MacOS 12.0.1 (21A559) ARM

title: Webpack 5.66.0: "Serializing big strings" warnings
body:
# Bug report

Starting with Webpack 5.66.0, my build began to issue these warnings:

```
<w> [webpack.cache.PackFileCacheStrategy] Serializing big strings (112kiB) impacts deserialization performance (consider using Buffer instead and decode when needed)
<w> [webpack.cache.PackFileCacheStrategy] Serializing big strings (109kiB) impacts deserialization performance (consider using Buffer instead and decode when needed)
```

I bisected the problem to https://github.com/webpack/webpack/commit/8ae9507196d99a52d5ea34192283b42a343c540a, "add support for url() in css". The previous commit (on both master and the [feature/css-modules branch](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/14894), https://github.com/webpack/webpack/commit/77b5589c1a6c8f5ab5583d40c3c38bbbd08236de, does not issue the warning.

[experiments.css](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/experiments/#experimentscss) is not enabled; all CSS is handled by a typical chain of `style-loader@3.3.1`, `css-loader@6.5.1`, `resolve-url-loader@5.0.0`, and `sass-loader@12.4.0`, and the slightly less typical `@teamsupercell/typings-for-css-modules-loader@2.5.1` (all versions latest). Removing the exotic `typings-for-css-modules-loader` did not resolve the issue.

**What is the current behavior?**

Warning regarding serializing large strings is emitted. Warning is unexpected based on webpack commit history and setting, and steps to resolve are unclear.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

**What is the expected behavior?**

If the warning is not legitimate for style-loader, do not emit a warning.
If the warning is legitimate, provide instructions directing the user to the provenance of the oversized string.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.66.0
Node.js version: 
Operating System: Linux 
Additional tools:  `style-loader@3.3.1`, `css-loader@6.5.1`, `resolve-url-loader@5.0.0`, `sass-loader@12.4.0`, `@teamsupercell/typings-for-css-modules-loader@2.5.1`

Possibly related to https://github.com/webpack-contrib/mini-css-extract-plugin/issues/643, but note that my build does not use any text extraction plugins

title: Value `assign-properties` of `output.library.type` not working with `export default class`
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
The library type `assign-properties` uses a loop to assign the exports. This works correctly when an object is exported, but not when a function is exported.

This is what `assign-properties` has created:
```js
/******/ function(__webpack_require__) { // webpackRuntimeModules
/******/ var __webpack_exec__ = function(moduleId) { return __webpack_require__(__webpack_require__.s = moduleId); }
/******/ var __webpack_exports__ = (__webpack_exec__("./javascripts/form-module.js"));
/******/ var __webpack_export_target__ = (ServerList = typeof ServerList === "undefined" ? {} : ServerList);
/******/ var __webpack_exports_export__ = __webpack_exports__["default"];/******/ for(var i in __webpack_exports_export__)  __webpack_export_target__[i] = __webpack_exports_export__[i];
/******/ if(__webpack_exports_export__.__esModule) Object.defineProperty(__webpack_export_target__, "__esModule", { value: true });
/******/ }
```
and `form-module.js` contains the code below:
```js
export default class Form {
  constructor() {
    console.log('Hello World');
  }
}
```
The variable `__webpack_exports_export__` contains the class Form as type `function` and therefore the loop doesn't run and 
 doesn't assign it to the library object.

In a different file I have the export below which results in an object and `assign-properties` working correctly.
```js
export default {
    Core
}
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Handle functions and other types which can't be looped and assign them correctly.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack config:
```js
{
    entry: {
        app: './javascripts/main.js',
        form: {
            dependOn: 'app',
            import: './javascripts/form-module.js',
        }
    },
    mode: 'development',
    devtool: "source-map",
    output: {
        library: {
            name: 'ServerList',
            type: 'assign-properties',
            export: 'default',
        },
        filename: '[name].bundle.min.js'
    },
    module: {
        rules: [
            {
                test: /\.js$/,
                exclude: /node_modules/,
                use: {
                    loader: 'babel-loader'
                }
            }
        ],
    },
}
```

Node.js version: 14.18.3
author: Sir-Will

title: Module Federation - add export for all modules available from a remote
body:
## Feature request

Export all components available on a remote for dynamic import being possible

**What is the expected behavior?**
Alongside with `get`  and `init` the remote scope on `window` have available a list of all the modules exposed so it can be loaded dynamic with some sort of ``modules.forEach(module => import(`remote/${module}`))`` approach.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
I am working on a master thesis project to integrate modules with MF, but our project is a SPA that is required to work offline, needing to cache all modules. I am working on this offline orchstration, so far i've a simple but work implementation as can be seen at this [issue](https://github.com/module-federation/module-federation-examples/issues/1323) on module-federation-examples, a big thanks to @ScriptedAlchemy.
But mine solution required to have a "much described" modules and remotes. I was thinking/planing/willing to do a more dinamic thing.


**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
I've made a fork of webpack and commited a version of what could work for this on [here](https://github.com/schirrel/webpack/commit/b27f058cf21d84ff64951c5cf94a1ab2e270abd3).
Even tho @ScriptedAlchemy realised that the dynamic string importation may not be work as is wishing (``import(`remote/${module}`)``), he said it on this [tweet](https://twitter.com/ScriptedAlchemy/status/1483964196949618688), having the modules available would at least turn possible to do something like
``await window[myRemoteName].get(`./${module}`);`` whitout having to specify all modules in a json within a project.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

author: mattlewis92

title: Question - Webpack Module Federation - Shared Services
body:
I have a remote application that defines several packages as shared services (singleton)

My host application that consumes it, is a legacy application that is already using these libraries.
When I declare the shared services in the module federation plugin settings, I get an error that module is not available for eager consumption (https://webpack.js.org/concepts/module-federation/#uncaught-error-shared-module-is-not-available-for-eager-consumption).
When I remove the shared property from the plugin settings object, everything seems to work.

My question is: since my host is *not* omnidirection (which is the use case discussed in the docs), is removing the shared from the *host* is a good approach ?
I am not sure if this way the host will still override the remote shared services or I will end with multiple downloads of them and/or multiple instance of the lib (which is more concerning in my case).

Thanks a lot !

* I tried to figure it from the created bundle, but it was a little hard to get.
author: hezi-bigpanda

title: Module federation: sync load support
body:
# Bug report

so, i would like to create this ticket to give more visibility to https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/11811 and see if we can finally get this fixed
<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

crash with error
`Uncaught Error: Shared module is not available for eager consumption: webpack/sharing/consume/default/react/react`

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

this is a repository to reproduce the problem
https://github.com/crodriguez-plitzi/webpack-federation-playground
* yarn
* yarn start (its using lerna)

**What is the expected behavior?**

this should works in sync ways without the requirements of "bootstraps" or lazy imports

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  latest (5.67)
Node.js version:  16.13
Operating System: ubuntu/linux
Additional tools:



@ScriptedAlchemy @alexander-akait  @sokra hi, i would like to revive this topic, after few days debugging the whole sharing modules i think we can do some changes to make sync works

basically the main problem is
![Screenshot from 2022-01-24 02-54-24](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86263126/150714473-9b8b7a0c-f5f6-476b-9e56-8eafcaae084e.png)

when we do the initial load and we try to load shared dependencies, like react in this example, this is trying to call the next code

`"webpack/sharing/consume/default/react/react": () => (loadSingletonFallback("default", "react", () => (__webpack_require__.e("vendors-node_modules_react_index_js").then(() => (() => (__webpack_require__(/*! react */ "./node_modules/react/index.js"))))))),`

logically this is wrong because, in the initial load we should not wait for the chunk, instead of that, dependencies should be loaded and stored in cache and when this be required by chunks we should be able to read the cache to get the references,

the second things is that this should be considered a cascade imports

![Screenshot from 2022-01-24 03-04-56](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86263126/150715649-cec13217-e8ed-4349-a949-2d7a0f58a013.png)

i was trying to fix it but im not very involved in the core of webpack, but after debugging this im thinking that the fix should not be too complicated to make MF be able to work in `sync` mode, and can be optional with props to keep original functionality like is actually,

the reason that MF works async is because primitive dependencies (from node_modules) are already loaded, but this doesnt make sense if from the beginning we dont have access to them, basically the `HOST` must have it loaded but the logic should not require to have `eager` enabled, this can be refactored to works in a better way to support scenarios like

scenario1: HOST1 -----> HOST2 ------> PLUGINS
scenario2: HOST1 -----> PLUGINS

what we should check if in the initial load shared dependencies arent loaded, if thats the case should be loaded

when we works based on shared we should consider that the `HOST` should provide these shared dependencies to keep some armony between dependencies if not the plugin have the "opportunity to load it from his bundle"

the main file where the problem i think is located is this one

`/webpack/lib/sharing/ConsumeSharedRuntimeModule.js`


the main problem is webpack and how he negotiate the shared resources, and thats why im suggesting it, and about versions can be designed in the way that we can store the versions also, and if the shared that is expecting the child MF plugin or the chunk is not there, we can implement a way to continue loading it in the current way getting this from `node_modules`

at high level should be like this
User Story
APP (entry point, chunk1, chunk2) and will load as MF (plugin1)
config = react will be singleton

how webpack should manage this complex scenario in sync way

something like this
![image (53)](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86263126/150754303-3857de44-62fe-489f-a426-d4adbf4d7b02.png)

webpack load process and negotiations should be based on tree logic, the first one that loads a shared dependency can be considered the host when this is configured as singleton, and works based on cache, if a MF plugin or chunk or anything else is trying to load a shared library and the version dont match, should load it and store it in the cache and keep the same flow

in any case if u want to use same resource across the whole execution this should keep using `requiredVersion: false` and also, this can solve the problem about run the whole stuff in sync way and finally remove the bootstrap hack way that is being used to make it works

```
But it does present a challenge to start an application if the dependencies are not hoisted and frontloaded asynchronously.

I believe there is a workaround that if you use shareKey and import, and give the object some key other than react, like fakeReact: it won't move out of the graph into another chunk. However you are then in host only mode, similar to eager:true
```
if a shared resource is remote, we can detect it and also we could add new attributes in webpack config to make it explicit,
but to be honest shared singleton shouldnt be remote, for remote we have explicitly `remotes` 

 ```
new ModuleFederationPlugin({
      name: "app1",
      filename: "remoteEntry.js",
      remotes: {
        app2: "app2@http://localhost:3005/remoteEntry.js",
      },
      exposes: {
        "./Button": "./src/Button",
      },
      // sharing code based on the installed version, to allow for multiple vendors with different versions
      shared: [
        {
          react: { singleton: true, requiredVersion: false },
          "react-dom": { singleton: true, requiredVersion: false },
          "react-redux": { singleton: true, requiredVersion: false },
        },
      ],
    })
```


hopefully this makes sense, sadly i still reading webpack to try be able to help, but the logic and negotiations i think is pretty clear

title: CompatibilityPlugin and Shared dependencies
body:
Still not 100% if this should be considered a bug, so before create an issue ticket i want to confirm it

after debug for a few days i found something that i still thinking is wrong

in webpack when we import a library that its already a bundle in webpack, during the build process we call the plugin `CompatibilityPlugin` that basically transforms `__webpack_require__` to `__nested_webpack_require_${pattern.range[0]}__`

my concern is that shared dependencies that were loaded by the main bundle are under `__webpack_require__` and these should be exposed to the whole application if these are `singleton`

but testing the bundle compiled i can see things like this
![Screenshot from 2022-01-26 09-12-56](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86263126/151135245-3ed69456-c0aa-4124-ad21-5775a4c5ad41.png)
![Screenshot from 2022-01-26 09-14-08](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86263126/151135404-18432975-30ff-4446-aa23-44443359202a.png)



if i marked that `react` should be singleton, why is trying to load it from the nested webpack bundle instance `__nested_webpack_require_${pattern.range[0]}__` and this is why is loading the "duplicated react" instead the original from the bundle

i can understand that each nested dependency in the bundle are completely isolated, but for scenarios where dependencies are shared/singleton doesnt make sense

should this be considered a bug ?

i did some tests doing manual changes in the bundle related to this, and was able to make works react instance

![Screenshot from 2022-01-26 10-11-55](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86263126/151144545-4c353d5a-f976-4c9a-adab-6a481c45b33f.png)

from
`var versions = scope[name] = scope[name] || {};`
to
`var versions = scope[dependencyName] = __webpack_require__.S[name][dependencyName] || scope[dependencyName] || {};` (keep on mind that this is for the scenario where `__webpack_require__` is nested and we need access to the root)

doing just 1 change i was able to make it works

title: Experimental native CSS runtime/loader throws error on async entry points
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Thanks for the great work on #14893, @sokra. I was recently testing it and it looks very promising.

However, I did find a quirk when trying to use the resulting bundle using `<script async>`.

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Suppose I have the following config:

```javascript
module.exports = {
	devtool: false,
	entry: {test: path.resolve('./src/test.js')},
	output: {
		path: path.resolve('./dist'),
	},
	experiments: {
		css: true,
	},
};
```

And two files, `src/test.js`:

```javascript
import './test.css';
```

and `src/test.css`:

```css
.example {
	font-weight: 800;
}
```


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Using webpack 5.67.0, this will create a `dist/test.css` with my CSS contents and a `dist/test.js` that also contains a `__webpack_require__` call:

```javascript
__webpack_require__(/*! ./test.css */ "./src/test.css");
```

However, this call will only succeed if the CSS has already been loaded. Otherwise, it will fail with:

```
Uncaught TypeError: __webpack_modules__[moduleId] is not a function
    __webpack_require__ /dist/test.js:23
    <anonymous> /dist/test.js:166
    <anonymous> /dist/test.js:171
```

When the JS is loaded using `async`, the chances of this are about 50:50 (even when the `<script>` tag for the JS is after the `<link>` for the CSS.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

I would assume the whole `__webpack_require__` call could be dropped since the entrypoint CSS is supposed to be a static include anyway (it would be a different story for a lazily-loaded chunk – which, by the way, seems to always work correctly).

But even if the `__webpack_require__` call is there to ensure the CSS gets loaded, it currently doesn’t do that, which seems to be a bug.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.67.0
Node.js version: 14.17.1
Operating System: WSL2
Additional tools: N/A
author: sabberworm

title: [Question]  NPM link works only when cache is set to `false`
body:
<!-- Bug reports and Feature requests must use other templates, or will be closed -->
<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter (https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack). -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What I am trying to do** :

I have a shared package as a node module that is added as a dependency in the parent app. During the development of the shared module, in the past, I usually `npm link` the shared module and then `npm link modue-name` in the parent app so I can see my changes right away rather than having to publish it. This speeds up development process as I have webpack watcher running at both ends. Any change the shared module is picked up by the watcher running in the parent app and I can right away see the changes in UI.

Version used : Webpack V4.x.x

**What is the issue** :

However, after updating Webpack to V5 (5.37.0), I noticed that the parent app was able to watch for dependencies and build the files but I was not able to see the changes in UI. I did some research and it seems to be an issue with Webpack caching. After setting the `cache: false` it just worked fine but now the watch takes couple of minutes and is trying to build the entire codebase for a single line change.

**What I need help with**

Is there a cache config I can set where it can watch for "linked modules using npm link" and rebuild quickly just like it does when I make a file change in the parent application?

**Other things I did to get `npm link` working** :

Along with the cache config, I had to also update the watchOptions and remove this `ignored: /node_modules/,`. I know we can use anymatch, regex and `not` ignore specific `node_modules`. 

Thank you!

title: In some cases, webpack is generating source maps that are not consumed by the node with --enable-source-maps flag
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behaviour?**
In some cases, Webpack is generating invalid source maps which cannot be consumed by node in `--enable-source-maps` mode. I've observed that when the project contains for example an [ajv](https://www.npmjs.com/package/ajv) or [mongodb](https://www.npmjs.com/package/mongodb) dependency the nodejs is not able to map stack trace using source maps. 

I believe this is a webpack issue because, when I bundle the same project with for example esbuild the node is working well with generated source maps. 

**If the current behaviour is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
I've prepared a reproduction repo with two super simple node + webpack setups. One with some standard dependencies like axios and moment where everything works correctly and second with ajv dependency where source maps are not consumed by the node. 

[Reproduction repository](https://github.com/dimitor115/webpack-source-maps-bug-reproduction)

To reproduce yourself:
- Create a simple node project with webpack bundler
- Add ajv dependency
- Create a file with some code that throws an error
- Bundle it using webpack (with `devtool: 'source-map' `option)
- Run with --enable-source-maps flag and see that stack trace is not mapped

**What is the expected behaviour?**

The source maps should be consumed by the node and I should see original files in the stack trace. 

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.67.0
Node.js version: v12.22.6, v14.18.1, v16.11.1
Operating System: Mac OS 12.0.1

title: asset module filename does not honor [fullhash]
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
When "[fullhash]" is included in the filename for an asset/resource, the compilation hash is not substituted into the final build output file name; the file name literally contains the substring "[fullhash]" instead.  By comparison, file-loader does support fullhash substitution in its output file names.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
In a webpack configuration, specify an assetModuleFilename (or a generator.filename) that includes "[fullhash]".  For example:

```
module.exports = {
  entry: './index.js',
  output: {
    path: path.resolve(__dirname, 'dist/'),
    filename: '[name].[fullhash].js',
    assetModuleFilename: '[name].[fullhash][ext]',
  },
  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.asset$/,
        type: 'asset/resource',
      },
      {
        test: /\.assetgen$/,
        type: 'asset/resource',
        generator: { filename: '[name].[fullhash][ext]' },
      },
      {
        test: /\.file$/,
        loader: 'file-loader',
        options: { name: '[name].[fullhash].[ext]' },
      },
    ],
  },
}
```

The build will report, for example:

```
asset bar.39bb7756e74a89205e71.file 0 bytes [emitted] [from: bar.file] (auxiliary name: main)
asset baz.[fullhash].assetgen 0 bytes [emitted] [from: baz.assetgen] (auxiliary name: main)
asset foo.[fullhash].asset 0 bytes [emitted] [from: foo.asset] (auxiliary name: main)
```

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
The substring "[fullhash]" should be replaced by the compilation hash when naming the build output files:

```
asset bar.39bb7756e74a89205e71.file 0 bytes [emitted] [from: bar.file] (auxiliary name: main)
asset baz.39bb7756e74a89205e71.assetgen 0 bytes [emitted] [from: baz.assetgen] (auxiliary name: main)
asset foo.39bb7756e74a89205e71.asset 0 bytes [emitted] [from: foo.asset] (auxiliary name: main)
```

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.67.0
Node.js version: 16.13.1
Operating System: macOS 10.15.7
Additional tools: webpack-cli 4.9.2, file-loader 6.2.0

title: Preserve star exports for externalized dependencies in module output
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**
When code contains exports from externalized libraries, the export statements are not preserved in output when they should be.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Simple isolated repro (with details in the README): https://github.com/dzearing/test-webpack-esm

We bundle a simple entry point which contains:

```js
export * from '@fluentui/utilities';
```

We turn on `output.library.type="module"` in the webpack config and mark the library as external. The bundle preserved should still emit the export. Instead it contains a bunch of unexpected content:

```js
import*as e from"@fluentui/utilities";var r={d:(e,t)=>{for(var o in t)r.o(t,o)&&!r.o(e,o)&&Object.defineProperty(e,o,{enumerable:!0,get:t[o]})},o:(e,r)=>Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(e,r)};r.d({},{});
```

**What is the expected behavior?**
The output should match the input and export * from the library.

webpack version:  5.67.0
Node.js version:  16.13.1
Operating System: Windows 11

title: moduleFederationPlugin load remote project  which with single runtime occur error
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Suppose I have two projects A and B, both of which use a single runtime, and then when I reference B in project A, I find that an error will be reported during debugging:  **"`__webpack_require__ is not defined`"**

### Config of project `A`

```js
const { ModuleFederationPlugin } = require('webpack/lib').container;
const name = 'A';
module.exports = {
  devtool: false,
  devServer: {
     port: 8061
  },
  output: {
    chunkLoadingGlobal: `${name}Chunks`,
    publicPath: 'auto',
    filename: `${name}/[name].js`
  },
  optimization: {
      runtimeChunk: { name: 'runtime' }
  },
  plugins: [
     new ModuleFederationPlugin({
       name: name,
       filename: `${name}/container.js`,
       runtime: 'runtime',
       remotes: {
          B:  ['B@http://0.0.0.0:8062/B/runtime.js', 'B@http://0.0.0.0:8062/B/container.js'] 
      }
    })
  ]
};
```

### Config of project `B`

```js
const { ModuleFederationPlugin } = require('webpack/lib').container;
const name = 'B';
module.exports = {
  devtool: false,
  devServer: {
     port: 8062
  },
  output: {
    chunkLoadingGlobal: `${name}Chunks`,
    publicPath: 'auto',
    filename: `${name}/[name].js`
  },
  optimization: {
      runtimeChunk: { name: 'runtime' }
  },
  plugins: [
     new ModuleFederationPlugin({
       name: name,
       filename: `${name}/container.js`,
       runtime: 'runtime',
       remotes: {
          A:  ['A@http://0.0.0.0:8062/A/runtime.js', 'A@http://0.0.0.0:8062/A/container.js'] 
      }
    })
  ]
};
```

Now, both project A and B has own `runtime.js` and `container.js`, and run both.

If we open project A, it will load some scripts in order of `A/runtime.js`, `A/main.js`, `A/container.js`.

and in `A/main.js`, it's script look like this:


```js
(self["AChunks"] = self["AChunks"] || []).push([
["main"],

{
  "webpack/container/reference/B/fallback-1": (module, __unused_webpack_exports, __webpack_require__) => {

"use strict";
var __webpack_error__ = new Error();
module.exports = new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
	if(typeof portal !== "undefined") return resolve();
	__webpack_require__.l("http://0.0.0.0:8062/B/container.js", (event) => {
		if(typeof B !== "undefined") return resolve();
		var errorType = event && (event.type === 'load' ? 'missing' : event.type);
		var realSrc = event && event.target && event.target.src;
		__webpack_error__.message = 'Loading script failed.\n(' + errorType + ': ' + realSrc + ')';
		__webpack_error__.name = 'ScriptExternalLoadError';
		__webpack_error__.type = errorType;
		__webpack_error__.request = realSrc;
		reject(__webpack_error__);
	}, "B");
}).then(() => (B));

}),

 "webpack/container/reference/B": ((module, __unused_webpack_exports, __webpack_require__) => {

"use strict";
var __webpack_error__ = new Error();
module.exports = new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
	if(typeof B !== "undefined") return resolve();
	__webpack_require__.l("http://172.16.220.28:8066/B/runtime.js", (event) => {
		if(typeof portal !== "undefined") return resolve();
		var errorType = event && (event.type === 'load' ? 'missing' : event.type);
		var realSrc = event && event.target && event.target.src;
		__webpack_error__.message = 'Loading script failed.\n(' + errorType + ': ' + realSrc + ')';
		__webpack_error__.name = 'ScriptExternalLoadError';
		__webpack_error__.type = errorType;
		__webpack_error__.request = realSrc;
		reject(__webpack_error__);
	}, "B");
}).then(() => (B));

}),

"webpack/container/fallback/webpack/container/reference/B/and 1 more":  ((module) => {

"use strict";
var ids = ["webpack/container/reference/B","webpack/container/reference/B/fallback-1"];
var error, result, i = 0;
var loop = (next) => {
	while(i < ids.length) {
		try {
                    // Here will found function `__webpack_require__`  is not defined
                    next = __webpack_require__(ids[i++]); 
                 } catch(e) { return handleError(e); }
		if(next) return next.then ? next.then(handleResult, handleError) : handleResult(next);
	}
	if(error) throw error;
}
var handleResult = (result) => {
	if(result) return result;
	return loop();
};
var handleError = (e) => {
	error = e;
	return loop();
};
module.exports = loop();

 })
},

// webpackRuntimeModules
__webpack_require__ => {
  var __webpack_exec__ = (moduleId) => (__webpack_require__(__webpack_require__.s = moduleId));
  // ...
});

]);
```

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

The module `"webpack/container/fallback/webpack/container/reference/B/and 1 more"` above is loaded correctly.


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

There is a way to use the runtime for both local and remote projects.

When some remote's scripts is configured as an array, it may be a good idea not to treat the latter item as a backup.

Maybe this might not be a bug, but hopefully there is the ability to allow a project that uses a single runtime to load a remote project that also uses a single runtime. If only one of them uses a single runtime, there won't be any errors here.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

title: `webpack.config.ts` ESM support
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

We should be able to import ESM modules inside a `webpack.config.ts` file.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

More and more packages are becoming ESM only. They can't be used right now in a TS configuration file.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Webpack is loading the configuration through `ts-node`, which [now supports ESM](https://github.com/TypeStrong/ts-node#commonjs-vs-native-ecmascript-modules) via the `ts-node/esm` loader.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: ESM output does not support ESM externals, because “the target environment doesn't support EcmaScriptModule syntax ”
body:
# Bug report
```javascript
//this is src/index.js
import * as Popper from '@popperjs/core';

export default Popper.createPopper(null, null);
```
```javascript
//this is webpack.config.js
import webpack from 'webpack'

export default {
    optimization: {
        minimize: false,
        concatenateModules: true
    },
    experiments: {
        outputModule: true
    },
    output: {
        library: {
            type: 'module'
        }
    },
    node: { global: false },
    externals: {
        "@popperjs/core": "@popperjs/core"
    }
}
```
My package.json has `"type": "module",`.

**What is the current behavior?**
Calling `yarn webpack` prints
```
$ /home/d/A/node_modules/.bin/webpack
assets by status 136 bytes [cached] 1 asset
orphan modules 42 bytes [orphan] 1 module
./src/index.js + 1 modules 202 bytes [not cacheable] [built] [code generated]

WARNING in configuration
The 'mode' option has not been set, webpack will fallback to 'production' for this value.
Set 'mode' option to 'development' or 'production' to enable defaults for each environment.
You can also set it to 'none' to disable any default behavior. Learn more: https://webpack.js.org/configuration/mode/

ERROR in ./src/index.js + 1 modules
The target environment doesn't support EcmaScriptModule syntax so it's not possible to use external type 'module'
while analyzing module external module "@popperjs/core" for concatenation
Error: The target environment doesn't support EcmaScriptModule syntax so it's not possible to use external type 'module'
while analyzing module external module "@popperjs/core" for concatenation
    at ExternalModule._getSourceData (/home/d/A/node_modules/webpack/lib/ExternalModule.js:590:12)
    at ExternalModule.codeGeneration (/home/d/A/node_modules/webpack/lib/ExternalModule.js:650:29)
    at ConcatenatedModule._analyseModule (/home/d/A/node_modules/webpack/lib/optimize/ConcatenatedModule.js:1659:29)
    at ConcatenatedModule.codeGeneration (/home/d/A/node_modules/webpack/lib/optimize/ConcatenatedModule.js:1102:9)
    at /home/d/A/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compilation.js:3323:22
    at /home/d/A/node_modules/webpack/lib/Cache.js:93:5
    at Hook.eval [as callAsync] (eval at create (/home/d/A/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:33:10), <anonymous>:6:1)
    at Cache.get (/home/d/A/node_modules/webpack/lib/Cache.js:75:18)
    at ItemCacheFacade.get (/home/d/A/node_modules/webpack/lib/CacheFacade.js:111:15)
    at Compilation._codeGenerationModule (/home/d/A/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compilation.js:3316:9)

webpack 5.68.0 compiled with 1 error and 1 warning in 132 ms
error Command failed with exit code 1.
info Visit https://yarnpkg.com/en/docs/cli/run for documentation about this command.
```

Removing `externals` from webpack.config.js and calling `yarn webpack` prints:
```
$ yarn webpack
yarn run v1.22.17
$ /home/d/A/node_modules/.bin/webpack
asset main.mjs 65.2 KiB [compared for emit] [javascript module] (name: main)
orphan modules 76.5 KiB [orphan] 58 modules
runtime modules 396 bytes 2 modules
./src/index.js + 52 modules 71.4 KiB [built] [code generated]

WARNING in configuration
The 'mode' option has not been set, webpack will fallback to 'production' for this value.
Set 'mode' option to 'development' or 'production' to enable defaults for each environment.
You can also set it to 'none' to disable any default behavior. Learn more: https://webpack.js.org/configuration/mode/

webpack 5.68.0 compiled with 1 warning in 485 ms
Done in 1.06s.
```
`dist/main.mjs` uses `import` and `export`, so webpack does think, that the target environment supports EcmaScriptModule syntax.

**What is the expected behavior?**
The expected behaviour is to support ESM-output with ESM-external dependencies.  That is, webpack shall not refuse to use external type 'module', when the output is ESM, reasoning that the target does not support ESM.

```
$ /home/d/A/node_modules/.bin/webpack --version
webpack: 5.68.0
webpack-cli: 4.9.2
webpack-dev-server not installed
$ node --version
v16.13.1
```

title: Exporting directories with `exports` field in package.json is now deprecated!
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

I am not sure if this is really a bug or not, but let's assume it is a bug in the documentations.

It seems that you still have the exported directories in your [documentations of package exports](https://webpack.js.org/guides/package-exports/) which is now deprecated when trying to export a directory and importing it from an the parent package that installed this exporting package (which exports), or by [self-importing](https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v14.x/docs/api/packages.html#packages_self_referencing_a_package_using_its_name) that directory.

```
(node:983501) [DEP0148] DeprecationWarning: Use of deprecated folder mapping "./" in the "exports" field module resolution of the package at /tmp/test-new-daunting-package-fields/package.json imported from /tmp/test-new-daunting-package-fields/src/a/b/c/index.js.
Update this package.json to use a subpath pattern like "./*".
(Use `node --trace-deprecation ...` to show where the warning was created)
```

title: Please consider using `CHANGES`
body:
## Feature request

#14596

**What is the expected behavior?**

To have a single `CHANGES.md`/`CHANGELOG.md` file, sync'd with git tags (moving forward)

Even better, to have it curated by hand

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26336/152665558-c7c445df-5d8c-46b9-8ba8-6a790f454a2c.png)

It's not currently possible for users to understand what's happening in the project without disproportionate effort.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

- One file.
- Markdown
- Named `CHANGES.md` or `CHANGELOG.md` or `RELEASES.md`
- Sync'd with git tags
- New PRs should be required to update change file and describe their change
- Upon releasing, the changes should be written out by hand and curated

Example, assume [v5.63.0](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/releases/tag/v5.63.0)

```markdown

## v5.63.0 - 2021-11-09

### Features
- allow passing chunkLoading: false to disable on-demand loading

#### Bugfixes
- fix import 'single-quote' in esm build dependencies
```

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
I will port it over by hand if maintainers can agree on a style / format ahead of time.

title: Can't resolve standard node library if require includes '/' (slash)
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

**doesnt_work.js:**
```
require('util/types');
```


The command below:
`node_modules/.bin/webpack --target=node --entry ./doesnt_work.js`

throws `Module not found: Error: Can't resolve 'util/types' in '/Users/pbadenski/workspace/test'`

Note: It works fine if require doesn't contain slash ie. `require('util').types`


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

**doesnt_work.js:**
```
require('util/types');
```

`node_modules/.bin/webpack --target=node --entry ./doesnt_work.js`

**What is the expected behavior?**

Running webpack `doesnt_work.js` should resolve `util/types` successfully as NodeJS documentation says `API is accessible via require('util').types or require('util/types').` (https://nodejs.org/api/util.html#utiltypes)

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
