title: Optional dependencies: as explained in #7713
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

The issue #7713 has been closed automatically. Despite considerable interest in 2020, it has not been reopened. I only assume that devs do not get notifications for comments and cross-references on closed issues. Hence this new issue.

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Optional dependencies are an NPM feature. In order to use them effectively, the JS code needs access to a mechanism that tells it whether a package actually exists in the project or not. Currently this can be done only with `require`, which has two downsides:
 * It is not the ES6 way. All packages are imported with `import`, so it's awkward to have to use a different convention.
 * Webpack still issues a warning that the imported module is missing, even if `require` is wrapped in `try .. catch`.

So, there should be a way to let Webpack know that an `import` refers to an optional dependency. If a package has been opted out of, it is, in principle, possible to list it as external in `webpack.config.js`. However, this breaks encapsulation because I would have to list optional dependencies of (recursive) dependencies as well.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
Should be clear from the previous paragraph.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
Perhaps an inline annotations along the lines of `/* webpackChunkName */`? Which would presumably silence the warning and also return `undefined` as the result of the import. For `import { stuff } from 'package'` it is a bit trickier, because returning `undefined` will make the code throw ReferenceError when loaded. I think returning `{}` or explicitly setting `stuff` to `undefined` is acceptable.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes, assuming there is some agreement about the convention that should be used
author: corwin-of-amber

title: Compilation Error while processing magic comment should not be downgraded to warning
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When building a project that contains an invalid webpackChunkName (eg. `/* webpackChunkName: foo */) the compiler emits the message

```
Compilation error while processing magic comment(-s): /* webpackChunkName: foo */: foo is not defined
```

Which then results in just a warning at the build level. This should be a build error instead of just a warning, as the chunk does end up getting built but with an unpredictable name, which can break other processes.

(The solution of course is that `foo` should be `'foo'`, but since this doesn't fail the build it can be undetected in large Webpack builds that might have lots of output)


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Reproduction here: https://github.com/ryanwilsonperkin/webpack-dynamic-import-compilation-warning
1. Fetch this repo
2. `yarn` to install dependencies
3. `yarn build`

Alternatively, create another minimal reproduction with two files that look like so:

```
// src/index.js
import(/* webpackChunkName: foo */ './foo')

// src/foo.js
console.log("A separate module")
```

And then run the default `webpack` build (which finds the src/index.js as default entrypoint)

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

I would expect that the term "Compilation Error" indicates that this is an error and should prevent the build from completing, which will make it easier for developers to detect that something is written incorrectly and correct it.
<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.73.0
Node.js version: v18.2.0
Operating System: MacOS

title: Support Workers using CommonJS syntax
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
For Webpack to support workers using CommonJS syntax when targeting node.

The following works in Node:
```js
const { Worker } = require("worker_threads");
const path = require("path");

const worker = new Worker(path.join(__dirname, "./worker.js"));
```

But is not currently transformed by Webpack.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
https://github.com/pinojs/pino/issues/1465

Essentially looking for a way that pino could support bundling it's workers without needing a specialized plugin for each existing bundler.

P.S. It could be useful for libraries starting workers dynamically to be able to get the URL/filename of a transformed worker module bundle instead of just immediately starting it or receiving a custom class that starts it.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
Recognize that as an additional syntax and transform it appropriately in a similar way the existing worker syntax is transformed.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes (But I'm not familiar with Webpack internals, and not sure where to start learning them in the docs).

title: Missing documentation for require function warning 'dependencies cannot be statically extracted'
body:
<!-- Bug reports and Feature requests must use other templates, or will be closed -->
<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter (https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack). -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

[Apparently](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/2675), there is a common issue when working with [Node's require function](https://nodejs.org/api/modules.html#modules_require) where Webpack attempts to perform static analysis of variables sent as arguments to the function, and the analysis fails. The warning is 'Critical dependency: require function is used in a way in which dependencies cannot be statically extracted'.

The [archived documentation](https://github.com/webpack/docs/wiki/context) mentions the error indirectly. As far as I can see, the [current documentation](https://webpack.js.org/guides/dependency-management/#requirecontext) doesn't mention the error at all.

Could a brief explainer of the error be added to the current documentation?
author: jzabinski-dolios

title: Module Federation shared deep dependency resolved incorrectly
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When using Module Federation, sharing a dependency and your other dependencies are using it too but in different versions, it won't respect semver matching and use the same version for everything.
For example, even when it is explicitly defined to be shared with `^8.3.2` in the top-level, it matches the `3.4.0` version that another deep dependency consumes.
```
3.4.0 does not satisfy constraint ^8.3.2
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**


Repo that reproduces the issue - https://github.com/tzachbon/mf-shared-deep-dependency

This example tries to reproduce the problem when sharing a dependency that exists in multiple versions across the node modules but resolves the request incorrectly.

In this example repo, I use `uuid` package.
```
└─┬ @startup-code/app1@0.0.0 -> ./app1
  ├─┬ lib@1.0.0 -> ./lib
  │ └── uuid@3.4.0
  ├── uuid@8.3.2
  └─┬ webpack-dev-server@4.8.1
    └─┬ sockjs@0.3.24
      └── uuid@8.3.2 deduped
```

This graph shows that the root `uuid` is 8.3.2 and the version under `lib` is 3.4.0.

`lib` uses the `v3` method from `uuid`.
This method exists only in version 8.3.2.

By default, if we build this project, serve it and click on `new uuid`, **everything crashes as expected**:
![console](https://github.com/tzachbon/mf-shared-deep-dependency/blob/master/screenshots/uuid-is-not-shared/console.png?raw=true)

But when we use Module Federation and share this package from `app1` like this:
```js
[
      new ModuleFederationPlugin({
      name: 'app1',
      shared: {
        lib: deps['lib'],
        uuid: deps['uuid'], // <-- resolve to ^8.3.2
        react: { singleton: true, requiredVersion: deps.react },
        'react-dom': { singleton: true, requiredVersion: deps['react-dom'] },
      },
    })
]
```

We can see that the runtime understands the difference between the packages:
![runtime](https://github.com/tzachbon/mf-shared-deep-dependency/blob/master/screenshots/uuid-shared/runtime.png?raw=true)

But when we click on `new uuid` which crashed our app, it works (not the desired behavior)...
![console](https://github.com/tzachbon/mf-shared-deep-dependency/blob/master/screenshots/uuid-shared/console.png?raw=true)

When we look inside the index file, we can see the `moduleToHandlerMapping` resolve it incorrectly:
![moduleToHandlerMapping](https://github.com/tzachbon/mf-shared-deep-dependency/blob/master/screenshots/uuid-shared/moduleToHandlerMapping.png?raw=true)

And when we remove the `uuid` entry from the `shared` section in ModuleFederationPlugin we can see that the app behaves as expected:
![console](https://github.com/tzachbon/mf-shared-deep-dependency/blob/master/screenshots/uuid-is-not-shared/console.png?raw=true)

For some reason, even when it is explicitly defined to be shared with `^8.3.2`, it matches the `3.4.0` version that `lib` consumes.

**What is the expected behavior?**

I would expect that each dependency would get the correct package resolution according to the node resolution algorithm.
In our example - 
* top level (App) - 8.3.2
* lib - 3.4.0


**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.72.1
Node.js version: v16.15.0
NPM version: 8.5.5
Operating System: MacOS Monterey 12.4

Also asked in the Module Federation examples repo - 
https://github.com/module-federation/module-federation-examples/issues/2033
author: tzachbon

title: warn / bail when invalid paths are used in rule.include
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

It happens sometimes that invalid paths are entered (via `path.resolve`) in `rule.include`. But webpack silently "accepts" this and the loader does nothing. But the user is confused why nothing has changed. Debugging of this simple but silent error is not that easy.

**What is the expected behavior?**

Invalid paths in `rule.include` (and probably also exclude) like for example `path.resolve(__dirname, 'src', 'node_modules'),` should throw a warning / error so the user knows that there is something not correct.


**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

Better DX and feedback from webpack, easier debugging.


**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Warning and optional setting to bail on invalid paths.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
no (not much time anymore)
author: DanielRuf

title: webpack native require doesn't work in windows
body:
I want to natively require modules without using webpack requiring mechanism, i.e keeping `require(..)` as it without touch.

to achieve my goal I created a file called `native-require.js` with this content
```
module.exports = require;
```

 and in webpack.config.js
```
module: {  
    noParse: /\/native-require.js$/,
}
```

now, in my script file I do:
```
let filePath = resolve(__dirname, `../config/file.js`);
let content = nativeRequire(filePath);

console.log(content)
```

it works fine in Linux but in windows I got the error `Cannot find module 'D:\Downloads\config\file.js'`

I also tried to remove the file extension from `filePath`


I don't want to lock my project to webpack-only using, I need my project to be able to run without webpact (for example using ts-node)

so I don't want to use webpack-specific variables such as `__webpack_require__`

title: Chrome 102 bug - workaround?
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**
There may be duplicate resource urls in the sourcemaps. Which is neither defined as valid, nor as invalid.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

This is actually a query about an existing bug report:
https://bugs.chromium.org/p/chromium/issues/detail?id=1329997

It's a known bug now in Chrome 102, which has been bugging me for 3 weeks now.  
I was wondering if there's a simple way to deduplicate urls in the sourcemap, so we can workaround the Chrome bug.  
People may come here in search of a bugreport on webpack (I initially though it was a webpack issue and downgraded but it did not help).

**What is the expected behavior?**

Should play nice with Chrome Devtools.
The guys at Chromium say that they have been going back and forth between considering duplicate urls and deduplicating them. One side of it causes one kind of bug, the other side causes another kind of bug.
So if there's an override from webpack, we could avoid this hassle altogether.

**Other relevant information:**

Operating System: WIndows 10

title: ModuleFederationPlugin shareKey bug when module has multiple libraries
body:
# Bug report

If you declare shared modules that consist of multiple internal libraries, and define shareKey for this module, you may get run-time JS exceptions `Type error: fn is not a function`, because ModuleFederationPlugin will register more internal libraries under the same shareKey, which will make some shared libraries inaccessible.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Some shared modules consist of multiple separated libraries. For example, declaring rxjs module as shared:

```
shared: {
  [ 'rxjs' ]
}
```

In this case ModuleFederationPlugin will generate and register two separated JS libraries: `rxjs` and `rxjs/operators`, and register them like so:

```
register("rxjs", "6.6.7", () =>  .... __webpack_require__(/*! ./node_modules/rxjs/_esm2015/index.js */ 5906)
register("rxjs/operators", "6.6.7", () => ... __webpack_require__(/*! ./node_modules/rxjs/_esm2015/operators/index.js */ 6370)
```

This works as expected. But there is a problem when you use shareKey in config:

```
shared: {
  [ 'rxjs': { shareKey: 'rxjs for Angular 13'  } ]
}
```

In this case ModuleFederationPlugin will register both libraries under the same key:

```
register("rxjs for Angular 13", "6.6.7", () =>  .... __webpack_require__(/*! ./node_modules/rxjs/_esm2015/index.js */ 5906)
register("rxjs for Angular 13", "6.6.7", () => ... __webpack_require__(/*! ./node_modules/rxjs/_esm2015/operators/index.js */ 6370)
```

This will produce unexpected run-time JS errors: `Type error: fn is not a function`, whenever a function from the first library is called, because the function will be looked-up in the second library because it will override the first registered library due to the same key.

**What is the expected behavior?**

ModuleFederationPlugin should correcly register and resolve multiple libraries (because developers are not aware if shared module consists with more libraries). 

There is currently a workaround for this problem if you need to use shareKey: you must explicitely define all internal libraries for a shared module, and define them unique shareKeys:

```
shared: {
  [ 
    'rxjs': { shareKey: 'rxjs for Angular 13'  },
    'rxjs/operators': { shareKey: 'rxjs/operators for Angular 13'  },
 ]
}
```

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.73.0
Node.js version: 16.15.1
Operating System: Windows 10
Additional tools:

title: Property 'webpackHot' does not exist on type 'ImportMeta'
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**
Running a typescript type check fails with the error `Property 'webpackHot' does not exist on type 'ImportMeta'`.  

**Steps to reproduce (I think)**
In `package.json` I have `"type": "module"`.  
In `webpack.config.js` I have `hot: true`.  
In `tsconfig.json` I have `"module": "nodenext"` and `"moduleResolution": "nodenext"`.  
The place where it breaks is after following the official documentation setup:

```
// @ts-expect-error
if (import.meta.webpackHot) {
  // @ts-expect-error
  import.meta.webpackHot.accept()
}
```

Then running `tsc --emitDeclarationOnly` on the codebase breaks the above `import.meta.webpackHot` and gives the above error.  

**What is the expected behavior?**
I am not sure what is wrong here, but I would expect that webpack should have a type for it.  The documentation shows to use it like above so I'm not sure why typescript says it doesn't exist.  

**Note:**
Webpack still seems to work fine, and the hot reloading actually works just fine, it seems like a false error, purely type related.  However it is breaking my lint-staged type check which is annoying so for now I've let typescript ignore the code using `@ts-expect-error`.  

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.73
Node.js version: 18
Other tools being used: react-refresh

title: Custom runtime chunk loader (because urls are unknown during build time)
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

Load chunks from locations, which are unavailable during build time.

When uploading JavaScript assets to Hubspot CMS, the files are automatically minified, post-processed and pushed to a CDN, for caching reason Hubspot delivers each asset **from a different location**. So I am looking for a solution to load chunks from a location, which is **not known during build time**.

Find more details at the end.

**What is the expected behavior?**

Possibility to inject a custom chunk loader which provided the correct filename without the need of hacking the created JavaScript files.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

During upload to Hubspot, JavaScript files are post processed and served from different locations for caching reasons.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Unclear.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

## More details

when uploading JavaScript assets to Hubspot CMS, the files are automatically minified, post-processed and pushed to a cdn, for caching reason Hubspot delivers each asset from a different location. So I am looking for a solution to load chunks from a location, which is not known during build time.

**Locally**

 - js/widget.js
 - js/41.widget.js
 - js/393.widget.js

**Remote**

- http://f.hubspotusercontent-eu1.net/hub/xxx/hub_generated/template_assets/50239061735/1656576587321/index.js
- http://f.hubspotusercontent-eu1.net/hub/xxx/hub_generated/template_assets/50238026977/1656576587779/41.widget.js
- http://f.hubspotusercontent-eu1.net/hub/xxx/hub_generated/template_assets/unknown/location/393.widget.js

And here the challenge starts, as 41.widget.js or 393.widget.js cannot be loaded, as they are not within the same url as index.js. Unfortunately, the final urls are not available during build time, the are created during upload to Hubspot.

During the upload to hubspot, some placeholders within the JavaScript assets are replaced (Hubspot Template language HubL). So, if I manage to define the output.publicPath **for each chunk** as ``https://{{get_asset_url('./js/<chunk>.js')}}`` Hubspot could replace this by the correct url. 

I tried to manually patch the created index.js which does the trick, but seems a bit risky for me, unstable and hard to automate.

```
!function () {

    /* much more */

    o.u = function (e) {
        console.log("Load chunk");
        console.log(e);
        var result = "";
        if (e === 41) result = "{{get_asset_url('/theme/js/widgets/41.widgets.js')}}";
        if (e === 393) result = "{{get_asset_url('/theme/js/widgets/393.widgets.js')}}";
        console.log(result);
        return result;

    }, o.p = "https://", 

    /* much more */
}();

```

import path from 'path';
import { fileURLToPath } from 'url';

const __filename = fileURLToPath(import.meta.url);
const __dirname = path.dirname(__filename);
```

And I can't remove `import.meta.url` because I'm using modules and I need to polyfill `__filename` and `__dirname`.   I've gone though the documentation, and I don't see what I'm missing.  So, I can only assume this is a webpack bug.  


Contents of `./dist` with `target: 'node'`:
```
$ ls -al ./dist
total 44512

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

  "module": "./dist/index.bundle.mjs",

  },
```

`tsconfig.json`:
```
{

    "allowJs": true,
    "target": "es2021",

Complete `webpack.config.js`:
```
import path from 'path';
import webpack from 'webpack';
import { fileURLToPath } from 'url';

const __filename = fileURLToPath(import.meta.url);
const __dirname = path.dirname(__filename);

export default {
    target: 'async-node18.3',

After I upgraded webpack 4 to wepack5 。The react-loadable plugin failed chunk.forEachModule is not a function.
code：

```
function buildManifest(compiler, compilation) {
  var context = compiler.options.context;
  var manifest = {};

  compilation.chunks.forEach(function (chunk) {
    chunk.files.forEach(function (file) {
      chunk.forEachModule(function (module) {
        var id = module.id;
        var name = typeof module.libIdent === 'function' ? module.libIdent({ context: context }) : null;
        var publicPath = url.resolve(compilation.outputOptions.publicPath || '', file);

        var currentModule = module;
        if (module.constructor.name === 'ConcatenatedModule') {
          currentModule = module.rootModule;
        }
        if (!manifest[currentModule.rawRequest]) {
          manifest[currentModule.rawRequest] = [];
        }

        manifest[currentModule.rawRequest].push({ id: id, name: name, file: file, publicPath: publicPath });
      });
    });
  });

  return manifest;
}

```


How can I fix this bug and make it work？

When I load it into the browser I get what appears to be a syntax error saying that a function is not defined. 

`Uncaught TypeError: $(...).datetimepicker is not a function`

To rule out any mistakes on my part, I installed the module from the npm repo, moved it to a different location, then installed it as a local module, but got the same error. This means that identical modules are treated differently depending on how they were installed. 

**Problems:** There are a number of problems / inconsistencies that I noticed:

1. I have to manually go into the local module and install it's dependencies, even though they are already installed in the main project.
2. After examining the bundle with minimization disabled, I noticed I have 2 jQuery entries
3. This duplicate jQuery chunk (both the exact same version) seems to be causing the syntax error I mentioned before.


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Download the attached minimal example and follow the steps:

```
cd test
npm install
webpack

```
At this point everything works. Open the html in your browser and click on the fields, you will get the datetimpicker and no errors.

Next install as local module:

```
mv node_modules/jquery-datetimepicker ~/
npm install --save ~/jquery-datetimepicker

```

At this this point you'll noticed that:

1. Package.json now specifies the path to the module
2. In node_modules, the jquery-datetimepicker module directory is replaced with a link to the actual module location


If you now try to run webpack, you'll get errors that jquery-datetimepicker is missing dependencies. To get pass that, you would need to:

```
cd ~/jquery-datetimepicker
nmp install

```

After you've done that, you'll be able to run webpack, but the bundle will contain a duplicate jQuery chunk (even if I force the use of the same version of jQuery by modifying jquery-datetimepicker's package.json file) and you'll get the syntax error when you try to render.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub gist. -->
<!-- Best provide a minimal reproducible repo -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
I would expect the module to be resolved the same way regardless of it's location. 
- I should not have to install the jquery-datetimepicker dependencies by hand.
If I understand the resolve behavior, it should go abc/node_modules/jquery-datetimepicker/node_modules. When it doesn't find that it should try abc/node_modules where it should be able to find everything it needs.
- I should get only a single jQuery chunk
- I should not be getting a syntax error.


I manually removed the duplicate jQuery chunk and fixed the dependency IDs, and that seems to fix the problem. Not sure why, as far as I can tell there is no syntax error in the file, but it could be some sort of chunk ordering issue.

**If this is a feature request, what is motivation or use case for changing the behavior?**

**Please mention other relevant information such as the browser version, Node.js version, webpack version, and Operating System.**

Here is the system info:
[system_info.txt](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/files/9084782/system_info.txt)


Minimal example: 

[test.zip](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/files/9085573/test.zip)

title: Automatic publicPath selection fails due to additional external script
body: # Bug report **What is the current behavior?** If the `publicPath` value (under `output`) is not set in the Webpack config, Webpack calls the `AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule` ([link](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/c181294865dca01b28e6e316636fef5f2aad4eb6/lib/runtime/AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule.js)) to find the current path by looking at the last included script file. This works if the last script file is [a] not an inline script and [b] part of the webpack output and therefore the src attribute is a reasonable reference to the intended public path. If neither of those criteria is matched, e.g. a script added by Google Tag Manager, the `AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule` cannot find the current path, and throws the following error: `Automatic publicPath is not supported in this browser` The red rectangle on the screenshot below highlights an example of an external script added to the end of the script list. It is added by Google Tag Manager on Linux devices only and results in the error described above. On Windows and macOS, this Google Tag Manager script is not added and the last script is `main.5dd691d8552e0c47.js`. This script is then selected by the `AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule` to find the current path, and works without issues. **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** See above. **What is the expected behavior?** While it can be solved by setting the `publicPath` value in the Webpack config file, the `AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule` should not use the last script to find the current path, as this is prone to errors caused by other packages adding additional external scripts. If that is not possible, the logged error message (`Automatic publicPath is not supported in this browser`) could be improved, as it is supported in this browser, but obstructed by a script added by a different package. **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.64.0 Node.js version: v16.11.1 Operating System: Linux (issue), Windows (no issue), MacOS (no issue) Additional tools: Chrome (V103), Firefox
author: yvanham

title: Is it possible for webpack module federation to support multiple versions of a singleton library running?
body:
So currently I have the following architecture:
- one host react app
- 2 microfrontend react apps
- a component library which I have developed

Currently, the only way the component library can work with the current setup is if we declare it as a singleton (I believe this was needed for theming and sharing state). However, for our use case this is not scalable as teams working on different apps may not want to upgrade their version of the component library at the same time as other apps. Therefore, we may need multiple versions of the component library running at the same time.

Is it possible for webpack module federation to support multiple versions of a singleton library running?

body:

<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
```
async nameOfFunc() {
  this.doStuff();
}
``` 
is transpiled to 

```
nameOfFunc() {
  var _this = this;
  
  return (0,node_modules_babel_runtime_helpers_esm_asyncToGenerator_js__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__["default"])(function* () {
    _this.doStuff();
  }
}
```

```
nameOfFunc = async () => {
  this.doStuff();
}
``` 
is transpiled to 

```
nameOfFunc = (0,node_modules_babel_runtime_helpers_esm_asyncToGenerator_js__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__["default"])(function* () {
    _this.doStuff();
}
```

```
nameOfFunc = () => {
  this.doStuff();
}
``` 
is transpiled to 

```
nameOfFunc = () => {
  this.doStuff();
}
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
I believe the current behavior is a bug, as shown above by simple switching from an async function to an async arrow function the `_this` is no longer created.

This issue also only starts occurring when changing the tsconfig target from 2020 -> 2022

title: [Webpack Stats] Improve how dynamically imported chunks are determined as "initial"
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

Right now when analyzing my compilation stats some async chunks are being marked as "initial" because they are also used in regular imports from within other entry points. Here's an example to illustrate this:
- first entry
  - Dynamic import for "hello.js"
    - "hello.js" then contains a dynamic import for "world.js"
- second entry
  - Regular import for "world.js"

When using the SplitChunksPlugin the result is that the chunk containing "world.js" is marked as an "initial" chunk, even though it is also used as an async chunk. This messes with reporting tools based on the compilation stats because since the "world.js" chunk is marked as "initial" it counts towards the "initial" size of the "first entry" bundle.

I mocked up a small example to reproduce this here: https://github.com/hyshka/webpack-async-chunk-marked-as-initial

**What is the expected behavior?**
I would expect to be able to determine through the compilation stats list of chunks when a chunk is included as "initial" or "async" for each location that it is used.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
The motivation for me purely comes from reporting on the size of entry points and how much code is loaded immediately versus asynchronously.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
I am not very familiar with how the compilation stats are implemented but I think you may be able to do this if you computed the "initial" value for each origin within the chunk. Obviously, this could add a lot of complexity.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
Not at this time.

author: ridhwaans

title: Global asset generator configuration with filename breaks usage of inline asset rules
body:
# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
Specifying a `filename` value in `config.module.generator.asset` breaks any usage of `asset/inline` module rules. The [docs](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/module/#modulegenerator) do not specify what the behavior should be if `filename` is set at the `asset` level, but once set there it is impossible to override on `asset/inline` or any `module.rules`.

**Steps to reproduce.**
A config with at least the below options:
```
{
  module: {
    generator: {
      filename: 'any_value',
    },
    rules: [
      { test: /some_file_regex/, type: 'asset/inline' }
    ]
  }
}
```

Import a file that matches the test for the rule and observe this error:
```
Module not found: Invalid generator object. Asset Modules Plugin has been initialized using a generator object that does not match the API schema.
 - generator has an unknown property 'filename'. These properties are valid:
   object { dataUrl? }
   -> Generator options for asset/inline modules.
```

**What is the expected behavior?**
Unsure, but nextjs is configured this way and it makes it unclear how to add custom `asset/inline` rules. See [this discussion](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/36981) for more details.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.73.0
Node.js version: 16
Operating System: MacOS Big Sur
Additional tools: next@12.1.6

body:

<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
Simplified description of my scenario:

I have a typical setup of 3 angular apps built with module federation plugin.

host-app (shell-app)
remote-app (mfe1)
remote-app (mfe2)
The shell app loads the remote-apps on demand according to user navigation. Pretty straight forward.

Problem:
When user visits the app, it loads the remoteEntry files of mfe1 and mfe2.
Let's say that user navigates to mfe1 and spend some time working with that app.
In the meanwhile, a new version of mfe2 has been deployed, replacing everything related to the previous version of mfe2.
Now if the user decides to go to app2, it will fail to load, because it tries to load chunks based on a stale remoteEntry.js

Note:
This can happen also if i don't initially load all remoteEntry.js files. It can happen even if remoteEntry.js is loaded dynamically and some chuck has been updated.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

body:

**What is the current behavior?**
I have a worker which imports a local js file using the ```self.importScripts()``` syntax, however when I try to trigger it, I receive a 404 error in the browser console. The 404 due to the worker trying to read the path provided in my code, rather than a bundled version (specifically it looks for http://localhost:8081/maths/decomposition.js). This leads me to believe that when the project is bundled, it does not update the path in the ```self.importScripts()``` function.

The worker code is as follows:
```js
self.onmessage = function ({data: question}) {
    console.debug("decomposer called");
    self.importScripts("../../maths/decomposition.js");
    let decomposer = new Decomposition(
        question.dataset1,
        question.dataset2,
        question.maxOrder
    );
    console.debug("Starting decomp");
    let startTime = Date.now();
    decomposer.overlap();
    decomposer.decompose();
    
    console.debug("That took:", (Date.now() - startTime)/1e3, "seconds"); 
    console.debug("fv1", decomposer.featureVector1Raw);
    console.debug("fv2", decomposer.featureVector2Raw);
    // Decomposition.printMatrix(concat(decomposer.featureVector1, decomposer.featureVector2));
    self.postMessage({decomposer});

};
```
I originally had been trying to import using the ```import { x } from y``` syntax but that gave the error "import declarations may only appear at the top level of a module".
The code calling the worker is:
```js
            let url = new URL("./decompWorker.js", import.meta.url);
            console.log("url", url);
            const worker = new window.Worker(url);
            console.debug("worker made");
            worker.postMessage({ 
                question: {
                    dataset1: this.dataset1,
                    dataset2: this.dataset2,
                    maxOrder: this.maxOrder
                }
            });
            console.debug("message posted");
            worker.onmessage = ({ data: {answer}}) => {
                console.debug(answer);
            };
```
as part of a Vue component. I believe this is related to #15165 , but not identical. One workaround I did try was to just combine the worker and the decomposition.js file, but this resulted in the same import declaration error.

     <div className="App">

```

```console

$ cat /tmp/_.diff | git apply
$ (npm run build && cd build && python3 -m http.server)

#### production

body:
# Bug report when i upgrade my builder to webpack5，it compile right, but it run failed with the error: ``` Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'render') ``` here is my demo code: ```js import { useState } from 'react'; import ReactDOM from 'react-dom'; import { Button } from 'antd'; import { StepBackwardOutlined } from '@ant-design/icons' const App = () => { const [cn, setCn] = useState(5); return (
cn value is {cn}
); }; ``` **What is the current behavior?** I debuged, and found the defference between from v5 and v4: v4: ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/22979584/182003784-4459e2f0-a56c-4681-aa44-55c483329fde.png) v5: ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/22979584/182003786-177a5eb3-4d1f-494a-923f-85d3c66e1321.png) webpack5 when set output.libraryTarget to system, the default import is directly from the module（module['default']）, but webpack4 import with __webpack_require__.n(__webpack_require__.n(module))，it will tell wether the module is esmodule。 and I also found, maybe the problem is all the extarnal treat as an esmodule, so when import it ignore whether is an esmodule or not. `but react or react-dom is not an esmodule, which don't have a default export` ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/22979584/182005742-b4df9100-8228-45ab-a437-2305e633c7d8.png) **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** here is my demo: git clone https://github.com/closertb/webpack5Demo.git npm i npm start open in chrome, you can find the bug. when checkout the branch to v4. and npm i, npm start, you can see it render ok in the browser **What is the expected behavior?** the code runs correct without any change; don't treat all the external module to `esmodule` when output.libraryTarget is system。 **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.74.0 Node.js version: 14 Operating System: macos Additional tools:

title: "Auto" publicPath does not work well with "amd"
body:
**Do you want to request a *feature* or report a *bug*?**
bug

**What is the current behavior?**
if publicPath is set to "auto" and an AMD module loader (like require.js) is used to load the output file, wrong publicPath gets computed. The publicPath that gets computed is the path of the last script loaded.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Please follow the gist at : https://gist.github.com/eramitmittal/1e41e8b96ca26dc7fefdc0310a5dd178
The gist uses webpack to prepare code-split AMD module. 
It has an index.html which loads the webpack output and then a script (loader.js) uses require.js to actually run the code captured in the AMD module. It can be seen from console output that the auto computed publicPath refers to location of loader.js instead of the webpack output.

**What is the expected behavior?**
Expected behavior is that publicPath should get auto-computed correctly.

Current behavior happens as document.currentScript.src does not work in case the code is being called in the context of a callback. AMD require, executes the code as callback. Due to this auto-compute logic of publicPath falls back to looking at the src property of the last loaded script. This is highly error-prone.

Instead of the last script, src property of the script tag with the required chunk name in it can be looked at.

For instance:
                        var scripts = Array.from(document.getElementsByTagName("script")).filter((script)=> /.*my-bundle.js$/.test(script.src)); //regEx should be prepared dynamically using the chunk name
                        if(scripts.length) scriptUrl = scripts[scripts.length - 1].src

instead of 
			var scripts = document.getElementsByTagName("script");
                        if(scripts.length) scriptUrl = scripts[scripts.length - 1].src


**If this is a feature request, what is motivation or use case for changing the behavior?**

**Please mention other relevant information such as the browser version, Node.js version, webpack version, and Operating System.**
webpack version: "5.74.0"
author: eramitmittal

title: Cannot read property '__esModule' of undefined
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
We have a components library where we have multiple entry points setup for components. Since we had nesting of components as well, we were ending up duplicating the code. 

Hence we used the optimization block inside the config.
```
optimization: {
    splitChunks: {
      chunks: 'all',
      minChunks: 1,
      minSize: 1000,
      maxInitialRequests: Infinity,
      cacheGroups: {
        vendorCss: {
          test: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/](@hotstar\/isomorphic-style-loader|css-loader)[\\/]/,
          name: 'vendor_css_loaders',
          chunks: 'all',
        },
      }
    },
```

But after using this to bundle our components lib, and using this library inside the next.js app we get the error
```
TypeError: Cannot read property '__esModule' of undefined
    at /Users/ajayjaggi/Desktop/Hotstar/web-website/node_modules/@hotstar/web-ui-components-lib/modern/components/Molecule/CustomError/src/index.js:328:34
    at Object.<anonymous> (/Users/ajayjaggi/Desktop/Hotstar/web-website/node_modules/@hotstar/web-ui-components-lib/modern/components/Molecule/CustomError/src/index.js:330:12)
    at Module._compile (internal/modules/cjs/loader.js:1085:14)
    at Object.Module._extensions..js (internal/modules/cjs/loader.js:1114:10)
    at Module.load (internal/modules/cjs/loader.js:950:32)
    at Function.Module._load (internal/modules/cjs/loader.js:790:12)
    at Module.require (internal/modules/cjs/loader.js:974:19)
    at require (internal/modules/cjs/helpers.js:93:18)
    at Object.@hotstar/web-ui-components-lib/modern/components/Molecule/CustomError/src (/Users/ajayjaggi/Desktop/Hotstar/web-website/packages/website/.next/server/pages/_app.js:1273:18)
    at __webpack_require__ (/Users/ajayjaggi/Desktop/Hotstar/web-website/packages/website/.next/server/webpack-runtime.js:33:42)

title: [Discussion] How to estimate the time complexity of the webpack build/compilation?
body:
<!-- Bug reports and Feature requests must use other templates, or will be closed -->
<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter (https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack). -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
Let's say we use typescript on a project, it has 1000 lines of code, and there have 10 esmodule dependencies, and we assume the base compilation complexity of the typescript loader is `n`, and the per dependency bundle is `m`, can we estimate a approximate time complexity of webpack build this project to a single js file? Is there a formula like `<line of code> * <loader complexity> + <number of dependecies> * <bundle complexity>` so the final time complexity of this project would be `1000 * n + 10 * m`

body:

**What is the current behavior?**

When using `swr/infinite` with `swr>1.1.2` dependency and sharing it with [Module Federation (SharePlugin)](https://webpack.js.org/concepts/module-federation/)
```js
    new webpack.container.ModuleFederationPlugin({
      shared: ['swr'],
    }),
```

We encountered this error:\
![with-mf](https://github.com/tzachbon/swr-mf-error/blob/main/screenshots/with-mf.png?raw=true)


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I created this repo that reproduces the issue: https://github.com/tzachbon/swr-mf-error

All you have to do is to use SWR >= 1.2.0 and add it to the shared array in the Module Federation Plugin.
In the change log here: https://github.com/vercel/swr/compare/1.1.2...1.2.0
I found a few things that might be the reason.

In my code, I try to import `swr/infinite`, which now has exports field in its package json:
```
  "exports": "./dist/index.mjs",
```

In the `swr` package json, the exports field now has a filename that ends with `mjs`extension:
```
    "./infinite": {
      "import": "./infinite/dist/index.mjs",
      "module": "./infinite/dist/index.esm.js",
      "require": "./infinite/dist/index.js",
      "types": "./infinite/dist/infinite/index.d.ts"
    },
```

**_When I manually changed the filename to `.js` it worked._**

title: discuss for remove unused argument in runtime function template in webpack5
body:
I'm not sure if this is a bug or expected feature(not compatibility for the wrong module library)，so I marked it as discuss.

## good case
when we not use module spec keyword like `module`,`require` ,`import`,`export` etc in js file
```js
// a.js
console.log(1)
```

in webpack4 ,we will get output like 
```js
function(module, exports){
  console.log(1)
}
```

but webpack5 ( [maybe this commit](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/commit/2525c8b7aa1db995f57f973f54a1c0989334f1a8) ) ,we will get output like
```js
function(/* argument is omit for unused */){
  console.log(1)
}
```
I think this is a useful optimization in most cases。

## bad case
However. suppose you have a library whick name "A"
```js
// my simple lib A
eval("if(typeof module === 'object') module.exports='A'")
```

in webpack4 we will get string `"A"` with  `import A from "A"`
```js
function(module, exports){
  eval("if(typeof module === 'object') module.exports='A'")
}
```
but in webpack5 we get an empty object `{}`. because arcon ast can not detect the correlation of the `module` variable.so that the first module argument will be remove due to unused
```js
function(/* argument is omit for unused */){
  eval("if(typeof module === 'object') module.exports='A'")
}
```

## real case
we use a lib which name watermark-dom(it has a star close to 1k [github](https://github.com/saucxs/watermark-dom) [npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/watermark-dom)) in webpack5

Below is the minimal reproduce link
https://github.com/VisonM/webpack-reproduce-demo

## Summarize
I have to say the npm package of watermark-dom is terrible, but do we still need to be compatible with such libs？if we should keep unused argument when code exist some keyword like `eval`,`new Function` etc

const path = require('path');

input code

const abc =  {

output code

console.log({c:()=>({cc:1})}); // Hopefully there's no arrow function

Webpack target should actively configure Terser's options.compress.arrows = False if ES5 is present

terserOptions = {

Exported enums are undefined at runtime.

`util_types__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__` contains no property named `SessionViewType`. This enum is exported from `util.ts` and imported into a Vue SFC using `import { SessionViewType } from "util/types"`. If import explicitly includes `.ts` extension, the issue is resolved. This also affects third-party packages.

Enums should be present Webpack module objects.

            {

            },
            {

title: Module federation dynamic remotes with objects containing React components.
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
I am currently trying to load a React component (functional) which is coming from an object as a remote.
```js
// config from remote app
export default (settings: Settings) => ({
  id: 'GARAGE',
  nameLocale: localizedAppName[settings?.language ?? defaultLanguage],
  color: '#fff',
  backgroundColor: '#333',
  path: '/garage',
  icon: AppIcon,
  app: App,
  theme,
});
```

Now, I am following a pattern from https://github.com/OmerHerera/dynamic_remote 
```js
export const loadComponent = (scope, module, props) => {
  return async (): Promise<any> => {
    await __webpack_init_sharing__('default');
    const container = window[scope];

    await container.init(__webpack_share_scopes__.default);
    const factory = await window[scope].get(module);
    const Module = factory();

    // Here I am accessing the `app` function from the config seen above.
    // and it does in fact log out a function
    console.log('module app', Module.default().app);
    const AppModule = Module.default(props).app;

    return AppModule;
  };
};
```
However, when used in a `ModuleLoader` component like this (below), the `Component` is suddenly undefined or simply and object (even though it logs out as a function, and here's the funny part. If I just expose the actual `App` component from the remote, it suddenly works and loads just fine. Is this expected? Can it be something with using React.lazy? 

And just to be clear, if we're using "static" remotes i.e predefined in the webpack.config in the host app, it works, but ofc with another approach to load these functions that is.
```js
function ModuleLoader(config) {
  console.log('loader config', config);
  const { ready, failed } = useDynamicScript({
    url: config.module && config.url,
  });

  // ... some if statements returning fallback messages

  const Component = lazy(loadComponent(config.scope, config.module, config.props));

  return (
    <Suspense fallback="Loading Module">
      <Component />
    </Suspense>
  );
}
```


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
That the function we return (`Module.default(props).app;`)  from `loadComponent` stays as a function and is loaded into the host app as a function, instead of being somehow turned into a an object/undefined.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.74.0
Node.js version:  16.13.0
Operating System:  Win10
Additional tools:

title: Should contentHash happen before the moduleIds process?
body:
## Feature request

I notice our static dependency file's hash will be changed sometimes. After my investigation it's caused by different `moduleIds`.
I take an experiment to prove it. 
First, I saved my project in two version while their `monaco-editor` versions are identity. When I used the `moduleIds: 'named'` and built my  project in prod env. They got the same content hash. 
But when I changed the `modulesIds` to an Id type, e.g. 'deterministic', 'size'. They got different hash values. 
I did not have idea for why it happened since it was hard to read in prod env. But I think it was caused by importing different modules.
However, the dependencies's content are identity. So I expect the content hash will be identity.


**What is the expected behavior?**
Hash content should not be disturbed by `moduleIds`.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
To avoid the content hash being disturbed by moduleIds

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
Hash content before moduleIds optimization will avoid this issue.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
no

**What is the current behavior?**

Webpack throws this error when using asset modules + disk caching:

> Error: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'get')
during rendering of asset asset/resource|/Users/mattlewis/Code/clickup/frontend/node_modules/.pnpm/resolve-url-loader@5.0.0/node_modules/resolve-url-loader/index.js??ruleSet[1].rules[0].use[0]!/Users/mattlewis/Code/clickup/frontend/node_modules/.pnpm/sass-loader@13.0.2/node_modules/sass-loader/dist/cjs.js??ruleSet[1].rules[0].use[1]!/Users/mattlewis/Code/clickup/frontend/libs/theme/quill/quill-lazy-styles.scss?lazy-styles

The build works fine the first time, but then when making a change to the source and rebuilding it throws this error.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I'm really struggling to reproduce this outside of our app, it seems very nuanced and even the conditions to consistently reproduce are difficult.

After some debugging I determined the source of the regression was this change: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/15515

The error is getting thrown here:
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/9fcaa243573005d6fdece9a3f8d89a0e8b399613/lib/asset/AssetModulesPlugin.js#L186 which is throwing because both `module.buildInfo.filename` and `codeGenResult.data` are `undefined`.

title: Paths to relative modules should be expressed as file names
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
Webpack is failing to bundle a file due to how an imported local module ('webpack/lib/Module.js') is imported (in 'webpack/lib/FileSystem.js'). 

See here in `FileSystemInfo.js`. 

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/9fcaa243573005d6fdece9a3f8d89a0e8b399613/lib/FileSystemInfo.js#L9

I am receiving the following error, which indicates that webpack is failing to resolve it's own local module. I suggest we simply change this line to be the name of the local file it's targeting. 

```
ERROR in ../node_modules/webpack/lib/FileSystemInfo.js 9:19-36
Module not found: Error: Can't resolve 'module' in '/home/work/s/REDACTED/node_modules/webpack/lib'
resolve 'module' in '/home/work/s/REDACTED/node_modules/webpack/lib'
  Parsed request is a module
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I am simply running webpack with the following configuration on one JavaScript file, a service-worker.template.js file.
Here is my relatively simple webpack configuration. I have quite a few fallbacks defined to polyfill parts of nodejs which I need while building. I've specified `preferRelative`, which I expect to fix the issue of resolving `import nodeModule = require('module');` to `Module.js`, which is a file sitting directly next to `FileSystemInfo.js`.

```
module.exports = {
    mode: 'production',
    entry: './service-worker.template.ts',
    output: {
        path: path.resolve(__dirname, '.'),
        filename: 'service-worker.js',
    },
    module: {
        rules: [
            {
                test: /\.ts$/,
                loader: 'ts-loader',
            },
        ],
    },
    resolve: {
        preferRelative: true,
        extensions: ['.js', '.ts'],
        fallback: {
            os: require.resolve('os-browserify/browser'),
            stream: require.resolve('stream-browserify'),
            constants: require.resolve('constants-browserify'),
            http: require.resolve('stream-http'),
            https: require.resolve('https-browserify'),
            vm: require.resolve('vm-browserify'),
            crypto: require.resolve('crypto-browserify'),
            process: require.resolve('process'),
            url: require.resolve('url'),
            querystring: require.resolve('querystring-es3'),
            child_process: false,
            fs: false,
            worker_threads: false,
            inspector: false,
            zlib: require.resolve('browserify-zlib'),
        },
    },
    plugins: [
        new webpack.ProvidePlugin({
            process: 'process/browser',
        }),
    ],
};
```

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

I'm expecting that when setting `resolve.preferRelative`, Webpack would resolve, when saying `require('module')` to attempt to load from the local `Module.js` file.

<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

Node.js version: 16.5.1
Operating System: Windows 11 (Build 22000)

Webpack build succeeds, but loading the page in the browser throws an error 

> Uncaught ReferenceError: vendor_lib is not defined

webpack.dll.common.js

const plugins = [

      glob: path.resolve(moduleDirectory, '../dist/js/*.dll.js'),

`

The webpack.common.js is used in a dev and a production configuration file. I am using the dev configuration with a devServer and hotReloadModule to serve the website. title: Automatically tree shake dynamic imports without needing magic comments
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
This is a feature request after I asked this question: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/15565. The answer to the question said to use `webpackExports` magic comment to manually tree-shake import. This feature request is for webpack to automatically tree shake common dynamic import patterns that only import some exports.

Example of common patterns:
```js
// Basically simplest way of just importing `meta` export.
(await import('./index.js')).meta
```

```js
// To import multiple exports, but not all of them
const { a, c } = await import('./exportsABC')
```
<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Code imported with `(await import('./index.js')).meta` should tree shake to only include `meta` export.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
There are two problems with doing `/* webpackExports: ["abc", "default"] */`:
1. Extra line of code, usually have to look up syntax, might be hard to confirm that it is actually working.
2. Renaming the exports with TypeScript will not automatically rename exports in comments, which could cause bugs.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
Change existing behavior to do some simple checks about which exports are being used by the script that does the dynamic import.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
no

```json

If i create a childCompiler and use `exposes` (ContainerPlugin) a webpack compile (initial compile and dev rebuilds) are twice as slow compared to if i do not expose any modules. 

If I run two separate instances of webpack, the speed rebuild speed is not impacted, if I use exposes on main compile, speed is not impacted. Only when it's in childCompiler

```js
const childCompiler = compilation.createChildCompiler(
        CHILD_PLUGIN_NAME,
        childOutput,
        [
          new ModuleFederationPlugin({
            ...this._options,
            exposes: {
              ...this._options.exposes,
              ...(this._extraOptions.exposePages
                ? exposeNextjsPages(compiler.options.context)
                : {}),
            },
            runtime: false,
            shared: {
              ...externalizedShares,
              ...this._options.shared,
            },
          }),
          new webpack.web.JsonpTemplatePlugin(childOutput),
          new LoaderTargetPlugin('web'),
          new LibraryPlugin('var'),
          new webpack.DefinePlugin({
            'process.env.REMOTES': JSON.stringify(this._options.remotes),
            'process.env.CURRENT_HOST': JSON.stringify(this._options.name),

title: Experimental module output drops external import assertions
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**
When using the experimental module output format import assertions are not emitted in the output.

This is related to #2933, @alexander-akait requested I open a new issue.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
`main.js`
```js
import test from "test" assert { type: "json" };
console.log(test);
```

`package.json`
```json
{
  "name": "test",
  "type": "module",
  "dependencies": {
    "webpack": "^5.74.0",
    "webpack-cli": "^4.10.0"
  }
}
```

`webpack.config.js`
```js
export default {
	entry: "./main.js",
	mode: "development",
	experiments: {
		outputModule: true,
	},
	externals: [
		"test",
	],
}
```

At the top of `dist/main.mjs` we get `import * as __WEBPACK_EXTERNAL_MODULE_test__ from "test";` which will make applications fail under runtimes which expect the assertion (nodejs v18).

**What is the expected behavior?**
The assertion should be preserved in the output.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.74.0
Node.js version: 18.9.0
author: laverdet

title: It's impossible to import a remote module with a dynamic URL
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

If importing a module of the form:

    `http://my.module/test?queryParam=${something}`

causes Webpack to throw a `Module not found: Error: Can't resolve http://my.module` regardless of externals configuration.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Create a function included in the build that looks something like this:

```
function importMyModuleDynamically(queryParam) {
  import(`http://my.module/test?query=${queryParam}`)
}
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

The expected behavior is for there to exist a configuration in which it's possible to load this module. Things I've tried:

1. Adding "externals" to the webpack configuration:

```
externals: [
  function(context, callback) {
    // This is invoked for all imports except for the http://my.module import
  }
]
```

2. Using `experiments: { outputModule: true }`. Setting this in the webpack config properly imports the module if the URL is a constant string, but it does not work when the URL is dynamic.

3. Adding a custom loader with the test:

```
{
  test: /^http:\/\/my.module/,
  use: 'my-loader'
}
```

`my-loader` is never invoked even though the test matches the URL.

4. Ignore the import:

```
import(/* webpackIgnore: true */ `http://my.module/test?query=${queryParam}`)
```

This still throws the `Module not found` error. 

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.74.0
Node.js version: 18.8.0
Operating System: MacOS 11.6.8
Additional tools:
author: mori-nobuteru

title: Module Federation: Remote Initialization fails with single runtime chunk + multiple entrypoints
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
If a federated import exists under a specific entrypoint that is not yet loaded, and webpack uses `runtimeChunk: single` - the initialization will fail. 

```
Initialization of sharing external failed: Error: Cannot find module 'checkout@http://localhost:3000/_next/static/ssr/remoteEntry.js'
```



**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

Import different remotes under different entrypoints that are not loaded on the page at the same time. 
For example, next.js - pages are entrypoints.

When booting the application, the webpack runtime will attempt to initialize the external, but crashes because the module containing the containerReference module is not yet loaded/on the page.

```js
/******/ 				case "default": {
/******/ 					register("next/dynamic", "12.3.0", function() { return __webpack_require__.e("node_modules_next_dynamic_js").then(function() { return function() { return __webpack_require__(/*! ../node_modules/next/dynamic.js */ "../node_modules/next/dynamic.js"); }; }); });
/******/ 					initExternal("webpack/container/reference/home"); // initialized correctly
/******/ 					initExternal("webpack/container/reference/shop"); // moduleID doesnt exist yet, entrypoint not loaded
/******/ 					initExternal("webpack/container/reference/checkout"); // module id doesnt exist yet, entrypoint not loaded
/******/ 				}
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

ContainerReferences should be hoisted to initial chunk so that webpack runtime can properly initialize all remotes. This should only happen when `runtimeChunk: single` is set
<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.64.4
Node.js version: 16
Operating System: MacOS
Additional tools: Next.js, module-federation/nextjs-mf plugin

title: Lack of javascript error messages with webpack when missing a dependency
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

To get an error message when a dependency is not found at runtime via the webpackBootstrap.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

Currently this functionality is not available. The problem is that compiling and bundling works. But once you load your page, the javascript does not execute and it just silently fails. This make its really HARD to determine what is the cause. I have been running around in circles for three days with this, thinking it was an issue with my ts code, ts configuration or webpack confguration.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
I am using the 'extract' option via Laravel MIx, so I get a manifest.js that has the  webpackBootstrap code. I hava emodified the code so it shows an error when a chunk can't be found. This is the relevant section:

`
/* webpack/runtime/chunk loaded */
/******/ 	(() => {
/******/ 		var deferred = [];
/******/ 		__webpack_require__.O = (result, chunkIds, fn, priority) => {
/******/ 			if(chunkIds) {
/******/ 				priority = priority || 0;
/******/ 				for(var i = deferred.length; i > 0 && deferred[i - 1][2] > priority; i--) deferred[i] = deferred[i - 1];
/******/ 				deferred[i] = [chunkIds, fn, priority];
/******/ 				return;
/******/ 			}
/******/ 			var notFulfilled = Infinity;
/******/ 			for (var i = 0; i < deferred.length; i++) {
/******/ 				var [chunkIds, fn, priority] = deferred[i];
/******/ 				var fulfilled = true;
/******/ 				for (var j = 0; j < chunkIds.length; j++) {
                                                  // This breaks the  Object.keys(__webpack_require__.O).every((key) => (__webpack_require__.O[key](chunkIds[j])))
                                                 // Statement into a loop, so when a chunk is misisng we can issue the error.
							var everyChunk = true;
							const keys = Object.keys(__webpack_require__.O);
							keys.forEach((key, index) => {
								if (!__webpack_require__.O[key](chunkIds[j])) {
									console.error(`Unable to find required file: ${chunkIds[j]}. Make sure the relevant file has been added via <script> or <style> element.`)
									everyChunk = false;
								}
							});
/******/ 					if ((priority & 1 === 0 || notFulfilled >= priority) && everyChunk) {
/******/ 						chunkIds.splice(j--, 1);
/******/ 					} else {
/******/ 						fulfilled = false;
/******/ 						if(priority < notFulfilled) notFulfilled = priority;
/******/ 					}
/******/ 				}
/******/ 				if(fulfilled) {
/******/ 					deferred.splice(i--, 1)
/******/ 					var r = fn();
/******/ 					if (r !== undefined) result = r;
/******/ 				}
/******/ 			}
/******/ 			return result;
/******/ 		};
`

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**

yes. But I am not 100% sure I completely understand the webpackBootstrap code to assert if the modification is correct.

title: `webpackAST` returned in `extraInfo` from custom loader appears ignored internally
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

I *think* this is some kind of bug, but it's a little confusing since this optimisation seems under-advertised. The [docs](https://webpack.js.org/api/loaders/#thiswebpack:~:text=It%20can%20be%20useful%20to%20pass%20an%20abstract%20syntax%20tree%20(AST)%2C%20like%20ESTree%2C%20as%20the%20fourth%20argument%20(meta)%20to%20speed%20up%20the%20build%20time%20if%20you%20want%20to%20share%20common%20ASTs%20between%20loaders.) do mention:

>It can be useful to pass an abstract syntax tree (AST), like [ESTree](https://github.com/estree/estree), as the fourth argument (meta) to speed up the build time if you want to share common ASTs between loaders.

but this omits the specific variable name of `webpackAST` (it implies the fourth argument should just be the AST itself), so I'm basing this report on the source code and checked-in testcases instead.

If I'm doing something wrong here, please let me know!

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When defining a custom loader which returns an `extraInfo` object containing an acorn `webpackAST` property, webpack appears to ignore the supplied ast.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

* Create a custom loader which looks like the ast-loader test case: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/9fcaa243573005d6fdece9a3f8d89a0e8b399613/test/cases/parsing/precreated-ast/ast-loader.js#L27
* Add a console.log to `NormalModule.js` here to log the value of `this._ast` and `extraInfo`: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/293e677b355da0d5bc4ddfc97d2afec114912711/lib/NormalModule.js#L794-L799
* Notice that the logs show `extraInfo` as `null` always:

```
{ extraInfo: null, ast: null }
```

According to a log earlier on, at [this spot](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/293e677b355da0d5bc4ddfc97d2afec114912711/lib/NormalModule.js#L768), `result` always appears to be a single-item array containing only the code, despite the loader using `this.callback(null, code, map, { webpackAST });`.


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Webpack and `NormalModule` should use the supplied `extraInfo` + `webpackAST` to avoid re-parsing files.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  `5.73.0`
Node.js version: `16.13.2`
Operating System: `Ubuntu 18.04.5 LTS`
Additional tools: `n/a`
