title: Source map names for context modules with parts that look like non-absolute paths are malformed
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
When a build includes context modules where the regular expression contains something which looks like a relative path, Webpack generates a source map with an incorrect source name that turns the regular expression into an absolute path.

This causes a _mismatch_ in the `getTaskForFile` method of the `SourceMapDevToolPlugin` because the actual module id in the compilation, only has the leading path of the context module as absolute:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/442808/190626503-116e61ee-1a49-4788-9119-28ac820ed48d.png)

The consequence of which, is that rather than passing the module instance, it passes the (malformed) string module ID to `ModuleFilenameHelpers.createFilename` to generate the source map source name. This in turn causes the logic in `createFileName` to _not_ be able to use `ContextModule.readableIdentifier` to create the friendly identifier that it should for context modules. Instead it will just use the mangled identifier as-is and push that into the source map.

The root of the path will be turned relative to the compiler context again; but the absolute path portion wrongly appended to the regex inside the identifier will remain and will leak into the source maps. This has the potential of changing content based on what machine actually ran the build from what directory structure, meaning caches being busted if content-hashes are added. Moreover; the fact that it leaks the directory structure of the build machine, means it also leaks the type of build agent and environment that was used - which might be information you don't want to slip to the public.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
(note: still working on extracting a minimal reproducing case)

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Webpack should not exhaustively attempt to make absolute paths from all pipe (`|`) separated components. It should only do so for the _first_ component of a pipe-separated sequence.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.74
Node.js version: 
Operating System: Windows 10
Additional tools:

title: incorrect "unused pure expression or super"
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Code like this,

```js
let _Foo;

class Foo {
  static #_ = [_init_foo, _initProto, _Foo, _initClass2] = _applyDecs(this, [[signal, 3, "lorem"], [signal, 0, "foo"]], [reactive]);
  foo = (_initProto(this), _init_foo(this, 123));

  get lorem() {
    return 123;
  }

  set lorem(v) {
    v;
  }

  static #_2 = _initClass2();
}

let _Bar;

class Bar extends _Foo {
  static #_ = [_init_bar, _initProto2, _Bar, _initClass3] = _applyDecs(this, [[signal, 3, "baz"], [signal, 0, "bar"]], [reactive]);
  bar = (_initProto2(this), _init_bar(this, 456));
  #baz = 789;

  get baz() {
    return this.#baz;
  }

  set baz(v) {
    this.#baz = v;
  }

  constructor() {
    super();
    console.log('Bar');
  }

  static #_2 = _initClass3();
}
```

turns into code like this,

```js
let _Foo;

class Foo {
  static #_ = [_init_foo, _initProto, _Foo, _initClass2] = _applyDecs(this, [[signal, 3, "lorem"], [signal, 0, "foo"]], [reactive]);
  foo = (_initProto(this), _init_foo(this, 123));

  get lorem() {
    return 123;
  }

  set lorem(v) {
    v;
  }

  static #_2 = _initClass2();
}

let _Bar;

class Bar extends (/* unused pure expression or super */ null && (_Foo)) {
  static #_ = [_init_bar, _initProto2, _Bar, _initClass3] = _applyDecs(this, [[signal, 3, "baz"], [signal, 0, "bar"]], [reactive]);
  bar = (_initProto2(this), _init_bar(this, 456));
  #baz = 789;

  get baz() {
    return this.#baz;
  }

  set baz(v) {
    this.#baz = v;
  }

  constructor() {
    super();
    console.log('Bar');
  }

  static #_2 = _initClass3();
}
```

and the class fails to run because `super()` tries to call `null`.

The input is output from Babel's new Stage 3 decorators. The `static #_` initializer runs code that sets up the `_Foo` variable.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I haven't published a reproduction yet.

title: Allow Dynamic Import in `commonjs2`
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
webpack should allow `module` dependencies to be declared using the `externals` setting.

Furthermore, dynamic `import()` calls should be preserved.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
As described in `Node.js`' page, `import()` calls are allowed in `CommonJS` files:
https://nodejs.org/api/esm.html#import-expressions

Despite this fact, `webpack` replaces all `import()` calls with `require`s.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
When targeting `commonjs2`, dynamic `import()` calls importing an external `module` should stay untouched. 

Furthermore, adding a `module` external should not report an error even if the target library type is `commonjs2`

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
no

title: Include information about ALL chunk groups in stats
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

Include information about _all_ chunk groups in stats.  Currently `stats.toJson` provides details about _named_ chunk groups, but there is no way (as far as I can tell) to know what the other chunk groups are.

**What is the expected behavior?**

Stats contains enough information to know all the chunk groups that exist (named and otherwise) and which chunks are in each).

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

This would be helpful information for bundle analysis, for understanding things like duplicated code and how bundles change between builds.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

This could be implemented with a new top-level `chunkGroups` property on the stats object, containing all the data about the chunk groups.

Alternately, a simple solution that would meet my needs would be for each chunk to list the chunk groups it is a part of (e.g. by the chunk group ids).

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**

Yes, given some guidance towards the preferred solution.
author: smikula

title: Modules in the output bundle are ordered differently on Unix and Windows
body:
**Steps to reproduce:**
Have two modules with names like:
```
src/Week/index.js
src/WeekYear/index.js
```
and import them in your project. Build the project on Unix and on Windows. We expect the build outputs on both platforms to be identical, but they aren't. On Unix, the output bundle renders the `Week` module first and the `WeekYear` second, while on Windows they are in the opposite order. If you use `contenthash`es in your project, the hashes won't match, the build is not exactly reproducible on Unix and Windows.

Here is a repo where you can reproduce the bug: https://github.com/jsnajdr/webpack-windows-order Clone the repo, run `npm install` and `npm build` and compare the `dist/main.js` output. They will be the same on Unix and and Windows except the two modules are ordered differently. The webpack config needs to disable module concatenation so that the full modules are rendered instead of being inlined into the importing module.

Why it happens? When rendering the modules, they are [sorted by `module.identifier()`](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/9fcaa243573005d6fdece9a3f8d89a0e8b399613/lib/javascript/JavascriptModulesPlugin.js#L691-L698), and `module.identifier()` returns `this.request`, which is an absolute local path that looks like:
```
C:\Users\Jarda\Documents\webpack-windows-order\src\Week\index.js
```
On Unix there are forward slashes in the path, on Windows there are backslashes. But when mixing slashes with uppercase letters, forward and back slashes sort differently!
```
> [ 'Week/index.js', 'WeekYear/index.js' ].sort()
[ 'Week/index.js', 'WeekYear/index.js' ]
```
vs
```
> [ 'Week\\index.js', 'WeekYear\\index.js' ].sort()
[ 'WeekYear\\index.js', 'Week\\index.js' ]
```
If there were only lowercase letters in the names, the sort would be same on both platforms. Because of how ASCII codes of `/`, uppercase letters, `\` and lowercase letters are related.

The `date-fns` package ships modules with mixed-case names like this.

Because the paths are platform specific and because they sort differently, the build output is different.

There is also a `module.readableIdentifier()` method on a module, and it uses a `RequestShortener` instance which sanitizes the Windows paths by replacing `\\`'s with `/`'s and doing a few other changes, so that the Unix vs Windows differences are erased. But `module.identifier()` is not sanitized at all.

title: `entry.chunkLoading = false` is overridden by `SplitChunksPlugin`
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Closely related: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/4819.

**What is the current behavior?**

Here is an example Webpack config, with two entry points `app` and `serviceWorker`. For `app`, the config uses `SplitChunksPlugin` to put `node_modules` in a "vendor" chunk to improve long-term caching. Trying to dynamically load chunks from a service worker has failed each time I have tested it, so I want the `serviceWorker` entry to be compiled to one big JS file.

```js
const path = require("path");

module.exports = {
  mode: "development",
  entry: {
    app: path.resolve(__dirname, "src/app.js"),
    serviceWorker: {
      import: path.resolve(__dirname, "src/serviceWorker.js"),
      chunkLoading: false, // This _should_ put everything in one JS file, but it doesn't
    },
  },

  output: {
    clean: true,
  },
  devtool: false,

  // Enable long-term caching of node_modules. See https://webpack.js.org/guides/caching/
  optimization: {
    moduleIds: "deterministic",
    splitChunks: {
      cacheGroups: {
        vendor: {
          test: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/]/,
          name: "vendor",

          // Include all node_modules that are required for the initial load
          // in one big chunk. Dynamically-imported node_modules will still be
          // put in their own chunks.
          chunks: "initial",
        },
      },
    },
  },
};
```

The Webpack docs state [here](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/entry-context/#entry-descriptor) that you can pass `chunkLoading: false` when defining an entry to put everything in the main chunk. It seems like `SplitChunksPlugin` ignores this option.


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

1. Clone my repo: https://github.com/srmagura/webpack-split-chunks-repro
2. `yarn install`
3. `yarn build`
4. Check `dist/serviceWorker.js` and observe that `lodash` is not in the file. Lodash is loaded dynamically from the `vendor` chunk.

**What is the expected behavior?**

`chunkLoading: false` should cause `serviceWorker.js` to include all chunks required for the service worker to run. This means Lodash should be in `serviceWorker.js`.

**Other relevant information:**

As a workaround, I could compile my service worker through a separate Webpack config, but I wanted to check if there was a better option before doing so.

webpack version:  5.74.0
Node.js version: 16.17.0
Operating System: Ubuntu 22.04
author: srmagura

body:

**What is the current behavior?**
Error when compiling typescript in target es2022

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
tsconfig.json
```
{
   ...
   "compilerOptions" {
       ...
       "target": "ES2022",
       ...
   }
    ...
}
```
module1.ts
```
export let userId = document.body.getAttribute("data-user-id")!
```
module2.ts
```
import { userId } from "./module1"; 

export class Theme {
    private constructor() {}
    private static Change(isCallback: boolean) {
        localStorage.removeItem('lightTheme_'+userId)
    }
    static {
        localStorage.getItem('lightTheme_'+userId) && this.Change(false)
    }
}
```
Compiling produces the following file:
```
...
;// CONCATENATED MODULE: ../ts/module1.ts
let module1_userId = document.body.getAttribute("data-user-id");

;// CONCATENATED MODULE: ../ts/module2.ts

class Theme {
    constructor() { }
    static Change(isCallback) {
        localStorage.removeItem('lightTheme_' + userId);
    }
    static {
        localStorage.getItem('lightTheme_' + module1_userId) && this.Change(false);
    }
}
...
```
the userId that is in the static block is renamed to module1_userId, and the userId that is in the Change function is not renamed

if you remove static, then all the same, userId will not be renamed inside the class.
In the global context it is renamed normally

title: Add types for devServer in Configuration type
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
When I  add the `Configuration` type to the Webpack config in a TypeScript file,  and add configuration for webpack-dev-server, it throws an error. For example:
```ts
import { Configuration } from "webpack";

const config: Configuration = {
  mode: development,
  // ...
  devServer: {  // throws an error as there is no interface for devServer
    port: 3000
  }
};
```
The `Configuration` interface therefore, should have a property for devServer.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
This should be added because if you want to use TypeScript for the Webpack config in a frontend project, and add configuration for `webpack-dev-server`, it should not throw an error.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
An interface for `devServer` should be added to the Configuration interface.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: Random chunk loading errors in production
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

I am using vue-cli, which uses Webpack in the background to build a vue3 library with multiple entries.
Shared vendor modules are output into separate chunks.
This works great in development, but I get errors related to dynamic chunk loading in random production builds.
I sometimes end up having to run the build process several times (without changing anything) before getting a working one.
I can't quite figure out what's causing this, and have tried moving to a single entry setup, but this didn't change anything.

The errors are also not always the same, and occur even though the chunk seems to be correctly loaded.
Here are some examples of the errors that occur:

- `Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: e[r] is undefined`
- `ChunkLoadError: Loading chunk 'xx' failed` (although I do see the chunk loading in the browser's network console)

I noticed that **when the errors occur vendor chunks seem to not be minified**, unlike in working builds.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Since the errors occur on random builds, I can't quite create a minimal reproduction example.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->

<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

I expect production builds to be consistent and to have dynamic chunks not produce errors when loaded.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->

**Other relevant information:**

Such cases seem to have been experienced by others using webpack (https://stackoverflow.com/questions/61090881/webpack-laravel-mix-chunk-error-typeerror-er-is-undefined, https://stackoverflow.com/questions/53704950/webpack-code-splitting-loading-chunk-failed-error-wrong-file-path), but are usually due to network/caching issues, which is not my case as I disabled caching in my browser all together and see the chunks loading fine in the network console.

Is there maybe a way to monitor the build process in a more verbose way that what is output by default ?

/******/ 			var onScriptComplete = function(prev, event) {
/******/ 				// avoid mem leaks in IE.
/******/ 				script.onerror = script.onload = null;
/******/ 				clearTimeout(timeout);
/******/ 				var doneFns = inProgress[url];
/******/ 				delete inProgress[url];
/******/ 				script.parentNode && script.parentNode.removeChild(script);
/******/ 				doneFns && doneFns.forEach(function(fn) { return fn(event); });
/******/ 				if(prev) return prev(event);
/******/ 			}
/******/ 			;
/******/ 			var timeout = setTimeout(onScriptComplete.bind(null, undefined, { type: 'timeout', target: script }), 120000); 
```

title: Webpack cache is not invalidated properly after updating one dependency
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When using webpack cache, I get contaminated build after updating one of dependencies. Specifically, my project has a git dependency, after updating commit ref webpack seems to "merge" old one and new one.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I tried to construct minimal reproducing example for this, but my sample project seem to work fine, but main project does not.

What I do:

1. Have my project depend on `git+https://git.server.local/repo#old-branch-name`
2. In my `webpack.config.js` I configure `ts-loader` + `babel-loader`, asking them to work on **dependent repo**:
   ```js
    module: {
        rules: [{
            test:    /\.tsx?$/,
            // also process TS files from the dependency
            exclude: /node_modules(?!\/REPO)/,
            use: [{
                    loader: 'babel-loader',
                    options: require(path.resolve("babel.config.js")),
            }, {
                loader: 'ts-loader',
                options: {
                    transpileOnly: true,
                },
            }]
        }]
    },
   ```
4. Build with webpack
5. In that (dependent) repo, I heavily rewrite some files, and rename others (in particular, bunch of `index.js` to `index.ts`).
6. Run `npm i git+https://git.server.local/repo#NEW-branch-name`
7. Build with webpack again

After second build, I actually get a _mix_ of old and new package files from `REPO` in resulting bundle: one of the new `index.ts` files imports another module, but webpack actually inserts OLD code for OLD `index.js` from that module. That old code does not even exist in `node_modules/REPO` anymore.

If I remove `node_modules/.cache/webpack` and run build again, I get only NEW code in the bundle.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

webpack should detect that `REPO` was updated and invalidate its cache for that package. 

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.73.0
Node.js version: 16.13.1
Operating System: macOS 12.4 Monterey
Additional tools:
```json
    "babel-loader": "^8.2.5",
    "ts-loader": "^9.3.1",
    "typescript": "^4.8.4",
```

title: Webworker target builds should work for module workers
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
When setting webpack config target to `'webworkers'`, the bundled code references `importScripts`. If we then want to run the code as a worker per se, it'd fail for `{type:'module'}` workers.
<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Webpack output code targeting webworkers should be able to run in both classic and module workers.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
The original intention is to insert compiled+bundled code from a project or script into another worker-specific script (from another project). Right now, you can stitch the raw contents together, and they may run in a classic worker but not a module worker since the latter references `importScripts`.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
Not too sure about the internals but ESM workers should follow ESM import behavior such as loading asynchronously and allowing dynamic imports.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
no

body:

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

`index.js:2 Unsatisfied version x.y.z from ------- of shared singleton module ----- (required ^x.y.z)` warnings should not appear on production console.

title: Module Federation shareScope doesn't work as intended
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**
shareScope flag in the shared library object doesn't work as intended. If the shareScope property is added to the “share” object, then the shared package is downloaded multiple times. (One for each microfrontend that imports the package with the shareScope)

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Here's a link to a github repository: https://github.com/daTrux/Module-Federation-Example
There you will find a simple example that consists in 4 angular projects in which:

- Two remotes have the shareScope set
- the shell and the remaining remote has no shareScope set

Run npm install in all projects and the use the ng serve command to start all the 4 applications.
In the browser dev tools, you will see that the projects which use the shareScope are downloading the package two times.

**What is the expected behavior?**
Adding a shareScope should result in a library to be downloaded only once per scope. (has explained in https://webpack.js.org/plugins/module-federation-plugin/#sharescope)


**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.73.0
Node.js version: 14.18.1
Operating System: Windows 11

I am running a child compilation whose entry is not same as the parent compilation. The entry for child compilation is some another file (Hello.js, not index.js).

Now I want the parent compiler to skip the compilation from Hello.js. So that no extra modules are added in the parent chunks. As in the end I will bundle both these chunks. I want to skip it like that after bundling both chunks from parent and child , it should work perfectly fine.


```
class AwesomePlugin {
  constructor() {
    this.childCompilerName = 'awesome-plugin-compilation';
    this.outputFileName = 'custom-file.js';
    this.compilationEntry = './src/Hello.js';
  }
  apply(compiler) {
    compiler.hooks.emit.tapAsync('AwesomePlugin', (compilation, callback) => {
      const childCompiler = compilation.createChildCompiler(this.childCompilerName, {
        filename: this.outputFileName
      });
      childCompiler.options.output.chunkFilename = `child-${compiler.options.output.chunkFilename}`;
      childCompiler.context = compiler.context;
      childCompiler.apply(new SingleEntryPlugin(compiler.context, this.compilationEntry, this.outputFileName));
      childCompiler.plugin('compilation', (compilation) => {
        if (compilation.cache) {
        }
      });
      childCompiler.runAsChild((err, entries, childCompilation) => {
        callback(err);
      });
    });
  }
}
```

PackFileCacheStrategy :: Error: Unable to snapshot resolve dependencies
![Screenshot from 2022-10-26 12-07-59](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8392953/197988209-4a40c681-bbb9-4a18-986c-6375e2c6b516.png)

title: Allow to specify how automatic public path should be determined
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

When building module script and my bundle contains a relative import statement then bundled code contains `import.meta.url` to detect public path. The problem is that the import keyword is supported from [chrome >64](https://caniuse.com/?search=import.meta) but support for javascript modules via script tag was added in [chrome >61](https://caniuse.com/?search=javascript%20modules%20via%20script%20tag). I use [module/nomodule approach](https://philipwalton.com/articles/deploying-es2015-code-in-production-today/) to load es5 in old browsers and es6 in new browsers but there are some versions such as chrome 61, 62, 63 which are not covered because of this unsupported import keyword.

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Currently, there are two ways to automatically detect public path:
- [ via `import.meta.url` - when script type is set to `module`
](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/runtime/AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule.js#L39)
- [ via document.currentScript.src](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/runtime/AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule.js#L46)

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
 Consider adding more options to a public path something like:

```js
  output: {
    publicPath: "auto/current-script" | "auto/import-meta",
  },
```

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  latest
Node.js version:  latest
Operating System: any
Additional tools: N/A

**What is the current behavior?**

This is my webpack config.
```
const path = require('path');
const HtmlWebpackPlugin = require('html-webpack-plugin');
const CleanWebpackPlugin = require('clean-webpack-plugin');
const SingleEntryPlugin = require('webpack/lib/SingleEntryPlugin');
const LibraryTemplatePlugin = require('webpack/lib/LibraryTemplatePlugin');

const outputDirectory = 'dist';

class AwesomePlugin {
  constructor() {
    this.childCompilerName = 'awesome-plugin-compilation';
    this.outputFileName = 'custom-file.js';
  }

  apply(compiler) {
    compiler.hooks.emit.tapAsync('AwesomePlugin', (compilation, callback) => {
      this.compilationEntry = './src/client/Hello.js'
      const childCompiler = compilation.createChildCompiler(this.childCompilerName, {
        filename: this.outputFileName
      }, [
        new SingleEntryPlugin(compiler.context, this.compilationEntry, this.outputFileName),
        new LibraryTemplatePlugin('childLibrary', 'umd')
      ]);
      childCompiler.options.output.chunkFilename = `child-${compiler.options.output.chunkFilename}`; 
      childCompiler.context = compiler.context;
      childCompiler.runAsChild((err, entries, childCompilation) => {
        callback(err);
      });
    });
  }
}

module.exports = {
  entry: ['babel-polyfill', './src/client/index.js'],
  output: {
    path: path.join(__dirname, outputDirectory),
    filename: 'bundle.js',
    chunkFilename: '[name].js',
    library: 'MyLibrary',
    libraryTarget: 'umd',
    //libraryExport: 'default',
  },
  externals: {
    "./Hello.js": 'childLibrary',
  },
  plugins: [
    new CleanWebpackPlugin([outputDirectory]),
    new HtmlWebpackPlugin({
      template: './public/index.html',
      favicon: './public/favicon.ico'
    }),
    new AwesomePlugin(),
  ]
};

title: webpack cannot re-export external modules
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

webpack seems to cannot compile aggregate modules syntax when the module is marked as external.
It works with modules that includes the original source (not marked as external), but not the ones marked as external.

**What is the current behavior?**

webpack cannot re-export external modules.

src/Main.js
```js
export * from 'fs';
```

title: Add an option to suppress webpack chunk loading errors when using Entry Points
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
Add an option to suppress webpack chunk loading errors when using Entry Points to split code. An error is currently thrown in the chunk loading module, which seems to be included by default in webpack 4 and 5:
- webpack 4: [JsonpMainTemplatePlugin.js](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/webpack-4/lib/web/JsonpMainTemplatePlugin.js)
- webpack 5: [JsonpChunkLoadingRuntimeModule.js](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/web/JsonpChunkLoadingRuntimeModule.js)

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
There should be an option to suppress webpack chunk loading errors when using Entry Points to do code splitting. I'd like to be able to control exactly what happens when webpack fails to load a chunk, without changing my implementation entirely to use dynamic imports instead of Entry Points.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
Currently, an error is thrown in the requireEnsure function when a file webpack tries to load is not found, and there doesn't seem to be a way to catch these errors. When the user uses dynamic imports to specify exactly when and how a chunk should be loaded, it IS possible to implement retry logic, or other custom behavior. With Entry Points however, there doesn't seem to be a way to specify what happens after webpack attempts to load a module. Even with webpack loaders, we seem to only be able to modify the filenames and contents of the files to load, not what happens if the file load fails.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
This could be an extra option on the `config` object for each module that suppresses Entry Point loading errors altogether, or a hook into the module loading function that lets devs specify exactly what behavior they want at that time in the module-loading lifecycle.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
no
author: sxguo

title: Allow chunks served from alternate locations
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Would like to specify loading of chunks from an alternate root path. E.g., config:

```
{
  optimization: {
    splitChunks: {
      resolve: '/opt/node_modules/chunks/'
    }
  }
}
```

...then the bundled code would use that path for loading chunks instead of a relative / 'undo' path back to the root of output directory. E.g., for the rendered output of [this code](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/9fcaa243573005d6fdece9a3f8d89a0e8b399613/lib/node/ReadFileChunkLoadingRuntimeModule.js#L163-L167), instead of:

```
var filename = require('path').join(__dirname, "../../../" + __webpack_require__.u(chunkId));
```

...we'd get:

```
var filename = require('path').join(__dirname, "/opt/node_modules/chunks/" + __webpack_require__.u(chunkId));
```

I.e., instead of using a variable called `rootOutputDir`, the code would use something like `chunksRoot` which could be something other than the root output dir.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

AWS Lambda has a feature called 'Layers' whereby code storage may be shared between multiple 'Function' instances. However, when creating the layer, there's no way to know which working directory each separate Function instance will end up working with. Instead, it'd be great if each Function instance could look in a predictable location to load any chunks it needs.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/configuration-layers.html


**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
I think it'd be pretty straight-forward to use a static path provided in the config. It'd be a little more ambitious but it might be interesting to also/alternately allow a function (e.g., defined in the config) to be injected:

```
{
  optimization: {
    splitChunks: {
      resolve: function(resolutionContext) {
        if (process.env.AWS_LAMBDA_FUNCTION_NAME !== undefined) {
          // The layers directory is already included as part of NODE_PATH when running on Lambda
          return 'chunks/';
        }
        
        return resolutionContext.rootOutputDir;
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
If I had some confidence my changes would be accepted, I could probably make some time to put a proposal together. I'm not sure how easy it'd be for me to test all of the various loading scenarios, though. My use case is mostly 'Node'-based.

title: Asset/resource rule type parses files for imports, when it should just take that file alone
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When configuring a resource via the type `asset/resource`, Webpack seems to _parse_ the file and resolve imports.. which I don't think it should be doing in an asset outside of standard compilation.

I'm trying to copy SASS/SCSS files to the output directory, as plain assets without parsing or resolving any imports therein.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Configure the modules to look like so, containing an `asset/resource` type:

```
{
  // ...
  entry: {
    someFile: "./src/file.scss"
  },
  module: {
    rules: [
      // ...
      {
        test: /\.s[ac]ss$/,
        type: "asset/resource"
      }
    ]
  }
  // ...
}
```

If `file.scss` contains an import like `@use './another-file.scss';`, Webpack will include that file in the output (when it should not).

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

When specifying type `asset/resource`, included assets (even SASS files) should not be parsed (and have their imports included in the bundle) and should simply be copied/processed as such with a binary file, for example.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

# Bug report
**What is the current behavior?**
using `npm run webpack` when `npm install --build-from-source` was previous specified ignores the build from source request and copies over prebuilt files to the `generated` directory.

title: Customize / make more readable output of promise new promise (promise based dynamic remotes)
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Promise new promise syntax ([promised based dynamic remotes](https://webpack.js.org/concepts/module-federation/#promise-based-dynamic-remotes)) gives excellent flexibility in defining remotes.  However, the build output of this is too verbose, showing the entire body of the function in the build output.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
Promise based dynamic remotes give great flexibility.  But, the build output is hard to read, particularly when more than 1 promised based dynamic remote is specified. Other details of the build output can be obscured by the volume of the promised based dynamic remote output.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
It looks like this output comes from the [readableIdentifier ](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/ExternalModule.js#L430)function in ExternalModule.js. readableIdentifier appears to be part of the Module class, and is implemented differently for different module types. For external modules, it looks like this:
```
         /**
	 * @param {RequestShortener} requestShortener the request shortener
	 * @returns {string} a user readable identifier of the module
	 */
	readableIdentifier(requestShortener) {
		return "external " + JSON.stringify(this.request);
	}
```

One possibly naive revision would be to truncate the stringified output to some maximum length.  For example, change ExternalModule.js' method to look like this:
```
         /**
	 * @param {RequestShortener} requestShortener the request shortener
	 * @returns {string} a user readable identifier of the module
	 */
	readableIdentifier(requestShortener) {
		const maxLength = 80;
		const stringifiedRequest = JSON.stringify(this.request);
		return "external " + (stringifiedRequest.length > maxLength ? stringifiedRequest.substring(0, maxLength) + '...': stringifiedRequest)
	}

```

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes, although someone else likely has a better understanding.  I'd be happy to test and otherwise assist

body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
Thx for this awesome library.
First of all, I don't know is this a bug or just not implemented yet.
This is my mini repo to reproduce this problem. The webpack version I used is 5.38.1
In `index.ts`, I'm trying to load the dependencies dynamically in order to get small bundle size.
``` typescript
export const treeShakingTestAsync = async () => {
    console.log('treeShakingTestAsync');
    //  Be replaced with 'false' by DefinePlugin
    if (__MOBILE__) {
        const { Baz } = await import(/* webpackChunkName: "bundle_mobile" */'./mobile');
        console.log(Baz);
    } else {
        const { foo } = await import(/* webpackChunkName: "bundle_pc" */ './pc');
        console.log(foo);
    }

};
```
`pc.ts` and `mobile.ts` both use some of the code from a common module.
```typescript
// pc.ts
export { bar , foo } from './lib-modules';
```
```typescript
// mobile.ts
export { Baz } from './lib-modules';
```
`lib-modules.ts` mimics a large esm package index file.
``` typescript
// lib-modules
export function foo() { console.log('FuncFoo') };
export function bar() {
    console.log('FuncFoo')
};
export class Baz {
    private c = 'ClassBaz'
}
```
In the webpack config , everything is default, except turning minimizer off to see what webpack does before passing to terser.
and bundle the pseudo package `lib-modules` in to one bundle.
``` js
const path = require('path');
const HtmlWebpackPlugin = require('html-webpack-plugin');
const webpack = require('webpack');
module.exports = {
  entry: './src/treeshaking-test.ts',
  mode: 'production',
  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.tsx?$/,
        use: 'ts-loader',
        exclude: /node_modules/,
      },
    ],
  },
  optimization: {
    minimize: false,
    splitChunks: {
      chunks: 'async',
      minSize: 20000,
      minRemainingSize: 0,
      minChunks: 1,
      maxAsyncRequests: 30,
      maxInitialRequests: 30,
      enforceSizeThreshold: 50000,
      cacheGroups: {
        defaultVendors: {
          test: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/]/,
          priority: -10,
          reuseExistingChunk: true,
        },
        default: {
          minChunks: 2,
          priority: -20,
          reuseExistingChunk: true,
        },
        test: {
          test: /lib-modules/,
          name: 'bundle-lib-modules',
          priority: 101,
          chunks: 'all',
          enforce: true,
        },
      },
    },
  },
  resolve: {
    extensions: ['.tsx', '.ts', '.js'],
  },
  plugins: [
    new webpack.DefinePlugin({
      __TEST__: true
    }),
    new HtmlWebpackPlugin(),
  ],
  output: {
    path: path.resolve(__dirname, 'dist'),
    clean: true
  },
};
```
The result is perfect, webpack removed the dead code block in bracket after "false"  before involvement of SplitchunkPlugin or TerserPlugin.
`Baz` is not in the output.
``` js
/* unused harmony export treeShakingTestAsync */
const treeShakingTestAsync = async () => {
    console.log('treeShakingTestAsync');
    if (false) {}
    else {
        const { foo } = await Promise.all(/* import() | bundle_pc */[__webpack_require__.e(910), __webpack_require__.e(941)]).then(__webpack_require__.bind(__webpack_require__, 422));
        console.log(foo);
    }
};
```
But when I add a babel-loader after ts-loader for polyfills
the code get transpiled to

```
/* harmony export */ __webpack_require__.d(__webpack_exports__, {

yes
But I am not familiar with webpack's compilation process, is there any docs could help?
Also I also wondering , there seems be a nuance in 'tree-shaking'(things not be in included, or 'rolled up') and 'dead code removal', Is there any doc elaborate on this subject?

title: Package imported solely in import with `webpackChunkName` must not be splitted by `splitChunks`
body:
# Bug report

When a package is imported solely with in import with `webpackChunkName` flag, such package must be not create an extra vendor chunk.

**How to reproduce?**

checkout https://github.com/atk4/ui/commit/034f075b056dd0436d966176c1a8763c214df2d0 and run `npm run build` in `js` dir

in https://github.com/atk4/ui/blob/034f075b056dd0436d966176c1a8763c214df2d0/js/src/services/vue.service.js#L84 this file:

https://github.com/atk4/ui/blob/034f075b056dd0436d966176c1a8763c214df2d0/js/src/vue-components/query-builder/query-builder.component.vue is imported which imports `fomantic-ui-group.component.vue` and `fomantic-ui-rule.component.vue` (in the same dir) which import from `vue-query-builder` package

no other files import from `vue-query-builder` package

**What is the current behavior?**

`public/js/vendor-vue-query-builder.js` is created but the content should go into `public/js/atk-vue-query-builder.js` especially when https://github.com/atk4/ui/blob/034f075b056dd0436d966176c1a8763c214df2d0/js/webpack.config.js#L41-L44 lines are removed (tested, the files are still vendored)

**What is the expected behavior?**

I would expect webpack to detect `vue-query-builder` is used from single (already chunked) exported (already chunked) `public/js/atk-vue-query-builder.js` file and NOT create additional/uneeded `public/js/vendor-vue-query-builder.js` chuck

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.75.0 / latest

title: Experimental module output nests "export" inside "__webpack_require__" callback
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When building my library (Typescript, ESM, Webpack 5, React), the output is broken due to a stray `export` line inside what appears to be a callback called `__webpack_require__`:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3869469/203342790-b45b9600-e820-4eb5-a123-7495cf0ac45d.png)

Entire contents of that callback:

<details>
<summary>Code block</summary>

    /******/ __webpack_require__ => { // webpackRuntimeModules
    /******/ var __webpack_exec__ = (moduleId) => (__webpack_require__(__webpack_require__.s = moduleId))
    /******/ var __webpack_exports__ = (__webpack_exec__(4421));
    /******/ var __webpack_exports__ConditionOptions = __webpack_exports__.tG;
    /******/ var __webpack_exports__ConditionTypeOperator = __webpack_exports__.vI;

    /******/ var __webpack_exports__Icon = __webpack_exports__.JO;
    /******/ var __webpack_exports__IconName = __webpack_exports__.uH;
    /******/ export { __webpack_exports__ConditionOptions as ConditionOptions, __webpack_exports__ConditionTypeOperator as ConditionTypeOperator, __webpack_exports__ConditionVariant as ConditionVariant, __webpack_exports__CustomFieldType as CustomFieldType, __webpack_exports__FilterType as FilterType, __webpack_exports__FilterVariant as FilterVariant, __webpack_exports__Icon as Icon, __webpack_exports__IconName as IconName };
    /******/ }
</details>

I haven't even begun to test it elsewhere as I can't enable Terser minification due to this problem. It fails when enabled with the error: `"Export" statement may only appear at the top level [index.ca.js:21787,9]`.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Using a config similar to the following:

<details>
<summary>Code block</summary>

```javascript
const path = require("node:path");
const ResolveTypeScriptPlugin = require("resolve-typescript-plugin");

const DIST = path.resolve(__dirname, "./dist");
const SOURCE = path.resolve(__dirname, "./src");

module.exports = {
  devtool: false,

  entry: {
    "index.ca.js":{
      import: path.join(SOURCE, "index.ca.ts"),
      dependOn: "shared.js"
    },
    "index.de.js":{
      import: path.join(SOURCE, "index.de.ts"),
      dependOn: "shared.js"
    },
    "shared.js": [
      "@mycompany/icons"
    ],
    sass: [
        "./some-file.scss"
    ]
  },

  experiments: {
    outputModule: true
  },

  externals: [
    "react",
    "react-dom"
  ],

  externalsType: "module",

  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.tsx?$/,
        use: [{ loader: "ts-loader" }],
        resolve: {
            fullySpecified: false
        }
      },
      {
        test: /\.s[ac]ss$/,
        type: "asset/resource"
      }
    ]
  },

  optimization: {
    minimize: false
  },

  output: {
    filename: "[name]",
    path: DIST,
    chunkFilename: "[name].chunk.js",
    assetModuleFilename: (pathData) => {
      // Remove "src/" prefix on SASS/SCSS files
      if (/^src\/.*\.s[ac]ss$/.test(pathData.filename)) {
        return pathData.filename.replace(/^src\//, "");
      }
      return "[path][name][ext]";
    },
    environment: {
      module: true
    },
    library: {
      type: "module"
    }
  },

  performance: {
    hints: false,
    maxEntrypointSize: 768000,
    maxAssetSize: 768000
  },

  resolve: {
    // No .ts/.tsx included due to the typescript resolver plugin
    extensions: [".js", ".jsx"],
    fallback: {
      buffer: false,
      fs: false,
      path: false
    },
    plugins: [
      // Handle .ts => .js resolution
      new ResolveTypeScriptPlugin()
    ]
  },

  target: "web"
};
```
</details>

author: perry-mitchell

title: Windows problem: Can we add endOfLine: "auto" to .prettierrc.json?
body:
Hello webpack,

Because of how `.gitattributes` is set and that [prettier default value](https://prettier.io/docs/en/options.html#end-of-line) for `endOfLine` option is `"lf"` from version 2.0.0 i ended up struggling with constant complain from vscode about my ugly `"crlf"` line ending.

Problem is solved by updating **.prettierrc.json** with `endOfLine: "auto"` and restarting vscode.

Thanks.
author: jhribal

title: Genereate a unique id for each compilation and use the id as key for map rather than reference
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Current behavior: `NormalModule.getCompilationHooks` is using a map whose key is the reference of compilation.
expected behavior: give each compilation a unique id, and use the id as the key of the map to find hooks.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
For myself, I am developing a successor of speed-measure-webpack-plugin to measure the time of each loader and plugin, to do this, I use Proxy(like speed-measure-webpack-plugin) to warp loader and tap method so that I could run extra logic before and after each loader and hook is really executed.
However, to proxy tap method, I need to proxy everything between compiler and the tap, including the compilation.
But if webpack is using reference-equal for some map, because the proxied compilation and the real compilation is different, there is some strange error.

There might be some other ways to do the trick, but it will be pretty good if webpack is able to make this small change.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

When each compilation is created, generate a uuid as ID. Then use the ID as the key of the internal map inside `NormalModule.getCompilationHooks` to get hooks.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes
author: ShuiRuTian

title: Invalid name of some module in production mode
body: # Bug report **What is the current behavior?** Sometimes without any reasons my project do not works properly in production mode. If I build up my project in development mode - everything is perfect, no any errors. But in production mode I have some weird problem. Sometimes after build up I see an error like this in the Developer Console: As you see, the d8498 is not defined. I have looked into the file 286_6660b.js and saw this: ``` /* harmony import */ var _code_editor__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_5__ = __webpack_require__(d8498); ``` The name of module just not in enclosed quotes, because `d8498` is not valid name for this situation. And if I fix this name adding enclosed quotes the error is gone. And it's not the most weird. I'v found the component of the module `code_editor` and just renamed the directory `code-editor` adding `_` in the beginning of name to make it `_code-editor`. After it the error is gone too. Very weird. **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** I don't know how to reproduce it, because it has periodic character. Sometimes I fix with renaming files/directories, sometimes deleting JSDoc tags. It always the same error regardless to whether use minimization in Webpack. **What is the expected behavior?** I expect a valid number name for all modules after building in production mode. **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.75 Node.js version: 16.13.2 Operating System: Linux Debian 10 Buster

title: webpack couldn't resolve an existing path
body:
I created an ESM package (i.e. contains `type: "module"` property) and imports `"#*": "./"`

it suppose that a path like `#somefile.mjs` to be translated into `./somefile.mjs` 

when I do something like ```import(`#configs/hello.mjs`)``` it can find the file './configs/hello.mjs',

but when I do something like 
```
let file = 'hello';
import(`#configs/${file}.mjs`)
```
it outputs an error that the file '#configs/hello.mjs' cannot be resolved (webpack already resolved it in the previous step)


I preprared a [minimal repo](https://stackblitz.com/edit/node-sdbnp9?file=package.json,webpack.config.ts,tsconfig.json,index.ts,dist%2Fmain.mjs) to demonstrate this behavior

title: Webpack ModuleFederationPlugin breaks the build of libraries with default import
author: sviat9440

title: [feat] original request
body:
when you build an ESM project and want to compile something like `import('#configs/file.mjs')`, setting externals to be
```
externals:{
function(){
   // receives the correct request: '#configs/file.mjs'
   // transform the request to: `import ${request}`

 if(request.match(/^#configs\/.+/))cb(null, `import ${request}`)
}
}
```
it works as expected like that:
- it outputs a dynamic import `import('#configs/file.mjs')`
- using the property `imports` in package.json of the dist, it could be resolved to the correct path
- the webpack's external function receives the exact original request i.e `#configs/file.mjs`
- webpack can internally resolve the specified request into the correct path using imports in package.json of the source.


but things get into issues when you make the exact same steps but the request contains a variable like that
```
let file = 'file'
import('#configs/${file}.mjs')
```

it supposes to be the exact same result, but here webpack's external function doesn't receive the original request,
instead, it mutates it and provides the resolved one

instead of:
```
request: '#configs/file.mjs'
```
it receives:
```
request: ./file.mjs
context: path/to/configs
```

so the check in the external function never matches.

I suggest providing the exact original request as it specified by the user without any touch, so we can test matches against the original request

```
externals:[
 function(){
  if(/^#configs\/.+/.test(originalRequest))cb(...)
]
```

title: commonjs Modules Do Not Work Correctly With externalsType of 'promise'
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

## What is the current behavior?

_**EDIT:**_ As per [this comment below](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/16544#issuecomment-1358333143) this behavior seems to be isolated to commonjs modules (and possibly other non-esm modules).

When using `externalsType: 'promise'`, all known used exports get wrapped in an `async` function, and their `externals` dependencies are awaited. Modules marked as `__unused_webpack_module` _do not_ get wrapped with `async`, and _do not_ `await` their dependencies.

This can be resolved by the upstream dependency doing a different thing in their package.json and `exports`, but it seems unreasonable to expect that to be the case for _all_ dependencies. Quite often upstream dependencies do have exports that are marked as unused, even if they actually are used. These exports will break when `externalsType` is set to `promise`.

## If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.

1. Pull down this repo: https://github.com/stevematney/webpack-promise-externals-bug-example
2. Run `npm install && npm start`
3. Open `localhost:9000` in a browser.
4. Because we are not _also_ externalizing `react-dom`, our build does not run.

Notice in the resulting webpack bundle we see `react-dom` defined with this pattern:
```javascript
        /***/
        "./node_modules/react-dom/cjs/react-dom.development.js": /*!*************************************************************!*\
  !*** ./node_modules/react-dom/cjs/react-dom.development.js ***!
  \*************************************************************/
        /***/
        ((__unused_webpack_module,exports,__webpack_require__)=>{

            "use strict";
            /** @license React v17.0.2 ... */
```
    
Whereas our base module is defined with this pattern:
    
```javascript
 /***/
        "./src/index.js": /*!**********************!*\
  !*** ./src/index.js ***!
  \**********************/
        /***/
        ((module,__webpack_exports__,__webpack_require__)=>{

            "use strict";
            __webpack_require__.a(module, async(__webpack_handle_async_dependencies__,__webpack_async_result__)=>{

```

See that `react-dom` is missing the `async` setup. When it loads `__webpack_require__('react')`, it gets a Promise which it does not `await`, thus breaking when it looks for `ReactSharedInternals.ReactCurrentDispatcher`. `ReactSharedInternals` gets defined as `undefined` instead of the property of the `react` dependency that it should be.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

## What is the expected behavior?
All modules should correctly resolve external dependencies using the async setup pattern, whether or not these modules are unused.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

## Other relevant information:
webpack version:  5.75.0
Node.js version: 16.18.1
Operating System: macOS Ventura - 13.0.1 (22A400)

title: Treeshaking and bundle splitting leads to 2 different treeshaked modules but only 1st/cached is used
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

I am running into problems when I bundle multiple entries, treeshake them and execute them on the same page, when the entries use different functions of a shared module. 

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

- A shared module contains 3 functions: 1,2,3. 
- Function 1 and 2 of the shared module are used in entry 1 
- Function 2 and 3 of the shared module are used in entry 2
- Both entries are executed on the same webpage
- Entry 1 succeeds 
- Entry 2 fails because it cannot find function 3

I think this problem is acknowledged in the issue below, but it is not clear if there was any follow up on this.

        Yes exactly. At runtime there is a module cache that makes sure that only a single instance of each module is instantiated. The first loaded chunk will add the module to the cache and the second chunk will use the module from the cache. So the modules in the chunks need to be identical as any of them might be used for both.

_Originally posted by @sokra in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/7782#issuecomment-786170909_

**What is the expected behavior?**

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/7782#issuecomment-786191051
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/7782#issuecomment-805274981

Option 1:
 - Split the common function 2 into a base.
 - Put function 1 in entry 1
 - Put function 3 in entry 3

Option 2: 
- Prevent entry 2 from using the cached shared module of entry 1

**Other relevant information:**

webpack version:  5.51.1
Node.js version: 14
Operating System: Ubuntu
Additional tools: -

title: Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'SOME_VARIABLE')
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
An error sometimes happens with an import path. It depends on a reexport variable with another code influence. The error looks like:
```
TypeError
Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS')
```

The same issue https://github.com/apollographql/apollo-client/issues/9659 doesn't solve the problem by solution:
```
"@apollo/client": "3.6.2",
"graphql": "15.8.0",
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
I don't know exactly way to reproduce it. Some reexport combinations do it or not. It disappears if the import path changes. It's really unpredictable error in the app code.

**With error** (It is reexport through index.ts: `export { FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS } from './fetchStorefrontSections.gql'`):

```js
import { FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS } from 'src/entities/Storefront/api/fetchStorefrontSections'
```

**Without error** (It is direct path):

```js
import { FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS } from 'src/entities/Storefront/api/fetchStorefrontSections/fetchStorefrontSections.gql'
```


I found out, that error throws from generated bundle.js code:

```js
......
/***/ "./src/entities/Storefront/api/fetchStorefrontSections/index.ts":
  /*!***********************************************************************!*\
    !*** ./src/entities/Storefront/api/fetchStorefrontSections/index.ts ***!
    \***********************************************************************/
  /***/ (function(module, __webpack_exports__, __webpack_require__) {
  
  "use strict";
  __webpack_require__.r(__webpack_exports__);
  /* harmony export */ __webpack_require__.d(__webpack_exports__, {
  /* harmony export */   "FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS": function() { return /* reexport safe */ _fetchStorefrontSections_gql__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__.FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS; },
  /* harmony export */ });
  /* harmony import */ var _fetchStorefrontSections_gql__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(/*! ./fetchStorefrontSections.gql */ "./src/entities/Storefront/api/fetchStorefrontSections/fetchStorefrontSections.gql.ts");
  __webpack_require__.$Refresh$.runtime = __webpack_require__(/*! ./node_modules/react-refresh/runtime.js */ "./node_modules/react-refresh/runtime.js");
......
```

If the `_fetchStorefrontSections_gql__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__` variable declaration and the assignment `FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS` property was swapped, the error would be disappear:

```js
var _fetchStorefrontSections_gql__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__("./src/entities/Storefront/api/fetchStorefrontSections/fetchStorefrontSections.gql.ts");

__webpack_require__.d(__webpack_exports__, {
  "FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS": function() { return _fetchStorefrontSections_gql__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__.FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS; },
});
```

title: support css/auto as css module type
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
I want to use webpack css module to handle less-loader result
```ts
{
   module: {
    rules: [{
       test: /\.less$/,
       use: ['less-loader'],
       type: 'css/auto'
     }]
   }
}
```
without css/auto we need to configure two rules
```ts
{
   module: {
    rules: [{
       test: /\.module.less$/,
       use: ['less-loader'],
       type: 'css/module'
     },{
       test: /(?<!module).less$/,
       use: ['less-loader'],
       type: 'css/global'
     }]
   }
}
```
**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
simplify the interop of experimental.css with less | sass
**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
add a separate `css/auto` to automatic handle css file like css-loader https://github.com/webpack-contrib/css-loader/blob/ef749f29f8696b93f9eea61e6d00f2bb30ce094c/src/utils.js#L543
**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes
author: hardfist

title: Should NormalModuleLoaderContext<OptionsTypes> have loaderContext._module as optional?
body:
**What is the current behavior?**
Doing a little webpack 4 => 5 upgrading and using the types as my guide, but then it struck me when I saw a type notation that `loaderContext._module` is of type `NormalModule | undefined` in the types file. 

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/4b4ca3bb53f36a5b8fc6bc1bd976ed7af161bd80/declarations/LoaderContext.d.ts#L21-L53

When I checked the code where the loaderContext is created: 

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/4b4ca3bb53f36a5b8fc6bc1bd976ed7af161bd80/lib/NormalModule.js#L553-L674

It is unconditionally set to `this` aka `NormalModule`. I think that the types for the `NormalModuleLoaderContext<OptionTypes>` should not declare _module as possibly undefined. I believe this should also probably apply to _compiler and _compilation. 

Is there something I'm missing that would make these possibly `undefined`?

Happy to produce a PR.

title: Sync store initial file-system cache when idleTimeoutForInitialStore is zero
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

When parameter [idleTimeoutForInitialStore](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cacheidletimeoutforinitialstore) set to `0` for file-system caches, force write initial cache on disk immediately after build, for now it will be still async.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

We use webpack in jest test suites - have a lot of small SSR applications for testing specific features, and for every test suite in setup hooks we build this application once.

And this tests are unstable, because of unpredictable cache persisting behaviour and timings (no way to wait before it will finished):
- we start application build
- wait for `done` hook before start test suite
- Jest run test cases
- somewhere in the middle of testing process webpack starting write caches on disk
- Node.js event loop blocks for 1-2 seconds
- requests fails on timeouts

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

When `cache: { idleTimeoutForInitialStore: 0 }`, run initial cache store synchronously on `afterDone` hook

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**

Not sure because of complex idle tasks flow

title: Target: electron-preload outputs non-working js
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
When setting the `target` in webpack to e.g. `electron22.0-preload`, the generated js includes webpack boilerplate code that uses `__dirname`, however `__dirname` is no longer available in electron preload scripts (https://github.com/electron/forge/issues/2931). The specific code that I am seeing is this:

```javascript
/******/ 	/* webpack/runtime/compat */
/******/ 	
/******/ 	if (typeof __webpack_require__ !== 'undefined') __webpack_require__.ab = __dirname + "/native_modules/";
```
The result is that electron cannot load the preload script, resulting in the whole app failing to load.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Webpack should output code that is valid for the specified target, in this case it should just not output the `__webpack_require__` boilerplate at all.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.75.0
Node.js version: 16.17.1
Operating System: Windows 10
Additional tools: electron 22.0.0
