title: Infinity loop in moduleFederationPlugin, in runtime loading module
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
If your shared module do not import all modules in main index.js file(use for tree shaking), fallback module infitity looping browser runtime.
[Consumes](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/sharing/ConsumeSharedRuntimeModule.js#L311) function should call infinity, while maximum call stack size exceeded.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
### Hints for moduleFederationPlugin:
#### Provider:
```
{
  ...,
  "ui/blocks/address/address": {
    "import": "./libs/ui/blocks/address/address.js",
    "requiredVersion": "^164.1.0",
    "version": "164.1.0",
    "packageName": "ui/build/blocks/address/address.js"
  },
  "ui/blocks/address/geoObjectHelper": {
    "import": "./libs/ui/blocks/address/geoObjectHelper.js",
    "requiredVersion": "^164.1.0",
    "version": "164.1.0",
    "packageName": "ui/build/blocks/address/geoObjectHelper.js"
  },
  "ui/blocks/address/location": {
    "import": "./libs/ui/blocks/address/location.js",
    "requiredVersion": "^164.1.0",
    "version": "164.1.0",
    "packageName": "ui/build/blocks/address/location.js"
  },
  ...
}
```
#### Consumer:
```
...,
{
  "ui/blocks/address/address": {
    "requiredVersion": "^168.1.3",
    "version": "168.1.3",
  },
  "ui/blocks/address/geoObjectHelper": {
    "requiredVersion": "^168.1.3",
    "version": "168.1.3",
  },
  "ui/blocks/address/location": {
    "requiredVersion": "^168.1.3",
    "version": "168.1.3",
  }
},
...
```
### Generated runtime module

#### Example moduleToHandlerMapping
```
...,
"webpack/sharing/consume/default/ui/blocks/address/addressContent/ui/blocks/address/address": () => (loadStrictVersionCheckFallback("default", "ui/blocks/address/address", [1,168,1,3], () => (__webpack_require__.e("ui-fallback").then(() => (() => (__webpack_require__(/*! ui/blocks/address/addressContent */ "./node_modules/@hh.ru/ui/build/blocks/address/addressContent.js"))))))),
"webpack/sharing/consume/default/ui/blocks/address/addressHeader/ui/blocks/address/geoObjectHelper": () => (loadStrictVersionCheckFallback("default", "ui/blocks/address/geoObjectHelper", [1,168,1,3], () => (__webpack_require__.e("ui-fallback").then(() => (() => (__webpack_require__(/*! ui/blocks/address/addressHeader */ "./node_modules/@hh.ru/ui/build/blocks/address/addressHeader.js"))))))),
"webpack/sharing/consume/default/ui/blocks/address/addressFooter/ui/blocks/address/location": () => (loadStrictVersionCheckFallback("default", "ui/blocks/address/location", [1,168,1,3], () => (__webpack_require__.e("ui-fallback").then(() => (() => (__webpack_require__(/*! ui/blocks/address/addressFooter */ "./node_modules/@hh.ru/ui/build/blocks/address/addressFooter.js"))))))),
...
```

#### Example ChunkMapping
```
{
    'ui-fallback': [
        'webpack/sharing/consume/default/ui/blocks/address/address',
        'webpack/sharing/consume/default/react/react',
        'webpack/sharing/consume/default/ui/blocks/address/geoObjectHelper/ui/blocks/address/geoObjectHelper',
        'webpack/sharing/consume/default/ui/blocks/address/location/ui/blocks/address/location',
    ],
    ...
}
```

#### Register
```
...,
register("ui/blocks/address/address", "168.1.3", () => (Promise.all([__webpack_require__.e("ui-fallback"), __webpack_require__.e("vendors-node_modules_react_jsx-runtime_js")]).then(() => (() => (__webpack_require__(/*! ./node_modules/ui/blocks/address/address.js */ "./node_modules/ui/blocks/address/address.js"))))));
...,
```
#### Example 
Consumer front should use fallback version, but runtime code is falled by maximum call stack.
I debugged the problem and found the problematic place: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/16625
return statement not work in forEach loop. I replaced to for loop and add check to start init modules(it fixes infinity looping).

<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Fallback modules loaded without infinity looping


<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->

Node.js version: ^14 | ^16 | ^18

title: Support of asynchronous computation in DefinePlugin.runtimeValue
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
DefinePlugin.runtimeValue supporting async function / promise / callback for resolving value.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
DefinePlugin.runtimeValue allow dynamic generation of define values and re-generation based on file-system changes. 
Lack of async support means that 

* If value is calculated from file contents they have to be read synchronously
* The processing can not use any library that provide exclusively async (promise/callback) api
* Blocking sequential operations will slow down the build
* Blocking operations will block the whole process which may be a big problem if webpack is as part of bigger program  


**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
I do not understand the code enough to suggest solution.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: CommonJS module receiving ESM export
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
I am building a typescript application with electron-forge. My tsconfig is set to module `CommonJS`, with `Node` moduleresolution, due to limitations in electron-forge libs. 
When I import `swr` I get the ESM export from the swr module when running in development. I assume this is due to the module's package.json having no exports field, but main/module, and webpack picking module in this case, which gives me the module export from `swr`. This is expected and works fine.
Another one of my (own) dependencies gets imported as CommonJS. I assume it's due to the module's package.json.export-field, which is set to give the CommonJS export for `require` and ESM for `import`. This also works fine, and is expected when building as CommonJS.

However, a problem occurs when that module also imports `swr`, but this time via a CommonJS require-call. It seems to receive the same module as the first import of `swr`, which was resolved to the ESM export. This unfortunately doesn't work as the ESM module uses a named default export, which doesn't seem to be resolved the same way via CommonJS require-calls. The CommonJS code gets the exported object, which can't be called as a function (the expected default export).

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Import `swr` and receive ESM export. Import another CommonJS module which itself imports`swr` via require. The second call receives the cached (i assume) ESM module, which doesn't have a valid CommonJS default.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
I think my expected behavior is that the require-call in my external CommonJS module should receive the CommonJS export from the `swr` module, and not the ESM export.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.74.0
Node.js version: 16.18.1
Operating System: macOS 12.6
Additional tools: Typescript 4.8.4

title: HMR Support for ModuleChunkFormatPlugin
body:
## Feature request

**What is the expected behavior?**

Importing experimental modules via webpack should allow HMR.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

I've been working on finding a good solution for dynamic module loading in an MFE Architecture, in doing so I found that you can pretty elegantly load MFE scripts for remotes in a way to downloads them only once using dynamic module loading import('') syntax. In these cases the ModuleChunkFormatPlugin throws an error when attempting to use hot reload functionality citing that is has yet to be implemented.


**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Ideally it would be comparable in use and implementation of other HMR features. I don't know enough to say what that implementation should be.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**

No, I don't have the level of expertise of this task.

If this is indeed a desired behavior, perhaps there needs to be a mention somewhere in the docs.

Treeshaking docs (https://webpack.js.org/guides/tree-shaking/#conclusion) mention all the conditions necessary for it to work. Again `devtool` is not mentioned.

Can see it in our repo
-  clone https://github.com/infor-design/enterprise-wc
-  edit `webpack.config.dev.js` so that  `devtool: 'cheap-module-source-map' ` (not `eval-source-map` as it is now as a workaround)
- `nvm use` (node 18 or 16 so not a node issue)
- `npm i` 
- `npm run start`
- change any file like src/components/ids-about/ids-about.ts (remove a `;`) and press save
- stack trace will appear

**What is the expected behavior?**
This seems to be a new issue with something introduced recently in the `npm` ecosystem, as it happens on old and new versions of our project with various versions of the dependencies.

devtool seems related ->https://webpack.js.org/configuration/devtool/ 
I also changed some of these options in webpack.config.dev.js and the "eval" ones seem to work...

Seems also to happen with more files like our repo has. We can run in "filter mode" with less entries and the issue does not occur `npm --components=about run start`

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.75.0
Node.js version: 18
Operating System: Mac
Additional tools:

author: hamzahamidi

title: Pre dead code elimination before module graph and bundle graph been built by native javascript parser, for smaller bundle size.
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
I know in the current webpack build process, dead code removal happens last, which relies on minifier like terser to drop the dead code on per chunk level thanks to @alexander-akait kind explanation.
However, this can lead to significantly bigger chunk sizes due to lack of information.

Take the below code for example,  let's say we have two different builds, one for PC users and one for mobiles. Each of the files refers to different esm modules of common node modules packages like lodash-es and MUI.
This common chunk is spilt into a separate chunk by SplitChunkPlugin for better cache opportunity.

``` typescript
export const treeShakingTestAsync = async () => {
    console.log('treeShakingTestAsync');
    //  Be replaced with 'false' by DefinePlugin
    if (__MOBILE__) {
        const { Baz } = await import(/* webpackChunkName: "bundle_mobile" */'./mobile');
        console.log(Baz);
    } else {
        const { foo } = await import(/* webpackChunkName: "bundle_pc" */ './pc');
        console.log(foo);
    }

};

```
`pc.ts` and `mobile.ts` both use some of the code from a common module.
```typescript
// pc.ts
export { bar , foo } from './lib-modules';
```
```typescript
// mobile.ts
export { Baz } from './lib-modules';
```
`lib-modules.ts` mimics a large esm package index file.
``` typescript
// lib-modules
export function foo() { console.log('FuncFoo') };
export function bar() {
    console.log('FuncFoo')
};
export class Baz {
    private c = 'ClassBaz'
}
```
For PC build, we won't want Baz to appear in the final bundle.
But If we perform dead code removal purely on chunks level, after the module graph is built, `Baz` is already marked as 'exported' and used. Terser or any other minifier would not know that it is never used, because this information happens across files, only webpack level's javascript compiler knows such kind of thing.

And in the above situation, webpack [does pre-optimize dead code removal](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/4b4ca3bb53f36a5b8fc6bc1bd976ed7af161bd80/lib/javascript/JavascriptParser.js#L1679).
It prevents further dependency build processes in dead branches.
However, this case is just a simple example of what can pre dead code elimination can achieve.

There are still a lot of scenarios not covered.
1. Per File level optimization, like if-return.
``` typescript
export const treeShakingTestAsync = async () => {
    console.log('treeShakingTestAsync');


    const { foo } = await import(/* webpackChunkName: "bundle_pc" */ './pc');
    console.log(foo);
    // Code wont't be reached after if_return, but still get to be bundled, and Baz get to be bundled as well, due to splitchunk.
    if (false) {
        return;
    }
    const { Baz } = await import(/* webpackChunkName: "bundle_mobile" */'./mobile');
    console.log(Baz);
};

```
2. unused exports
``` typescript
export const treeShakingTestAsync = async () => {
    console.log('treeShakingTestAsync');
    
    const { foo } = await import(/* webpackChunkName: "bundle_pc" */ './pc');
    console.log(foo);
};

export const unusedExport = async () => {
    console.log('unusedExport');
    // this never be refered from entry.
    const { Baz } = await import(/* webpackChunkName: "bundle_mobile" */ './mobile');
    console.log(Baz);
};
```
<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
dead code won't appear in module graph.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
Much Smaller bundle size for giant project.
I work on a large private company SPA codebase with its bundle size over 70MB before gizpped, with many reasonable or unreasonable splitchunk rules. I found that most unused code can be avoided by simply just not letting the PC user download the Mobile version code.
Furthermore, more modules involved not only affect download size but also compile time. With 5K esm modules, the webpack_ requires process alone[ takes nearly one whole second](https://nolanlawson.com/2016/08/15/the-cost-of-small-modules/). 
Even concatenated modules won't help, because the file is so large, way above the 170 KB [suggested best practice](https://web.dev/performance-budgets-101/), so we have to spilt the initial chunk into several chunks. 
But more split chunk rules lead to more unused code which ends up with huge code size bloat.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
Webpack's level javascript parser code removal to avoid unnecessary modules in the module graph and chunks in the chunk graph.
If for some reason this cannot be achieved, would running terser as loader after ts loader in order to drop dead code in advance be a dumb idea? 
@sokra @alexander-akait 
**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes
author: icy0307

title: Expensive module hashing for all files on every rebuild
body: # Bug report **What is the current behavior?** When running `webpack` with `watch` mode and collecting a CPU profile, I can observe that the `createModuleHashes` step in the `seal` phase is costing us a large amount of time (often 1s+). In the screenshot below, it took ~400ms **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** The steps on our project are: 1. Run `npx webpack --watch`, then making a change to a file **What is the expected behavior?** My expectation is that webpack would keep a cache of hashes for all modules that have _not_ triggered a change, and would re-use those hashes. This may be a bad expectation depending on what webpack is using the hash for (is it just file contents? maybe includes changes to deps?). It _looks_ like `lib/Compilation.js` does keep a cache for hashes in `createModuleHashes` (`moduleMemCaches2`), but it's empty in our project whenever a rebuild is triggered: At the end of this method, `statModulesFromCache` is always a number > 2k **Other relevant information:** webpack version: `5.73.0` Node.js version: `16.12.0` Operating System: macOS Additional tools:

title: NormalModule original source is undefined when cache is enabled
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When the webpack cache is enabled, the module source inside the compilation is not accessible, it returns _randomly_ `undefined`. The source is get with [`NormalModule.originalSource()._value`](https://github.com/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/blob/master/src/index.ts#L159) in the plugin `svg-chunk-webpack-plugin`.

The plugin uses the `processAssets` hook with the stage `PROCESS_ASSETS_STAGE_ADDITIONAL`. All stages listed in the webpack documentation produces the same error ([list of asset processing stages](https://webpack.js.org/api/compilation-hooks/#list-of-asset-processing-stages)).

The cache is enabled with this code

```js
cache: {
	type: 'filesystem'
}
```

The error log produced by webpack is the following. The error appears when the module source is decoded from JSON.

```txt
[webpack-cli] HookWebpackError: Unexpected token u in JSON at position 0
    at makeWebpackError (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/node_modules/webpack/lib/HookWebpackError.js:48:9)
    at /Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compilation.js:3060:12
    at eval (eval at create (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:33:10), <anonymous>:20:1)
    at processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:96:5)
-- inner error --
SyntaxError: Unexpected token u in JSON at position 0
    at JSON.parse (<anonymous>)
    at SvgSprite.optimizeSvg (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:41:24)
    at /Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:120:52
    at Array.map (<anonymous>)
    at SvgSprite.processEntry (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:120:21)
    at /Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:97:68
    at Array.map (<anonymous>)
    at SvgSprite.addAssets (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:97:32)
    at fn (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compilation.js:536:19)
```

title: Module federation warns "Unable to find required version" when using package self referencing
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

In an esm package, I have two modules, one imports another by [self referencing](https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html#packages_self_referencing_a_package_using_its_name)

```json
{
  "name": "my-pkg",
  "type": "module",
  "exports": {
    "./module-a": "./module-a.js",
    "./module-b": "./module-b.js"
  }
}
```

```js
// module-a.js
export function a() {}
```

```js
// module-b.js
import { a } from "my-pkg/module-a";

export function b() {
  return a();
}
```

In another app, I use module federation and share the two modules:

```js
// Partial webpack.config.js
new ModuleFederationPlugin({
  shared: {
    "my-pkg/module-a": {
      singleton: true,
      version: "1.0.0"
    },
    "my-pkg/module-b": {
      singleton: true,
      version: "1.0.0"
    },
  }
});
```

When I start the dev-server, it reports a warning of:

```
WARNING in shared module my-pkg/module-a
No required version specified and unable to automatically determine one. Unable to find required version for "my-pkg" in description file (.../packages/my-pkg/package.json). It need to be in dependencies, devDependencies or peerDependencies.
```

And according to the warning, I added the package itself as a dependency, the warning disappears:

```json
{
  "name": "my-pkg",
  "type": "module",
  "exports": {
    "./module-a": "./module-a.js",
    "./module-b": "./module-b.js"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "my-pkg": "*" // It's weird to do this...
  }
}
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

It's a bit complex in my real repo, I can provide a minimal repro if you require, when I got time.

**What is the expected behavior?**

No warnings. It should use the description file (`my-pkg/package.json`) itself to determine the required version, since it's a self referencing.

title: Cross-origin Web Workers still require worker-loader
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

It's known that Web Workers must be served from same origin as the page itself. Seems that even `Content-Security-Policy: worker-src http://xxx` has no effect on this.

We can use `worker-loader` to [create a blob](https://github.com/webpack-contrib/worker-loader#cross-origin-policy) instead of a worker file.

BUT documentation https://webpack.js.org/loaders/worker-loader/ says:

```DEPRECATED for v5: https://webpack.js.org/guides/web-workers/```

The problem is, despite deprecation, this is the only way for now to overcome cross origin issues, [documentation](https://webpack.js.org/guides/web-workers/) does not say anything about cross domain.

There are various solutions, but none of them work properly with Webpack...

- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/14648 — using this approach worker file will contain sources (with `import` statements etc.)
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/23953543/cross-domain-web-workers
- https://benohead.com/blog/2017/12/06/cross-domain-cross-browser-web-workers
- https://medium.com/@krishnachirumamilla/content-security-policy-worker-src-cd06ecfa2fe8

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I have put together the repo: https://github.com/kirill-konshin/cross-domain-worker/tree/original-issue, clone, run `npm install` and `npm start`, then just open `http://localhost:3000` and check console. The demo exhibits following:

2 servers, static HTML on `:3000` and Webpack Dev Server on `:4001`.

Page served from `:3000`:

```html
<html>
  <script src="http://localhost:4001/entry.js"></script>
</html>
```

JS served from `:4001`:

```js
// entry.js
const worker = new Worker(new URL('./worker', import.meta.url));

// worker.js
console.log('Foo');
```

If you open the `http://localhost:3000`, you'll get: 

```Uncaught DOMException: Failed to construct 'Worker': Script at 'http://localhost:4000/test/src_worker_js.js' cannot be accessed from origin 'http://localhost:3000'.```

[Documentation](https://webpack.js.org/guides/web-workers/) does not suggest any solution for this.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Two options:

1. Provide a good example how to make it work using modern approach _or_
2. Un-deprecate `worker-loader` and provide documentation when to use it, and hopefully fix the additional issue

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Additional issue**

If 2nd approach will be taken, I'd like to report an additional problem, but the loader repo is archived, so I'll put it here, with the workaround.

Since `worker-loader` will create a blob, it has no access to proper `__webpack_public_path__`, in worker it will be `blob:http://localhost:3000/`, e.g. host origin, so worker loaded this way won't have access to assets.

I've assembled a short demo, where I added `devServer.proxy` which can serve same content from `/test/` and `/` to mimic how content can be served from different CDNs and paths using `output.publicPath='auto'`.

Solution is to `postMessage` proper `__webpack_public_path__` from main thread, and then wrap all asset requests in the worker:

```js
// entry.js
const worker = new Worker('./worker', import.meta.url);
worker.postMessage({msg: 'base', payload: __webpack_public_path__});

// worker.js
function joinSegments(a, b) {
    return '/' + a.split('/').concat(b.split('/')).filter(Boolean).join('/');
}

function replaceOrigin(blob, base) {
    const url = new URL(blob.replace('blob:', ''));
    const search = url.searchParams.toString();
    const baseUrl = new URL(base);
    return new URL(joinSegments(baseUrl.pathname, url.pathname) + (search ? '?' + search : ''), baseUrl.origin).toString();
}

self.onmessage = ({data: {msg, payload}}) => {
    if (msg === 'base') {
        const image = require('./package.png');
        console.log(payload); // returns http://localhost:4001/test/ which is right
        console.log(image); // returns blob:http://localhost:3000/xxx.png, should be localhost:4001
        console.log(__webpack_public_path__); // returns blob:http://localhost:3000/, should be http://localhost:4001/test/

        const imageProper = replaceOrigin(image, payload);
        console.log(imageProper);
        fetch(imageProper).then(r => console.log(r));
    }
};
```

Very awkward, but works well.

Hopefully others can either copy-paste the hack, or, fingers crossed, `worker-loader` can be revived and can provide some normal way to recover dynamic public paths.

title: createRequire not detected when imported with `node:` prefix
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

using `createRequire` isn't detected when imported with `node:` prefix. i.e. the imported module is not resolved.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

A dependency (`@adobe/fetch`) uses createRequire to import the package.json:

```js
import { createRequire } from 'node:module';
const require = createRequire(import.meta.url);
const pkg = require('../../package.json');
```

the generated bundle contains:

```js
const require = (0,node_module__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__.createRequire)("file:///..../node_modules/@adobe/fetch/src/core/request.js");
const pkg = require('../../package.json');
```

which doesn't work, since the `package.json` will not be available once using the bundle.

when we change the import and remove the `node:` prefix:

```js
import { createRequire } from 'module';
const require = createRequire(import.meta.url);
const pkg = require('../../package.json');
```

then bundle package.json is resolved and included in the bundle:

```js
const require = /* createRequire() */ undefined;
const pkg = __webpack_require__(/*! ../../package.json */ "./node_modules/@adobe/fetch/package.json");
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

`createRequire` should be detected even if it's imported via the `node:module` prefix. 

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.75.0
Node.js version: 18.13.0
Operating System: macOS
author: tripodsan

title: get rid of `new Function('return this')()` in the final bundle
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

it looks like we still have `new Function('return this')()` in the final bundle even just target for web, i assume its from the global shim: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/b67626c7b4ffed8737d195b27c8cea1e68d58134/lib/runtime/GlobalRuntimeModule.js#L19


![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/121891361/219204465-4aba3af5-e959-49be-b962-57d2aadad275.png)

it will not cause any csp violation in most browsers as the path will not be executed, but when it does, it might hit CSP issues. I just saw this post (https://mathiasbynens.be/notes/globalthis) and it seems that we can get rid of the `new Function` for most browsers (except IE9, 10).

Wondering if it's possible we adapt that and makes an exception just for IE9,10 so if  IE9,10 not included in the `browserslist`, then the final bundle does not need the `new Function('return this')()`

**What is the expected behavior?**

no `new Function('return this')()` if target browser does not include IE9, 10

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

it's weird to see `new Function()` call when work on CSP, would be great if we can live without it.

title: webpack 4 hardcode md4 as hash function which not able to use on node 17 or above
body:
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/webpack-4/lib/optimize/SplitChunksPlugin.js#L25

Seriously???

```
const hashFilename = name => {
	return crypto
		.createHash("md4")
		.update(name)
		.digest("hex")
		.slice(0, 8);
};
```

You guys provide output.hashFunction option but it is useless.

- I'm using nuxtjs, which only support webpack 4: https://github.com/nuxt/nuxt/issues/9268
- webpack4 hardcode md4 in hash filename
- md4 is forbidden on node 17 and above

PLEASE provide a option for webpack4 to use custom hash function for hashFilename.

Related stackoverflow question (anwsers are useless).
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/69692842/error-message-error0308010cdigital-envelope-routinesunsupported

title: topLevelAwait is still under experimental section
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

According to the latest docs, `topLevelAwait` should be used via `experimental` [section](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/experiments/). In the same time, top level await is already a part of [specification](https://tc39.es/ecma262/#sec-async-function-definitions) (stage 4). I think it's time already to remove it from experimental section.

**What is the expected behavior?**

Excluded `topLevelAwait` from `experimental` section.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

No reason to consider this feature as experimental since it's already in specification.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Just exclude this option and this behaviour should be enabled by default.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
no

title: Importing webpack-bundled ESM into a webpack app gets error due to shadowed variable
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

- Bundling a library into an ES Module with webpack outputs `var __webpack_exports__ = {};` at the top level.
- Trying to include this ES Module in an app also built with webpack, using the eval devtool (as is default with webpack-dev-server), it outputs `__webpack_require__.r(__webpack_exports__);` at the very start of the `eval`'d code. This code seems to be expecting to reference a variable from outside the `eval`, but instead `__webpack_exports__` is **shadowed**.
- At runtime, it gets an error due to `__webpack_exports__` being `undefined`:
    ```
    Uncaught TypeError: Object.defineProperty called on non-object
        at Function.defineProperty (<anonymous>)
        at __webpack_require__.r (app-bundle.js:82:21)
        at eval (library-bundle.esm.js:1:21)
        at ../library/dist/library-bundle.esm.js (app-bundle.js:29:1)
        at __webpack_require__ (app-bundle.js:53:41)
        at eval (app-source.js:2:93)
        at ./app-source.js (app-bundle.js:19:1)
        at __webpack_require__ (app-bundle.js:53:41)
        at app-bundle.js:93:37
        at app-bundle.js:95:12
    ```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

[Here is a minimal reproduction](https://github.com/1j01/webpack-issue-16744), with instructions in the README.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

I don't know whether the library or app bundle output should change, or both,
but the app should be able to `import` or `require` the library without error.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.75.0  
Node.js version: v19.0.0  
Operating System: Ubuntu  
Additional tools: webpack-cli@5.0.1

title: Types: `target` not available on loader context
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

The `target` property is not available on the `LoaderContext` (`this`) type, despite [the documentation stating that it should be there](https://webpack.js.org/api/loaders/#thistarget), and my experience with an existing loader that successfully accesses this property at runtime.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Import the `LoaderContext` type and try to access the `target` property.
```ts
import { type LoaderContext } from 'webpack';

const myLoader = (this: LoaderContext<any>) => {
  console.log(this.target); // TypeScript error: Property 'target' does not exist on type 'LoaderContext<any>'
}
```

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->

<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

The property should be accessible on the type and there should be no TypeScript error.

author: askoufis

title: When compiling dynamic async import(), webpack always return package under default property
body:
# Bug report

Webpack will always transpile `await import('module')` into a syntax that returns `{default: module}` even if the importing module doesn't have a default export.

**What is the current behavior?**
```ts
import assert from 'node:assert';

async function handler() {
  const { Readable } = await import('node:stream');
  assert(typeof Readable !== 'undefined', 'Readable is undefined.');
}

void handler();
```

Real world use case, I'm using a package which does dynamic import, and I'm unable to bundle it with webpack for this solely reason: https://github.com/puppeteer/puppeteer/issues/8531.

I tried to use a loader like this one: https://github.com/morganney/magic-comments-loader to add `/*webpackIgnore: true*/` to any dynamic imports, but it's super slow, unfortunately.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

1. Install repo: https://github.com/rdsedmundo/webpack-bug-dynamic-import-default
2. Run `npm test`.

**What is the expected behavior?**

I realize this may be expected behavior due to design limitations (found a few references to this on the documentation as outlined below), but I was wondering if there are any workarounds on this besides marking the package as an external. It feels overkill that no matter which package it is, if they try to use a dynamic import on this fashion, then we automatically need to skip bundling it completely.

https://webpack.js.org/guides/code-splitting/
> The reason we need default is that since webpack 4, when importing a CommonJS module, the import will no longer resolve to the value of module.exports, it will instead create an artificial namespace object for the CommonJS module. For more information on the reason behind this, read [webpack 4: import() and CommonJs](https://medium.com/webpack/webpack-4-import-and-commonjs-d619d626b655).

https://webpack.js.org/guides/lazy-loading/
> Note that when using import() on ES6 modules you must reference the .default property as it's the actual module object that will be returned when the promise is resolved.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.75.0
Node.js version: 18.13.0
Operating System: macOS 13.1
Additional tools: N/A
author: rdsedmundo

title: Performance issue in RealContentHashPlugin on large builds with many assets
body: # Bug report When an application contains a large number of assets and uses `optimization.realContentHash`, the RealContentHashPlugin takes a very long time on the first asset that it processes for hash renaming. On a build with ~3200 assets, this results in approximately 40s spent in `RealContentHashPlugin` which is almost entirely on the first invocation of the use of an expensive regular expression. **What is the current behavior?** Here's a CPU profile showing time spent in RealContentHashPlugin during a build: Our application/config contains private code, so i can't share the complete results, but I've traced it to the regular expression that's generated from the asset keys in `RealContentHashPlugin`. For an application like ours with ~3200 assets, this results in a regular expression that is the combination of each of those asset names joined by `|` to form a group. Instantiating this regular expression is inexpensive, but the first time it is invoked in takes ~40s to compile the underlying expression. The evaluation is then very fast, but the first invocation delays the entire process. **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** Reproduction repo: https://github.com/ryanwilsonperkin/webpack-16758 **What is the expected behavior?** RealContentHashPlugin should avoid using a large group in this case, and instead should use individual regular expressions to match/replace each of the assets in a loop. Profiling this yielded a reduction from `41.87s to 0.006s`. **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.74.0 Node.js version: v16.18.1 Operating System: macOS 13.2 Additional tools:

title: Reference to class name in body of class expression is replaced with reference to import when class name shadows import name
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

The calls to static members of the class fail inside the class body when the name of the class in a class expression shadow a import name.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

a.mjs:

```js
export const a = 1;
```

index.mjs:

```js
import { a as C } from "./a.mjs"

let CV = class C {
  static name = "test";
  static test() {
    console.log(C.name);
  }
}

console.log(C);
CV.test();
```

Bundled code snippet in `none` mode:

```js
let CV = class C {
  static name = "test";
  static test() {
    console.log(_a_mjs__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__.a.name);
  }
}

console.log(_a_mjs__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__.a);
CV.test();

})();
```

Bundled code in `production` mode:

```js
(()=>{"use strict";console.log(1),class{static name="test";static test(){console.log(1..name)}}.test()})();
```

Running dist output in either mode:

```console
> node dist/index.js
1
undefined
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

Running src code:

```console
> node src/index.mjs
1
test
```

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.75.0
Node.js version: 19.7.0
Operating System: 
Additional tools:

This code pattern came about as a result of the changes in Rollup PR https://github.com/rollup/rollup/pull/4827 and how Terser will shadow the import when possible when mangling.

title: Webpack 5 incorrectly compiles CJS module `marked`
body:
# Bug report


## What is the current behavior?

When loading [`marked`](https://github.com/markedjs/marked) (CJS) through a [`pnpm`](https://pnpm.io/) monorepo package with Webpack 5, it compiles incorrectly. `marked` compiles to:

```js
/***/ "../node_modules/.pnpm/marked@4.2.12/node_modules/marked/lib/marked.cjs":
/*!******************************************************************************!*\
  !*** ../node_modules/.pnpm/marked@4.2.12/node_modules/marked/lib/marked.cjs ***!
  \******************************************************************************/
/***/ ((module, __unused_webpack_exports, __webpack_require__) => {

"use strict";
module.exports = __webpack_require__.p + "static/media/marked.79f9f27bbd94805ba150.cjs";

/***/ }),
```

According to the maintainers of `marked` [this looks like a bug in Webpack](https://github.com/markedjs/marked/issues/2265#issuecomment-1454358096). 

## If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.

There's a minimal repo showcasing the bug here: 
https://github.com/laustdeleuran/marked-2265-module-import-export-issue

And some deeper context in this issue in `marked`: 
https://github.com/markedjs/marked/issues/2265#issuecomment-1451124186

## What is the expected behavior?

`marked` gets compiled correctly. 

## Other relevant information:
**webpack version:** 5
**Node.js version:** 14 or 16
**Operating System:** MacOS
**Additional tools:** [`pnpm`](https://pnpm.io/) and [`create-react-app`](https://create-react-app.dev/)

title: Add `{ withFileTypes: boolean }` options type on `compiler.inputFileSystem.readdir`
body:
Today `compiler.inputFileSystem.readdir` doesn't include the type information for passing `{withFileTypes: boolean}` options parameter. This is useful if you are leveraging inputFileSystem for and you want to read `Dirint` objects instead of `string | buffer` types. 

This is low hanging fruit that anyone could contribute to. Location to code: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/util/fs.js#L113-L205
author: TheLarkInn

title: optimization.realContentHash does not respect output.hashSalt
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
Assets with `[contenthash]` in the filename template do not produce different hashes when changing the value of the `output.hashSalt` webpack configuration property, if `optimization.realContentHash` is `true`.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Create a trivial webpack project with a `[contenthash]` in the output filename template and `optimization.realContentHash` set to `true`.
Vary the value of the `output.hashSalt` property and observe that emitted filenames don't change.

**What is the expected behavior?**
Changing the value of `output.hashSalt` should change the results of *all* filename hashes.

Inspection of the code here: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/b67626c7b4ffed8737d195b27c8cea1e68d58134/lib/optimize/RealContentHashPlugin.js#L365-L368
Shows that `RealContentHashPlugin` does not look up `outputOptions.hashSalt` and apply it to the newly created Hash object.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.75.0
Node.js version: 16.19.1
Operating System: Linux
Additional tools:
author: dmichon-msft

title: Further improvements to performance of hash regex lookups in RealContentHashPlugin
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

Continuing idea for improvements carried over from https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/16759#discussion_r1128302292

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Calculation of whether or not a hash has matched can be reused in our logic for performing hash replacements
Per @alexander-akait ([source](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/16759#discussion_r1128302292)):

> I think there is a another potential improvement - we can store location of found hashRegExps and then avoid using result.replace in hashReplace, , I think it will be faster, but this need to be checked and we can do it latest and merge this as is, because we alreadyh have a good improvement here, so approved

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
Performance speed of RealContentHashPlugin

yes

```
^C<i> [webpack-dev-server] Gracefully shutting down. To force exit, press ^C again. Please wait...
```
</details>
</summary>


### Expected Behavior

The server should run regardless of the characters in the folder path.

### How Do We Reproduce?

Here is a minimal project that can trigger this behavior. 

[webpack.tar.gz](https://github.com/webpack/webpack-dev-server/files/10922481/webpack.tar.gz)

This script should reproduce the behavior if you are using something POXIX-y.

```bash
mkdir "test#"
tar -xzvf webpack.tar.gz -C "test#"
cd "test#/webpack"
npx webpack serve
```

title: Dynamic expression imports are broken on Typescript 4.9.2 and higher
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

I have encountered the same issue since upgrading to Typescript 4.9.5 with a issue that looks the same as the one discussed here: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/16566.

It looks like Typescript 4.9.2+ changed the code that it emits for dynamic imports: the import path is assigned to a variable (see https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/51554) prior to passing it to the import() function. Therefore Webpack does not seem to understand what to import and emits `Critical dependency: the request of a dependency is an expression` warnings. Before v4.9.2 it does not emit any warnings and it works fine.

What's emitted by Typescript 4.9.5:
```
await Promise.all(filesToImport.map((e) => { var _a; return _a = `./relative/path/to/files/${e}`, Promise.resolve().then(() => tslib_1.__importStar(__webpack_require__("./path/of/app sync recursive")(_a))); }))
```

What's emitted by Typescript 4.9.1-beta (and earlier) with the same project and Webpack configuration:
```
await Promise.all(filesToImport.map((e) => Promise.resolve().then(() => tslib_1.__importStar(__webpack_require__("./relative/path/to/files sync recursive ^\\.\\/.*$")(`./${e}`)))))
```

The solution for me is to use Typescript 4.9.1-beta for the time-being, I tried using the dynamic import magic comments but they don't seem to influence much either.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Created a reproduction repo here: https://github.com/jsprw/webpack-typescript495.

Use the following npm scripts to reproduce the error (it will set the correct Typescript version in the script):

- Working fine on Typescript@4.9.1-beta: `npm run typescript-4.9.1-beta`
- Broken on Typescript@latest `npm run typescript-latest`

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

The expected behavior is seen with Typescript version 4.9.1-beta (`npm run typescript-4.9.1-beta`).

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.76.0
Node.js version: v16.15.0
Operating System: MacOS Ventura
Additional tools: Typescript 4.9.5
author: jsprw

title: ReferenceError: Worker is not defined when call new Worker in react useMemo
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**
When call `new Worker()` within React useMemo hook, an error `ReferenceError: Worker is not defined` is thrown.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
I've created [an issue](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/46946) in `next.js` repo. The code is in [this broken repo](https://github.com/yf-yang/with-web-worker-app-broken). Run `pnpm dev` or `pnpm build` to reproduce.

Code that cause the bug:
``` typescript
function createWorker(): Worker {
  return new Worker(new URL("./worker.ts", import.meta.url));
}

function Component() {
  const worker = useMemo(createWorker, []);
}
```

title: [Module Federation] Development server hot reload updates are failing
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

My development server starts fine and serves everything correctly. However, whenever I save a file I get an error message like this and the hot reload doesn't update the file.

```
Uncaught TypeError: Cannot set properties of undefined (setting '../node_modules/ts-loader/index.js??clonedRuleSet-1!../node_modules/vue-loader/dist/index.js??ruleSet[0]!./src/components/my-component.vue?vue&type=script&lang=ts')
    at self.webpackHotUpdateLocalTestBuild (remoteEntry.js:5491:39)
    at src_initialize-app_ts-data_image_png_base64_iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAAAAEAAAACCAIAAAAW4yFwAAAAA-651236.e337b2d8e1999818c118.hot-update.js:10:39
self.webpackHotUpdateLocalTestBuild @ remoteEntry.js:5491
(anonymous) @ src_initialize-app_ts-data_image_png_base64_iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAAAAEAAAACCAIAAAAW4yFwAAAAA-651236.e337b2d8e1999818c118.hot-update.js:10
```

I am unsure if this issue is vue-loader specific, because most updates to regular *.ts files completely refresh the page instead of trying to update in-place.

Interestingly, and importantly: this hot reload error **only occurs** when one of the module federation "remotes" is running. If I shut off the remote, my main shell application complains that it can't reach it when it tries to import it, but then the hot reloads start working again for it!

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Create a standard Vue 3 application with typescript. Implement module federating by referencing the settings in the webpack configuration below.

Unfortunately I cannot share the source application, it is closed source. I am mainly interested if there is some incompatibility in the webpack configuration I am using with module federation that would cause this issue, or perhaps an incompatibility with module federation and the vue-loader. Or maybe plugin order?

title: Disabling evaluation of `import.meta` doesn't work reliably
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Disabling evaluation of `import.meta` doesn't work reliably

**What is the current behavior?**

I have instructed WebPack not to evaluate `import.meta`:

```js
{
  // ...
  module: {
    parser: {
      javascript: {
        importMeta: false,
      },
    },
  },
  // ...
}
```

It works fine in simple cases, but will still evaluate `import.meta` if found in `new Url(path, import.meta.url)`:

```js
// Will not be evaluated (as intended)
console.log(import.meta.url);
console.log(new URL(import.meta.url).toString());

// If used as `base` for `new URL`, import.meta will still be evaluated (the bug):
console.log(new URL("./app.css", import.meta.url).toString());

// Workaround: Storing import.meta in a temporal variable disables evaluation:
const importMeta = import.meta;
console.log(new URL("./app.css", importMeta.url).toString());
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

- Instruct WebPack to disable evaluation of `import.meta` as [described in the docs](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/module/#moduleparserjavascriptimportmeta)
- Try using `new Url("./my-file.ext", import.meta.url)`

**What is the expected behavior?**

WebPack should leave `import.meta` untouched, even if being used inside `new Url("./my-file.ext", import.meta.url)`

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.76.0 
Node.js version: v16.13.2
Operating System: OSX
Additional tools: –

title: webpack is not noticing changes to lib delibered via commit id
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
1. Initial state
I am using angular + yarn + a dependecy which is delievered via git commit e.g.
```json
"dependencies": {
    "@myorg/myproj": "git+https://myproj.git#somehash1111",
}
```
then I do a `yarn install` and `ng serve` and all is good. 
2. Make a change in the `myproj` dependency and commit it inside the `myproj` repo. After the commit a build is triggered for this dependency and the `dist` folder is uploaded as a commit in the `https://myproj.git` project (the `dist` folder has essentialy the output of the `ng build` command).
3. Consume the new version `somehash2222` by changing the `package.json` to
```json
"dependencies": {
    "@myorg/myproj": "git+https://someurl.git#somehash222",
}
```
3. Run `yarn install` and `ng serve`. The content of the old `somehas1111` is delivered. To resolve the issue one needs to clean the angular cache (which in turn cleans the `webpack` cache from the `.angular` folder).

Now at that point I know that `node_modules` is considered immutable and to my understanding the new `immutable` version of the `@myorg/myproj` is correctly delivered via a version change `somehash1111` -> `somehash2222`. To permanently workaround that I have used the [managedpaths](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/other-options/#managedpaths) option to instruct `webpack` to start observing this dir via a regexp similar to the `@asure` example. Which it does and now whenever someone changes the hash delievered dependency it is automatically picked up. But according to my understanding the additional config shall not be needed as there is a clear version change. Can it be that 'webpack' is confused as this is not a semver or because it does not look as dir?

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
See steps 1 to 3 for retproduction

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
when I change the dependency from ` "@myorg/myproj": "git+https://myproj.git#somehash1111"` to ` "@myorg/myproj": "git+https://myproj.git#somehash2222"` would be nice for webpack to notice the change then rebuild and most importantly update the cache.
 
<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  (should be 5.75.0)
We are using it from angular 15 like that (snippet from yarn.lock)

title: Implement new benchmark test/ci system
body:
## webpack performance test harness

For a while, we had setup a performance benchmark system on CI which would compare the performance of any fix/feature at PR time made to webpack/webpack. However, this was so flakey that we had to get rid of it for PR reliability reasons. 

I'd like to rebuild a test/CI system that will run performance benchmarks against PR's that we submit to webpack. This could incorporate any of the systems we've set up in `/benchmark` in the repository. 

Performance is one of the key competitive features in bundlers today, and since we are not making any clear stances so far on native technology bets, I would like to keep performance in a tight feedback loop for the project. 

### Scenario

- [ ] As a maintainer, I want to see a clear performance impact on every PR made to the repository
- [ ] As a maintainer, I want to block any PR on large performance regressions
- [ ] As a maintainer, I want the CI/PR quality gate to be _reliable_ and something I can trust 
- [ ] As a contributor, I want a clear/understandable feedback from the performance benchmarking system when I submit a PR.

title: Build using ModuleFederationPlugin crashes in the browser when using splitChunks
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Build using ModuleFederationPlugin crashes in the browser when using `splitChunks` in `webpack.config.js`.

A workaround solution is available as of now - https://github.com/module-federation/module-federation-examples/issues/692#issuecomment-1382670317

This workaround works by ignoring all chunks that are managed by ModuleFederationPlugin from SplitChunksPlugin

This should be solved in webpack core instead. Willing to fix this myself but not clear whether to fix in SplitChunksPlugin or ModuleFederationPlugin

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

The page crashes completely with the following error in the console

```shell
Uncaught (in promise) ScriptExternalLoadError: Loading script failed.
(missing: http://localhost:8081/remoteEntry.js)
while loading "./RemoteApp" from webpack/container/reference/remote
    at webpack/container/reference/remote (main.js:29:25)
    at __webpack_require__ (main.js:71:33)
    at initExternal (main.js:336:28)
    at __webpack_require__.I (main.js:349:15)
    at main.js:838:47
    at webpack/sharing/consume/default/react/react (main.js:892:67)
    at main.js:923:56
    at Array.forEach (<anonymous>)
    at __webpack_require__.f.consumes (main.js:906:36)
    at main.js:154:40
```

Edit: additional info
On further trial and error:
The build works when splitChunks is enabled in host but disabled in remote.
Here's a diff of the remote entry files generated without and with split chunks - https://www.diffchecker.com/O7K3DdGs/

The error disappears and page works as expected when splitChunks config is commented out

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Reproduction repo with steps in the readme:
https://github.com/burhanuday/webpack-mf-split-chunks-repro

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->

title: Preloading modules with magic comments does not result in modulepreload injection
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

Webpack magic comments do not inject the correct HTML syntax for preloading modules.

**What is the current behavior?**

Preloading a module results in the following HTML injection:
```
<link rel="preload" as="script" href="http://example.com/js/123.456789.chunk.mjs">
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
I believe this is a bug, but I am aware that it could be to do with my configuration...

My configuration is large so I will only include the bit that I think is relevant:

```
target: [ 'web', 'es6' ],
output: {
    filename: 'js/[id].[contenthash].mjs',
    chunkFilename: 'js/[id].[contenthash].chunk.mjs'
}
```

I also enabled `experiments.outputModule` and added `module: true` to the `output`, but I got errors with this, so reverted.

And in one of my files, I have the following line:
```
component: () => import(/* webpackChunkName: "about", webpackPreload: true */ '../views/About.vue')
```


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
I would expect the injected HTML to look like this instead:

```
<link rel="modulepreload" href="http://example.com/js/123.456789.chunk.js">
```

I may be mistaken here as obviously `/* webpackPrefetch: true */` would not work with a module, so perhaps this is intentional, and module preloading is not meant to be supported. If this is the case, then I apologise - I did search the documentation and Google from top to bottom before posting here...

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.76.1
Node.js version: 19.7
Operating System: WSL 2 (Linux on Windows)

title: Caching issue when generating files in the build folder
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
This is a minimal reproduction of the issue we are facing - we are trying to generate files inside the build folder at build time thanks to a custom loader and a custom plugin. However, this has been behaving randomly and the files aren't always moved and populated properly, which means that the different pages can't access the files at runtime. We have managed to narrow it done to the cache as every time we delete the build folder, it would be working. Using `useCache: false` in the webpack config means the issue isn't happening.


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Minimal reproduction on a NextJS app: https://github.com/MeganAlexia/webpack-nextjs-bug-repro 
* Run `yarn` to install the dependencies.
* Make sure to delete the /build folder if you have one.
* Run yarn dev and open "localhost:3000", it should be an empty page with "Hello world".  
* We are expecting the custom plugin (https://github.com/MeganAlexia/webpack-nextjs-bug-repro/blob/main/webpack/plugin/test-plugin.js) and the custom loader (https://github.com/MeganAlexia/webpack-nextjs-bug-repro/blob/main/webpack/loader/test-loader.js) to have run. In the IDE, open /build/testing/text.txt and make sure it's populated with the string ", one, two, three, four".
* Kill the yarn dev and re-run it. Re-check /build/testing/text.txt - it should be an empty file. /!\ You might need to do this step a couple of times as the issue appears randomly and not necessarily on the second run.

title: Chunk modules are not shared with module federation
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
I have the following configuration
remote which uses a vendor library (@design-system) from version 4.15.0
a host which uses a vendor library from version 4.18.0 and loads the remote using module federation
both the remote and the host share the vendor library using module federation, with the following configuration:
```
share: {
'@design-systems/components/': {
    singleton: true,
    strictVersion: true,
    requiredVersion: ">=4.0.0 || <=5.0.0"
  }
}
```
The thing is that components from the library are shared correctly, and are loaded once
But, I did noticed that chunks from that library contain the same components, and are loaded several times
Here is an example

Consider the following components that exist in the design system package
```
//button component
export class Button

//card component
import * from '../button';
export class card

import * from '../button';
export class tab

```
now because the button component is shared, it is extracted to a chunk on build.
So, the dist folder of the design system looks like this:
```
@4.15.0
dist/
      --- chunks/
            chunk.sdhfiuhsad - // button component
      button.js //import chunk.sdhfiuhsad 
      card.js //import chunk.sdhfiuhsad 
      tab.js  //import chunk.sdhfiuhsad 
```
```
@4.18.0
dist/
      --- chunks/
            chunk.asdasdasdasd - // button component
      button.js. //import chunk.asdasdasdasd
      card.js. //import chunk.asdasdasdasd
      tab.js. //import chunk.asdasdasdasd
```
Note that the chunk name which contains the button component is different in both versions

Consider the following implementation of the remote component
```
import * from '@design-system/button'
import * from '@design-system/tab'
import * from '@design-system/card'
```
Now, when I do the following imports on the host app, I had noticed that button code is imported several time
```
import {Button} from 'remote/button';
import {Card} from 'remote/card';
import {Tab} from 'remote/tab';
import * from remote/cmp;
```
I guess it has something to do with the fact that it is part of a chunk, and the chunk name is different in both versions of the design system
I had assumed that since the button component is part of the @design-system, and the @design-system is configured to be single, the button file would only be loaded once.


Consider the following diagram for clarity:

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
![mfe chunks issue](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/22292278/227286671-9e2f73e3-b920-4523-9e8a-8cbcedf4eebd.jpeg)


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
The expected behavior would be that Webpack would (somehow) Identify that the file contains the button component had already been imported, and would not import in again, although exist as a chunk

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**

Node.js version: 
Operating System: 
Additional tools:

title: some bug of realcontenthash plugin
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
some string (which matched old file hash) will be replace in realContentHashPlugin.
(ps. 'old hash' means the content hash before dealing with realContentHashPlugin)

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
First, the hash matched src content is a very small probability event. So I try to mock some data to  reproduce it.

```javascript
// webpack.config.js
module.exports = {
  mode: 'production',
  entry: {
    app: './src/index.js',
  },
  output: {
    path: "dist",
    filename: '[name].[contenthash:8].chunk.js',
  },
  devtool: 'source-map',
  optimization:{
    minimize: false,
  }
};

// src/index.js
import React from 'react';
import ReactDOM from 'react-dom';
import { renderToString } from 'react-dom/server'
import(/* webpackChunkName: 'index' */ './x');
console.log('renderToString: 11c46ae742 ', renderToString);
console.log('ReactDOM: ', ReactDOM);
console.log(React);

// src/x.js
console.log('123123xx~c46ae742');
```

in webpack/lib/optimize/realContentHashPlugin, mock a hash and generate the hashRegexp by it.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37408756/227441818-9c363d16-6301-4174-b98d-9d1cecebad6a.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37408756/227442032-77033308-20a5-49ed-ae91-c15d5b20abbf.png)


Then you can run npm run build. In dist, you can find that all string of 'c46ae742' has been replaced.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37408756/227442344-22cf7d0c-4fd1-4fcd-b2dc-53535ab70e27.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37408756/227442395-79be3ec2-f498-4520-ab98-0d7c06186881.png)

the string of 'c46ae742' will not be replaced

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.75.0
Node.js version:  14.16.0
Operating System:  macOS

**My point of view**
webpack should not replace asset source by  hash regexp (global [`/c46ae742|a1563cb9/g`]), the regexp may be more limited
