title: @swc/helpers is incorrectly removed/tree shaked from the production bundle
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When using `swc-loader` with `@swc/helpers` and mixing ESM and CommonJS it seems that the tree shaking process in webpack incorrectly marks `@swc/helpers` as not used. When changing the repro below to only use ESM I can no longer reproduce it.

My first instinct was that this was an SWC issue and not a webpack issue, but from what I can tell SWC correcty determines the module type and injects `import` statements into ESM code and `require` statements into the CJS code.

Injected into ESM:
```js
import _create_class from "@swc/helpers/src/_create_class.mjs";
```

Into CJS:
```js
var _create_class = require("@swc/helpers").create_class;
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I've created a small repro here:
https://github.com/andersekdahl/webpack-swc-helpers-repro

entry.js:
```js
import common from "./common";
import esm from "./esm";
console.log(common, esm);
```

common.js:
```js
const x = class {
  method() {}
};

console.log(x);

module.exports = "commonjs";
```

esm.js:
```js
const x = class {
  method() {}
};

console.log(x);

export default "esm";
```

webpack.config.js:
```js
module.exports = {
  entry: "./src/entry.js",
  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.js$/,
        use: {
          loader: "swc-loader",
          options: {
            jsc: {
              externalHelpers: true,
            },
            isModule: "unknown",
          },
        },
      },
    ],
  },
};
```

Prettified final bundle:
```js
(() => {
  var e = {
      915: (e, r, o) => {
        var n = o(835).class_call_check,
          t = o(835).create_class,
          a = (function () {
            "use strict";
            function e() {
              n(this, e);
            }
            return t(e, [{ key: "method", value: function () {} }]), e;
          })();
        console.log(a), (e.exports = "commonjs");
      },
      835: () => {},
    },
    r = {};
  function o(n) {
    var t = r[n];
    if (void 0 !== t) return t.exports;
    var a = (r[n] = { exports: {} });
    return e[n](a, a.exports, o), a.exports;
  }
  (o.n = (e) => {
    var r = e && e.__esModule ? () => e.default : () => e;
    return o.d(r, { a: r }), r;
  }),
    (o.d = (e, r) => {
      for (var n in r)
        o.o(r, n) &&
          !o.o(e, n) &&
          Object.defineProperty(e, n, { enumerable: !0, get: r[n] });
    }),
    (o.o = (e, r) => Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(e, r)),
    (() => {
      "use strict";
      var e = o(915),
        r = o.n(e);
      function n(e, r) {
        for (var o = 0; o < r.length; o++) {
          var n = r[o];
          (n.enumerable = n.enumerable || !1),
            (n.configurable = !0),
            "value" in n && (n.writable = !0),
            Object.defineProperty(e, n.key, n);
        }
      }
      var t = (function () {
        function e() {
          !(function (e, r) {
            if (!(e instanceof r))
              throw new TypeError("Cannot call a class as a function");
          })(this, e);
        }
        var r, o;
        return (
          (r = e),
          (o = [{ key: "method", value: function () {} }]) && n(r.prototype, o),
          e
        );
      })();
      console.log(t), console.log(r(), "esm");
    })();
})();
```

Note in the final bundle that module id `835` has an empty function that doesn't return the exports `create_class` and `class_call_check` as expected.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

That the needed exports from `@swc/helpers` are still in the final production bundle.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.76.3
Node.js version:  Tested on 18.13.0 and 16.19.1
Operating System: Tested on Windows and Ubuntu

author: andersekdahl

title: Dynamic import of resources/assets with variable path is broken because such imports are always processed as not having a file extension and are being treated as modules
body:
# Bug report **What is the current behavior?** It's impossible to perform a dynamic import of a non-JS module (a PNG picture for example) using variable paths.
The next code will not add a file to a bundle, even if ithe file exists: > const name="picture"; > import( `./resources/${name}.png`); The problem is in the current module resolving algorithm. I quote the [documentation](https://webpack.js.org/concepts/module-resolution/#module-paths): > Once the path is resolved based on the above rule, the resolver checks to see if the path points to a file or a directory. If the path points to a file: > > If the path has a file extension, then the file is bundled straightaway. > Otherwise, the file extension is resolved using the [resolve.extensions](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/resolve/#resolveextensions) option, which tells the resolver which extensions are acceptable for resolution e.g. .js, .jsx. But if an import has a variable path (no matter which kind - even if it's a full path with an insertion of a variable containing an empty string), webpack decides that there is no extension and performs a module resolution, which means that the file must have a valid module extension. So only files with ".js", ".ts" and are included into a bundle. The module resolution performed before any module rules are checked but after include/exclude import rules. **What is the expected behavior?** The documentation states and the commons sense tells it must be possible to import other module types than plain JS-modules. If an extension is being provided in the import path, then it must be taken in account. And a specified extension it seems to be the most popular and common case when a non-module is dynamically imported. If no extension is provided/detected then the imported files must be checked and if there are any non-empty "module"-extensions then the module resolution must be performed. The good question is: What should be done if both JS-modules and other file types are detected? There are two main possible choices: not import non-modules in a module has been found (but it can be tricky to execute if a whole subtree is imported), or just import everything and let the user decide (but I suppose that there can be some side effects with importing complex modules). Because of that, I see as the most simple solution is when, webpack might require to always specify an extension if anything else apart from a module is being imported with dynamic import AND variable path. If a user wants several file types to be imported, they can use multiple import statements or use other ways to force webpack to include the files/ **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.76.3 Node.js version: 18.15.0 Operating System: Linux Additional tools:

title: Treeshake dynamic imports based on destructuring
body:
Given the following code: 

```javascript
import('lodash-es').then(({uniq}) => uniq)
```

When webpack bundles this code, it should only include the module tree for `uniq` and tree-shake the rest of the exports in `lodash-es` as unused.
author: TheLarkInn

title: Webpack preload only loads js assets.
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
The magic comment Webpack preload only loads js assets when `(/* webpackPreload: true */` is added. CSS and other assets that are required by js assets are delayed until preloaded javascript is executed.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
await-import-by-initial-chunk.js
``` js
export async function awaitImportByInitialChunk() {
    const { awaitImportByAsyncChunk } = await import(/* webpackPreload: true */'./await-imported-by-async-chunk');
    awaitImportByAsyncChunk();
}
```
await-imported-by-async-chunk.js
``` js 
import './await-imported-by-async-chunk.css';
export async function awaitImportByAsyncChunk() {
    console.log('awaitImportByAsyncChunk');
}
```
await-imported-by-async-chunk.css
```css
@font-face {
  font-family: 'Roboto';
  font-weight: 400;
  src: url('../../../../Roboto-Regular.woff2') format('woff2'),
  url('../../../..//Roboto-Regular.woff') format('woff');
}
```
<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Every type of asset that link preload support can be preloaded, not being held until javascript execution.
So `await-imported-by-async-chunk.css` and the corresponding font file should be preloaded as well.

(I don't know if this is a bug or an intentional behavior) 

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.24.4
Node.js version: v19.7.0
Operating System: 
Additional tools:

title: webpack tries to resolve `new URL('./', import.meta.url)` to a module and fails or succeeds (both are wrong)
body:
# Bug report **What is the current behavior?** Webpack's resolution of `new URL(..., import.meta.url)` does something unexpected when the target is a directory, which doesn't correspond to what either the browser or Node will do. For comparison, try this in $BROWSER_OF_YOUR_CHOICE: ```html 


```
or in Node, create `index.mjs` and run it:
```js
console.log(new URL("./", import.meta.url).href);
```
In both cases you should see something like:
```
file:///home/dhd/work/webpack-wasm-test/importmeta/
```
Webpack does ... something else.  See below.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Given the `index.mjs` file above, run:
```
webpack ./index.mjs
```
It will fail with an error like this:
```
ERROR in ./index.mjs 1:12-42
Module not found: Error: Can't resolve './' in '/home/dhd/work/importmeta'
resolve './' in '/home/dhd/work/importmeta'
  Parsed request is a directory
  No description file found in /home/dhd/work/importmeta or above
  No description file found in /home/dhd/work or above
  as directory
    existing directory /home/dhd/work/importmeta
      No description file found in /home/dhd/work/importmeta or above
      using path: /home/dhd/work/importmeta/index
        No description file found in /home/dhd/work/importmeta or above
        no extension
          /home/dhd/work/importmeta/index doesn't exist
```
(if there is a `package.json` somewhere in a parent directory it will output some other stuff about that, but it will still fail in the same way)

I can make it "work" if I initialize the current directory as a module:
```
npm init
npm install -D webpack-cli webpack
touch index.js
npx webpack ./index.mjs
```
This is *not* what I want, because it will resolve the `new URL` above to the module in the current directory, then bundle whatever it can find in that module.  All I wanted was a URL that pointed to the current directory! (and a Pepsi)



See the example here: https://github.com/dhdaines/webpack-wasm-test/tree/main/importmeta







**What is the expected behavior?**

Webpack should just pass `new URL('./', import.meta.url)` through unchanged.  It should not try to resolve a directory as a module. 
 Ideally it should also pass through unchanged any other instance of `new URL(..., import.meta.url)` where the target can't be resolved as an asset, since the user might create them after the fact.




**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  ^5.76.3 => 5.76.3
Node.js version: 14.18.2
Operating System: Linux 5.19 Ubuntu 22.04.2 LTS 22.04.2 LTS (Jammy Jellyfish)

title: Webpack 5 LoaderContext:  `addContextDependency` causes  loader to rerun once after any change in watch mode
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
Our project relies heavily on custom 'heavy' loaders, and fast compilation is crucial. In watch mode, every loader that utilizes `addContextDependency` reruns one more time after any project change, leading to longer build times. Trying to find a solution to minimize the re-running of these loaders and improve watch mode performance.

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

After initial compilation in watch mode every loader that uses `addContextDependency` will rerun one more time after any change in the project.

## What is the current behavior?

Custom loaders that use `addContextDependency` will rerun only if supplied context dependencies are changed.

## If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.

Reproducible example here:  
https://github.com/modjke/webpack-5-add-context-dependency-issue
`txt-loader.js` will run twice: at startup and for the first time `index.js` is changed

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

## What is the expected behavior? 

Loaders with `addContextDependency` should run only once on initial compilation in watch mode.

## What have we tried?
- debugging `watchpack`, as it turns out `watchpack` reports changes correctly
- using async/sync loaders (same bug appears)
- increasing `aggregateTimeout` 
- disabling hot module reload

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

## Other relevant information:
webpack version:  5.76.3
Node.js version: 18.15.0
Operating System:  Mac OS (M1), Windows 11 H22, Windows 10
Additional tools: ~

title: Can we generate the exported part at the end of current module?
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

If there is a ES module like this:

```js
console.log('module');
export default const a = () => {};
```

Webpack currently generates the code like this:

```js
// EXPORTS
__webpack_require__.d(__webpack_exports__, { 'default' : function () { return a } });
console.log('module');
const a = () => {};
```

I hope that it can generate the exported part at the end of the module:

```js
console.log('module');
const a = () => {};
// EXPORTS
__webpack_require__.d(__webpack_exports__, { 'default' : function () { return a } });
```

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

In ES3 context, we hope to use a ES3 harmony plugin to redefine `Object.defineProperty` like this:

```js
console.log('module');
var a = () => {};
// EXPORTS
__webpack_exports__['default'] = (function () { return a })();
```

If the part is at the top of the module, `a` will be `undefined`, which cannot be easily patched.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Add an option to the `ConcatenatedModule` to specify the position?

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: ResolverCachePlugin: Allow dynamic multiple time resolve of the same path
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
In Webpack 4, resolver resolve path multiple time (no cache), therefor plugin like this works: https://www.npmjs.com/package/dynamic-resolve-webpack-plugin. But in Webpack 5, ResolverCachePlugin automatically snapshot a resolve, this can't be modified without affecting other resolve.

Therefor, I am requesting ResolverCachePlugin adding condition whether the file need to be resolved again or not. The condition might either based on webpack option or plugin signal to determine.

**What is the expected behavior?**

```
{
        resolve: {
            plugins: [
                new DynamicAliasResolvePlugin({
                    alias: "@template",
                    dynamic: (request, alias) => {
                        return request.context.issuer.replace(/\.js$/, '/template/');
                    },
                }),
            ]
        },
}
```

Using it like these on multiple files:

page-a.js
```
// Resolve to "./page-a/template/"
import Template from "@template/homepage";
```

page-b.js
```
// Resolve to "./page-b/template/"
import Template from "@template/homepage";
```

Two of the paths will be resolved on different path and not cached. 

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
The main motivation is the use of plugin such as DynamicAliasResolvePlugin. Currently, only `page-a.js` get resolved and `page-b.js` will use the previous resolve which led it to be unusable.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
There are two way to implements this,

1. Add more options to `snapshot.resolve` that will be used to determine whether a file path need to be cached or not.
2. Pass and get config to resolver plugin whether the resulting resolve should be snapshot or not.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes, with guidance from the maintainers.

title: Concern for running Multiple Angular Micro-frontends in Angular App Shell
body:
We want to have multiple versions of Angular [micro-frontends](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microfrontend#:%7E:text=Microfrontend%20is%20a%20front%2Dend,for%20multiple%20front%2Dend%20applications) running within an Angular [app shell](https://angular.io/guide/app-shell#:%7E:text=Application%20shell%20is%20a%20way,automatically%20after%20the%20code%20loads). Is module federation using webpack5 currently the best approach? Looking towards future iterations of Angular, such as when Angular Signals are released, are there alternative methods that we should consider? Additionally, when webpack6 is released, it may not be backwards compatible, so we may need to continue using Webpack5. Would this cause any issues?

For example: The Angular Host app is running Angular 15 with one child Angular micro frontend running Angular 14 and one child Angular micro front end running Angular 13. We will need both Angular 14 and 13 micro frontends to communicate with each other and with the Angular 15 host.

title: CSS Background-image better support with public folder assets.
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
When using the `background-image` CSS property with webpack, and the path to some asset is given like `background-image: url('any-img.png')`, webpack should first try to resolve it from `src`, but if not found, webpack should consider it as a public asset which would be served from the public folder.


**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
Suppose we've configured a web app with webpack, and we've a nicely working public folder, and all public folder assets are directly served at `/` (root) of the application. But when I try to use the public assets as `background-image` in CSS (eg: `background-image: url('/assets/bg.png')`) it doesn't work, because it just tries to resolve it in `src`. I think it is clearly mentioned in the syntax (`background-image: url('/assets/bg.png')`) that path given is a URL, but still, webpack tries to load it from src, instead of considering it a URL.


**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Note: this might be an issue with my webpack implementation, kindly suggest me some resources if it is already possible with webpack. Thanks!


**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: experiments.css generate wrong url path
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

## Case 1: publicPath is not "auto"

**What is the current behavior?**

With following webpack config:

```js
// webpack.config.js
module.exports = {
  entry: 'index.css',
  output: {
    publicPath: 'aaa.com/aa/',
  },
  module: {
    rules: [{ test: /\.png$/, type: 'asset/resource' }],
  },
  experiments: {
    css: true,
  },
}
```

```css
/* index.css */
.a {
  background-image: url(./bg.png);
}
```

will generate:

```css
.foo {
  background-image: url(bg.png); /* <-- wrong */
}
head{--webpack-main:\.\/index\.css;}
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

It should generate:

```css
.foo {
  background-image: url(aaa.com/aa/bg.png);
}
head{--webpack-main:\.\/index\.css;}
```

## Case 2: publicPath is "auto"

**What is the current behavior?**

With following webpack config:

```js
// webpack.config.js
module.exports = {
  entry: 'index.css',
  output: {
    // publicPath: "auto" is default value
    cssFilename: 'css/[name].css',
    assetModuleFilename: 'assets/[name][ext]',
  },
  module: {
    rules: [{ test: /\.png$/, type: 'asset/resource' }],
  },
  experiments: {
    css: true,
  },
}
```

```css
/* index.css */
.a {
  background-image: url(./bg.png);
}
```

will generate:

```css
.foo {

title: Import, unreachable after applying the values in `DefinePlugin`, appears in built product anyway
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

Observe that when `__BROWSER__` is made `true` through the use of `DefinePlugin`, this code:

```ts
import nodeFetchImpl from "node-fetch";

let fetchImpl;
if (__BROWSER__) {
  fetchImpl = globalThis.fetch;
} else {
  fetchImpl = nodeFetchImpl;
}

export default fetchImpl;
```

…gets transformed to this:

```js
import*as e from"node-fetch";var r={544:r=>{r.exports=e}},t={};function o(e){var a=t[e];if(void 0!==a)return a.exports;var s=t[e]={exports:{}};return r[e](s,s.exports,o),s.exports}var a,s,p={};s=p,Object.defineProperty(s,"X",{value:!0}),o(544),a=globalThis.fetch,s.Z=a;var f=p.X,v=p.Z;export{f as __esModule,v as default};
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Repro: https://github.com/steveluscher/webpack-dce-repro

**What is the expected behavior?**

Since:

1. `node-fetch` has `"sideEffects": false` in its `package.json`, and
2. The `node-fetch` import is unreachable when `__BROWSER__` is `true`

I would expect the compiled output not to contain `import*as e from"node-fetch";` or any of the code associated with `e`.

**Other relevant information:**

```
Binaries:
  Node: 16.20.0 - /usr/local/bin/node
  npm: 8.19.4 - /usr/local/bin/npm
Packages:
  ts-loader: ^9.4.2 => 9.4.2

```

title: "fetchPriority" for script tags
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Also consider trying the webpack@beta version, maybe it's already fixed. -->

# Feature Request
Ability to set _fetchPriority_ attribute on script tags.

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter (https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack). -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

Webpack doesn't offer any way to set _fetchPriority_ attribute on script tags that are managed/added by webpack.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub gist. -->
<!-- Best provide a minimal reproducible repo -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->

I propose adding `output.scriptFetchPriorty` option with values `boolean = false, string: 'high' | 'low' | 'auto'`
When !== false, it is used in LoadScriptRuntimeModule to add the attribute to the script tag.

## Relevant links
https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/scripting.html#the-script-element
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/script#browser_compatibility
author: aboktor

title: Webpack repo fails lint out of the box on windows
body:
<!-- Bug reports and Feature requests must use other templates, or will be closed -->
<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter (https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack). -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
Lint fails on the CR in EOL of files on windows. I tried cloning the repo on linux and things worked as expected.
I tried with git's `core.autocrlf` set to `true` and set to `false` and they both failed. My analysis is that this happens because `.gitattributes` has `* text=auto` which is equivalent to having `autocrlf` to `true` all the time.
On windows, this means that EOLs will be CRLF, which fails lint.

## Options to solve this problem
- Set `* text eol=lf` in `.gitattributes`
- Add `*.js eol=lf` below `* text=auto` in `.gitattributes`

Talked to @TheLarkInn briefly about this issue.

body:
I have a `dist/index.html` file that refers to a bundled JS script. However, hot reloading only works when this script is in the same directory as `index.html` but fails when I place the script in a subdirectory (`dist/assets`). Check out my entire project structure:

```
webpack-test
│   .babelrc
│   webpack.config.js
│   package-lock.json
│   package.json
│
├───dist

│   │   bundle.js

        index.js
```

Here is my webpack config:

```
const path = require('path');

module.exports = {
    entry: './src/index.js',
    output: {
        path: path.resolve(__dirname, 'dist/assets'),
        filename: 'bundle.js'
    },
    devServer: {
        port: 9000,
        compress: true,
        hot: true,

So when I indicate `<script src="./index.js"></script>` in `dist/index.html` hot reloading works as expected. It doesn't work when I do `<script src="./assets/index.js"></script>` though. I suppose the config is correct but I'm not sure.

title: Introduce a new option for workers chunks' filenames
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

The source of this feature request is this [discussion](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/16843). The aim is to specify the filename for worker chunks only. Currently, there is no straightforward way to determine whether the chunk being processed in `chunkFilename` is a worker. Therefore, the request is for an option similar to [`workerPublicPath`](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/16671), but only for the worker chunk's filename, such as `workerChunkFilename`.

During the aforementioned discussion, @alexander-akait suggested identifying worker chunks by checking if `chunkLoading === 'import-scripts'` in `pathData?.chunk?._groups` within the `chunkFilename` function. However, this approach is considered hacky and not very robust.

title: Webpack generating nondeterministic hashes, seems to not merge duplicate chunks
body: We are facing an issue where the contenthash for entry points in our project changes between builds, without any source file changes. . Eg. with this config- ``` output: { publicPath: '/static/bundles/', filename: '[contenthash:8]-[name].js', chunkFilename: '[contenthash:8]-[name].js', }, ``` For entrypoint `abc`, build 1 generated `1b2c3d4e-abc.js`, build 2 may generate `f5a6b7c8-abc.js` On introspecting these bundles, and setting `optimization.chunkIds` to `named`, it seems to occur because of a single chunk, duplicated several times. The contents of the file are the same, only the succeeding hash is different (and interestingly seems to be incrementing). As a result the bundle contenthash is changing. On checking the contents of this vendor chunk, it looks like there are several such chunks that have been created. ``` (self.webpackChunk = self.webpackChunk || []).push([ [ "vendors-node_modules_lodash__baseIsEqual_js-node_modules_lodash_get_js-node_modules_react-lif-22f2913", "vendors-node_modules_lodash__baseIsEqual_js-node_modules_lodash_get_js-node_modules_react-lif-b9b55d0", "vendors-node_modules_lodash__baseIsEqual_js-node_modules_lodash_get_js-node_modules_react-lif-22f2910", "vendors-node_modules_lodash__baseIsEqual_js-node_modules_lodash_get_js-node_modules_react-lif-22f2911", "vendors-node_modules_lodash__baseIsEqual_js-node_modules_lodash_get_js-node_modules_react-lif-22f2912", "vendors-node_modules_lodash__baseIsEqual_js-node_modules_lodash_get_js-node_modules_react-lif-22f2914", "vendors-node_modules_lodash__baseIsEqual_js-node_modules_lodash_get_js-node_modules_redux-dev-d37e7d", "vendors-node_modules_lodash__baseIsEqual_js-node_modules_lodash_get_js-node_modules_react-lif-b9b55d1", "vendors-node_modules_lodash__baseIsEqual_js-node_modules_lodash_get_js-node_modules_react-lif-b9b55d2", "vendors-node_modules_lodash__baseIsEqual_js-node_modules_lodash_get_js-node_modules_react-lif-b9b55d3", ], ``` Webpack optimization config is as follows, and we are running production mode: ``` optimization: { nodeEnv: 'production', minimize: true, removeAvailableModules: false, removeEmptyChunks: true, chunkIds: 'named', minimizer: [ new TerserPlugin({ parallel: true, terserOptions: { ecma: 5, ie8: false, safari10: false, }, }), ], }, ``` Continuing to investigate on our end, but any help would be appreciated! Ideally only one such chunk should exist for this group of modules, but there seems to be 5. One of the big questions that I had about these named chunks was what is the 7 character hash at the end of the chunk name based on, and what could cause it to increment? Webpack version: 5.79.0 Node version: 16.13.2 OS version: mac OS Ventura (Apple M1)

**What is the current behavior?**

Webpack cannot resolve Windows paths.

https://github.com/falsandtru/spica/blob/v0.0.719/webpack.config.js doesn't work with the latest glob.

```
{
  entry: glob.sync('./src/**/*.ts', { dotRelative: true }),
}
```

```
ERROR in main
Module not found: Error: Can't resolve '.\src\uuid.ts' in '...'
Did you mean './.\src\uuid.ts'?
Requests that should resolve in the current directory need to start with './'.
Requests that start with a name are treated as module requests and resolve within module directories (node_modules).
If changing the source code is not an option there is also a resolve options called 'preferRelative' which tries to resolve these kind of requests in the current directory too.
```

No error.

body:

**What is the current behavior?**

I am trying to load a large wasm file into my web app. The build works, however it's incredibly slow:

```bash
npx webpack --mode development                                                                                                   
asset c37ad502f0b49101ff66.module.wasm 22.6 MiB [emitted] [immutable] (auxiliary name: main)
asset main.js 797 KiB [emitted] (name: main)
runtime modules 4.99 KiB 10 modules
cacheable modules 610 KiB (javascript) 22.6 MiB (webassembly)
  modules by path ./wasm/pkg/ 78.7 KiB (javascript) 22.6 MiB (webassembly)
    ./wasm/pkg/wasm.js 139 bytes [built] [code generated]
    ./wasm/pkg/wasm_bg.js 74.5 KiB [built] [code generated]
    ./wasm/pkg/wasm_bg.wasm 4.04 KiB (javascript) 22.6 MiB (webassembly) [built] [code generated]
  ./src/index.js 344 bytes [built] [code generated]
  ./node_modules/lodash/lodash.js 531 KiB [built] [code generated]
webpack 5.80.0 compiled successfully in 25725 ms
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I have created a small project that reproduces the problem based on the webpack quickstart [here](https://github.com/Dig-Doug/bug-reproductions/tree/webpack-wasm-slow). To reproduce, do:

```bash
cd /tmp
git clone -b webpack-wasm-slow https://github.com/Dig-Doug/bug-reproductions.git
cd bug-reproductions/webpack-wasm-slow/
npm install
npx webpack --mode development
```

The wasm library is built from a rust library. I have included the generated JS/wasm files so you do not need to build them, but if you would like to regenerate them you can do the following:

- Setup rust: https://www.rust-lang.org/learn/get-started
- Setup wasm-pack: https://github.com/rustwasm/wasm-pack
- Do:

```bash
cd wasm
wasm-pack build
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

I do not need the wasm file to optimized or changed in any way. As far as I can tell, webpack is not modifying the file so I'd expect the wasm file to not impact the build latency significantly.

webpack version:   5.80.0

Operating System: Ubuntu

Clone the repro git repository (I have made the repo private as this project contains some code that I do not want to publicly share. This is a high priority issue for us at my company though, so we are willing to share the code with individuals that are looking to debug the problem).

define(function (require) {
  console.log(foo); // not work
  console.log(bar); // ok
});

noop(function () {
  console.log(foo); // ok
  console.log(bar); // ok
});

function noop() {}

```

output:

```JavaScript
// omitted

/* harmony import */ var _lib_js__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(/*! ./lib.js */ "./src/lib.js");

const bar = __webpack_require__(/*! ./lib.js */ "./src/lib.js");

define(function (require) {
  console.log(foo); // not work
  console.log(bar); // ok
});

noop(function () {
  console.log(_lib_js__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__["default"]); // ok
  console.log(bar); // ok
});

function noop() {}

// omitted
```


**What is the expected behavior?**

I am not sure if it's a bug.
But I expect that `foo` should be transpiled into `_lib_js__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__["default"]`.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.38.1
Node.js version: 16.20.0
Operating System: Darwin arm64
Additional tools:

related issues:
- #14307 
- swc-project/swc/issues/7334
author: magic-akari

title: RFC: Webpack Diagnostics Meta Data
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
**What is the expected behavior?**
Hope that webpack can provide a built-in data structure for analysis and diagnosis of the bundle process and product.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
The existing stats data is not conducive to bundle analysis and diagnosis. In order to deeply analyze the entire webpack bundle process, developers often need to do some extra work to reassemble a data structure, such as
- Data Collection: Register various hooks to obtain sufficient information
  - Intercept loader to collect the loader data, for querying the loader that the file has passed through, as well as the time and intermediate products of each loader
  - Intercept compiler/compilation hooks to collect the plugin data,  for recording the running order and running time of external plugins in hooks
  - Reassembling resolver data, for tracking the process status of compiling all paths within the project, aiming to help users identify how the erroneous path is generated
  - Stringify the webpack configuration, allow users to easily view the currently effective configuration
- Data Processing: In the process of collecting data, create data structures for analysis and diagnosis
  - Generate chunk graph
  - Generate module graph
  - Generate package graph
- Data Diagnosis: Users often need to manually troubleshoot issues, so we could establish a set of Linter mechanisms, and write rules to take processed data as input and output diagnostic results, used to diagnose such things as
  - Writing rules to take processed data as input and output diagnostic results, used to diagnose such things as
    - Duplicate Packages
    - Default Import Check
    - Loader Performance Optimization
- Data Display: Write web pages to display processed data in a user-friendly interface and assist users in troubleshooting problems through visual aids, even directly locating problematic code and fixing it with one click

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
1. Design a standardized diagnostic data structure and linter mechanism that allows developers to investigate the bundle process and develop rules around this data structure. To avoid affecting the build time of the production environment, it is necessary to use a configuration to enable it.
2. Develop a webpack plugin that reads this meta data through a diagnostic service and provides users with web pages and api for troubleshooting and diagnosis:
  - Compile Analysis
    1. Webpack Loaders: Investigate and analyze Webpack Loaders-related call data and time-consuming information
    2. Webpack Plugins: Query the number of calls and time of Webpack Compiler / Compilation Hooks
    3. Module Resolve: Help you quickly query the final path after the module path of an import/require in a file is parsed

  - Bundle Analysis
    1. Bundle Size: The volume data, first screen resource size, duplicate package, module reference relationship and other information of the current compiled product
    2. Tree Shaking: Help you further analyze and investigate the tree shaking bailout reason of a file in the project

- **Prototype Page**
  - Overall Page: In addition to giving an overall overview, we will also provide a Bundle Alerts module to scan for problems to be fixed through rules, such as duplicate package detection. It supports the tracing of each duplicate package introduction chain.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11739753/234492082-c1acb696-20f7-4cbe-8bed-eabdd13deca5.png)
  - Loader Page: Show the loader timing diagram, as well as the loader time consumption and changes before and after the loader of each module.It not only supports viewing the Loaders details of a single file, but also supports viewing the Loaders data of the file directory.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11739753/234492105-b5dddf54-e5c7-4fba-acea-77433884282f.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11739753/234492131-da767e02-5b25-4801-9655-dd05fad82985.png)
  -  Plugin Page: Support viewing the call data information of all plugin-in-used Compiler Hooks and Compilation Hooks in the current Webpack project
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11739753/234492160-ae0799d0-d0a3-463b-b41b-ac57bd024c56.png)
  - Bundle Page: Show the parsed size of each module, that is, the volume actually packaged into the bundle product.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11739753/234492186-4ff4f9b0-79ba-4081-8927-3b2ad84aee89.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11739753/234492200-0baccd6d-25e8-40f9-8805-3cd465d1d518.png)

  - Tree shaking Page: You can know the reason why the code is not shaken for reasons such as "where variables are used" through it.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11739753/234492236-55365271-338d-4080-9fc0-ccd727aebaed.png)

Consider this too - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/20435

Are you willing to work on this yourself?
yes

title: `resolve.extensionAlias` is not taking in account for the dynamic imports expressions evaluation
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**
For the dynamic imports which contains some expression, the `resolve.extensionAlias` configuration option is not used to resolving the imports.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

```js
// Config
const config = {
    ...
    resolve: {
        extensions: [ '.ts', '.tsx', '.js', '.jsx' ],
        extensionAlias: {
            '.js': [ '.js', '.ts', '.tsx' ]
        }
    }
  ...
};
```

```js
// Input:
// /index.ts
const x = 'a';
import(`./lazy-${x}.js`);
// /lazy-a.ts
export {}
```

```js
// Output:
...
/***/ "./. lazy recursive ^\\.\\/lazy\\-.*\\.js$":
/*!*****************************************************!*\
  !*** ././ lazy ^\.\/lazy\-.*\.js$ namespace object ***!
  \*****************************************************/
/***/ ((module) => {

function webpackEmptyAsyncContext(req) {
	// Here Promise.resolve().then() is used instead of new Promise() to prevent
	// uncaught exception popping up in devtools
	return Promise.resolve().then(() => {
		var e = new Error("Cannot find module '" + req + "'");
		e.code = 'MODULE_NOT_FOUND';
		throw e;
	});
}
webpackEmptyAsyncContext.keys = () => ([]);
webpackEmptyAsyncContext.resolve = webpackEmptyAsyncContext;
webpackEmptyAsyncContext.id = "./. lazy recursive ^\\.\\/lazy\\-.*\\.js$";
module.exports = webpackEmptyAsyncContext;

/***/ })
...
(() => {
/*!******************!*\
  !*** ./index.ts ***!
  \******************/
const x = 'a';
__webpack_require__("./. lazy recursive ^\\.\\/lazy\\-.*\\.js$")(`./lazy-${x}.js`);
})();
...
```

When we will change the code to use `.ts` extension in the import like:
```js
...
import(`./lazy-${x}.ts`);
...
```

Or we remove the the extension from the import expression like
```js
...
import(`./lazy-${x}`);
...
```

The output code is fine and contains the resolution map like:

```js
// Output:
...

/***/ "./. lazy recursive ^\\.\\/lazy\\-.*\\.ts$":
/*!*****************************************************!*\
  !*** ././ lazy ^\.\/lazy\-.*\.ts$ namespace object ***!
  \*****************************************************/
/***/ ((module, __unused_webpack_exports, __webpack_require__) => {
var map = {
	"./lazy-a.ts": [
		"./lazy-a.ts",
		"lazy-a_ts"
	]
};
...
```

The full gist: https://gist.github.com/majo44/e774abdc4aea027d11df1ee8582e97ee

**What is the expected behavior?**

In case of es module javascript/typescript code the extensions formally are obligatory, the typescript resolution logic also supports the '.js' extensions. Webpack perfectly handling that for the static imports and for the dynamic imports without expressions by using of provided `extensionAlias` configuration property. Unfortunately it is not align with the resolution of dynamic imports with expressions. The expectation is to properly resolve the dynamic imports with expression by taking the `extensionAlias` in consideration. 


<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.80.0
Node.js version:  v18.16.0
Operating System: Windows 11

title: Federated Modules: harmony import a package Miss `["default"]` if the package shared
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
I installed a package named `redux-register` , and I just import it and print it, like this:
```javascript
import Register from 'reudx-register'

alert(typeof Register); // function
```

Register is a function and Look at `src_index_js.js` that webpack created:

```javascript
var redux_register__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(/*! redux-register */ "./node_modules/redux-register/lib/index.js");
alert(typeof redux_register__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__["default"]);
```
Because of redux-register was a esm package, therefore webpack add `["default"]` automatically.

But, If i shared the package like this:

```javascript
new webpack.container.ModuleFederationPlugin({
    runtime: 'test',
    shared: {
        'redux-register': {
            singleton: true,
        },
    },
}),
```
`Register` will be a `object Module`. because `["default"]` was missing:

```javascript
var redux_register__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(/*! redux-register */ "webpack/sharing/consume/default/redux-register/redux-register");
alert(typeof redux_register__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ );
```
**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Reproducing repository: https://github.com/dexbol/module-federation-test .
npm install && npm run start

Whether or not using Module Federation shared package, the `Register` should be a function.

title: `importModule` emits error when `experiments.css` and HMR is enabled
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When `experiments.css` and HMR is enabled, calling `importModule` inside a loader will result in this error:
```
ERROR in webpack/runtime/css loading
Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'getChunkConditionMap')
TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'getChunkConditionMap')
    at CssLoadingRuntimeModule.generate (.../webpack-async-css-bug/node_modules/webpack/lib/css/CssLoadingRuntimeModule.js:72:35)
    at CssLoadingRuntimeModule.getGeneratedCode (.../webpack-async-css-bug/node_modules/webpack/lib/RuntimeModule.js:182:44)
    at CssLoadingRuntimeModule.codeGeneration (.../webpack-async-css-bug/node_modules/webpack/lib/RuntimeModule.js:137:30)
    at .../webpack-async-css-bug/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compilation.js:3331:22
    at .../webpack-async-css-bug/node_modules/webpack/lib/Cache.js:93:5
    at Hook.eval [as callAsync] (eval at create (.../webpack-async-css-bug/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:33:10), <anonymous>:6:1)
    at Cache.get (.../webpack-async-css-bug/node_modules/webpack/lib/Cache.js:75:18)
    at ItemCacheFacade.get (.../webpack-async-css-bug/node_modules/webpack/lib/CacheFacade.js:111:15)
    at Compilation._codeGenerationModule (.../webpack-async-css-bug/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compilation.js:3324:9)
    at codeGen (.../webpack-async-css-bug/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compilation.js:4863:11)
```

*Possible reason:* Because CSS and HMR ist enabled, [these hooks](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/css/CssModulesPlugin.js#L327) are active. Calling `importModule` seems to result in [CssLoadingRuntimeModule.generate](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/css/CssLoadingRuntimeModule.js#L60) being called before `chunkGraph` got initialised on the compilation.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->

title: `output.chunkLoading: 'import'` results in runtime errors
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
The code throws [here](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/46cdedb4ba67aa75ccb00919e6cc8594d7445146/lib/esm/ModuleChunkLoadingRuntimeModule.js#L200). I'm not sure how it was supposed to work. [`installChunk`](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/46cdedb4ba67aa75ccb00919e6cc8594d7445146/lib/esm/ModuleChunkLoadingRuntimeModule.js#L142)  expects `{ ids: string[], modules: string[], runtime }` and [`import()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/import) returns [module namespace object](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/import#module_namespace_object).

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

`webpack.config.js`
```js
module.exports = {
    entry: {
        main: __dirname + '/entry.js',
    },
    mode: 'production',
    output: {
        chunkLoading: 'import'
    }
}
```

`entry.js`
```js
import('./lazy.js').then((module) => console.log(module.value))
```

`lazy.js`
```js
export const value = "from lazy module"
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

Specifying `output.chunkLoading: 'import'` loads async chunks via `import()`

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.82.0
Node.js version: 19.7.0
Operating System: Windows 11
Additional tools: N/A
author: lvivski

title: [Bug] using Module Federation with an additional Entrypoint prevents availability of init and get
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->


**What is the current behavior?**

i am using module federation with two angular 15 projects.

now i wanted to add an additional entrypoint script ( like described in your [docs ](https://webpack.js.org/concepts/module-federation/#infer-publicpath-from-script)), but doing this creates the issue that the init and get functions are not available anymore

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/40056245/f4910bf0-c825-45ea-a074-bbd845b1dc5b)



**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

using a webpack config like this works:

`module.exports = {
    output: {
        publicPath: "http://localhost:4201/",
        uniqueName: "appAnalyzer",
        scriptType: "text/javascript"
    },
    optimization: {
        runtimeChunk: false
    },
    plugins: [
        new ModuleFederationPlugin({
            name: "appAnalyzer",
            filename: "remoteEntry.js",
            exposes: {
                ...
            },
        })
    ]
};`

using a webpack.config with an additional entrypoint creates the issue:

`module.exports = {
    entry: {
        main: "./app/main.ts"
        appAnalyzer: "./app/setPublicPath.js" // same with  "./app/main.ts"
    },
    output: {
        publicPath: "http://localhost:4201/",
        uniqueName: "appAnalyzer",
        scriptType: "text/javascript"
    },
    optimization: {
        runtimeChunk: false
    },
    plugins: [
        new ModuleFederationPlugin({
            name: "appAnalyzer",
            filename: "remoteEntry.js",
            exposes: {
                ...
            },
        })
    ]
};`

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

the get and init Methods should still be available 


i debugged the generated "remoteEntry.js" files and noticed that here is a difference 

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/40056245/98249055-eacb-4298-9694-c4d7819804d6)

i created a small sciprt in my frame appliction and
switching 12196 with 34731 in the loaded files resolves the issue.
(with 34731being the entrypoint script and 12196 the container entry script)

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.81
Node.js version: 16.16.0

my package.json contains

'
"webpack": "5.81.x",
"@angular/common": "15.2.8",
"@angular/core": "15.2.8",
"@angular/cdk": "15.2.8",
"@angular/cli": "15.2.7",
'

Thanks in Advance
author: MariusZeger

body:

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->

<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->


My `webpack congig` depends on `package-a`.

`package-a` is depends on `package-b`;

For development purposes packages `package-a` and `package-b` symlinked to the `node_modules`.

I set `NODE_PRESERVE_SYMLINKS=1`, so nodejs will not resolves symlinks.

I added my `webpack congig` location to the `cache.buildDependencies`.

Whe I am running build process I got error:

`package-b` can't be resolved from the `package-a`.
And in error message it shows original `package-a` location (not symlink location).

So, seems, that when webpack resolves `cache.buildDependencies` dependencies it does not respect `NODE_PRESERVE_SYMLINKS=1` and follwos symbolic links.

This disables filesysten cache.

**What is the expected behavior?**

I am expecting, that when `NODE_PRESERVE_SYMLINKS=1` is set - modules resilver will not follw symbolic links. As it implemented in the nodejs.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

Node.js version: 20.0.1

title: Env variables shouldn't get revealed in the bundled js file
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
Make webpack compiled env variables end-user safe

**What is the expected behavior?**
Currently, any process.env variable gets shown "as it is" in the compiled bundled.js.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
This clearly defeats the purpose of having environmental variables, because anyone can see the data by analyzing the js file.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
Instead of being added to the bundled.js file, an env file should be generated at runtime called bundled.env, also to be placed in the dist folder, and in such a way that end users aren't able to access the values to the env variables.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
it's beyond my paygrade, sorry

title: Using resource query strings is incompatible with a package's "exports" field for defining entry points
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
Currently, you cannot use resource queries in an import for a package who has defined an `"exports"` field, because webpack thinks that the entire import path should be resolvable in the target package's entry points.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Say you have the following construction:

**Some package that is in `node_modules` with the following `package.json`**
```jsonc
// my-package/package.json
{
  "name": "my-package",
  "exports": {
    ".": "main.js"
  }
}
```

**Entry point of bundle**
```js
// entry-point.js
import sourceString from 'my-package?source';
```

**Webpack configuration**
```js
// webpack.config.js
module.exports = {
  entry: "./entry-point,js",
  module: { rules: [
    {
      resourceQuery: "source",
      type: "asset/source"
    }
  ] }
};
```

Attempting to build this will produce the following error:
```
ERROR in ./entry-point.js
Module not found: Error: Package path ./?source is not exported from package <path>/node_modules/my-package
(see exports field in <path>/node_modules/my-package/package.json)
```

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

It seems that Webpack is not stripping out the resource query part of the import URL prior to attempting to resolving it against the package's entry points. Moreover, attempting to actually add an entry point defined as `"./?source"` in `my-package/package.json` will result in webpack skipping the entire resource query rule entirely (as in, it will assume there is no resource query, and replaces the resource path with whatever is resolved from the `"./?source"` export condition.

I expected the resolution logic to resolve the package entry point without the query string first (aka to `my-package/main.js`), and then apply the resource query afterwards (as if the import was to `"my-package/main.js?source"`)

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.82.1
Node.js version: 16.17.0
Operating System: Win 10
Additional tools:

body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->


I have a `ModuleFederationPlugin` shared dependency configured as 

```javascript
shared: {
  ...,
  ramda: {
    requiredVersion: '^0.29.0',
  }
}
```

but I get this error: 
`
WARNING in shared module ramda -> path_to_project/node_modules/ramda/es/index.js
No version specified and unable to automatically determine one. No version in description file (usually package.json). Add version to description file path_to_project/node_modules/ramda/es/package.json, or manually specify version in shared config.
`

This is because ramda versions itself in `node_modules/ramda/package.json`, not `node_modules/ramda/es/package.json`.

`node_modules/ramda/es/package.json` is simply 
```json
{"type":"module"}
```

and if i edit it to 
```json
{"type":"module", "version": "0.29.0"}
```

the above error is solved. 

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->
---
**What is the expected behavior?**

`ModuleFederationPlugin` should read a version from `package.json` at the root of the package when a subfolder does not specify it.

---

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.81.0
Node.js version: 19.6.0
Operating System: macOS 12.06 Monterey

body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
When bundled files are served publicly, any code inside the index.js (entry point for bundle) does not execute or run. However, all imports into index.js which include functions run with no problem. When bundle is served using webpack-dev-server, there is no problem there. To test, I deleted all the code in index.js and wrote a console.log() inside a function. The function did not execute at all. 

The following error is thrown: 
```

l

    e = "function" == typeof Symbol ? Symbol("webpack exports") : "__webpack_exports__",
    n = "function" == typeof Symbol ? Symbol("webpack error") : "__webpack_error__",
    r = t=>{
        t && !t.d && (t.d = 1,
        t.forEach((t=>t.r--)),
        t.forEach((t=>t.r-- ? t.r++ : t())))
    }
    ,
````

The imports include files that use top-level await. Other than that, there are no promises in the index.js. 

Css, html, and images in bundle all display correctly. 

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->

<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

https://github.com/gdkoo/SleepWakeRegulate/blob/main/webpack.config.cjs 

**What is the expected behavior?**

Using miniCSSExtractPlugin and HTML Webpack Plugin, index.html, style.css, and index.js should all be bundled into main.js and main.css, and an index.html should be generated in a public folder. This is all happening correctly. When running npm run build and npm run serve, the code all executes correctly. However, when served to Firebase hosting or another emulator, the bundled code executes everything except code from index.js. This ranges from simple console.log()s to images that are added to the DOM. 

After some debugging, I found that removing the './style.css' import specifically (which thereby prevents the main.css bundle), rebundling, and serving publicly led to the rest of index.js file executing as expected.  However, doing so removes the css stylesheet. I suspect the issue in my case might have to do with the miniCSSExtractPlugin.

title: Module exposed via ModuleFederationPlugin gets initialized twice
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

The issue is related to the Module Federation plugin. I have two applications `app1` and `app2`. `app1` exposes a module named `test.js` using the ModuleFederationPlugin. Both `app1` and `app2` use this module. The module named `test`contains a console.log() on top-level (i.e. outside of any function) which is expected to run only once the first time when the module is imported. However, the console.log() is triggered twice. First time when `test.js` is imported by `app1` and second time when it's imported by `app2`. I can see that the `__webpack_require__` is triggered twice for the same file in the two apps.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

This is a link to the repo which reproduces the problem:
https://github.com/alex-vukov/mf-issue
To reproduce it run the following steps:
1. npm install
2. npm start
3. Open localhost:3001 and open dev tools.
4. Reload the page and you'll see the console warning 'test.js loaded' logged twice.

In`app1` the `test.js` file is used only in `App.js` and is exposed in `ModuleFederationPlugin` in the webpack config file. In `app2` the same `test.js` file is dynamically imported inside `Button.js` and a function from it is used. That's when the duplicate initialization happens.


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Expected behavior is that an exposed module should behave just like a regular module and should only be initialized once. `__webpack_require__` should somehow synchronise the cache for the already loaded exposed modules between the two bundles. This issue is directly causing problems with sharing Redux stores between applications where the store is usually initialized at the top-level of the store.js file.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.83.1
Node.js version: 18.16.0
Operating System: Linux Ubuntu
Additional tools:

title: 5.84.0 has broken storybook - causing `ReferenceError: Preview$1 is not defined`
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

Storybook has started failing immediately after upgrading to webpack:`5.84.0`.
We do not have control over which version of webpack the end-user is using, this is a pressing issue for us.

Here's what I know:
- storybook has e2e sandboxes for all sorts of integration-points, such as CRA, NextJS, AngularJS, some of those use webpack.
- Every single one using webpack has started failing immediately after upgrading them to the latest version of webpack (something we do automatically every 24h) [commit](https://github.com/storybookjs/sandboxes/commit/d6df513f1bae40034c75b4adfd43eafaa22f21be#diff-2c000fb2c412aabe5df4d72ac073cd9dd60e21c8b1c5d81fa8bb29ecc204717eL147) [ci](https://app.circleci.com/pipelines/github/storybookjs/storybook/50204/workflows/c983cc20-3e09-4b23-8451-72736e5ddde4/jobs/531774/parallel-runs/0?filterBy=ALL)

- storybook uses the `externals` feature of webpack [your docs](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/externals/), I mention this because I believe this to be highly related to the problem/error
- storybook also uses resolve.aliasses, again I think this is related

The way storybook fails, seems to be in a few ways...

1. see the error for yourself [here](https://6377871ac35b4b579c6b8bfc-ffecdbzauy.chromatic.com/?path=/docs/example-introduction--docs)

   ```
   Error: page.evaluate: ReferenceError: Preview$1 is not defined

    at __webpack_require__ (https://6377871ac35b4b579c6b8bfc-ffecdbzauy.capture.chromatic.com/runtime~main.a02828eb.iframe.bundle.js:1:381)
    at ./src/stories/Introduction.mdx (https://6377871ac35b4b579c6b8bfc-ffecdbzauy.capture.chromatic.com/stories-Introduction-mdx.6e4fdf47.iframe.bundle.js:2:2747)
    at __webpack_require__ (https://6377871ac35b4b579c6b8bfc-ffecdbzauy.capture.chromatic.com/runtime~main.a02828eb.iframe.bundle.js:1:381)
    at https://6377871ac35b4b579c6b8bfc-ffecdbzauy.capture.chromatic.com/main.3e8a31a1.iframe.bundle.js:1:569535
    at _ZoneDelegate.invoke (https://6377871ac35b4b579c6b8bfc-ffecdbzauy.capture.chromatic.com/6187.8c80c92e.iframe.bundle.js:421:668315)
    at Zone.run (https://6377871ac35b4b579c6b8bfc-ffecdbzauy.capture.chromatic.com/6187.8c80c92e.iframe.bundle.js:421:660708)

    → Failed to publish build
   ```
   This error occurs according to webpack in `@storybook/blocks/dist/index.mjs`, I check the webpack output:
   ![Screenshot 2023-05-25 at 13 13 55](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/3070389/f9e2f555-3af4-45fa-912e-636c8269e728)
   
   When I look into the original dist file:
   ![Screenshot 2023-05-25 at 13 15 26](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/3070389/d5260d07-0b2c-493d-a182-d5d26cbea2f6)

What's interesting to note is that `Preview$1` is imported as a named import (but renamed) from a module, we later externalize:
```ts
import { filterArgTypes, composeConfigs, Preview as Preview$1, DocsContext as DocsContext$1 } from '@storybook/preview-api';
```

2. see the error for yourself [here](https://app.circleci.com/pipelines/github/storybookjs/storybook/50204/workflows/c983cc20-3e09-4b23-8451-72736e5ddde4/jobs/531775)
   ```
   error: Unhandled Promise rejection:, Cannot access '__WEBPACK_DEFAULT_EXPORT__' before initialization, ; Zone:, <root>, ; Task:, Promise.then, ; Value:, {}, ReferenceError: Cannot access '__WEBPACK_DEFAULT_EXPORT__' before initialization

      at Module.default (http:/localhost:8001/stories-mdx-basic-stories-mdx.f299e8c2.iframe.bundle.js:2:5935)
      at processCSFFile (http:/localhost:8001/sb-preview/runtime.js:34:77)
      at StoryStore.memoizerific [as processCSFFileWithCache] (http:/localhost:8001/sb-preview/runtime.js:1:4768)
      at http:/localhost:8001/sb-preview/runtime.js:40:8542
      at _ZoneDelegate.invoke (http:/localhost:8001/6187.8c80c92e.iframe.bundle.js:421:668315)
      at Zone.run (http:/localhost:8001/6187.8c80c92e.iframe.bundle.js:421:660708)

      at drainMicroTaskQueue (http:/localhost:8001/6187.8c80c92e.iframe.bundle.js:421:672963)
    ```
    I have no leads on what's happening here.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

- create a storybook sandbox with webpack: `npx storybook sandbox`
- choose a sandbox that has webpack, the first option: "Create React App (Javascript)" will work
- choose any location you wish
- cd into the directory you choose
- run `yarn install`
- run `yarn build-storybook`
- run `npx http-server ./storybook-static`
- open your browser and navigate to http://127.0.0.1:8080/?path=/docs/example-introduction--docs

**What is the expected behavior?**

Webpack should ensure the outputted code is valid, and does not have runtime errors when executing, there must be something in this changeset:
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/compare/v5.83.1...v5.84.0
... that is causing the above problem.

author: ndelangen

title: Function support for __webpack_public_path__
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

I am requesting the addition of function support for the __webpack_public_path__ variable in webpack. Currently, __webpack_public_path__ is a variable that allows for setting a static public path at compile time. However, there are scenarios where the ability to dynamically compute the public path at runtime would be highly beneficial.

**What is the expected behavior?**

This would allow developers to provide a function that dynamically computes and returns the appropriate public path at runtime based on custom logic or environment variables.

For example:
```
__webpack_public_path__ = (fileName) => {
  // Custom logic to determine the public path based on the environment or configuration
  if(fileName.indexOf('javascript') != -1) {
    return "/assets/js"
  } else {
    return process.env.BASE_URL || '/';
  }
};
```

By introducing function support, webpack would call the function whenever it needs to determine the public path, allowing for runtime calculations and dynamic behavior.


**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

The current limitation of __webpack_public_path__ being a static variable poses challenges in certain use cases. For example, when deploying an application to different environments or serving assets from different CDNs, it would be advantageous to calculate the public path dynamically based on the current environment or configuration. This would enable more flexibility and ease of deployment for different environments without the need for manual configuration changes.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

If function support is implemented for __webpack_public_path__, instead of directly assigning the public path, we could call a public path assignment function with the fileName or chunkId as an argument (__webpack_require__.p(chunkId)). This would allow us to dynamically compute and retrieve the appropriate public path based on the specific chunkId or fileName.
For example
```
var fullhref = __webpack_require__.p(chunkId) + href;
```
**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
No
