title: Apply same ESM/CJS interop in `.mts`/`.ts` files as in `.mjs`/`.js` files
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

Webpack changes the behavior of imports of CJS files with `__esModule` to be more compatible with Node when the importing file extension is `.mjs`, or `.js` in scope of a package.json with `"type": "module"`:

```js
// @Filename: dep.js
Object.defineProperty(exports, "__esModule", { value: true });
exports.default = "default";

// @Filename: main1.js
import dep from "./dep.js";
console.log("from .js", dep);

// @Filename: main2.mjs
import dep from "./dep.js";
console.log("from .mjs", dep);
```

```
from .js default
from .mjs { [__esModule], default }
```

For TypeScript to offer checking behavior that reflects this, the behavior of `.ts` files needs to match the behavior of `.js` files, and the behavior of `.mts` files needs to match the behavior of `.mjs` files. However, Webpack doesn’t know what these extensions are, so they don’t get considered for Webpack’s Node-like ESM/CJS interop layer. If I add `main3.mts` with identical content, I get:

```
from .js default
from .mjs { [__esModule], default }
from .mts default
```

The fact that a default import behavior is different in a `.mjs` and `.mts` file makes it impossible for TypeScript to implement a module checking mode for Webpack.

**What is the expected behavior?**

1. The behavior of an import in Webpack does not change when renaming the importing file extension between `.ts` and `.js`, `.mts` and `.mjs`, or `.cts` and `.cjs`.
2. Module syntax and interop restrictions placed on `.js`/`.mjs` files based on their extension or package.json `"type"` are also applied to their TypeScript counterparts. For example, whenever it’s an error to `require` an ESM file or to use `import`/`export` syntax in a file in Webpack, that error should persist when renaming the file to its equivalent TypeScript file extension. (This is less important to us than (1).)

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

I’m trying to implement better support for bundlers in TypeScript, and this behavior of differentiating module interop based on file extension, but not including TS file extensions in that differentiation, is a blocker. (I can explain why if necessary, but I don’t think it’s super relevant here.) We’re tracking at https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/54102

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

I’m not a Webpack expert, but it seems like there could be advantages to letting the loader API set the module format:

- No TypeScript-specific carve-outs need to be implemented in Webpack core
- Other non-TS loaders that consume non-JS file extensions could adopt this behavior if desired
- Webpack could expose the new API in a SemVer minor, while ts-loader et al. could adopt it in a SemVer major, which would probably be easier for users to adopt than waiting for a new Webpack major version

The goal for us would be that TypeScript/Webpack users get consistent behavior between .ts/.js files by default in the near future.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**

Happy to discuss solutions, and can potentially put up a PR with guidance if necessary

**Additional info**

I forked Tobias’s interop-test repo while researching this issue, and have changed quite a bit to show how various bundlers handle TS extensions and what they do with this issue in particular. Notably, esbuild exhibits the behavior we’re advocating for. Take a look at https://andrewbranch.github.io/interop-test/#synthesizing-default-exports-for-cjs-modules if interested.

title: Webpack 5.85.0 fails to correctly strip out parenthesis with barrel imports
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

With the following code:
```

import * as barrel from './barrel';

console.log((barrel.something).nested);
```

Webpack will generate (notice the extra `)`):
```
(() => {
/*!*********************!*\
  !*** ./src/main.js ***!
  \*********************/
__webpack_require__.r(__webpack_exports__);
/* harmony import */ var _barrel__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(/*! ./barrel */ "./src/barrel.js");



console.log(_barrel__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__.something).nested);


})();
```

Under webpack 5.84.0, the code is correctly generated:

```
(() => {
/*!*********************!*\
  !*** ./src/main.js ***!
  \*********************/
__webpack_require__.r(__webpack_exports__);
/* harmony import */ var _barrel__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(/*! ./barrel */ "./src/barrel.js");



console.log(_barrel__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__.something.nested);


})();
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

https://github.com/edusperoni/webpack-import-paren-issue

Just run npm start

**What is the expected behavior?**

The parenthesis should be correctly stripped.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.85.0

Reverting the change in`lib/dependencies/HarmonyImportSpecifierDependency.js` from @bworline fixes the issue:

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17203/files#diff-96335405b47eef62b17ba6fe88f6b35a1e37c9cf98fd762fe74599aca1c65d94R318-R353

title: Default-importing UMD module in ESM file didn't take `default` named export from UMD module
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
Suppose we are consuming a library published with UMD module (actually it's a Webpack-bundled UMD library module) that exports the following members
* `HighchartsReact`: a React component
* `default`: exactly the same instance as `HighchartsReact` when being exported.
* `__esModule`: `true`

I'm importing this library in a ESM js file (I've enabled ESM in `package.json` for my own project) with something like this

```jsx
import HighChartsReact from 'highcharts-react-official';

// inside a React component...
return <HighChartsReact ... />;
```

and I see the following Error

> Element type is invalid: expected a string (for built-in ccmponents) or a class/function (for composite components) but got: object. 
Check the render method of `[SomeComponent]`

I've checked the runtime value of `HighChartsReact` and I see something like this

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/50703224/ba3215d2-da12-47a5-9abb-203c41a742d8)

Note that I'm default-importing `highcharts-react-official` as `HighChartsReact`, but during runtime, it looks almost the same as if I were importing the module with star import

```js
import * as HighChartsReact from 'highcharts-react-official';
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

https://github.com/chenxinyanc/webpack-esm-default-import-issue

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

I expect by calling
```js
import HighChartsReact from 'highcharts-react-official';
```

I receive similar effect as
```js
const HighChartsReact = (await import('highcharts-react-official')).default;
```

rather than
```js
const HighChartsReact = await import('highcharts-react-official');
```

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.85.0
Node.js version: 18.16.0
Operating System: Windows 11
Additional tools: yarn

I've done some preliminary investigation and it seems  the `getDefaultExport` helper didn't get emitted, because `exportsType` was `default-with-named` during build-time.
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/cc3fd6ee19c295dd322a643093a5e280061a115b/lib/RuntimeTemplate.js#L771-L784

title: Splitchunks.chunks supports regex
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
splitChunks.chunks supports regex to match agains chunk.name
```js
module.exports = {
   optimizations: {
     splitChunks:{ 
         chunks: /antd/
     }
  }
}
```
**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
function call cost is much higher than regex(because function involves napi communication and we can implement regex in rust side) in rspack and the call frequency of splitChunks.chunks is very high, so if webpack could support regex here then rspack could align this with webpack.
**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
use chunk.name to match regex
**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: Native.js not packed
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

Typically when I can't find any information on an issue, it means it's so simple that everyone else knows it, and I'm the slow one.  With that in mind, I apologize in advance for my question.  I'm using lmdb with webpack for a vs code extension.
The code crashes when it tries to grab native.js which was not packed.

let yu = Rl(
          (0, n.dirname)(
            (0, Dl.fileURLToPath)(
              "file:///D:/a/1/s/src/LanguageServer/Client/node_modules/lmdb/native.js"
            )
          ).replace(/dist$/, "")
        );
        function vu(e) {  
You can see, in the built extension code that the path is actually the path built as part of the build pipeline meaning it most likely wasn't packaged.

Webpack lmdb

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

I just need to know how to handle native.js, or whether I "should". I have found discussions saying that some modules should not be used in some places.

https://www.npmjs.com/package/lmdb

Here are examples of other non-typescript code that I've had to treat differently in webpack.  I'm not sure if this is one of those.

```
			{
				test: /\.ts$/,
				exclude: /node_modules/,
				use: [
					{
						loader: 'ts-loader',
                        options: {
                            compilerOptions: {
                                module: "esnext"
                            }
                        }  
					}
				]                      
			},
            {
                test: /playwright-core.*(md|ttf|apk|exe|png$|ps1$|sh$|html$)/i,
                use: 'raw-loader',
            },
            {
                test: /eslint-(config|plugin)-prettier/i,
                use: 'raw-loader',
            },
            {
                test: /\.node$/,
                loader: "node-loader",
            },
```

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.0 
Node.js version: 16
Operating System: windows
Additional tools: vs code

title: require.context + sass-loader produces the same output for different layers
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Webpack `5.87.0` produces the same output for different layers when using `require.context`, even if the rules per layer should produce a different output.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

- Clone the repository https://github.com/denzelem/webpack5-issue-20230615
- Install Node `v19.7.0`
- Run `npm install`
- Run `npm run build`

```bash
├── app
│   └── assets
│       ├── dark.js
│       ├── light.js
│       ├── test1
│       │   └── shared.sass
│       └── test2
│           └── shared.sass
├── package.json
├── package-lock.json
├── public
│   └── assets
│       ├── dark-a050d6fca14afa704ffb.css
│       ├── dark.js
│       ├── light-0892e4f074e52b30fb53.css
│       └── light.js
├── README.md
└── webpack.config.js
```

`webpack.config.js`:

```js
const path = require("path")
const webpack = require("webpack")
const MiniCssExtractPlugin = require('mini-css-extract-plugin')

module.exports = {
  mode: "production",
  entry: {
    light: { import: "./app/assets/light.js", layer: 'light' },
    dark: { import: "./app/assets/dark.js", layer: 'dark' },
  },
  output: {
    filename: "[name].js",
    sourceMapFilename: "[file].map",
    path: path.resolve(__dirname, "public/assets"),
    clean: true,
  },
  experiments: {
    layers: true,
  },
  plugins: [
    new MiniCssExtractPlugin({
      filename: '[name]-[contenthash].css'
    }),
  ],
  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.sass$/i,
        oneOf: [
          {
            issuerLayer: 'light',
            use:
              [
                MiniCssExtractPlugin.loader,
                "css-loader",
                {
                  loader: 'sass-loader',
                  options: {
                    additionalData: '$color: white',
                  }
                },
              ],
          },
          {
            issuerLayer: 'dark',
            use:
              [
                MiniCssExtractPlugin.loader,
                "css-loader",
                {
                  loader: 'sass-loader',
                  options: {
                    additionalData: '$color: black',
                  }
                },
              ],
          },
        ]
      },
    ],
  },
}
```

```diff
diff --git a/app/assets/dark.js b/app/assets/dark.js
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..25200ec
--- /dev/null
+++ b/app/assets/dark.js
@@ -0,0 +1,2 @@
+require.context('./test1', true, /\.sass$/)
+require('./test2/shared.sass')
diff --git a/app/assets/light.js b/app/assets/light.js
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..25200ec
--- /dev/null
+++ b/app/assets/light.js
@@ -0,0 +1,2 @@
+require.context('./test1', true, /\.sass$/)
+require('./test2/shared.sass')
diff --git a/app/assets/test1/shared.sass b/app/assets/test1/shared.sass
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..2f615b1
--- /dev/null
+++ b/app/assets/test1/shared.sass
@@ -0,0 +1,2 @@
+.test1
+  color: $color
diff --git a/app/assets/test2/shared.sass b/app/assets/test2/shared.sass
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..dd46a99
--- /dev/null
+++ b/app/assets/test2/shared.sass
@@ -0,0 +1,2 @@
+.test2
+  color: $color
```

It produces two files in `public/assets/`.

Current result:

`public/assets/light-a050d6fca14afa704ffb.css`:

```
.test1{color:#000}
.test2{color:#fff}
```


`public/assets/dark-a050d6fca14afa704ffb.css`:

```
.test1{color:#000}
.test2{color:#000}
```

**Note**: 

- `test1` was required with `require.context` (and is broken)
- `test2` was required with `require` (and works as expected)

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->


Expected result:

`public/assets/light-a050d6fca14afa704ffb.css`:

```
.test1{color:#fff}
.test2{color:#fff}
```


`public/assets/dark-a050d6fca14afa704ffb.css`:

```
.test1{color:#000}
.test2{color:#000}
```

Maybe related to https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17310.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.87.0
Node.js version: 19.7.0
Operating System: Linux
Additional tools: 
  - `sass-loader`: `13.3.2`
author: denzelem

title: import TaggedTemplateExpression should generate indirect call
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

 The input code 
``` js
import a from "a"
a();
a``;
```
The output code
``` js
/* harmony import */ var _a__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(/*! a */ "./a.js");

(0, _a__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__["default"])()
_a__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__["default"]`` // here should be indirect call
```


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->

<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

The expected output

``` js
/* harmony import */ var _a__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(/*! a */ "a.js");

(0, _a__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__["default"])()
(0, _a__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__["default"])`` // here should be indirect call
```

Here has babel playground output. https://babeljs.io/repl#?browsers=&build=&builtIns=usage&corejs=3.21&spec=false&loose=false&code_lz=JYWwDg9gTgLgBAEzgMyhEcBEA6A9AQwCMBjTAKDIQAoBKAbgoQAMmg&debug=false&forceAllTransforms=true&modules=false&shippedProposals=true&circleciRepo=&evaluate=false&fileSize=false&timeTravel=false&sourceType=module&lineWrap=true&presets=&prettier=false&targets=&version=7.21.8&externalPlugins=%40babel%2Fplugin-transform-modules-commonjs%407.20.11%2C%40babel%2Fplugin-transform-function-name%407.18.9&assumptions=%7B%7D

author: underfin

title: Add `fetchpriority="high"` to <script /> for loading async js source
body:
For example, if you load an asynchronous route, the JS load after the first screen is a low-priority load, and then your chunks are split into dozens of chunks, then you will be blocked by some requests.The most serious problem is that the chunks are cached locally by the browser, but it takes hundreds of milliseconds to load them
see [Network requests stuck in pending state 
](https://bugs.chromium.org/p/chromium/issues/detail?id=891940)

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/48762792/b6cc0f52-66fd-45bd-9e35-1825b2bbbafa)
to resolve: `script.setAttribute("fetchpriority","hight")`
see [MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/script)
author: xingxinglieo

title: Inbuilt support for alternate minifiers
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

Have out of the box support for a faster minifier (possibly behind an experiment with the intention to make it default with webpack 6)

https://github.com/privatenumber/minification-benchmarks contains benchmark information for available minifiers. At this time, i dont have a strong preference towards an option

We have an open PR for a CSS minifier that we should consider under this issue as well https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17309

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Faster minification

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

To improve the build speed and DX, we should make it easy to switch to an alternate minifier

I am aware that another minifier can be passed explicitly but most devs dont know about this and face long build time issues. Minification is an area where we depend on an external tool that is not closely coupled to webpack

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Let terser be the default choice for now. Have a flag `experiment.<minifier-name>` that when enabled, changes the JS (and possibly CSS) minifier

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

author: ahabhgk

title: allow end users to customize module sharing via federation
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Developers should be able to customize the sharing behavior for a dependency based off where's it's being loaded in, or what version of the dependency is being shared. This issue is the first in line for the companion [PR here](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17396)

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

In standard module federation implementation, developers can specify that a given dependency is "shared" for consumption, i.e other consumers can remotely consume a shared module. They do this by adding the module to `shared` property in `ModuleFederationPlugin`. However, developers are not allowed to customize how this sharing works with a complex nested dependency tree.

Example repository showcasing the problem: [Different major versions of a shared dependency d3-array.](https://github.com/tmpaul/module-federation-enhancements-showcase/tree/main/exclusion#different-major-versions-of-a-shared-dependency-d3-array).
   * Clone, `yarn` + `yarn serve` for production builds, `yarn start` for development builds

In this example repo, there are two versions of `d3-array` (`3.x` and `1.x`). By marking `d3-array` as shared, both versions are automatically marked for shared consumption. In many cases, this is undesirable. Older versions may contain non-treeshakeable code, buggy code, or obsolete code. The main use-case involves sharing "top" level dependencies present in the root `package.json` file which is what developers have control over. They do not directly control how another version of the same package is brought in transitively. 

> Today, there is no way to tell `webpack`: "Please don't share a package when it is requested in a specific context `x` or if the major version is `y`, but only share newer versions".

The problem then is that the older version is also built out as a shared chunk, preventing tree-shaking. The bundle-size with exclusion in effect (i.e excluding 1.x from sharing) is lower than standard webpack sharing. In a real life project with tons of nested dependencies, this number can be staggeringly high. In our company, I have seen remoteEntry files ~ 200kb due to tons of nested dependency sharing.

The following table shows the bundle size for not sharing vs sharing all versions of a package vs sharing only
a specific major version of a package

| Sharing Type | JS size |  Versions |
---------------|-----------|---------|
| Not shared |   92 kb |  N/A |
| Not excluded |  101 kb     | 3.2.4,1.2.4 |
| Excluded  |    98.5 kb   | 3.2.4 |

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Developers must be able to specify when to exclude a given dependency from sharing. This can come in a few varieties:
a. Exclude from sharing when imported by a file in a specific directory (`context` or `issuer` in webpack terms).
b. Exclude from sharing when a specific version or range criteria is satisfied.

To this end, I can think of two ways to implement this:

```javascript
shared: {
   "d3-array": {
        "exclusionCriteria": {
              /*To match issuer or context. For example, if d3-array is imported by d3-scale, exclude, */
              "context": string | RegExp | undefined | (context: string) => boolean
              /*To match a specific version or range */
              "version": "1.2.4" | "^1.0.0"
         }
    }
}
```

I added the `context` behavior in the PR, but in all honesty, I think the `version` or `range` based exclusion is far more meaningful because the `context` can be confusing in monorepo setups like `lerna`

**Open Questions**
1. How do we handle the scenario where a package is excluded based off a specific `context`, but is allowed to be shared in another context with a matching version ?

     For instance, if `d3-array@1.2.4` is excluded for all imports from `d3-scale`, but is not excluded for any other contexts, and another context pulls in `d3-array@1.2.4`, then excluding from sharing is actually detrimental. Because, there is now a shared chunk@1.2.4 and a tree-shaken bundled in version@1.2.4. This can be solved by restricting the exclusion criteria to just version or range.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

Related issue: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15971

title: Performance slowdown and high memory consumption when using dynamic imports
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

When using dynamic `import` call over sync `require` I see increase in build time and in memory consumption.

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

I have some private application that uses dynamic imports to implement lazy loading.

After some code simplification and enabling additional logs, If I run it I see next numbers for time and memory:
```
DEBUG LOG from webpack.buildChunkGraph
<t> visitModules: prepare: 2.5296 ms
<t> visitModules: visiting: 6.703999 ms
<t> visitModules: calculating available modules: 0.6491 ms
<t> visitModules: merging available modules: 0.939 ms
<t> visitModules: check modules for revisit: 0.3369 ms
<t> visitModules: prepare: 45.050496 ms
<t> visitModules: visiting: 778.289864 ms
    549028 queue items processed (265085 blocks)
    380 chunk groups connected
    380 chunk groups processed for merging (380 module sets, 0 forked, 0 + 0 modules forked, 0 + 0 modules merged into fork, 0 resulting modules)
    380 chunk group info updated (0 already connected chunk groups reconnected)
<t> visitModules: 788.805262 ms
<t> connectChunkGroups: 1.801699 ms
<t> cleanup: 0.1743 ms

client (webpack 5.87.0) compiled with 1 warning in 110049 ms
{
  clientBuildTime: 110050,
  maxMemoryRss: 2221084672
}
```

If I replace dynamic imports with sync requires I get this:
```
DEBUG LOG from webpack.buildChunkGraph
<t> visitModules: prepare: 113.764402 ms
<t> visitModules: visiting: 187.628703 ms
    4353 queue items processed (2031 blocks)
    0 chunk groups connected
    0 chunk groups processed for merging (0 module sets, 0 forked, 0 + 0 modules forked, 0 + 0 modules merged into fork, 0 resulting modules)
    0 chunk group info updated (0 already connected chunk groups reconnected)
<t> visitModules: 189.600003 ms
<t> connectChunkGroups: 0.2773 ms
<t> cleanup: 0.0831 ms

client (webpack 5.87.0) compiled with 3 warnings in 100983 ms
{
  clientBuildTime: 100984,
  maxMemoryRss: 1147891712
}
```

You can see that build with dynamic imports executing for 10 seconds longer and requires almost twice of memory. 

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I've created an [reproduction repo](https://github.com/meskill/webpack5-performance-dynamic-import) that shows the issue, although the numbers are not that high as in real application.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

I hope there could be a room to improve this to make lazy chunking less expensive for building. 

For example in case of dynamic imports the more time is spend in `visitModules` function that has [comment](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/8241da7f1e75c5581ba535d127fa66aeb9eb2ac8/lib/buildChunkGraph.js#L541) about possibility of removing some traversals.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.87.0
Node.js version: 16
Operating System: Debian bullseye

title: v5.78.0 causing errors on watcher recompilations
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Looks like [PR 16477](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/16477) introduced a regression for us while running in watch mode for recompilations. Specifically the addition of the if stmt in NormalModuleFactory.js.




<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->



**What is the current behavior?**
The initial build reports no errors but anytime it re-compiles due to a file change, the errors show up. They're all asset related errors and it doesn't seem break the build as far as I can tell. The assets in question are mostly css images but it's also occurring for a json file we're importing in our js code. That initially made me thing it was css-loader related but the json file error throws a wrench in that theory.

The one common feature is that both sets of assets are requested with alias paths but the error messages report the absolute path.

```
ERROR in ./docs/assets/definitions/default.json 2:11
Module parse failed: Unexpected token (2:11)
You may need an appropriate loader to handle this file type, currently no loaders are configured to process this file. See https://webpack.js.org/concepts#loaders
| {
>   "default": {
|     "desktop": [
|       {
```

It's an unexpected error considering importing json files in JS should just work, and does on the initial compilation.



**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
I haven't had time to create a simplified reproduction since our setup is pretty complex but I figured I'd at least report something in case someone else has encountered this before or it sparks an ah-ha moment. If that's ultimately what's needed me or my colleague will try to get something together.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->

<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

If the loader for the resources were truly missing, then I'd expect to to affect all assets and it's only affecting some of them.

<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

webpack version:  5.78.0
Node.js version: v20.3.0

title: Importing package bundled by webpack as library with split chunks does not import chunks
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

Importing package bundled by webpack as library with split chunks does not import chunks.


**What is the current behavior?**

##### Just before exaplaining the bug, I need to clarify the setup a bit:

(Description of the demo, which is based on my actual issue in a more complex setup.)

I have two packages `a` and `b` in a npm workspace.
`a` is a package that contains a small web application. `b` is a library, consumed by `a`.

`b` has two files, just an `index.js` file and a `translation.json` file. There is only a `getTranslation` function that is exported by the library. This function asynchrounously load `translation.json` file and return it.
When compiling `b`, two files are generated, `main.mjs`, and `translation_json.mjs`.

##### Now the current behavior:

In `a`, the `b` package can be successfully imported, both synchronously and asynchronously. However, when calling `b.getTranslation`, the `translation_json.mjs` is not found by the dev server. A build of `a` also does not output `b`'s `translation_json.mjs` contents in any of its files.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Clone the following repo:
https://github.com/pedro00dk/webpack-library-split-chunks

Then:
```shell
$ # inside repo root directory
$ npm install
...

$ cd ./a/
$ npm run start # it will build 'b' automatically
```

Now go to `http://localhost:5000` and click the button with text: `click to load "b" translation file`

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

When building `a`, the `translation_json.mjs` from`b` should be included in the bundle somehow, and accessible when running the dev server.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.88.0 (cli: 5.1.4)
Node.js version: 20.2.0
Operating System: Ubuntu 23.04
Additional tools: None

title: `import` name mangling not applied to class constructor in one specific case
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Consider this program:

```
import bar_string from './bar';

console.log("outer log", bar_string);

var my_class = class {
    constructor() {
        this.bar = bar_string;
        console.log("inner log", bar_string)
    }
  },  my_instance = (new my_class())
```

Webpack transforms it to something like this:
```
(() => {

const bar = ("bar");

console.log("outer log", bar);

var my_class = class {
    constructor() {
        console.log("inner log", bar_string); /// <<< this is wrong
    }
  },  my_instance = (new my_class())
```

Notice how "bar_string" inside the class constructor is left as-is, while the other references to imported module "bar_string" are replaced with "bar".

Oddly, if I add "new my_class()" at the end of the program, it works fine. The bug appears to be due to the assignment of `my_class` and `my_instance` in the same `var` statement.

When running the program that Webpack outputs, I get the following error:
```
ReferenceError: bar_string is not defined
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I wrote a test in the Webpack codebase:
https://github.com/jluxenberg/webpack/commit/c249f1231a3fa188db267d19e85210c43d386403

`bar_string` in the class's constructor should have been replaced with `bar`.

title: Webpack should not replace process.env at build time when target = node
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

```
console.log("process.env.NODE_ENV", process.env.NODE_ENV);
```

This should be kept as-is when target starts with "node".

**What is the current behavior?**

Webpack will replace the line at build time e.g.: `console.log(\"process.env.NODE_ENV\", \"development\")`

This is normally undesired in a Node env.

**What is the expected behavior?**

To have an option to not replace it in `node` settings e.g.

```
node: {
  // false means let Node handle them as-is
  global: false,
  __dirname: false,
  __filename: false,
  process: false, // Allow NodeJS to deal with process
}
```

Node.js version: any

title: Production mode seems to break worklet detection
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

The following code gets correctly parsed in development mode but doesn't work in production mode.

```js
// index.js
let audioContext = null;

if (audioContext === null) {
    audioContext = new AudioContext();
}

audioContext.audioWorklet.addModule(new URL("./worklet.js", import.meta.url));
```

Please note that the if statement in the snippet above is rather useless. The original code that made me aware of this issue was way more complicated but this seems to be enough to reproduce the issue.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Save the snippet above as `index.js`. Create a dummy worklet saved as `worklet.js`.

```js
// worklet.js
import { TRUE } from './dependency.js';

class SomeProcessor extends AudioWorkletProcessor {
    process() {
        return TRUE;
    }
}

registerProcessor('some-processor', SomeProcessor);
```

Add another file to fake a dependency.

```js
// dependency.js
export const TRUE = true;
```

Create a config file named `config.js` with the following content.

```js
// config.js
module.exports = {
    module: {
        parser: {
            javascript: {
                worker: [
                    "*audioContext.audioWorklet.addModule()",
                    "*audioWorklet.addModule()",
                    "..."
                ]
            }
        }
    }
};
```

With all this in place running `npx webpack --config ./config.js --entry ./index.js --mode production` will not produce the expected result. The `worklet.js` file remains unprocessed and seems to be treated like an asset. The import statement remains untouched.

However running `npx webpack --config ./config.js --entry ./index.js --mode development` will produce the expected outcome. The `worklet.js` file gets processed as JavaScript and the dependency will be inlined.


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

I would expect the `worklet.js` file to get processed in both modes.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: v5.88.1
Node.js version: v18.16.1
Operating System: macOS
Additional tools: npm/npx v8.19.3

title: How to decouple env files in module federation between microfrontends?
body:
My current set up has 5 remotes and 1 host. They are all React applications. The host has a config.env.js file where we have tokens which are used throughout the application. The tokens are replaced during deployment by injecting the correct ENV variable from our pipeline. The host then passes these variables to each of the remotes using the window object. 

I'd like to move away from this structure to decouple the micro frontends further so they can have their own independent config.env.js file and they don't need to rely on the host. 

The way I am thinking this would work is that the host has a config file which is used for common ENV variables to share between all of the remotes. Each remote can have its own independent config file too, for any independent ENV variables which the other micro frontends do not need. I do not need separate config files for each environment as my CI/CD already takes care of injecting the correct values for each environment.

This would help the micro frontends be more decoupled and not require a host deployment any time an independent ENV variable is updated.

title: Minification step of production mode breaks build if key of object is diacritic character
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

The minification step of the production mode breaks the build and the code cannot be run inside the browser.

Explanation (please see the reproduction repo):
The `match-sorter` dependency includes a dependency called `remove-accents`, that maps characters with a [diacritic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diacritic) to a character without a diacritic, e.g. `À` to `A`. This dependency is used in the `match-sorter` dependency to remove accents from strings before sorting them.

The original code is similar to this:

```js
var characterMap = {
  "À": "A",
  "Á": "A",
  "Â": "A",
  "Ã": "A",
  "Ä": "A",
  // ...
};
```

During the production build process (enabled by `config.mode === "production"`), webpack minifies the code. This results in the following code:

```js
// prettified for readability
var r = {
  // notice the missing quotes around the keys
  À: "A",
  Á: "A",
  Â: "A",
  Ã: "A",
  Ä: "A",
  // ...
};
```

The browser interprets this as a syntax error, because the keys are not quoted. This is not a problem in development mode, because the code is not minified and the quotes aren't removed. When we take a look at the source code in the browser, the code looks like this:

![Source code viewed in browser](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/unzico/webpack5-prod-minify/main/image.png)

The result: `Uncaught SyntaxError: Invalid or unexpected token (at main.js:233:14)`.
As soon as we put the key in quotes, the error is gone.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Clone the following repo and follow the instructions described in the README.
https://github.com/unzico/webpack5-prod-minify

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

The minifier shouldn't remove the quotes from characters that have a diacritic, if the character is the key of an object.

Instead of 
```js
var r = {
  À: "A",
  Á: "A",
  Â: "A",
  // ...
};
```
it should be
```js
var r = {
  "À": "A",
  "Á": "A",
  "Â": "A",
  // ...
};
```

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

new Worker(new URL('./worker.js', import.meta.url));

"exports": {

title: PUBLIC_URL is ignored if set in .env
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

I try to host my application beneath different paths. To control this is se the `PUBLIC_URL` variable in my .env files. 
<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

In my generated index.html the script tag for static js does not start with my path which I set via the `PUBLIC_URL` in .env file. If I set the variable on the host like `PUBLIC_URL=/some-path rpm run build`, the static path will start with `/some-path`. Additionally if I inspect `webpack.output.publicPath` the correct value is displayed.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

- react-scripts project with craco enabled
- use `CracoEnv` plugin 
- create `.env.production`
- run `craco build --mode production`

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

The static js path should have a prefix, which can be set in .env.


I am aware that react-scripts is not scope of this repository. But that the behavior diverges when using a host environment vs a .env variable seems to be a bug.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  ^5.64.4

Operating System: MacOs Ventura 13.2.1

title: ModuleId conflict results in incorrect object being referenced
body: # Bug report **What is the current behavior?** in the dist, a variable "a" was defined in module 261, but in module 374 the object was undefined, causing an error. **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** repository: https://github.com/chrisbing/demo-for-webpack-bug-module-id webpack.config.js ```js const HtmlWebpackPlugin = require('html-webpack-plugin') module.exports = { entry: { a: './src/index-a.js', b: './src/index-b.js' }, mode: "production", plugins: [ new HtmlWebpackPlugin({ chunks: ['b', 'a'] }) ] } ``` source code: dist: Because module 70 was loaded first, module 261 in 70.js was registered preferentially, and the dataA variable in this module has already been tree-shaken. **What is the expected behavior?** It was expected that module 374 would be able to access the corresponding object "a" in module 261 and successfully execute console.log. **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.88.2 Node.js version: v16.20.0 Operating System: macOs 13.4.1 arm64 Additional tools: html-webpack-plugin: 5.5.3

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

I noticed that building a chunk with `webpackChunkName` and using the experimental `layer` feature results in duplicate content withing the chunk.

I created an example application to reproduce the issue: https://github.com/denzelem/webpack5-issue-20230725


*webpack.config.js*

```
const path = require("path")

module.exports = {
  mode: "production",
  entry: {
    light: { import: "./app/assets/light.js", layer: 'light' },
    dark: { import: "./app/assets/dark.js", layer: 'dark' },
  },
  output: {
    filename: "[name].js",
    sourceMapFilename: "[file].map",
    path: path.resolve(__dirname, "public/assets"),
    clean: true,
  },
  experiments: {
    layers: true,
  },
}
```

*app/assets/dark.js*

```
require('./javascripts/main')
```

*app/assets/light.js*

```
require('./javascripts/main')
```

*app/assets/javascripts/large_lib.js*


```
console.log('hello large lib')
```

*app/assets/javascripts/main.js*


```
import(/* webpackChunkName: "largeLib" */ './large_lib').then(() => {
  console.log('hello main')
})
```

**Result**:

```
cat public/assets/largeLib.js
(self.webpackChunkwebpack5_issue_20230725=self.webpackChunkwebpack5_issue_20230725||[]).push([[849],{616:()=>{console.log("hello large lib")},850:()=>{console.log("hello large lib")}}]);
```

**What is the current behavior?**

Having multiple layers results in duplicate content in a shared chunk. Notice the duplicate `hello large lib` in `public/assets/largeLib.js`.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

Checkout https://github.com/denzelem/webpack5-issue-20230725:

- Install Node version `v19.7.0`
- `npm install`
- `npm run build`

And inspect `public/assets/largeLib.js`.

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

I would expect that having different layers should not result in duplicate content within shared chunks.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.88.2
Node.js version: 9.7.0
Operating System: Ubuntu

title: Loader with wrong include path does not throw error
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Babel transpilation is ignored without warning/error when the loader includes a wrong path. The problem is transparent and production code can be broken without transpilation.

The following repository illustrates the problem
https://github.com/yoriiis/webpack-loader-wrong-path

The loader include path is intentionally incorrect in [webpack.config.js#L27](https://github.com/yoriiis/webpack-loader-wrong-path/blob/main/config/webpack.config.js#L27), it should be `path.resolve(__dirname, '../src')`

Instruction to reproduce the problem on the repository:

- `git clone https://github.com/yoriiis/webpack-loader-wrong-path.git`
- `cd ./webpack-loader-wrong-path`
- `nvm use`
- `npm install`
- `npm run build`

**The build with the incorrect path and without transpilation**

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/2563298/9a2a0f55-6a80-4eab-a47d-f1d78744973c)

**The build with the correct path and with transpilation**

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/2563298/2b8aa155-751f-4c51-af79-dc2e77c034b7)

**What is the expected behavior?**

Webpack should trigger an error when the path is incorrect.

For example if the path to the Babel configuration file with [configFile](https://github.com/yoriiis/webpack-loader-wrong-path/blob/main/config/webpack.config.js#L32) is incorrect, it will throw an error.

I also notice that the path error for the Babel file is not triggered if the path of the includes field of the loader is incorrect. Is it linked? If not, I can report this issue on `babel-loader`, tell me.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: `5.88.1`
Node.js version: `18.12.0`
Operating System: `MacOS 13.4`
Additional tools: -

body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

When a webpack build creates a lot of chunks (1000 chunks), the logic used in webpack runtime to resolve chunkId -> assetUrl are easily causing maximum call stack on some browsers.

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

A webpack build page that have 1000+ chunks to resolve on runtime.js will throw stackoverflow on Chrome, while it will still work on Firefox.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

This fiddle is to reproduce the logic on webpack runtime with 1200 chunks will fail on Chrome:
https://jsfiddle.net/a2y46Lo7/

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->

<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

This could work if instead of using ternary operations webpack runtime use some object mapping instead, on
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/1f99ad6367f2b8a6ef17cce0e058f7a67fb7db18/lib/runtime/GetChunkFilenameRuntimeModule.js#L279

Supposedly a build with thousands of chunks are very rarely needed, but some browsers may actually have less max stack and these errors can happen with just a few hundred of chunks. We have a lot of constraints on our build process and this stackoverflow error happened in our case.

title: Webpack Loader time statistics error
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

I think the current loader time statistics for webpack are not accurate, please refer to the [code](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/Compilation.js#L2609C18-L2609C18):

```JS
const t = d / p;

// It should be

const t = d * p;
```
Referring to the [`ParallelismFactorCalculator.js`](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/util/ParallelismFactorCalculator.js#L64C47-L64C47) file, the variable `p` is the concurrency per ms, and the variable `d` is duration.

title: Webpack internal EntryPlugin does not work with multiple entries
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

EntryPlugin does not produce multiple chunks in the case multiple entries are present. Currently, EntryPlugin always produces one chunk. If you try to pass an array of entries there it fails with a TS error, as it's not supported:

```Type 'string[]' has no properties in common with type 'EntryDescriptionNormalized'.ts(2559)
(property) metadataFetcher: string[]
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

Entry plugin produces as many entries as passed in the parameters.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

Consistency between EntryPlugin and webpack entry configuration. It unlocks usage of EntryPlugin for the applications with multiple entries.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Accept an array of entries, compile each.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**

No. I am not aware what are the reasons why MultiEntryPlugin is deprecated in latest Webpack, seems like this functionality was present in Webpack4 but removed.

title: Webpack can generate incorrect code if the original source defines async iterator in a class
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Webpack 5.84.0 and newer can generate incorrect code when the source is an esm module and uses async iterator in some class definition.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Create two files, one called `my.esm.js` and the other one called `webpack-index.js`. Create a `webpack.config.js` as well.

Each file has the following code

my.esm.js
```javascript
var eP=[1,2],
Ky=class{
    constructor(){}
},
Io=class{
    constructor(t){if(!eP.includes(t.version))throw"invalid parquet version";}
    async*[Symbol.asyncIterator](){yield ""}
}
var SP=Io,w8={ParquetReader:SP};export{w8 as default};

```

webpack-index.js
```javascript
const parquetjs = require("./my.esm");

const reader = new parquetjs.default.ParquetReader(1);
```

webpack.config.js
```javascript
module.exports = (env, argv) => {
    const mode = argv.mode || "production";

    return {
        mode: mode,
        devtool: 'source-map',
        entry: './webpack-index.js',
        output: {
            path: __dirname + "/prebuilt-bundles",
            filename: (mode === 'production' ? 'webpack-bundle.js' : 'webpack-bundle.dev.js')
        },
        target: "web",
    }
}
```

Running webpack in production mode with the given source and config will reproduce the bug.

The generated code looks like this (only ~700 characters feel free to paste to a separate editor and toggle word wrap to read it). To quickly get to what's wrong, search for "null.includes".
```javascript
(()=>{var e={12:(e,r,t)=>{"use strict";t.r(r),t.d(r,{default:()=>o});var o={ParquetReader:class{constructor(e){if(!null.includes(e.version))throw"invalid parquet version"}async*[Symbol.asyncIterator](){yield""}}}}},r={};function t(o){var a=r[o];if(void 0!==a)return a.exports;var n=r[o]={exports:{}};return e[o](n,n.exports,t),n.exports}t.d=(e,r)=>{for(var o in r)t.o(r,o)&&!t.o(e,o)&&Object.defineProperty(e,o,{enumerable:!0,get:r[o]})},t.o=(e,r)=>Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(e,r),t.r=e=>{"undefined"!=typeof Symbol&&Symbol.toStringTag&&Object.defineProperty(e,Symbol.toStringTag,{value:"Module"}),Object.defineProperty(e,"__esModule",{value:!0})},new(t(12).ParquetReader)})();
//# sourceMappingURL=webpack-bundle.js.map
```

Class `Io` references a local variable `eP` that has array literal value [1,2]. In the generated code, the local variable `eP` is gone. The reference of `eP.includes` in class `Io` is translated to `null.includes` which will always throw when user runs the generated code.

**What is the expected behavior?**

The expected behavior is so that webpack keeps the local variable and use it at the right place.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.84.0 and up to 5.88.2
Node.js version: 18.17.0
Operating System: Windows/Mac
Additional tools: N/A

**Additional context**

The code is from npm package @dsnp/parquetjs which we use in our product. The original file is too big so I trimmed it down to make it more feasible for troubleshooting. The problem seems to be very closely related to the async iterator defined in class `Io`. I tried tracing what webpack is doing. It turns out that when webpack walks through the definition of class `Io`, it attempts to evaluate the expression `Symbol.asyncIterator` and as a result flags the class as "not pure". Because of these changes added in 5.84.0 around this line in `InnerGraphPlugin.js`, since the class `Io` is flagged as "not pure", it will then walk through the body of its methods (constructor in this repro case) without calling `classWithTopLevelSymbol.set` to set the correct class scope. https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/optimize/InnerGraphPlugin.js#L167
Basically, it incorrectly believes that the variable `eP` is referenced in class `Ky` which is defined above. Since class `Ky` is actually not used, both class `Ky` and the variable `eP` are wiped out during the optimization phase and results in the `null` value ended up where the variable should be referenced. If I delete the async iterator from class `Io` or simply move the definition of class `Ky` to be after the definition of `Io`, the generated code will become correct.

In general, I think the scenario is too subtle for developers to keep in mind while using webpack. I think the fix should be in webpack instead of in the client code. I made a PR proposing a fix for the aforementioned issue.

title: Support default rule for internal types
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->


Currently, for those who use `experiments.css` would encounter an issue for custom loader pipelines. I don't know if it is the design by webpack:

less file
```less
@import "variable.css";
```

The rule is configured as follows:

```js
{
  test: /\.less$/,
  use: ['less-loader'],
  type: 'css'
}
```

The transformation will result in a failure as `preferRelative` for this rule is not configured. This leads to an misalignment with the builtin rule for `css` files. In order to make it work, developers should configure like this:

```js
{
  test: /\.less$/,
  use: ['less-loader'],
  type: 'css',
  resolve: { preferRelative: true  }  // There might be other default rules to be applied.
}
```

To avoid the logic difference, we'd better to introduce a default rule for the internal type.

See more details here: https://github.com/web-infra-dev/rspack/issues/3913

@alexander-akait Any bias on this one?

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: Module Federation: duplicated module instances for exposed modules
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Also consider trying the webpack@beta version, maybe it's already fixed. -->

**But Report**
I'm trying with the module federation in our current project. My case is to create physically seprated plugins which can be loaded/replaced at runtime by the application.  The application has modules  exposed for plugins and also consumed by it self. This shall be a popluar scnario, I think.  

**What is the current behavior?**
I found there're duplicated module code/instance in both host's bundle and its trunk files (will be loaded by the remote entry file when remotes call the exposed modules.)   Consequently, the build size is bloated unncessarily and states are incorrect because of different module instance at runtime.

**Steps to reproduce**
1. Use the [repo](https://github.com/pedrokehl/module-federation-nodejs-example.git)
2. Build the app2 project as follows:
`cd app2`
`npm i`
`webpack`
3. Search "function mapBook" in both app2/dist/main.js and src_mapBook_js.js (this will be loaded by remoteEntry.js). There're duplicated.

I found there's simliar scenario and issue on StackOverflow without answer yet. See [Webpack module federation - module instance duplicated by import](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72575299/webpack-module-federation-module-instance-duplicated-by-import).   The reporter also sugested an SharedModuleCachePlugin.

In such case, host will be surely loaded upfront and its exposed modules will be used by itself.  I guess that current webpack does not know the dependency order and has to always duplicates exposed modules.   

**Expected behavior**
There're no duplicated module instance and both host and remotes can consume and share the same module instance.

Any solution? Looking forward to any ideas.  Thank you!

Webpack 5.88.2, 5.74.0

title: Parser consider call of function named with JS keyword breaks compilation
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Imagine we have following code:
```
(this, function (_) {
  'use strict';
  const foo = {};
  foo.if = function() {
    return 4.0;
  }
  function main() {
    const bar = foo.if() / 2.0;
    console.log(bar);
  }
  main();
  return _;
})(module.exports);
```

Webpack failed during parsing
```
ERROR in ./app.js 8:26
Module parse failed: Unterminated regular expression (8:26)
You may need an appropriate loader to handle this file type, currently no loaders are configured to process this file. See https://webpack.js.org/concepts#loaders
|   }
|   function main() {
>     const bar = foo.if() / 2.0;
|     console.log(bar);
|   }
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Use code above and minimal configuration
```
let config = {
  mode: 'production',
  entry: require('path').resolve(__dirname, "app.js"),
  output: {
      filename: "bundle.js"
  }
};

module.exports = config
```

For convenience I uploaded minimal project here
https://github.com/ilgonmic/webpack-keyword-parse-fail
[Archive.zip](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/files/12304385/Archive.zip)

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->

The whole code are uploaded here [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/webpack5-vue3-forked-qq9xhf?file=%2Fwebpack.config.js%3A1%2C1-56%2C1). **my-component.js**: ```js import SecondComponent from './second-component.vue'; app.component('global-component', { template: `

title: Allow opt-in tree-shaking of class properties
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

Disclaimer: I assume this is not possible, and has already been discussed and rejected many times – but I couldn't find any previous discussion, so I thought I'd raise the topic in case there have been any advances in tree-shaking that could allow this.

**What is the expected behavior?**

Given a library that exposes an instance with methods for most operations ([real-world example](https://github.com/openai/openai-node/blob/v4/src/index.ts#L123-L124)):

```ts
import Completions from './completions'
import Images from './images'

export default class OpenAI extends APIClient {
  completions: Completions = new Completions(this);
  images: Images = new Images(this);
}
```
which is used like so:
```ts
import OpenAI from 'openai'

const client = new OpenAI()

const result = await client.completions.create()
```

It would be nice to have a way to tree-shake away the `images` API in the above example. 

Of course, this would have the dangerous result of changing the structure of the `OpenAI` class, here, in such a way that properties which are supposed to be a certain type would throw at runtime – eg, `client[calculateService()]` would either have to disable tree-shaking or throw.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

There is a [tension between those who want efficient builds](https://twitter.com/jaredpalmer/status/1690420651117477888) (e.g., for fast boots on edge runtimes), and [developers](https://twitter.com/SheltonLouisX/status/1690421167373303808) who [like fluent APIs](https://twitter.com/mattpocockuk/status/1690446495542067200). 

For example, AWS had to fully [rewrite their SDK](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/developer/modular-packages-in-aws-sdk-for-javascript/) to allow modularity, but this unfortunately hurts discoverability – per one developer I know, "I've used the new version of the AWS SDK and it is completely terrible when it comes to being able to actually find what you need". 

Devlopers would ideally [like to see](https://twitter.com/dillon_mulroy/status/1690423856320626688) bundlers improve their ability to handle patterns like this, to dodge the tradeoff entirely.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Perhaps like so:

```ts
import Completions from './completions'
import Images from './images'

export default class OpenAI extends APIClient {
  /** webpackTreeShakeableProperty: true */
  get completions(): Completions {
    return this._completions ??= new Completions(this);
  }
  
  /** webpackTreeShakeableProperty: true */
  get files(): Files {
    return this._files ??= new Files(this);
  }
}
```

which, with the above usage, would get transpiled to this:

```ts
import Completions from './completions'
export default class OpenAI extends APIClient {
  get completions() {
    return this._completions ??= new Completions(this);
  }
  
  get files() {
    throw new Error('property `files` on class `OpenAI` removed by webpack tree-shaking; see https://some.url/with-docs')
  }
}
```

Even better if the library author can use normal properties instead of getter methods.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**

I'd be willing to fund this work, depending on the scope.

title: Feature request: Add `__non_webpack_import__`
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Add a ~`import.meta.import()`~ `__non_webpack_import__()` that would behave exactly like the native `import()` from browsers, but that wouldn't be modified by webpack (nor transpilers like babel)

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

I have a piece of code like this: `import(dynamicVariablePointingToACDN)`

But due to wepback's default behavior, it tries to find this dynamically in its module context.

I tried to use `externalsPresets: { asyncWeb: true }}` with ``import(`https://${dynamicVariablePointingToACDN.replace('https://', '')}`)`` but it didn't seem to work (and instead I used `eval(import(dynamicVariablePointingToACDN))` to avoid webpack's transpilation)

Either way, all of those seem like hacks / seem really brittle (could break at any change in the config). Whereas `import.meta.import` would just represent how browser work (so more robust in the long term)

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

At compile time, we can transform those to native `import()` (maybe if other flags are also enabled, like `experiments: { outputModule: true }`, or we could always compile them to `import()` and in case of errors, use the default browser mechanism for those, and not built-in logic in webpack).


**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**

I have no knowledge of webpack's internal so no, but I can give it a try
