title: Side-effect-free modules with namespace re-exports are not always elided, causing nondeterministic output
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Repro: https://github.com/pgoldberg/webpack-namespace-reexport-side-effects-bug

Webpack doesn't always elide side-effect-free modules that only perform namespace re-exports. This results from the `modules` set being unordered, and the `SideEffectsFlagPlugin` not handling it properly. The module doing the re-export must have its incoming connections optimized before the module that's being re-exported, but because the `modules` set is in any order, that isn't always the case.

**What is the expected behavior?**

Side-effect-free modules should always be elided (unless side effect optimization is off or the module is marked as side-effectful in the package.json).

Just opening this issue for tracking, proposed fix is open here: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17595

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  latest
Node.js version: any
Operating System: any

title: Module concatenation causes Terser bailout conditions
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

I have created a simple 5-line reproduction case here:
https://gist.github.com/laverdet/1b8537f56dca2d990fe63decf66a2cc6

When we export hoistable function declarations which consume concatenated modules Webpack creates bailout conditions in Terser.

If you run this example with `npm i && npx webpack && cat dist/344.js` we can see the output:
```
// [runtime noise omitted]
const e={title:"main",aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa:"bbbbbbbbbbbb"};function s(){console.log(e.title)}
```

If instead you you comment out line 2, and uncomment line 3 in `async.js` we get this output instead:
```
// [runtime noise omitted]
const c=()=>{console.log("main")}
```

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

The code that Webpack generates cannot be optimized well because Terser assumes that as soon as the function is referenced it may be invoked. Therefore it would be incorrect to inline the object keys since the hoisted function would throw if invoked before the object is defined.


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

This deopt is a bug because we know from the es-262 specification that the default export of `payload.js` will be evaluated fully before any other module can reference the function defined in `async.js`.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

If concatenated modules were injected *before* the export runtime invocation (sorry I don't know what you call this internally) then the code could be optimized. You can observe this effect pretty easily at the Terser REPL by pasting the two examples in:

https://try.terser.org/

```js
const a = { aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa: 'a' };
globalThis.foo = function() {
  console.log(a.aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa);
}
```

output: `const o="a";globalThis.foo=function(){console.log(o)};`

```js
globalThis.foo = function() {
  console.log(a.aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa);
}
const a = { aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa: 'a' };
```

output: `globalThis.foo=function(){console.log(a.aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa)};const a={aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa:"a"};`

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.88.2
Node.js version: main [996f3904bc]
Operating System: macOS 13.3.1(a)

title: HookWebpackError: HMR is not implemented for module chunk format yet [webpack 5]
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

We're currently encountering an issue while attempting to build our webpack based projects using the target browserlist (last 10 version) configuration. The error message we're encountering is: "HMR is not implemented for module chunk format yet."
Our goal is to transition our application to use ESM (ECMAScript Modules), and to achieve this, we've been working with the required ESM configuration. While we have been able to successfully build the project, this HMR-related error is causing a roadblock in our development process.

Upon investigation, we've traced the source of this error to the code snippet found at this location: Webpack [ModuleChunkFormatPlugin](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/853bfda35a0080605c09e1bdeb0103bcb9367a10/lib/esm/ModuleChunkFormatPlugin.js#L55).

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

N/A

**What is the expected behavior?**

We would greatly appreciate your guidance and assistance in resolving this issue. We believe that enabling HMR support for the module chunk format would be a valuable enhancement, especially for projects like ours that are moving towards ESM adoption

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5
Node.js version: 18

title: [Feature] Dynamic filenames based on window attribute
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
I want it to be possible to pass dynamic variables into filename generation. This would make cache busting more reliable.

```typescript
module.exports = (env) => ({
  ...

  output: {
      filename: '[name].js?v=[window.my_variable]', // Not possible
      filename: '[name].js?v=[contenthash]', // Possible today
  ...
```

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

The possible solution would work just fine in most situations, but not ideal when you have sequential uploads to a live site.

- The entry file may be uploaded first (The file responsible for loading chunks)
- User hits the site and loads the new entry file
- This makes the server/client cache the chunks with the new `v` param, but old content
- Site breaks

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Add a new [window] variable, where you can select any window param.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes, but unsure where to start/implement this.

title: experiments.css generator options support
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

move experiments.css options to css module parser/generator options

```js
module.exports = {
  module: {
    generator: {
      'css/module': {
        exportsOnly: true,
      },
    },
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.client\.css$/,
        generator: {
          exportsOnly: false,
        },
        type: 'css/module',
      }
    ]
  },
  experiments: {
    css: true
  }
}
```

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

because `exportsOnly` is css generate related feature, css module generator options seems a better place for `exportsOnly`, so `test` or other rule condition in module rule can control whether the module should be `exportsOnly` or not

besides that, new feature like [namedExport](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/17403) (if it will be implemented), [localIdentName](https://github.com/webpack-contrib/css-loader?tab=readme-ov-file#localidentname), or [exportLocalsConvention](https://github.com/webpack-contrib/css-loader?tab=readme-ov-file#exportlocalsconvention) also applicable 

```js
module.exports = {
  module: {
    generator: {
      'css/module': {
        namedExport: true, // named export by default
      },
    },
    rules: [
      {
        include: 'legacy-code',
        generator: {
          namedExport: false, // default export for legacy code
        },
        type: 'css/module',
      }
    ]
  },
  experiments: {
    css: true
  }
}
```

and if it is necessary for compatible with the existing `experiments.css.exportsOnly`, it can be an alias of `module.generator.[css|css/auto|...].exportsOnly`

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

pretty similar with how asset module parser/generator options is implemented

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

export default (/** @type {NodeJS.ProcessEnv} */ env) => {

  allConfigurations.parallelism = parallelism

}

title: Content hash not matching file hash
body:
Im using webpack v5 to add content hashes to the file name. However when i try to compare that hash to the File Hash generated from external tools they dont match. Does `[contentHash]` add data outside of the actual file contents?

*Webpack.config*
output: {
            filename: '[name].js',
            chunkFilename: 'chunks/[name].[contenthash].js?v=[contenthash]',
            hashFunction: 'sha256',
            hashDigest: 'base64url',
            hashDigestLength: 500,
        },

*Sample File Name*
`Components-Shared-Modals-FeedbackModal-jsx.jLTegxjMB4UskmwXWx6-T94R71wp7ftQyCc5GUlxVtk.js`

*Sample PS Script and results*
I know this one be 100% exact as Base64 and base64url varies slight for special characters (like +,\, etc..) but you can still see the hash are totally different.
```
# Path to File
$file = '\Components-Shared-Modals-FeedbackModal-jsx.jLTegxjMB4UskmwXWx6-T94R71wp7ftQyCc5GUlxVtk.js'

# Get the hash of the file
$hash = Get-FileHash $file -Algorithm SHA256

# Convert hash to bytes
$hashBytes = [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes($hash.Hash)

# Convert byte to base64url
[System.Convert]::ToBase64String($hashBytes)
```

Output: `REQ5Q0E3QzJFNkVBNjJDQjg3QTk1QjdCOTJGMzU1ODExOTc4OUYzMERBNTZEQTRGMkZGRTdCRjRBOTBGNjU0Qw==`

title: An object of exported library is undefined if a project uses DllReferencePlugin plugin.
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
- I have a simple main project which contains `dll` and `app` Node.js projects.
[Here is the link of the project.](https://github.com/codesanook/webpack-issues/tree/main/webpack-issue-1)
- The `dll` project uses DllPlugin to create `vender.js` and `vendor-manifest.json`. They are consumed by `app` project.
- The `app` project is exported as a library and consumes `vendor.js` with DllReferencePlugin.
- When using app.js in a browser and call `app.int()`, a browser show an error because app variable is undefined. 
- However, when I remove `DllReferencePlugin` from webpack.config.js, app is exported correctly and there is `init` function in the app object. 

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

- Clone the demo project from git@github.com:codesanook/webpack-issues.git
- CD to `webpack-issue-1` folder.
- Build Node.js projects with the following commands: (I use Yarn Workspaces).
  ```sh
  yarn
  yarn workspaces run dev
  ```
- Launch Web Dev server to serve index.html in a dist folder with:
  ```sh
  yarn workspace app run serve
  ```
- You will find an error message in a developer console as a following message:
```html
Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'init')
    at index.html:11:23
```

**What is the expected behavior?**
<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

- The `app` project is exported as a library correctly.
- There must be `window.app` object in a browser and it must contain `init` function (defined in index.js).
- We can call app.int() in a script block of index.html without any issue.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.88.2
Node.js version: 16.20.1
Operating System:  Linux 5.15 Ubuntu 18.04.5 LTS (Bionic Beaver)
Additional tools:
- Yarn: 1.22.10 
- npm: 8.19.4
- webpack-cli: 5.1.4
- @babel/core: 7.22.17
- babel-loader: 9.1.3
- webpack-dev-server: 4.15.1


```js
// webpack-issue-1/src/app/webpack.config.js

const webpack = require("webpack");
const path = require("path");
const resolve = path.resolve;
const outputPath = resolve('../../dist');

module.exports = {
  entry: {
    'app': './src/index',
  },
  mode: 'development',
  output: {
    path: outputPath,
    filename: '[name].js',
    library: {
      name: '[name]',
      type: 'var',
      export: 'default',
    },
  },
  resolve: {
    extensions: ['.js']
  },
  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.(ts|js)x?$/,
        use: [
          'babel-loader',
        ],
        exclude: /node_modules/
      },
    ]
  },
  plugins: [
    new webpack.DllReferencePlugin({
      manifest: path.join(outputPath, "./vendor-manifest.json")
    }),
  ],
  devServer: {
    static: {
      directory: outputPath,
    },
    compress: true,
    port: 9000,
    open: true,
    client: {
      overlay: false,
    },
  },
};
```

```js
// webpack-issue-1/src/dll/webpack.config.js

const webpack = require("webpack");
const path = require("path");
const resolve = path.resolve;
const outputPath = resolve('../../dist');

module.exports = {
  entry: {
    'vendor': './src/index',
  },
  mode: 'development',
  output: {
    path: outputPath,
    filename: '[name].js',
    library: '[name]',
  },
  resolve: {
    extensions: ['.js']
  },
  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.(ts|js)x?$/,
        use: [
          'babel-loader',
        ],
        exclude: /node_modules/
      },
    ]
  },
  plugins: [
    new webpack.DllPlugin({
      path: path.join(outputPath, "[name]-manifest.json"),
      name: "[name]"
    })
  ],
};
```
author: aaronamm

title: Expose OptimizationStages
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

OptimizationStages should be exposed to allow plugin authors to use them. Webpack 5 release notes mention that they should be used instead of the original "basic" and "advanced" stage hooks. https://webpack.js.org/blog/2020-10-10-webpack-5-release/#staged-hooks

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

We have plugins which hardcode this value. We would like to directly use values from webpack.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Expose OptimizationStages.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes
author: ronakj

author: directcuteo

title: Custom conditionNames break default conditionNames
body:
Hi, I was looking to figure out the correct Webpack configuration to load with condition names, and there is very little information about what default condition names apply.

I'm using the Webpack `resolve` `conditionNames` configuration to add a custom platform condition for `"fastly"`. But from reading the documentation I'm unable to determine how this will interact with other conditions in Webpack.

It took a lot of searching to find https://gist.github.com/sokra/e032a0f17c1721c71cfced6f14516c62, which is a great resource, although doesn't clarify the interaction with custom `resolve: { conditionNames }` either.

When specifying custom conditionNames, I do not want the Webpack default handling of standard conditions to be disabled.

Specifically I had the following questions:

1. Do I need to explicitly configure the `"default"` condition? Reading the code, I can't see this condition being always used, despite the fact that Node.js specifies that it must always be resolved.
2. From reading other issues, I see that `"import"` and `"require"` aren't automatically used and must be specified, due to the resolver having no context bit to know if the importer is CJS or ESM. Again, this is breaking against the Node.js specification of how resolution should work.
3. I know Webpack automatically uses the `"module"` condition, again there is no documentation on whether this condition is automatically applied, or if I need to specify it manually.
4. It's also not clear to me if the `"browser"` or `"node"` conditions are being automatically added.

esbuild has really great handling for the above in doing "the right thing" and covers all these details really succinctly in https://esbuild.github.io/api/#conditions.

Perhaps Webpack should offer a new `platformConditions` or otherwise field that simply composes with the default condition names handling. The final list is always a union of different rules, having users provide it explicitly is just not a great user experience.

If there's a better way and I've missed it, I would really appreciate a pointer as well.
author: guybedford

title: Bundles generated with `output.library.type = 'module'` don't bind exported symbols correctly
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When using `output.library.type = 'module'` the bundles webpack is generating contain code like this:

```javascript
var __webpack_exports__event = __webpack_exports__.event;
var __webpack_exports__setEvent = __webpack_exports__.setEvent;
export { __webpack_exports__event as event, __webpack_exports__setEvent as setEvent };
```

The declarations of `var`s which are then exported with the proper name is unbinding the exported symbols from the webpack exports table, which contains synthetic objects that mimic the underlying symbol by means of getters and setters.

In other words if `event` is declared like this in the webpacked source code:

```javascript
export var event = null;
```

Internally, `__webpack_exports__.event` is evaluating the actual value of the `event` variable every time it is accessed, whereas the exported symbol `__webpack_exports__event` only evaluates it once at the time the module is defined, but it never gets updated for the rest of run time.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I'm attaching a ZIP file with a sample project that contains two simple modules:

**the_event.mjs**
```javascript
export var event = null;

export function setEvent(x) {
	event = x;
}
```

and

**test.mjs**
```javascript
console.log('==================================================================================');
import {event, setEvent} from './the_event.mjs';

console.log('>>>', event);

try {
	event = 7;
}
catch(err) {
	console.log('>>>', err);
}

setEvent(42);

console.log('>>>', event);

console.log('==================================================================================');
import {event as event2, setEvent as setEvent2} from './dist/the_event.mjs';

console.log('>>>', event2);

try {
	event2 = 7;
}
catch(err) {
	console.log('>>>', err);
}

setEvent2(42);

console.log('>>>', event2);
```

In the `test.mjs` module the same code retrieving the value of `event`, setting it and retrieving it again, is executed twice. The first time it uses the `the_event.mjs` module directly, the second it uses a webpacked version of that module that has been built with `output.library.type = 'module'`. Both should show the same output, but that doesn't happen:

```bash
$ npm run test

> webpack-exports-issue@1.0.0 test
> webpack && node test.mjs

asset the_event.mjs 1.99 KiB [compared for emit] [javascript module] (name: the_event) 1 related asset
runtime modules 670 bytes 3 modules
./the_event.mjs 70 bytes [built] [code generated]
webpack 5.88.2 compiled successfully in 87 ms
==================================================================================
>>> null
>>> TypeError: Assignment to constant variable.
    at file:///home/ivan/Temporal/webpack-exports-issue/test.mjs:7:8
    at ModuleJob.run (node:internal/modules/esm/module_job:193:25)
    at async Promise.all (index 0)
    at async ESMLoader.import (node:internal/modules/esm/loader:530:24)
    at async loadESM (node:internal/process/esm_loader:91:5)
    at async handleMainPromise (node:internal/modules/run_main:65:12)
>>> 42
==================================================================================
>>> null
>>> TypeError: Assignment to constant variable.
    at file:///home/ivan/Temporal/webpack-exports-issue/test.mjs:23:9
    at ModuleJob.run (node:internal/modules/esm/module_job:193:25)
    at async Promise.all (index 0)
    at async ESMLoader.import (node:internal/modules/esm/loader:530:24)
    at async loadESM (node:internal/process/esm_loader:91:5)
    at async handleMainPromise (node:internal/modules/run_main:65:12)
>>> null
```

The same behaviour is shown if the code is executed in a browser (in my tests I used Firefox).

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

Both the native import and the webpacked import should work the same. In particular, when `event` is imported through the webpacked version of the `the_event.mjs` module, the binding should be maintained and `42` should be returned, instead of `null`, which was the initial value of the underlying `event` variable.


**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.88.2
Node.js version: 16.19.1
Operating System: Arch Linux (`6.4.13-200.fc38.x86_64 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC`)
Additional tools: none

title: `experiments.css` is not equivalent to the `mini-css-extract-plugin` it's meant to replace
body:
# Bug report

`mini-css-extract-plugin` can be used to extract every imported stylesheets into a single file, without _also_ loading it. This is the default behavior of the plugin.

This behavior is absent from `experiments.css`.

**What is the current behavior?**

Using `experiments.css = true`:

- `import "./style.css"` creates `main.css` and it will attempt to load the file
- `new  URL("./style.css", import.meta.url)` creates multiple `[hash].css` files and it won't attempt to load them

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

You can find the source and `dist` results in https://github.com/fregante/webpack-sandbox/tree/main/experiments.css

In short, the config is just `{experiments: {css: true}}`

**What is the expected behavior?**

It should match the behavior of the previous plugin, which just created a unified `main.css` file and _did not attempt to load it_. As a bonus, the resulting `main.js` is [completely empty](https://github.com/fregante/webpack-sandbox/tree/main/experiments.css/dist-import-mini-css-extract):


```js
module: {
	rules: [
		{
			test: /\.css$/,
			use: [
				MiniCssExtractPlugin.loader,
				'css-loader',
			],
		},
	]
},
plugins: [
	new MiniCssExtractPlugin(),
],
```

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.88.2
Node.js version: 18
Operating System: macOS
Additional tools:


_Originally reported in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/14893#issuecomment-1704046657_

title: loader importModule api breaks webpack silently
body:
# Bug report **What is the current behavior?** Using `this.importModule` on a `.js` file causes webpack to exit without output and without error. https://webpack.js.org/api/loaders/#thisimportmodule ## 1. Create a new project with the following 4 files: loader.js ```js module.exports = async function (source) { const config = await this.importModule(this.rootContext + "/config.js"); result = source.replace("hello", config.text); return result; }; ``` config.js (the file loader by loader.js) ```js export const text = "Greetings" ``` index.js (the file to be compiled) ```js console.log("hello WORLD!") ``` webpack.config.js ```js module.exports = { entry: "./index.js", module: { rules: [ { test: /\.js$/, use: [ { loader: require.resolve("./loader.js"), }, ], }, ], }, } ``` ## Run webpack `npx webpack` ## Result: `webpack-cli` **exits without any error and without generating any file** `webpack-dev-server` freezes in ` [webpack-dev-middleware] wait until bundle finished: /` **What is the expected behavior?** - webpack should show an error or create a bundled file - importModule should provide an easy way to exclude the current loader **Other relevant information:** the problem seems to be an endless recursion: 1. webpack loads `index.js` 2. webpack calls `loader.js` 3. the loader calls importModule on `config.js` 4. import module calls `loader.js` 5. goto step 3
webpack --version ``` System: OS: macOS 13.4 CPU: (10) arm64 Apple M1 Max Memory: 140.73 MB / 64.00 GB Binaries: Node: 18.16.0 Yarn: 1.22.19 npm: 9.5.1 Browsers: Brave Browser: 116.1.57.64 Chrome: 116.0.5845.187 Chrome Canary: 119.0.6028.0 Safari: 16.5 Safari Technology Preview: 17.0 Packages: webpack: ^5.88.2 => 5.88.2 webpack-cli: ^5.1.4 => 5.1.4 webpack-dev-server: ^4.15.1 => 4.15.1 ```
Please let me know if I can be of any help to understand, reproduce or fix this problem

title: Superclass replaced with `null` in production build
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When building the following JavaScript file with Webpack in production mode, it generates invalid code that throws a `TypeError` at runtime:
```javascript
var SuperClass = class {};

var UnusedClass = class extends SuperClass {
    constructor() {
      super();
    }
  },
  unusedVariable = new UnusedClass();
```
The resulting code looks like this:
```javascript
new class extends(null){constructor(){super()}};
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

See https://github.com/MattiasBuelens/webpack-minify-issue for a full reproduction case, or [open it on Stackblitz](https://stackblitz.com/github/MattiasBuelens/webpack-minify-issue?file=src%2Findex.js&terminal=).

Run:
```
npm install
npm run build
npm run start
```
Then open the resulting app in a web browser. You should see the text "Running test...", and the following error will appear in the browser console:
```
Uncaught TypeError: Super constructor null of anonymous class is not a constructor
    at new document.body.innerText (main.js:1:81)
    at main.js:1:43
```

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

You should see the text "Test succeeded!" in the web browser. The resulting app should not throw a `TypeError`. 

The app works fine when building in development mode, so changing `mode` to `"development"` in `webpack.config.js` demonstrates the expected correct behavior.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.88.2
Node.js version: 18.18.0
Operating System: Windows 11 Pro 22H2 (build 22621.2283)
Additional tools: N/A

**Initial investigation:**
If you set [`optimization.minimize`](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/optimization/#optimizationminimize) = `false`, then you get:
```javascript
/******/ (() => { // webpackBootstrap
var __webpack_exports__ = {};

var SuperClass = class {};

var UnusedClass = class extends (/* unused pure expression or super */ null && (SuperClass)) {
    constructor() {
      super();
    }
  },
  unusedVariable = new UnusedClass();

/******/ })()
;
```

The "unused pure expression or super" comes from [PureExpressionDependency.js](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/v5.88.2/lib/dependencies/PureExpressionDependency.js#L129-L133), so the bug is probably somewhere around there.

Also interesting: if you split the variable declaration for `UnusedClass` and `unusedVariable` into two separate `var` statements, the bug does not show up.
```javascript
var SuperClass = class {};

var UnusedClass = class extends SuperClass {
    constructor() {
        super();
    }
};
var unusedVariable = new UnusedClass();
```

title: Promise is resolved when retrying an import that threw an error
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

- Given a file `otherModule.js` that has a runtime exception throughout the file
- When importing another module through `import('./otherModule.js')`
  - The first time it's imported, the promise returned by import rejects with the runtime exception of `otherModule.js`
  - The subsequent times it's imported, the promise resolves with the partially-incomplete module, without throwing the error.
- When using the imported module
  - The variables exported before the exception work correctly
  - The variables exported after the exception throw a different error (`Cannot access {{randomSymbol}} before initialisation`)

This is relevant to us because we have a retry logic for when a module can't be loaded (e.g. due to network error), but if the promise is resolved on retry, the exception raised it's a different from the original one, which makes it hard to debug what was the cause of it.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

https://github.com/voliva/webpack-import-retry-issue

This repo contains:
* src/module.js: The file to be imported. It exports a variable, then it executes an undefined function (throws error), then it exports another variable.
* src/index.js: The index file. It tries to load `module.js`, on failure it will log it failed and will schedule a retry after 1 second. Once the load succeeds, then it runs both functions from `module.js`
* native/: Folder that includes an ESM website that does the same, to compare it against the default behaviour from ESM.

Steps to reproduce:
* Build the project
* Run an http server on /dist folder
* Open the console to see what's going on:
  * The try {} catch log will say the load failed and will retry
  * After one second
  * It runs the function before the exception successfully
  * The second function throws an exception "Cannot access {{randomSymbol}} before initialisation" 

**What is the expected behavior?**

On retry it should throw the original exception and keep on looping on retry. This is what happens currently with the "Native" implementation in Chrome.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.88.2
Node.js version: 18.17.1
Operating System: MacOS 14.0
Additional tools: N/A

This happens either with `module: true` in Output (experimental) or without it, and with both `mode: "development"` and production.
author: voliva

title: NormalModuleReplacementPlugin doesn't resolve relative imports of also replaced modules within replaced modules anymore in webpack 5
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Apologies for the cryptic title but I'm not sure how to describe this better. I fear what follows below might not be much easier to understand either, sadly.

We're using `NormalModuleReplacementPlugin` in element-web to replace files within matrix-react-sdk (which is in `node_modules`) with files from element-web: https://github.com/vector-im/element-web/blob/develop/webpack.config.js#L79 (the replacement map is read from https://github.com/vector-im/element-web/blob/develop/components.json)

Some of the replaced-with files import other replaced-with files. E.g. `VectorAuthPage` from element-web (which replaces `AuthPage` from matrix-react-sdk) imports `VectorAuthFooter` from element-web (which replaces `AuthFooter` from matrix-react-sdk): https://github.com/vector-im/element-web/blob/develop/src/components/views/auth/VectorAuthPage.tsx#L20

This has worked fine up until the latest current version of Webpack 4 but stopped working in Webpack 5. With:

```
new webpack.NormalModuleReplacementPlugin(
	new RegExp(oldPath.replace(/\//g, "[\\/\\\\]").replace(/\./g, "\\.")),
	path.resolve(__dirname, newPath))
```

we're now hitting this error

```
[webpack-cli] ModuleNotFoundError: Module not found: Error: Can't resolve './VectorAuthFooter' in '/Users/jm/Code/element-web/node_modules/matrix-react-sdk/src/components/views/auth'
```

Note how it tries to look for `VectorAuthFooter` (which is imported from `VectorAuthPage` and in element-web) in `matrix-react-sdk/src/components/views/auth` (which is the parent directory of the replaced file `AuthFooter` in matrix-react-sdk).

To get it to work, we have to manually set the context now as in:

```
new webpack.NormalModuleReplacementPlugin(
	new RegExp(oldPath.replace(/\//g, "[\\/\\\\]").replace(/\./g, "\\.")),
	function (resource) {
	    resource.request = path.resolve(__dirname, newPath);
	    resource.createData.resource = path.resolve(__dirname, newPath);
	    console.log(resource)
	    // Starting with Webpack 5 we also need to set the context as otherwise replacing
	    // files in e.g. matrix-react-sdk with files from element-web will try to resolve
	    // them within matrix-react-sdk
	    /resource.context = path.dirname(resource.request)
	    resource.createData.context = path.dirname(resource.createData.resource);
	  }
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->

<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

–

**What is the expected behavior?**

Setting `context` manually should not be necessary.

title: CommonJS imports should preserve chained expressions
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

Similar to Issue #17218, chained calls off of commonjs imports are completely rewritten by webpack.

e.g.,

```
const ww = require('./es6-module1').obj1["bing"].bang;
```

generates

```
const ww = (__webpack_require__(/*! ./es6-module1 */ 960).obj1.bing.bang);
```

Some optimizing compilers such as the Google Closure Compiler ADVANCED mode and Terser with keep_quoted enabled have a problem with this rewrite. They use the quote notation as a way to say, "hands off" when it comes to minifying variable names so that external interfaces can be preserved. This leads to it seeing `x["somevar"]` and `x.somevar` as two different properties, such that `x.somevar` is minified and `x["somevar"]` is converted to `x.somevar` (shorter by one char but not minified). [See the Closure docs](https://developers.google.com/closure/compiler/docs/api-tutorial3#propnames).

**What is the expected behavior?**

The expression after the imported object (obj1 in this case) should remain untouched. That is,

```
const ww = (__webpack_require__(/*! ./es6-module1 */ 960).obj1)["bing"].bang;
```

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

Get webpack 5 to generate output compatible with the Google Closure Compiler.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Factor the code made in #17203 `HarmonyImportSpecifierDependency.js` into a shared location and call it from `CommonJsFullRequireDependency.js` as well.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**

Yes. See PR #17718.

title: Doesn't work for workspace / common dependencies.
body:
# Bug report

As far as I can tell webpack currently doesn't work very well in monorepos where projects depend on each other.

**What is the current behavior?**

Assume you have 2 packages in your monorepo

 - `monorepo/packages/common`
 - `monorepo/packages/project`        <- Webpack project that has `common` as a dependency

The monorepo/package.json has
```
"workspaces": [
  "packages/common",
  "packages/project",
],
```

and `monorepo/packages/project/package.json` has

```
"dependencies": {
  "@monorepo/common": "1.0.0",
}
```

As part of the build process webpack will create a package.json in `.webpack/dependencies` with the minimal dependencies it needs and runs `yarn install --non-interactive --frozen-lockfile`. Now because `monorepo/packages/common/.webpack/dependencies` isn't in the list of workspaces in `monorepo/package.json` yarn will not look for  `@monorepo/common` in the workspace, and instead tries to find it on yarnpkg.org, where it doesn't exist, resulting in `
error An unexpected error occurred: "https://registry.yarnpkg.com/@monorepo/common: Not found".`.

You can get around it by adding the webpack directory, but that seems very hacky / brittle

```
"workspaces": [
  "packages/common",
  "packages/project",
  "packages/common/.webpack/dependencies"        <- new
],
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

See steps above

**What is the expected behavior?**

Webpack bundling should work in a monorepo with shared dependencies

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  webpack@5.88.2
Node.js version: v18.16.0
Yarn version: v1.22.19
Operating System: Mac OS Ventura 13.4.1
Additional tools: 

-----

NB: I'm doing this through the Serverless Framework, so there's a chance it's specific to that, although from what I can tell it's a webpack issue.
author: foges

title: Webpack 5 output with "commonjs2" and "externals" can't be loaded by Webpack 4
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

When using Webpack5 with `library.type` of `commonjs2` and `externals` set, the resulting output cannot be consumed by Webpack 4.

The app doesn't load, and the following error appears in dev tools:

```
index.js:45 Uncaught TypeError: __webpack_require__ is not a function
```

**Repro steps**

1. clone https://github.com/sslepian/webpack5-externals-bug and be in the root directory
2. run the following commands
    1. cd lib
    2. npm install
    3. npm build
    4. npm link
    5. cd ..
    6. cd app
    7. npm install
    8. npm link @sslepian/webpack5-lib
    9. npm run dev
3. navigate to http://localhost:8081/

*** Additional details ***

Looking at `lib/dist/index.js`, we see the following line at the start:

```
var __webpack_require__ = {};
```

This overwrites the `__webpack_require__` global that Webpack 4 expects, which leads to the error detailed above.

Setting minimzation in `lib/webpack.config.js` as follows will avoid this issue:

```
optimization: {
    minimize: true,
}
```

However, it's not actually necessary to minimize - simply renaming the `__webpack_require__` var in `lib/dist/index.js` will likewise fix the issue, since it will no longer override the Webpack 4 global.


**What is the expected behavior?**

The app should load with no errors in the console and output "Webpack 4 App Loaded!" in the console.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.88.2 for the lib, 4.47.0 for the app
Node.js version: 14
Operating System: macOS 
Additional tools:

title: `optimization.innerGraph` creates invalid code for class extends expressions
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

The InnerGraph plugin creates invalid JS for class extends clauses.  Given a class like this where `ExtendedError` is an unused export

```
export class ExtendedError extends Error {
    constructor(message = 'ExtendedError') {
        super(message);
    }
}
```

it will produce this

```
class ExtendedError extends (/* unused pure expression or super */ null && (Error)) {
    constructor(message = 'ExtendedError') {
        super(message);
    }
}
```

which is effectively

```
class ExtendedError extends null { ...
```

This is a problem when running the resulting code through the Closure Compiler minifier when targeting ES5 because based on that input, Closure will generate this code:

```
$$jscomp$$.$inherits$($ExpectedError$$, null);
```

instead of this code:

```
$$jscomp$$.$inherits$($ExpectedError$$, Error);
```

which leads to a null ref error inside the `inherits` function when it tries to access `null.prototype` instead of `Error.prototype`.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

#17739  has a repro.  You will have to look in the bundle0.js generated by the test to see the issue.

**What is the expected behavior?**

When emitting code, you have to assume it will be processed by other tools or plugins so it must be valid JS.  So webpack should either emit 

```
class ExtendedError extends Error { ...
```

or not emit the `ExtendedError` class at all.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.88.2
Node.js version: 16.20.1
Operating System: Windows 11 22H2 (Build 22621.2428)
Additional tools: n/a

I've looked through the release notes for that version, but I can't see anything that could explain this breakage.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I've created a simple reproduction in [the plugin repo](https://github.com/iconduit/html-webpack-plugin):

- `git clone git@github.com:iconduit/html-webpack-plugin.git`
- `cd html-webpack-plugin`
- `git switch webpack-5-78-repro`
- `npm ci`
- `cd test/integration/simple`
- `npx webpack`

You can also reproduce the (working) behavior under `5.77.0` by using the `webpack-5-77-repro` branch:

- `git clone git@github.com:iconduit/html-webpack-plugin.git`
- `cd html-webpack-plugin`
- `git switch webpack-5-77-repro`
- `npm ci`
- `cd test/integration/simple`
- `npx webpack`

I've also verified that problem is reproducible under GitHub Actions. See these workflow runs for working and non-working results:

- https://github.com/iconduit/html-webpack-plugin/actions/runs/6516561925/job/17700204695#step:5:10 (not working under `5.78.0`)
- https://github.com/iconduit/html-webpack-plugin/actions/runs/6516564935/job/17700211008#step:5:10 (working under `5.77.0`)

My Webpack config is pretty simple: 

```js
const HtmlPlugin = require("html-webpack-plugin");
const IconduitHtmlPlugin = require("../../../src/index.js");

module.exports = {
  mode: "development",
  plugins: [
    new HtmlPlugin(),
    new IconduitHtmlPlugin({
      manifestPath: "./src/assets/site.iconduitmanifest",
    }),
  ],
  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.(png|svg|xml|webmanifest)$/,
        type: "asset/resource",
      },
    ],
  },
  output: {
    assetModuleFilename: "[name].[contenthash][ext][query]",
  },
};
```

What I think is happening, is that my plugin is adding a `.xml` and `.webmanifest` asset to the build, but despite the above module rules that should match these and mark them as `asset/resource`, the rules are ignored for some reason.

It's also completely possible that I'm doing something the wrong way in my plugin. Any help would be greatly appreciated 🙏

**What is the expected behavior?**

The Webpack build should succeed. In particular, it seems like the `rules` section of the Webpack config is being ignored for assets that are emitted by the plugin.

title: The production build is not working.
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

The webpack inner semver function computed result is different from the npm package semver , and the webpack inner function result seems not right.

**What is the current behavior?**

Running the function satisfy from [semver]( https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/util/semver.js)  and [parseRange](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/util/semver.js#L91) results in a possible bug. Specifically:

```js
satisfy(parseRange('~1'), '1.2.3') === false
```

**What is the expected behavior?**
```js
satisfy(parseRange('~1'), '1.2.3') === true
```

**Other relevant information:**
the result is not the same with [semver](https://www.npmjs.com/package/semver)
```js
const semver = require('semver');
semver.satisfies('1.2.3','~1') === true
```
author: 2heal1

title: Webpack build's [contenthash] diverges from build to build
body: # Bug report **What is the current behavior?** I am noticing that the contenthash on my production builds is not consistent from build to build. A few observations: 1. Sometimes contenthash is consistent for 2 builds, and other times it is consistent for 5, and so on and so forth. Typically the first two builds are inconsistent with one another, and subsequent builds are consistent, but this is not always the case. 2. The offending files typically appear in a chunk tree that is imported by workers, i.e. `new Worker(new URL('...', import.meta.url));`. 3. I attempted to reproduce the issue without thread-loader, babel-loader, and `mode="production"`, but I was unable to do so. I was also unable to reproduce the issue with a significantly less complex source tree. That is not to say that the issue cannot be produced under these conditions, only that 30 rebuilds did not reproduce the issue for me. 4. The offending files contain (or import a tree which contains) minified variable names which differ. This appears to be the only difference. Since the build is not deterministic, my production builds are not reproducible, and the contenthash breaks caching. Additionally, in this case it appears that the output is functionally identical, but in another case my webpack configuration had a bug (introduced by me) that created functional differences in the build output. Because an inconsistent contenthash is often produced, the the bug I introduced was harder to detect, as I could not simply diff filenames as a sanity check. I have created a repository with a minimal reproduction - https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757. In my experience the issue is typically reproduced within 5 builds, but sometimes more are required. Here is an example diff of an offending file: **What is the expected behavior?** If no build inputs have changed, all filenames and file content should be identical from build to build. **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.89.0 Node.js version: 18.18.2 Operating System: Ubuntu 20.04.6 LTS running docker "node:18" image Additional tools: None

Hello! #16544 was closed and marked as "Ready To Merge", but I don't think any work was done toward resolving it. I can at least be sure that no work was done that referenced the issue itself.

In the discussion on the issue, it was acknowledged as a bug (at least that's how I understood [this comment](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/16544#issuecomment-1516727562)), but one which may be difficult to resolve.

We have done a lot of work to get around the issue, but it still persists, and still causes us some problems occasionally. I'm making this new issue because I would hate for the discussion on #16544 to be lost and for the original concern never to be addressed.

title: Seeking For A Permanent Fix: NodeJs-18.17.1: ERR_OSSL_EVP_UNSUPPORTED
body:
Hi,
          Can anybody help on the permanent fix on the below issue?

Our application is throwing error 'ERR_OSSL_EVP_UNSUPPORTED' with below configuration. From the nodejs [documentation](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/doc/changelogs/CHANGELOG_V17.md#2021-10-19-version-1700-current-bethgriggs) we came to know that webpack 4.x is still trying to use an algorithm or key size which is no longer allowed by default with OpenSSL 3.0 and flag '--openssl-legacy-provider' can be used(to fix this error) as a temporary solution. Since its only a temporary solution and as a permanent fix, webpack module need to be upgrade.

Electron: 27.0.3
NodeJs: 18.17.1
webpack: 4.29.5

Can anybody share the webpack version which has the fix for this issue, if its already fixed. If not then is there a plan to fix this issue on webpack module and when the fix will be released?

Also in one of the [comment](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/14532#issuecomment-947525539), we read that it will be fixed with "webpack 6" which is not yet released, any timelines for its release?

title: Files marked sideEffects are missing from webpack build products.
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

Files marked sideEffects are missing from webpack build products.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

This is minimal github repository to reproduce: https://github.com/jiesia/webpack-side-effects-bug. And here I give a rough description.

Suppose there is a package named `package`, and sideEffects is declared in its package.json:

```json
{
  "name": "package",
  "sideEffects": [
    "has-side-effects.js"
  ]
}
```

The entry file of this package exports two packages, one with sideEffects and one without sideEffects:

```js
export { sideEffects } from './has-side-effects';
export { noSideEffects } from './no-side-effects';
```

When the code using this package only imports  `noSideEffects`, the module `has-side-effects.js` is not packaged into the final product. As a result, the side-effect code is not executed

**What is the expected behavior?**

The build artifact contains side-effect code for module `has-side-effects.js`.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.89.0
Node.js version: 20.9.0
Operating System: mac m1 pro

title: Aggregate errors or errors with cause are not logged
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

If an [`AggregateError`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/AggregateError) or `Error` with [`cause`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Error/cause) is thrown, Webpack intercepts and only logs the primary message.  For these cases, that message is often just an annotation on the real error, making it impossible to debug them from a Webpack plugin.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Throw an instance of `AggregateError` or an instance of `Error` with a `cause` property from any plugin, and then just examine the console output.

- For `AggregateError`, each of the `errors` should be logged
- For `Error` with `cause`, the `cause` should be logged as well

title: Module Federation with library.type (window) is not working
body:
What is the current behavior?

I currently have three applications. Due to the need to integrate with micro-frontends, JavaScript needs to be loaded within a scoped environment, so it's necessary to set 'library.type' to 'window'. When there are two applications (where the first application provides components, and the second application only uses components without providing any), setting the 'library.type' of the provider to 'window' meets the expectations. However, when the second application starts providing components to the outside, setting its 'library.type' to 'window' leads to issues. The second component fails to find the components from the first application when attempting to load them.

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/42717106/657aaab7-946f-4bff-8762-ee592400f5f3)


What is the expected behavior?

The expectation is that the components provided by multiple applications can be accessed through mounting on the 'window'.
I have provided a simple repository with [reproducible code.](https://github.com/OnlyYu1996/wujie-federation-demo)

title: Memory leak from child compilation in watch mode (WeakMap references)
body: Note: this is very similar to https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/17857 (another child compiler memory leak), but these are two separate leaks. This one is related to references in WeakMaps from `_lastCompilation`'s children not being removed. # Bug report **What is the current behavior?** Child compiler compilations are not completely cleaned up during watch mode, causing a memory leak. **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** I created a minimal reproduction in this repo: https://github.com/helloitsjoe/webpack-memory-leak, which includes a simple webpack config, `index.js` and `HtmlWebpackPlugin` (which creates a child compiler). I initially thought the leak was in `HtmlWebpackPlugin` but I have traced the leak to Webpack, I'll have a PR ready for review shortly. I believe this is related to https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/13127#issuecomment-872019502, that issue was closed due to inactivity but appears unresolved. **Repro steps:** 1. `git clone https://github.com/helloitsjoe/webpack-memory-leak.git` 2. `yarn` 3. Run `webpack serve` in inspect mode: `NODE_OPTIONS=--inspect $(yarn bin webpack) serve` 4. Open a memory profiler, for example `chrome://inspect` in Chrome 5. Take a heap snapshot 6. Save `index.template.html`, which will cause `HtmlWebpackPlugin` to create a child compiler. Each time you save, memory should grow ~2mb 8. Take a second heap snapshot and notice the difference in size Some of the child compilation objects aren't being garbage collected. Inspecting the diff between heap snapshots you can see a number of object instances are added on each recompilation. Notice these in particular: `Compilation`, `JavascriptParser`, `FileSystemInfo`, `NormalModuleFactory`, `HookMap`, `Hook`. Instances of these objects (and others) should remain constant no matter how many recompilations are triggered. Instead we see new instances added on each recompilation. Note that you must modify `index.template.html` to see this behavior, since this creates a new child compiler in `HtmlWebpackPlugin`. Modifying `index.js` will not produce this leak. **Heap diff:** **What is the expected behavior?** Memory should remain stable on each recompilation during watch mode. 2mb per recompilation isn't large in absolute terms, but I discovered this leak on a repo where each recompilation adds ~20mb, causing an out-of-memory crash before too long. **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.89.0 Node.js version: 20.5.1 Operating System: MacOS (darwin 22.5.0) Additional tools: `HtmlWebpackPlugin`

title: Memory leak from child compilation in watch mode (`Sources` strings)
body:
# Bug report NOTE: This issue is very similar to https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/17851 (child compilations), but these are two separate leaks. This one is related to duplicate strings added to the in-memory cache. **What is the current behavior?** When running in watch mode, Webpack caches source code from child compilations (e.g. `HtmlWebpackPlugin`) without removing them:
Duplicated strings in memory
Retained Sources
The leak is worse when we have build-time dependencies, as in the reproduction repo:
Duplicated build-time dependencies source
**Investigation done** One place these are being cached is [in a `Map` in `MemoryCachePlugin`](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/87660921808566ef3b8796f8df61bd79fc026108/lib/cache/MemoryCachePlugin.js#L27). When a child compiler caches them it uses the child compiler ID, for example (note the `HtmlWebpackPlugin_0-7`, the 7th time I ran a recompilation): ``` 'HtmlWebpackCompiler|0|Compilation/codeGeneration|javascript/esm|data:text/javascript,__webpack_public_path__ = __webpack_base_uri__ = htmlWebpackPluginPublicPath;|HtmlWebpackPlugin_0-7' ``` This seems problematic because entry will never be found, since the key uses an ID unique to each iteration. **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** Minimal reproduction repo including steps to reproduce here: https://github.com/helloitsjoe/webpack-cache-memory-leak **What is the expected behavior?** Dependency source code should be cached only once (or maybe twice including the child compilation). A few potential options: 1. Remove the child compiler sources from the cache during `_cleanupLastCompilation`, but I don't see a way to clear entries from the `MemoryCache` map currently. 2. Only cache compiler sources for the parent compilation, or replace the previous child compilation's cached sources. I'm also not sure `MemoryCache` is the only place the sources are being referenced, so fixing this leak might require more changes. I'm happy to help however I can. **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.89.0 Node.js version: 20.5.1 Operating System: MacOS (darwin 22.5.0) Additional tools: `HtmlWebpackPlugin` (child compiler), custom loader in https://github.com/helloitsjoe/webpack-cache-memory-leak to cache build-time dependency sources

title: module.buildInfo.fileDependencies is undefined in webpack 5
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

I am migrating an app from webpack 4 to webpack 5 and I'm having an issue with a plugin that uses `module.buildInfo.fileDependencies` which is undefined in webpack 5 (it was fine in webpack 4).

Following [this guide](https://webpack.js.org/contribute/plugin-patterns/#exploring-assets-chunks-modules-and-dependencies) in the documentation, I've reproduced it in a minimal example, with the following `webpack.config.js`:

```js
class MyPlugin {
  apply(compiler) {
    compiler.hooks.emit.tapAsync('MyPlugin', (compilation, callback) => {
      compilation.chunks.forEach((chunk) => {
        compilation.chunkGraph.getChunkModules(chunk).forEach((module) => {

          // This is undefined in webpack 5 (it was defined in webpack 4)
          console.log(module.buildInfo.fileDependencies);
        });
      });

      callback();
    });
  }
}


export default {
  mode: 'production',
  entry: './index.js',
  plugins: [new MyPlugin()],
}
```

If I log the entire `module.buildInfo` object, here is what I get:
```js

    _cachedMissingIterable: SnapshotIterable {

      getMaps: [Function (anonymous)]                                 
    },                                                                
    startTime: 1699455586802,                                         
    fileTimestamps: undefined,                                        
    fileHashes: undefined,                                            
    fileTshs: Map(1) {                                                
      'C:\\coding\\stubhub\\webpack-test\\index.js' => [Object]       
    },                                                                
    contextTimestamps: undefined,

title: Shared Module Not Available for Eager Consumption
body: ## Description ### Context I have a React application initially created with Create React App (CRA), where I modify the Webpack configuration using CRACO. Additionally, I have developed a simple Microfrontend (MF) application that directly utilizes Webpack for its setup. ### Issue I'm facing an issue related to Module Federation in this setup. The problem occurs when I try to share modules between my CRA application (configured with CRACO) and the separate MF application. The specific challenge is an error indicating difficulties with the eager consumption of a shared module. The error message I encounter is: `Uncaught Error: Shared module is not available for eager consumption: webpack/sharing/consume/default/react/react` ### Troubleshooting Efforts - I have extensively searched through many discussions, forums, and Webpack's official documentation, trying out various suggested solutions, but none have resolved the issue. - I've carefully configured the shared dependencies in both the CRA and MF applications. - I've confirmed the successful loading and response of remoteEntry.js from the MF application. - I've experimented with various configurations, including removing the eager flag, in an attempt to solve this issue. ### Environment **Container app** - React version: 18.2.0 - CRA version: 5.0.1 - CRACO version: 7.1.0 **MF app** - React version: 18.2.0 - Webpack version: 5.89.0 ### Project Structure and Code Samples **Container app** _craco.config.js_ _App.jsx_ **MF app** _structure_ _webpack.config.js_ _index.js_ _bootstrap.js_ _App.jsx_ Also I would like to share remoteEntry.js response

title: Webpack 5 is tree shaking all modules required using require.resolve
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Webpack marks all the modules as  **unused harmony export** which gets tree shaken on the prod bundle

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

Most of the code is taken from this repo for reproduction - https://github.com/bradtraversy/webpack-starter/tree/main/src

**Changes Made**

- Added a **testMethod.js** file 
```
export default function testMethod() {
  console.log("test method called");
}

```

- Added a **registry.js** file with following content
```
export default {
  testMethodModule: require.resolve("./testMethod"),
};
```
- Added following code to **generateJoke.js**

```
import registry from "./registry";
  
...
const testMethod = __webpack_require__(registry.testMethodModule);
  console.log(testMethod);
```

- Modified webpack.config.js to the following 

```
mode: "production",
  entry: {
    bundle: path.resolve(__dirname, "src/index.js"),
  },
  output: {
    path: path.resolve(__dirname, "dist"),
    filename: "[name][contenthash].js",
    clean: true,
    assetModuleFilename: "[name][ext]",
  },
  devtool: "source-map",
  devServer: {
    static: {
      directory: path.resolve(__dirname, "dist"),
    },
    port: 4000,
    open: true,
    hot: true,
    compress: true,
    historyApiFallback: true,
  },
  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.js$/,
        exclude: /node_modules/,
        use: {
          loader: "babel-loader",
          options: {
            presets: ["@babel/preset-env"],
          },
        },
      },
    ],
  },
  plugins: [
    new HtmlWebpackPlugin({
      title: "Webpack App",
      filename: "index.html",
      template: "src/template.html",
    }),
    new BundleAnalyzerPlugin(),
  ],
  optimization: {
    minimize: false,
  },
```

When I run webpack dev server `NODE_ENV=production webpack serve`, following output is generated in bundle.js

```

/***/ 735:
/***/ ((__unused_webpack_module, __webpack_exports__, __webpack_require__) => {

"use strict";
/* unused harmony export default */
function testMethod() {
  console.log("test method called");
}

/***/ }),
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

testMethod should not be marked as unused. This has broken in webpack 5 since the same app with webpack 4 produces the below output.

```
/***/ "./src/testMethod.js":
/*!***************************!*\
  !*** ./src/testMethod.js ***!
  \***************************/
/*! exports provided: default */
/***/ (function(module, __webpack_exports__, __webpack_require__) {

"use strict";
__webpack_require__.r(__webpack_exports__);
/* harmony export (binding) */ __webpack_require__.d(__webpack_exports__, "default", function() { return testMethod; });
function testMethod() {
  console.log("test method called");
}

/***/ }),

```

We rely heavily on this feature to register modules in our library.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.88.2
Node.js version: 20.8.0
Operating System: Mac OS ventura 13.6.2
Additional tools:
author: sachetsharma1

title: Web Workers in a project with `experiments.outputModule = true` call `importScripts` despite being loaded with `{type: "module"}`
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

In a project with `experiments.outputModule = true`, the following code:
```js
// main code
new Worker(new URL("<worker path>", import.meta.url))
```
```js
// worker code
import { Stuff } from "some-really-big-module-that-gets-its-own-chunk"
```

will generate a main file that calls `new Worker("<processed URL>", { type: "module" })`, but a worker that calls `importScripts()` to load `some-really-big-module-that-gets-its-own-chunk`. This results in an error when the worker is loaded.

**Reproduce**

A minimum viable example may be found at https://github.com/gingershaped/webworker-mre.

**What is the expected behavior?**

Workers loaded in a project under these conditions should only use ES module imports to load dependencies.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.89.0
Node.js version: 18.13.0
Operating System: Debian Bookworm
Additional tools: None
author: gingershaped

title: watchDirectory option for watchOptions
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

To be able to add multiple custom directories to "watch" other than "assets".

We have "ignored" option but not the the opposite.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

I explained with more details here: https://github.com/symfony/webpack-encore/issues/1246

But it is also related to webpack, without using any external plugin which are not maintainted, like: https://github.com/Fridus/webpack-watch-files-plugin

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

```javascript
module.exports = {
  // other webpack configurations...

  watchOptions: {
    aggregateTimeout: 300, // Delay in milliseconds before rebuilding
    poll: 1000, // Check for changes every second
    // Specify the directory you want to watch
    // You can use an absolute or relative path
    watchDirectory: "customfoler/**/*.php",
  },
};
```

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

body:

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**
Currently, we are using webpack through the Electron React Boilerplate. We are trying to use sentry. 
One of the sentry files references:

Which is:
`const [major] = __WEBPACK_MODULE_REFERENCE__145_5b22474c4f42414c5f4f424a225d_call_asiSafe1__._ ).process.versions.node.split('.').map(Number);`

[0] |

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

1. Install electron react boilerplate and get setup
2. Install sentry through the sentry webpack method
3. Run 'npm run package'

It is expected to return the major number of node's version. When I change the dynamic reference in sentry's source files to `const [major] = 18;` it works fine.

title: New case for "__webpack_modules__[moduleId] is not a function" error with Module Federation
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
With a certain dependency graph, webpack module federation applications crash with error:
```
__webpack_modules__[moduleId] is not a function
or Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'call')
```
This is indeed a known bug and had been fixed, but it happens again with module federation https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/11770
Issue describe:
* Folder structure:
```
/src
├── /components
│   ├── Button.js
├── /helpers
│   ├── index.js
├── /utils
│   ├── index.js
├── App.js
```
* Dependency graph:

![example remote app](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/41647635/955ec8c3-7751-485e-a17e-f3ca847beb50)

* Bundle result: 

![chunking result](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/41647635/a49bd137-2474-48ff-a5cc-a23ae184b5de)

* What is the problem:
`./src/utils/index.jsx` module is declared in `src_helpers_index_js` but required in `src_comopnent_Button_js`, so in runtime, it cannot find the module

Like I mentioned, this is a known issue and @sokra had fixed it in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/11770. But he didn't handle for module federation case, because in this case, two federated modules `./RemoteApp` and `./helpers` are using the same runtime id

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
https://github.com/tqnghia1998/webpack-chunking-error-demo  (latest webpack version, just yarn install, then yarn start)

No runtime errors, `./src/utils/index.jsx` should be initialized when loading RemoteApp
