title: runtime.js cannot find bundles to require when code splitting among multiple bundles is enabled
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

I have encountered a bug while trying to split code into multiple bundles, while preventing 
duplication of said code. In other words, I want to create a "main" bundle which is the "true" 
entry point of the application, and one or more auxiliary bundles. To this end, I have followed
the documentation [present at this page](https://webpack.js.org/guides/code-splitting/#prevent-duplication).

**What is the current behavior?**
The following is the config object contained in my `webpack.config.js` file:
```
const config = {
    entry: {
        lib: {
            import: './src/foo.js',
        },
        main: {
            import: './src/index.js',
            dependOn: ['lib']
        }
    },
    output: {
        path: path.resolve(__dirname, 'dist'),
        filename: '[name].js',
    },
    target: 'node',
    optimization: {
        runtimeChunk: 'single',
    },
};
```

After launching the `npx webpack` command and executing `node .\dist\main.js`, node throws the following `Error`:
```
Error: Cannot find module './undefined'
Require stack:
- d:\svi\webpack-codesplit-test\dist\runtime.js
- d:\svi\webpack-codesplit-test\dist\main.js
    at Module._resolveFilename (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1077:15)
    at Module._load (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:922:27)
    at Module.require (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1143:19)
    at require (node:internal/modules/cjs/helpers:119:18)
    at __webpack_require__.f.require (d:\svi\webpack-codesplit-test\dist\runtime.js:110:28)
    at d:\svi\webpack-codesplit-test\dist\runtime.js:48:40
    at Array.reduce (<anonymous>)
    at __webpack_require__.e (d:\svi\webpack-codesplit-test\dist\runtime.js:47:67)
    at Array.map (<anonymous>)
    at __webpack_require__.X (d:\svi\webpack-codesplit-test\dist\runtime.js:74:22) {
  code: 'MODULE_NOT_FOUND',
  requireStack: [
    'd:\\svi\\webpack-codesplit-test\\dist\\runtime.js',
    'd:\\svi\\webpack-codesplit-test\\dist\\main.js'
  ]
}
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

I have written a simple test project that is [available at this GitHub repository](https://github.com/Arandhras/webpack-codesplit-test).
After downloading the project simply run `npm run dev` to get the required node_modules
(webpack, webpack-cli and their dependencies) and then automatically execute `node .\dist\main.js`.

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

It seems that `.\dist\runtime.js` is broken, specifically in two points.

```
/******/ 	/* webpack/runtime/get javascript chunk filename */
/******/ 	(() => {
/******/ 		// This function allow to reference async chunks and sibling chunks for the entrypoint
/******/ 		__webpack_require__.u = (chunkId) => {
/******/ 			// return url for filenames based on template
/******/ 			return undefined;
/******/ 		};
/******/ 	})();
```
Why is this function returning `undefined` regardless of the `chunkId` being passed?
In development mode, if I change the return line into
```
return `${chunkId}.js`
```
the `require()` that is immediately called after, is working.


Another issue I've identified, lies within this function:
```
/******/ 		var installChunk = (chunk) => {
/******/ 			var moreModules = chunk.modules, chunkIds = chunk.ids, runtime = chunk.runtime;
/******/ 			for(var moduleId in moreModules) {
/******/ 				if(__webpack_require__.o(moreModules, moduleId)) {
/******/ 					__webpack_require__.m[moduleId] = moreModules[moduleId];
/******/ 				}
/******/ 			}
/******/ 			if(runtime) runtime(__webpack_require__);
/******/ 			for(var i = 0; i < chunkIds.length; i++)
/******/ 				installedChunks[chunkIds[i]] = 1;
/******/ 		
/******/ 		};
```
Sometimes the `chunk` argument being passed is an empty object `{}`, therefore `chunkIds` is `undefined`.
In this case when the for loop is reached, accessing `chunkIds.length` results in an `Error` being thrown.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.91.0
Node.js version: v18.18.0
Operating System: Windows 10 Enterprise 22H2

title: Suggestion for improve the code splitting mechanism
body:
Hi, everyone)
The diagram illustrates the architecture and request flow:
![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/96373865/68186636-ce63-460e-9c78-d930c0fcbcbb)
We need a simple way to separate the JS bundle of the solution into different folders. This way, in the BFF, we can check the requested part of the FE by root: if the request is for the content of a folder, for example, '/secure/..', then we can verify at the BFF level whether the user is authenticated.
This is necessary so that we do not return the entire SPA with potentially sensitive information for unauthenticated users.
Do you plan to enhance the code splitting mechanism to specify the folder where js chunks should be placed?) Do you have any recommendations?)

body:
Before I explain the issue that I have in my case, here is my general info:
![webpack-cli INFO](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/107140611/3dc47c48-4d31-417c-b6f0-f12f76d30afc)

- And my Front-end web-pack server runs on: (URL: http://127.0.0.1:8080/bundle.js)
- Therefore my Project structure is something like this:

![project-struc](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/107140611/5aa63dae-4ab6-4421-812a-a7cc3ae99033)


**Now coming back to problem.**
So far everything was working fine, since I'm using web-pack V-5, I was aware that the url-loader and file-loader are depreciated, and instead asset/modules are introduced. Therefore, that's what I used, here is my webpack.config.js :

![webpackconfig](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/107140611/660f8187-c254-436c-a41e-a47578ae6734)

Even after that when I run my project I, couldn't load my Image to the project:

![Browser debugger image prob](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/107140611/a744987a-0042-4af9-a60c-6cf6d721de6b)

Now, here is what I found, the problem was never with web-pack or with my path inside my main.css file which has property(background-image) with url for the image. I found that the problem is with the dynamic URL with hash for image that is loaded by the web-pack, since I am using a different PORT ( 8080 ) for my bundle.js, the web-pack loads the file with URL without the port number, which creates a problem. Here, is how when I change or add port number to URL, image seems to be loading.

![Screenshot 2024-05-21 183755](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/107140611/9b3e44d4-a4d1-4b54-9ed9-6a43e71e65a7)

**Now here is my question:**

Is there a way I can give correct port to the web-pack so that it knows the correct URL to reach my asset ? or if not then what should be my approach, given that my back-end would remain on python, since I have specific use for the language. 

Thanks~

title: Source maps returned by loaders are ignored for asset modules
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

When a source file is bundled as an asset module (e.g. `asset/resource`) using one or more loaders that returns a source map, the source map is discarded. Some types of assets (such as CSS files transpiled from SCSS/LESS/etc.) benefit from having source maps, so it doesn't make sense to assume that no asset will ever have a source map.

For example, `sass-loader` can be used to produce `.css` asset files from `.scss` source files. (This is in contrast to using something like `css-loader`/`style-loader`...sometimes a large website may need to reference site-wide static styles instead of having a script import them.)

**What is the current behavior?**

Webpack assumes all modules with a type starting with `asset/` (such as `asset/resource`) are "binary", and discards any source maps returned by loaders for these modules.

In my case, `sass-loader` returns source maps generated by the SASS implementation, which `NormalModule.js` retrieves as such: 

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/ac8e83a4a2984ed977fd6c23a609da5cb43da492/lib/NormalModule.js#L851-L853

At this point, `source` is of type `string` (not `Buffer`), and `sourceMap` is a valid source map object.

However, several lines later, the `source` string gets converted to a `Buffer` because the constructor for `NormalModule` sets `this.binary` to `true` for all asset modules:

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/ac8e83a4a2984ed977fd6c23a609da5cb43da492/lib/NormalModule.js#L315
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/ac8e83a4a2984ed977fd6c23a609da5cb43da492/lib/NormalModule.js#L870-L875

This causes `createSource` to ignore the source map passed into it and return a `RawSource` for the buffer:

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/ac8e83a4a2984ed977fd6c23a609da5cb43da492/lib/NormalModule.js#L782-L796

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I can't provide my exact configuration due to proprietary company information, but this is reproducible with any Webpack configuration that uses SASS for asset modules.

- Set up `sass-loader` in a rule for modules with `type: "asset/resource"`. This will need the `sass-loader` and `sass-embedded` packages to be installed.
  ```js
  {
    test: /\.scss$/i,
    type: "asset/resource",
    use: [
      {
        loader: "sass-loader",
        options: {
          api: "modern",
          sourceMap: true,
          sassOptions: {
            sourceMapIncludeSources: true,
          },
        },
      },
    ],
  }
  ```
- Enable Webpack's source maps, e.g. with `devtool: "inline-source-map"`
- Provide a `.scss` file as an entry point
- Run the Webpack compiler
- View the `.css` file in the output directory and observe that it does not contain a source map file adjacent to it, nor does it contain an inline source map comment

**What is the expected behavior?**

Loaders for Asset Modules should be able to return a source map for certain types of assets, and these source maps should be included just like they would be for JavaScript modules instead of being unconditionally ignored.

If I change line 872 in `NormalModule.js` to the following, the source maps from SASS start working as expected:
```js
    (this.binary && !sourceMap) ? asBuffer(source) : asString(source),
```

Obviously this is not suitable as a one-size-fits-all fix; it's a hacky workaround that fixes the problem in my specific situation. Non-text assets (and even many text assets) would not be compatible with source maps (although I wouldn't expect loaders for these asset types to return a source map in the first place).

Perhaps there needs to be a way to opt into source maps on a per-module-rule basis, possibly in the `generator` settings? 

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.91.0
Node.js version: 20.13.1
Operating System: Windows 10 x64 (version 22H2)
Additional tools:
- `sass-loader = 14.2.1`
- `sass-embedded = 1.77.2`
author: ArcanoxDragon

title: CSS Modules: Nested CSS Classes
body:
## Feature request

When importing CSS Modules, [nested classes](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Nesting_selector) are not supported.

```css
.foo {
    color: blue;

    .bar {
        color: red;
    }
}
```

```javascript
import styles from "styles.module.css";

console.log(styles.bar);
```

Current Console Output:
```
> undefined
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

```
> src-styles-module-css__bar
```

Or something similar.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

[CSS Nesting](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/CSS_nesting) has been available in all major browsers since December 2023. This ticket is to track the implementation to bring Webpack up to spec.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Nested CSS classes should be treated the same way as top-level classes.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**

Unfortunately, I don't have the capacity to right now :(

title: Allow custom ModuleDependency by exposing `webpack/lib/util/makeSerializable`
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

**What is the expected behavior?**

I need `webpack/lib/util/makeSerializable` exposed as part of the public/top-level API surface to be able to implement a custom ModuleDependency. 

In a standard setup I might be able to import the submodule directly when installed via NPM, but frameworks like next.js ship a bundled version of webpack where these nested files are not accessible anymore.  

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

As a plugin author I am creating custom `ModuleDependency` to add new functionalities to WebPack like a specialized WebWorker and Audio Worklet translation. 

WebPack internally uses [ModuleDependencies heavily](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/tree/main/lib/dependencies) to implement features. e.g. looking at the `WorkerPlugin` it [registers](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/400db90db807d630b6c9f5e7ccde2b8f01690f3b/lib/dependencies/WorkerPlugin.js#L104) the `WorkerDependency` + its template in the compilation and [adds](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/400db90db807d630b6c9f5e7ccde2b8f01690f3b/lib/dependencies/WorkerPlugin.js#L352) `WorkerDependency` instances in the plugin logic.

Aspects like `ModuleDependency`, `RuntimeModule`, `NormalModuleFactory` are already exposed but in order to fully implement a custom dependency you need `makeSerializable`: 

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/400db90db807d630b6c9f5e7ccde2b8f01690f3b/lib/dependencies/WorkerDependency.js#L131

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Add `makeSerializable` as export like other utils:  https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/index.js#L566

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
code wise yes, but I'm not willing to sign the CLA with my real life mailing address and signature. 

```diff
	util: {
		get createHash() {
			return require("./util/createHash");
		},
		get comparators() {
			return require("./util/comparators");
		},
		get runtime() {
			return require("./util/runtime");
		},
		get serialization() {
			return require("./util/serialization");
		},
+		get makeSerializable() {
+			return require("./util/makeSerializable");
+		}
		get cleverMerge() {
			return require("./util/cleverMerge").cachedCleverMerge;
		},
		get LazySet() {
			return require("./util/LazySet");
		}
	},

```

title: ModuleFederation modules conflict with entry output modules
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
ModuleFederation conflict with Entry chunk
**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

```js
// index.ts
export const a = 1;
export const b = 2;
export const c = 3;

// remote.js
import { a } from '.'

// main.js
import { b, c } from '.'
```
I set `main.js` to entry and expose `remote.js` to ModuleFederation.

They will use same moduleId to generate code in chunks.

But `remote.js` chunks will haven't  exports `b` and `c`  because of  tree-shaking.

So, if loaded remoteEntry.js first, webpack runtime will use moduleId to cacheModule, and then loaded `main.js` will can't find constant `b` or `c`.

It shouldn't have same moduleId

body:

<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
When using minimize: true webpack inlines some variables.
In our case it inlines WASM bytecode, which is 15 MB string.
It does so 2 times, which inflates bundles size significantly.

This may affect other projects which use packages with WASM as base64 encoded strings.

https://github.com/ArepQuantori/test-ketcher-minify

title: FlagDependencyExportsPlugin is slow with large JSON imports
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Projects importing large JSON files take multiple seconds to build, even incremental builds in watch mode. Most of the time in incremental rebuilds is spent in FlagDependencyExportsPlugin loading values from memory cache, in [`restoreProvided`](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/d9ce3b1f87e63c809d8a19bbd92257d65922e81f/lib/FlagDependencyExportsPlugin.js#L81)

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

I've created a minimal repro here: https://github.com/albertxing/webpack-json-issue
With a large, deeply nested JSON default import, a lot of time (on my computer, 2-3s on every rebuild) is spent computing and re-hydrating the export tree, even though we only use the default export, and no tree shaking is required.

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

FlagDependencyExportsPlugin shouldn't take multiple seconds to process large JSON files, especially during incremental rebuilds in watch mode.
I can think of a few options we could explore:
- Skip some of the expensive export tree computations specifically for default imports of JSON modules
- Allow a maximum export depth to be specified in the webpack config for JSON files
  - webpack 4 only computed top-level exports for JSON: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/v4.47.0/lib/JsonParser.js#L20
- Allow disabling FlagDependencyExportsPlugin entirely for JSON modules via config

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.91.0
Node.js version: v20.12.2
Operating System: macOS
Additional tools:

title: Conflicting issue resolutions: #13125 & #15885 vs. #15545
body:
# Issue

The recommended resolution for issues #13125 & #15885 conflicts with the resolution for #15545.

#15545 is about the use of `"this"` as the `output.globalObject`.  The fix PR #15551 was to start using the `output.environment.arrowFunction` setting to generate the IIFE in umd.  Taking advantage of that fix **_requires_** one to use arrow functions.

#13125 & #15885 are about a large performance regression when enabling arrow functions in webpack-generated glue.  The regression came when `output.environment.arrowFunction` started being used to generate the IIFE.  The recommended workaround from those issues is to **_stop_** using arrow functions until the issue is fixed on the V8 side.  

# Discussion

Looking at the [V8 issue 42209446](https://issues.chromium.org/issues/42209446) that refers to [this document](https://docs.google.com/document/d/19xTAM4A75tz0xUq_velMzGA4JHEgXpyflUxXTcuNiyE/edit#heading=h.lwf44zsfrtq6) and the description of the PIFE heuristic, it's fairly clear that V8 feels that this existing heuristic is well known and tools such as webpack should continue to format IIFEs in a particular way to trigger the heuristic.  That is, they won't be fixing V8 issue 42209446.

Large projects like the one I work on are looking for ways to reduce bytes-over-wire.  Given the size of our codebase and the number of modules and exports we have, enabling arrow functions is a measurable performance win for us--or would be, if not for the performance loss due to the failure to eagerly parse and compile arrow-function IIFEs.

The fix for #15545 caused a new issue, #15885, which is the subject of _this_ issue. In addition, #15545 wasn't actually fixed in PR #15551, as you have to enable arrow functions to get the fix and that doesn't work for those that have to use standard functions.  An alternative solution for #15545 would be to set `output.globalObject` to [`"globalThis"`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/globalThis) and perhaps even explicitly disallow passing `"this"`.

# Suggested Resolution

Decouple IIFE generation from the `output.environment.arrowFunction` setting.  Create a second, separate setting called `output.environment.arrowFunctionForIIFE` that governs IIFE generation and defaults to whatever `output.environment.arrowFunction` is.  Performance testing is required to determine if this new setting should be used for only chunk-level IIFE generation or additionally for module-level IIFE generation.

title: [Feature request]: Support friendly worker import
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

The current use of Worker requires ESM

```js
new Worker(new URL('./worker.js', import.meta.url));
```

It is not very friendly to older browsers.

**What is the expected behavior?**

It should support older browsers, just like other modules

Parsing the syntax of new Worker, If a string is given as an argument, it should be a dependency of this file

**Input:**

```js
new Worker('./test.worker.js');
```

**Output:**

```js
// __webpack_require__($0) --> return "./test.worker.[contenthash].js"
new Worker(__webpack_require__($0));
```

`__webpack_require__($0)` is a javascript module that return the asset URL


**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

For compatibility with older browsers, and this is more universal.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Keep the same functionality as `worker-loader`. Since `worker-loader` is no longer maintained, it should become a built-in feature of webpack5.

1. Bundle worker file (process with other loaders)
2. Emit bundled file as assets
3. Replace import statement with a wrapper

```is
// test.worker.js
self.onmessage = () => {
  // ...
}
```

Wrap into

```js
// test.worker.js
module.exports = "./asset/path/to/test.worker.js"
```

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
no, I can't

**Relative Issue**

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/66710729/how-do-you-use-web-workers-with-webpack-5
https://github.com/GoogleChromeLabs/worker-plugin/issues/88

I investigated many issues and found no other solution except worker-loader

title: Dynamic Import Retry
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

When regular JS resources fail to load, retrying their errors is quite straightforward - you can simply listen to the `window.error` event. However, this approach does not work for JS resources that are dynamically imported.

To achieve this, one would need to perform complex hacks on webpack's internal functionality, such as defining a plugin to manipulate `RuntimeGlobals.require`.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

There is a desire for a standardized way to address this issue.

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/56772771/how-to-manually-load-webpack-async-chunks-when-any-dynamic-import-fails-to-load
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/62063737/what-is-the-best-practice-for-dealing-with-a-failed-dynamic-import-in-webpack
https://github.com/mattlewis92/webpack-retry-chunk-load-plugin/blob/master/src/index.ts

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Add a webpack configuration option, such as dynamic.hosts. When a request for a dynamically imported JS resource fails, it will automatically retry requests to other CDN domains in the hosts.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
no

body:

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When enabling the CSS experiment and using the `[contenthash]` placeholder in `output.chunkFilename` and with async import in JavaScript files, the following error is thrown on build: 

```
ERROR in Path variable [contenthash] not implemented in this context: [name].[contenthash].css
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

See https://github.com/titouanmathis/repro-webpack-contenthash-error

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->

The build should not throw an error, and we should be able to use the `[contenthash]` placeholder for chunks' filename.

webpack version: 5.92.0

Operating System: macOS

**Fix**

I was able to identify the commit (c5617482b739578b29682f71b9439e26b58ef743) introducing the bug with `git bisect`. The bug seems to occur when `modules` is `undefined` in `lib/css/CssModulesPlugin.js:363`. Wrapping the following lines in the condition below fixes the error.

title: Problem with namespace import + default object destructuring getting mangled in export but not resolved in destructuring
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When using the latest version of Webpack (5.92.1), a module default export imported as namespace and later on destructured ends up with the `default` being undefined, because it is mangled at the export site but kept as `default` at the destructuring assignment.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

[Reproduction repository](https://github.com/jduthon/webpack-default-mangled-repro/tree/main)
There you can try out the example and see the problem (I added instructions to the README), it can also be observed that with the previous version of Webpack the problem does not exist.

Most relevant part of the build with the latest version of Webpack (`NOTES:` comments added by me to explain the issue):

```js
// NAMESPACE OBJECT: ./src/import.js
var import_namespaceObject = {};
__webpack_require__.r(import_namespaceObject);
__webpack_require__.d(import_namespaceObject, {
/* NOTES: Here `A` is technically the default export, it has been mangled */
  A: () => (src_import)
});

;// CONCATENATED MODULE: ./src/import.js
const foo = "bar";
/* harmony default export */ const src_import = (foo);
;// CONCATENATED MODULE: ./src/index.js

const {
/* Here in the destrucuring assignment `default` should be `A`, but the mangling isn't followed through so this will be undefined */
  default: baz
} = import_namespaceObject;
console.log(baz);
/******/ })()
;
//# sourceMappingURL=index.js.map
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

There would be two choices I believe:
1. The default export shouldn't be mangled, so in the example above `A` should be kept as `default`
2. The `default` property in the destructuring should be updated to the mangled value, so `default: baz` above should be `A: baz`

In terms of final result for the specific code example, `baz` should end up with the value `'bar'` instead of `undefined`.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.92.1
Node.js version: 20.12.2
Operating System: Mac OSX
Additional tools:
author: jduthon

title: When can a patch release be published for the default mangle bug fixed last week in master? 5.92.0 and latest 5.92.1 are still broken

I got bit by the 5.92.0 default mangle regression which was identified and already fixed a week ago and merged to master. So we just need a release to pick up the change.

- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/18530
- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/18531

As for the nature of the error for anyone reading, you won't get an error at build but at runtime. It will fail to find objects from a default import, they will be undefined.

I am posting this so anyone who hits it will know to downgrade to 5.91.0 until a patch or minor update is published. 

It would be great if a patch release could be created soon to prevent others from hitting this. Thanks!!

title: hmr might fail if there are new initial chunks
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

HMR might fail if one initial chunk occur at second compilation.

The reason:

If one chunk is not initial chunk in the first compilation, but is initial chunk in the second compilation, when apply hot updates, there will be some imports to the initial chunk, but at this moment, the initial chunks are not loaded, so webpack will go fetching that chunk, and treats it as async chunk, so if async chunk path is different from initial chunk path, it cannot find that chunk

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

repro repo:

https://github.com/JSerFeng/webpack-lazy-compilation-issue/tree/hmr

run

```bash
pnpm i
pnpm run dev
```

open the browser

modify `src/initial.js`

```diff
try {
-  require("core-js/package.json");
+  require("core-js");
} catch (_err) {}

module.hot.accept();

module.exports = 1;
```

title: Improve access to resolver data in `module.rules` and `rule.use`
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

When writing loader criteria that should select specific modules of npm dependencies, it is more ergonomic to be able to specify the module to be matched as a combination of the package name (currently doable via `descriptionData.name`) and the relative path within the package (currently not doable). The latter path is always normalized by the resolver to use POSIX `/` as the directory separator, and is therefore cross-platform friendly, compared to the `resourcePath` value, which is the fully-resolved platform-specific disk path.

It would be beneficial to have access to the `relativePath` value from the resolver as a field to be used in conditions, and also for the `descriptionFile` object (or at least the path, name, version) and relative path within the package to be available as parameters to custom `rule.use` functions.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
Ergonomics, portability, performance. If the description file data and relative path are passed to `rule.use`, then it becomes possible to perform dynamic dispatch based on package name to select the appropriate loaders.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
Since the description file data, relative path and other resolver properties are already present in the module factory, forward them into the rule execution engine.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes
author: dmichon-msft

title: DefinePlugin breaks references to imports
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When an imported identifier shadows a nested DefinePlugin definition, references to the import are broken when they are in a more complex expression (e.g. assignment to a new variable). This results in a `ReferenceError` being introduced.

```js
import foo from './foo.js';

function works() {
  return foo.bar;
}

function broken() {
  const bar = foo.bar;
  return bar;
}

works(); // does not throw
broken(); // throws ReferenceError

```

```js
DefinePlugin({"foo.bar.baz": "baz"});
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Added failing minimal test here: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/18572

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

```js
import foo from './foo.js';

function works() {
  return foo.bar;
}

function broken() {
  const bar = foo.bar;
  return bar;
}

works(); // does not throw
broken(); // should NOT throw ReferenceError

```

```js
DefinePlugin({"foo.bar.baz": "baz"});
```

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.93.0
Node.js version: 20
Operating System: MacOS
Additional tools:

---

Note that this does not appear to be a recent regression, this has been broken for a long time.

I originally found this bug in this scenario:

```js
import process from 'global/process';

const foo = process?.env?.SOME_VAR;
```

Babel transforms this optional chaining into:

```js
var _process$env;
import process from 'global/process';
const foo = process === null || process === void 0 || (_process$env = process.env) === null || _process$env === void 0 ? void 0 : _process$env.SOME_VAR;
console.log(foo);
```

This bug is triggered by this code: webpack breaks the reference to the imported `process.env` within the chained logical expression, resulting in a `ReferenceError` in browsers because `process.env` is obviously undefined.

Notably, this code breaks with _default_ webpack configuration, as `DefinePlugin` is automatically applied:

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/277460b33bcc49c51acbbcd688672aa4ec685732/lib/WebpackOptionsApply.js#L569-L574

title: Allow instantiation of WebAssembly module with `SharedArrayBuffer` memory
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
Currently, loading a WebAssembly module will always be done with an `ArrayBuffer` for the backing memory. But, by passing a `SharedArrayBuffer` in the WebAssembly.instantiate/Streaming `importsObj` parameter, that buffer will be used instead.

**What is the expected behavior?**
Somehow, in my `webpack.config.js` file, I can configure the wasm loader to use a `SharedArrayBuffer` memory backend instead of `ArrayBuffer`.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
This would allow fast sharing of memory between WASM modules across Web Worker boundaries.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
The `importObj` parameter is already dynamically created here: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/7b80b2b18db66abca6feb7b02a9089aca4bc8186/lib/wasm-sync/WasmChunkLoadingRuntimeModule.js#L57-L63

so by checking if a "useSharedMemory" flag is set, one can easily instantiate a buffer there and store it in the `importsObj`.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
Yes, although I'm new to the codebase and don't have a great sense of how config is passed around, especially to loaders.

title: Worker entry points using `importScripts` should load synchronously.
body:
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/00e61027935295004199eb230200506db62a56c3/lib/webworker/ImportScriptsChunkLoadingPlugin.js#L22-L25

It appears that webpack unnecessarily makes loading additional chunks in worker entrypoints asynchronous, given that the method used to load additional chunks is in fact synchronous.

I ran into this trying to create a custom worker for monaco. It uses `importScripts` to load a file from a given URL, and expects something to be synchronously defined after `importScripts` has returned ([code](https://github.com/microsoft/monaco-editor/blob/c321d0fbecb50ab8a5365fa1965476b0ae63fc87/src/language/typescript/tsWorker.ts#L507-L514)). This fails if the URL is a webpack bundle generated using `importScripts`, but it works if I change `asyncChunkLoading` to `false` in the above code.
author: tomprince

title: Provide a way to get a worker bundle without wrapping in `new Worker()`
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

**What is the expected behavior?**

I'd like some way to get the URL of a module that is to be loaded into a web worker, to be able to pass it to some existing code
that creates a webworker, or that passes it to an existing webworker to be loaded into it.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

I'm using monaco, and trying to create a custom typescript web worker for the editor. To do so, I need to pass
the URL of a script to be loaded (via `importScripts`) in an existing worker.
```javascript
languageDefaults.setWorkerOptions({
  customWorkerPath: workerUrl,
});
```

At the moment, I'm using a dedicated module to get this to work:
```javascript
// Webpack has syntax for passing the URL of a script to be loaded in a web worker to
// various functions (such as `new Worker(...)`). However, since we just want the URL of
// the script, we tell webpack that in this module, `String(new URL(...))` should be
// treated as creating a web worker. That gives us access to the URL of the script,
// which we can pass to monaco.
const url = String(
  new URL(
    /* webpackChunkName: "custom-ts" */
    "./worker.ts",
    import.meta.url,
  ),
);
export default url;
``` 
specifying `parser: { worker: ["String()"] }`  for that module.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

This can perhaps be implemented by better documenting the syntax for `parser.worker`. I tried a number of ways to define a `getWorkerURL` function which was either the identity function, or an alias for `String`, which would be recognized by the worker plugin but couldn't figure out the right incantation.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
possibly
author: tomprince

title: Webpack not extracting "default" with externals and mjs file extension
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Webpack does not extract the default export when I mark one dependency as external and there's another dependency the exports a file with .mjs extension.
That is, instead of outputting something like this (which extracts "default" from the module)

```
/* harmony import */ var external_dep__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0___default = /*#__PURE__*/__webpack_require__.n(external_dep__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__);
external_dep__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0___default()();
```

It outputs just this
`external_dep__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__();`


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

I created my own configuration: https://github.com/AlekseiRusanov/Webpack_Issue.
It has the following dependency graph:
Main App -> internal_dep -> external_dep
external_dep is marked as externals in webpack config:

```
    externals: (ctx, callback) => {
        if(["external_dep"].indexOf(ctx.request) !== -1){
            callback(null, `assign-properties ${ctx.request}`);
            return;
        }
        callback()
    }
	
```
internal_dep has "index" file that exports one function as default

```
import external_dep_default from 'external_dep'
export default () => {
    console.log("Hello from internal_js_dep");
    external_dep_default();
}
```

If internal_dep exports "index" with .mjs extension (in package.json):
```

  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "import": {
        "default": "./index.mjs"
      }
    }
  },
  
```
Then after building Main App, I see this in the output:

```
/* harmony import */ var external_dep__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(/*! external_dep */ "external_dep");

/* harmony default export */ const __WEBPACK_DEFAULT_EXPORT__ = (() => {
    console.log("Hello from internal_mjs_dep");
    external_dep__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__();
});

/***/ })
```
Which is wrong because "external_dep__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__" does not equal "default".

If choose not to mark "external_dep" as "external", or if "internal_dep" exports index with .js extension instead of .mjs then it works as expected:

```
/* harmony import */ var external_dep__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(/*! external_dep */ "external_dep");
/* harmony import */ var external_dep__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0___default = /*#__PURE__*/__webpack_require__.n(external_dep__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__);

/* harmony default export */ const __WEBPACK_DEFAULT_EXPORT__ = (() => {
    console.log("Hello from internal_js_dep");
    external_dep__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0___default()();
});

```

**What is the expected behavior?**

I expect it to extract "default" correctly, even if internal_dep exports index.mjs and I mark "external_dep" as "external", i.e. like this:

```
/* harmony import */ var external_dep__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(/*! external_dep */ "external_dep");

title: [Feature request] Port resolveWeak to javascript/esm
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
CommonJS code allows the usage of `require`, and so the corresponding webpack rule [type](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/module/#ruletype) `javascript/auto` will transform `require.resolveWeak` into the corresponding runtime code.

ESM code does not allow the usage of require, and so `javascript/esm` type does not transform `require.resolveWeak`, as we don't expect to see `require` in this context.

Still, libraries like `@lodable/components` or `react-loadable` leverage this `require.resolveWeak` capability to implement their code-splitting abstractions. When generating ESM code that use them, we end up with `require.resolveWeak` inside ESM, which webpack does not understand by default. This creates runtime failures.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
As far as I know multiple options can mitigate the issue:
1. not using ESM
2. not using those libraries 
3. forcing `javascript/auto` for the concerned ESM files (see issue [here](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15372))

   ```js
    {
      test: /\.js$/,
      include: /path\/to\/esm\/files\/with\/resolveWeak/,
      type: 'javascript/auto',
    }
   ```

No option is perfect here:
1. is not future-proof
2. means that we lose key features of those libs like server-side rendering chunk extractions (eg. `@loadable/server`)
3. requires extra config (and maybe it has negative side effects on other aspects like performances?)

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
Webpack started to leverage `import.meta`, which is free to use, to implement `import.meta.webpackContext`, for [example](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/15446). 

Can we imagine following this path to port the `resolveWeak` capability to `javascript/esm` also? This is also mentioned in this [comment](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15372#issuecomment-1070234970).

This way, code historically leveraging `require.resolveWeak` will be able to migrate to `import.meta.resolveWeak` or something similar when bundled in ESM.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
I can help, but I've never contributed to webpack so this might be a very challenging tasks for me 😅 
I'm probably better suited to do update PRs on https://github.com/gregberge/loadable-components/ and https://github.com/swc-project (loadable plugin).

Funny thing that i tried it on my cloud Linux dev machine and there is no problem. Now it happens only on my local machine, so the problem with cache i guess.

{

 ```

title: Async imports in tree-shaken functions still end up in the bundle
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
Dynamic imports that are tree-shaken away still end up including their contents in the bundle. This does not occur with re-exports. See code example below.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
```typescript
// barrel.ts
export async function getAsyncChunk() {
    const module = await import('./lazy');
    return module.foo;
}

export function doSomething() {
    console.log("Done")
}

// entry.ts
import { doSomething } from "./barrel"
doSomething()
```
With the above code, Webpack will treeshake and eliminate `getAsyncChunk`. However, it will create chunks for `lazy` if `asyncChunks: true`, and will bundle the code in the main bundle if `asyncChunks: false`. 

The following code works as expected
```typescript
// wrapper.ts
export async function getAsyncChunk() {
    const module = await import('./lazy');
    return module.foo;
}

// barrel.ts
export { getAsyncChunk } from './wrapper'

export function doSomething() {
    console.log("Done")
}

// entry.ts
import { doSomething } from "./barrel"
doSomething()
```
Now, the contents of `lazy` are not included in the final output at all.

Repro project: https://github.com/abettadapur/webpack-chunk-treeshake-repro

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

I'd expect that because `getAsyncChunk` was eliminated from the final bundle, the async imports it contains should also be eliminated.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.91.0
Node.js version: 20.x
Operating System: MacOS Sonoma

title: Type packages in webpack dependencies - move to devDependencies
body:
<!-- Bug reports and Feature requests must use other templates, or will be closed -->
<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

Is it intended that packages `@types/eslint-scope` and `@types/estree` are listed in dependencies in package.json? I think they would be better to be in devDependencies. After updating from webpack 5.91.0 to version 5.92.1, there is conflict in our project with eslint types. We are using eslint version 8 but now eslint 9 types are brought in and they do not match. :( This can be fixed locally for our project, but I think it would be also improvement for all users to remove their requirement.

I don't think that webpack users actually need to have those type packages installed when they are just using webpack themself. I may be wrong of course. But moving them devDependencies would mean that there is less packages to be installed locally.

title: Webpack hangs at 100% CPU when attempting to read a symlink-like file that it can't read
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When running webpack in a sandbox (like [Bazel's](https://bazel.build/docs/sandboxing)), webpack might find a file that appears to be a symlink, in that `fs.lstat` says that it `isSymbolicLink`, but when we go to `readlink` the file, `readlink` fails because the target is outside the sandbox.

In this situation, webpack runs in an infinite loop at 100% CPU, and never exits.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I apologize for this being a bad news/good news situation. The bad news is that a reproduction is hard to do without a Bazel's sandbox setup which is very burdensome. The good news is, I think the erroneous code is obvious by inspection: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/8bf907b23916d47c52ecb7fd2814f168e0082121/lib/util/fs.js#L622-L630
In this snippet, I think we can all agree that in the terminal error (`if (err || !target)`) we should *not* just do the same thing we did in the intermediate error case just above, i.e., we shouldn't call `doStat` again. If after three tries `readlink` is still erroring out, we should call `callback` with an error code.

I have a fork with a unit test that reproduces this unusual situation https://github.com/fasiha/webpack/commits/fasiha-fix-infinite-readlink/ and will open a PR shortly.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

In these situations where webpack sees a symlink but can't read it, it should give up instead of hanging at 100% CPU. In terms of the code snippet above, it should call the callback with an error (which it never does).

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.93.0
Node.js version: v21.6.2 (macOS), v16.18.1 (Linux Rocky9)
Operating System: macOS, Linux
Additional tools: (Bazel)
author: fasiha

title: Error destructuring from default import if a default property has the same name as a named export in ESM module
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Please see the provided repo. In an ESM module. If a property on an object exported by default and a named import use the same name, destructuring of the imported default does not get the correct value if the named import is also used. The compiled code references a key that doesn't exist.

https://github.com/EJLearner/webpack-issue

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

Instead of the compiled destructuring code referencing a non-existent key. It should use the correct object key from the code.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.93.0
Node.js version: seen in node 19 and node 20
Operating System: seen in redhat linux and windows 10
author: EJLearner

title: Minification produces "let t,t,t,t,t,t..."
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Minification of duckdb-browser-eh.worker.js from @duckdb/duckdb-wasm (a file which is already minified) changes this:

```js
"use strict";var duckdb=(()=>{var Gc=Object.create;var xi=Object.defineProperty;
```

to this:

```js
"use strict";var duckdb=(()=>{let t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t,t;var Ai,Li,Sn,Gc=Object.create,xi=Object.defineProperty
```

This causes a SyntaxError.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

1. Create a webpack project
2. Add @duckdb/duckdb-wasm 1.28.1-dev106.0 as a dependency
3. Include dist/duckdb-browser-eh.worker.js, such as via the following snippet:

```js
  const workerUrl = new URL('@duckdb/duckdb-wasm/dist/duckdb-browser-eh.worker.js', import.meta.url);
  const moduleUrl = new URL('@duckdb/duckdb-wasm/dist/duckdb-eh.wasm', import.meta.url);
  const worker = new Worker(workerUrl);
  const logger = new duckdb.ConsoleLogger();
  const db = new AsyncDuckDB(logger, worker);
  await db.instantiate(moduleUrl.toString());
```

4. Run a production webpack build.
5. Observe that the output file (e.g. /_next/static/media/duckdb-browser-eh.worker.a53c8b41.js) contains syntax errors.

**What is the expected behavior?**

The file should minify with no syntax errors.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.90.0 (bundled with next^14.2.5)
Node.js version: 20.12.2
Operating System: Windows 10
author: arperry

title: Webpack performance issue when using webpack-merge
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When using official docs [production setup ](https://webpack.js.org/guides/production/#root) I've noticed significant performance loss in development and production build times.

Before moving to `webpack-merge` approach I was using the following way to split dev and prod config:

```
const config = { ... }
module.exports = (webpack, args) => {
  const isProduction = args.mode === "production";

  config.plugins.push(
    new DefinePlugin("process.env.NODE_ENV", JSON.stringify(args.mode)),
  );

  if (isProduction) {
    config.mode = "production";
    delete config.devServer;
    delete config.devtool;
  } else {
    config.mode = "development";
  }

  return config;
};
```
And In `package.json`
```
  "scripts": {
    "start": "webpack serve",
    "build": "webpack --mode=production"
  },
```

Also something interesting that I observed is bundle sizes are very different with `webpack-merge` approach they are much smaller than my original which was reason I started witching to `webpack-merge` approach.


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

For reproduction please visit the following repo:
1. "Slow" `webpack-merge` approach https://github.com/nparoski/webpack-performance/tree/webpack-merge
2. Faster, beefy bundles approach https://github.com/nparoski/webpack-performance/tree/master

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

title: Entry module isn't executed when targeting webworker with a runtime chunk
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When targeting `webworker` with a runtime chunk, the entry module isn't executed unless it has a dynamic import.

From some debugging, it seems that the code of each module is wrapped in functions and they sit in a `self[chunkLoadingGlobal]` array that's not used by the runtime at all.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

I've put up a sample repo here, which has three entries: one without runtime chunk, one with runtime chunk but no dynamic import, and one with runtime chunk and dynamic import.

https://github.com/gustavohenke/webpack-webworker-bug

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

The entry module in a config that has `target: 'webworker'` + `optimization.runtimeChunk: true` should always execute.

**Other relevant information:**
- webpack version:  5.93.0
- Node.js version: 20.11.1
- Operating System: macOS Sonoma 14.6.1
- Additional tools: -

title: EnvironmentPlugin types don't allow passing null
body: It seems like [this PR](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/18647) broke the types for EnvironmentPlugin. [According to docs](https://webpack.js.org/plugins/environment-plugin/), both undefined and null are accepted as values (and have different behavior), however with this change it is no longer allowed to pass null: ([playground link](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?#code/JYWwDg9gTgLgBAbwKIDsBuwoRSApimABQBsBXAc2BQF84AzLEOAIgHdcAjMAQwGMBrZgG4AUCJS5WcVBiw58RMpRQAKBCLia4AMQDyugFxxSKACa46VXKYA0GrQCEAggCUjKUsWJ3qASiFAA)) _Originally posted by @jtbandes in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/18647#discussion_r1735125053_

title: mangle destructuring default in dynamic import
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Dynamic import is mangled. (Only happens on mode `production`!)

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

The following code worked in up to `5.76.1`(**at least**) but when I did upgrade to `5.94.0` produces a wrong output.

```javascript
const {default: data} = await import(/* webpackChunkName: "xxx" */ 'foo/bar.json');
```

In working versions, `data` becomes something like `{"x": 123}`. In the broken, the module is `{}` with no default.

If I avoid the destructuring with rename, then there is no problem.

```javascript
const json = await import(/* webpackChunkName: "xxx" */ 'foo/bar.json');
const data = json.default;
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

Dynamic import must not be mangled. This recent fix and may be others before could be involved as it is also about similar topic:

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/18531

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.94.0
Node.js version: 20.13.1
Operating System: OSX 
Additional tools:
author: artola

title: Using aliases with dynamic imports in 5.94.0
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

I do have the following section in *imports* area in *package.json* file:

```json
"imports": {
  "#root/*": "./*",
  "#core/*": "./src/core/*",
  "#components/*": "./src/core/components/*",
  "#build/*": "./.build/*",
  "#lib/*": "./src/core/lib/*",
  "#bin/*": "./src/bin/*",
  "#site/*": "./site/*",
  "#etc/*": "./site/etc/*",
  "#src/*": "./src/*"
}
```

Then, I'm using dynamic imports and do include those aliases there, as in example:

```js
const api = await import(`#site/../${m.path}/api/index.js`);
this.fastify.register(async () => {
    api.default(this.fastify);
});
```

When I do this, I get the following error during build process:

```
ERROR in ./src/core/lib/heretic.js 311:30-71
Module not found: Error: Invalid "imports" target "./site/.." defined for "#site/*" in the package config /Users/A2506402/Documents/GitHub/heretic/package.json, targets must start with "./"
   @ ./src/index.js 1:0-41 4:24-31
```

title: Dynamically importing the same file twice in a specific way, incorrectly processes the second request.
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

```
  async *firstFunction() {

    const { generateSummary } = await import(
      /* webpackInclude: /[/]selectors[/](?!.*\.test).*\.m?js$/ */
      /* webpackChunkName: "store-selectors" */
      /* webpackMode: "lazy-once" */
      `./selectors/${this.type}.mjs`
    );
   console.log(typeof generateSummary) // = "function"
  }

  async *secondFunction() {

    const {entityActionQueue} = await import(
      /* webpackInclude: /[/]selectors[/](?!.*\.test).*\.m?js$/ */
      /* webpackChunkName: "store-selectors" */
      /* webpackMode: "lazy-once" */
      `./selectors/${this.type}.mjs`
    );
   console.log(entityActionQueue)  // =undefined

    const logFunctions = await import(
      /* webpackInclude: /[/]selectors[/](?!.*\.test).*\.m?js$/ */
      /* webpackChunkName: "store-selectors" */
      /* webpackMode: "lazy-once" */
      `./selectors/${this.type}.mjs`
    );
   console.log(logFunctions)  // = { generateSummary: function, [randomLetter]: function } // <-- the randomLetter turns out to be the entityActionQueue function, but is mangled.
}

```

If I have two functions (within the same file) dynamically importing another file (also same file), but a different function for that file, I get as result that the second function name is mangled. See the code above.

However, if I import the full module and use the magic comment `webpackExports`, the second function name remains, as expected. See working example below:

```
  async *firstFunction() {

    const selectorModule = await import(
      /* webpackInclude: /[/]selectors[/](?!.*\.test).*\.m?js$/ */
      /* webpackChunkName: "store-selectors" */
      /* webpackMode: "lazy-once" */
      /* webpackExports: ["generateSummary", "entityActionQueue"] */
      `./selectors/${this.type}.mjs`
    );
   console.log(typeof selectorModule.generateSummary) // = "function"
  }

  async *secondFunction() {

    const selectorModule = await import(
      /* webpackInclude: /[/]selectors[/](?!.*\.test).*\.m?js$/ */
      /* webpackChunkName: "store-selectors" */
      /* webpackMode: "lazy-once" */
      /* webpackExports: ["generateSummary", "entityActionQueue"] */
      `./selectors/${this.type}.mjs`
    );
   console.log(typeof selectorModule.entityActionQueue)  // = "function"
}

```
<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

As described as comment in the code above, only through `webpackExports` the exported functions can be specified (and function correctly). I would expect webpack to recognize that multiple exports from the same file are being requested.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I wouldn't be surprised in the webpack config I'm using is needed for reproduction (I have set 'modern' settings).

```
import path from "node:path";
import { fileURLToPath } from "url";
import TerserPlugin from "terser-webpack-plugin";
import fs from "node:fs";
import webpack from "webpack";

const __dirname = path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url));

const releaseDir = {
  "lambda": path.resolve(__dirname, "dist/lambda"),
};

const globalConfig = {
  mode: "production",
  experiments: {
    outputModule: true,
    futureDefaults: true,
    asyncWebAssembly: true,
    topLevelAwait: true,
    layers: true,
  },
  context: __dirname,
  output: {
    filename: 'index.js',
    clean: true,
    module: true,
    scriptType: "module",
    chunkFormat: "module",
    enabledLibraryTypes: ['module'],
    ignoreBrowserWarnings: true,
    environment: {
      module: true,
      nodePrefixForCoreModules: true,
      dynamicImport: true,
      bigIntLiteral: true,
    },
    library: {
      type: "module",
    }
  },
  plugins: [
    new webpack.DefinePlugin({
      'process.env': "process.env",
    })
  ],
  devtool: "source-map",
  cache: {
    type: "filesystem",
    compression: 'gzip',
    hashAlgorithm: 'sha1',
    cacheDirectory: path.resolve(__dirname, './node_modules/.cache/webpack'),
  },
  optimization: {
    checkWasmTypes: false,
    removeAvailableModules: true,
    usedExports: true,
    minimize: false, // IMPORTANT: this is set to false, I have the issue with this setting set to both false as true.
    realContentHash: true, // Should always be set in production mode, enforcing it.
    minimizer: [
      {
        apply: (compiler) => {
          new TerserPlugin({
            extractComments: false,
            terserOptions: {
              parse: {},
              compress: {
                defaults: true,
                passes: 4, // Adjusted
                ecma: 2024, // Adjusted
                module: true,// Adjusted
              },
              mangle: true,
              module: true, // Adjusted
              ecma: 2024, // Adjusted

            },

        },
      },
    ],
  },

    node: true
  },
};


const configurations = (env) => {

    {

      entry: {
        lambda: path.resolve(__dirname, "./index.mjs"),
      },
      cache: {
        ...globalConfig.cache,
        memoryCacheUnaffected: true,
      },
     output: {
        ...globalConfig.output,

        filename: "[name].mjs",
      },

export default configurations;

**What is the current behavior?**
when use dynamic import with variable like this:

import(varableA)

webpack will try to load any files in the same directory, which cause error:

You may need an appropriate loader to handle this file type, currently no loaders are configured to process this file. See https://webpack.js.org/concepts#loaders

only try files defined in the `resolve.extensions`

../../node_modules/webpack/types.d.ts:13611:2 - error TS2416: Property '[Symbol.iterator]' in type 'SortableSet<T>' is not assignable to the same property in base type 'Set<T>'.
