title: Single Runtime Chunk breaks Module Federation Container init
body: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/18811 # Bug report **What is the current behavior?** When setting runtimeChunk:single, or specifying a separate runtimeChunk. Module are not hoisted correctly. externals and `webpack/container/reference/name` modules are hoisted to the entry point chunks, however module federation is a runtime module and when it initializes it will registerShare, then initExternal. - If there are multiple entrypoints, and some entry points do not import a remote module thats listed in `remotes` - If the other entry point is not loaded on the page Then you will get failed to init external , call of undefined warning because not all the `container/refernce` modules are in the `__webpack_modules__` factory, as the other entry chunks may only show up or load for another route or later in the application. When runtimeChunk is not set to single, the modules are hoisted correctly to the top entry. But when there is a runtimeChunk single, the modules only hoist to the entry point not the root chunk (runtime chunk) If you share a module as eager, it does the same - it will never hoist into the runtime where it is supposed to be. **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** Set runtimeChunk to single, add 2 entrypoints - each entrypoint imports a different remote, load only 1 entrypoint on the page and you will see init failure warning for the other remote in the other entrypoint **What is the expected behavior?** Container References, Eager Modules, and Externals should hoist to the highest possible location, the runtime chunks they belong to. **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.95 Node.js version: 20 Operating System: macos Additional tools: module federation
author: ScriptedAlchemy

title: Remove Need for Async Import in Module Federation
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

Module Federation currently forces the user to manually implement a import() in their user code in order to avoid eager consumption errors. 
Webpack should internalize the async boundary in the entrypoint startup or use require.X for active chunk startup, this ensures that chunk handlers are called for subsequent chunks and ideally for the entrypoint itself. 

**What is the expected behavior?**

I should not need to import() in my codebase manually. 
My entrypoint should behave like a async chunk load, and call ensureChunkHandlers for itself and any chunks it depends on before evaluating the EntryDependencies. 

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
Most frameworks control how entrypoint is loaded, like next.js - there is no way for user to alter the startup of these apps from the outside. the eager consumption issue is a yearslong complaint from the community with most who just start getting stuck for hours wondering what is wrong, not realizing that the import() is critical to operations. 

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

We should add a runtime requirement to any entrypoint whose tree contains module federation dependent modules, like shared module or remote module consumption that is not wrapped in AsyncDependencyBlock
 
In StartupChunkDependenciesPlugin or example. 
```js
        compilation.hooks.additionalChunkRuntimeRequirements.tap(
          'MfStartupChunkDependenciesPlugin',
          (chunk, set, { chunkGraph }) => {
            if (!isEnabledForChunk(chunk)) return;
            if (chunkGraph.getNumberOfEntryModules(chunk) === 0) return;
            set.add(federationStartup);
          },
        );
```

in StartupHelpers + ESM startup / renderStartup hook. 

```js
   if (federation) {
        const chunkIds = Array.from(chunks, (c: Chunk) => c.id);

        const wrappedInit = (body: string) =>
          Template.asString([
            'Promise.all([',
            Template.indent([
              // may have other chunks who depend on federation, so best to just fallback
              // instead of try to figure out if consumes or remotes exists during build
              `${RuntimeGlobals.ensureChunkHandlers}.consumes || function(chunkId, promises) {},`,
              `${RuntimeGlobals.ensureChunkHandlers}.remotes || function(chunkId, promises) {},`,
            ]),
            `].reduce(${runtimeTemplate.returningFunction(`handler('${chunk.id}', p), p`, 'p, handler')}, promises)`,
            `).then(${runtimeTemplate.returningFunction(body)});`,
          ]);

        const wrap = wrappedInit(
          `${
            passive
              ? RuntimeGlobals.onChunksLoaded
              : RuntimeGlobals.startupEntrypoint
          }(0, ${JSON.stringify(chunkIds)}, ${fn})`,
        );

        runtime.push(`${final && !passive ? EXPORT_PREFIX : ''}${wrap}`);
      } else {
        const chunkIds = Array.from(chunks, (c: Chunk) => c.id);
        runtime.push(
          `${final && !passive ? EXPORT_PREFIX : ''}${
            passive
              ? RuntimeGlobals.onChunksLoaded
              : RuntimeGlobals.startupEntrypoint
          }(0, ${JSON.stringify(chunkIds)}, ${fn});`,
        );
        if (final && passive) {
          runtime.push(`${EXPORT_PREFIX}${RuntimeGlobals.onChunksLoaded}();`);
        }
      }
```

The output of a entrypoint should look like this:
```js
// load runtime
var __webpack_require__ = require("../webpack-runtime.js");
__webpack_require__.C(exports);
var __webpack_exec__ = (moduleId) => (__webpack_require__(__webpack_require__.s = moduleId))



var promises = [];
var __webpack_exports__ = Promise.all([
	__webpack_require__.f.consumes || function(chunkId, promises) {},
	__webpack_require__.f.remotes || function(chunkId, promises) {},
// since call handler for this chunk
].reduce((p, handler) => (handler('pages/_document', p), p), promises)
// use require.x which already calls require.f for all other initialChunks
).then(() => (__webpack_require__.X(0, ["vendor-chunks/next"], () => (__webpack_exec__("./pages/_document.js")))));
module.exports = __webpack_exports__;
```

In `RemoteRuntimeModule` and `ConsumeSharedRuntimeModule`, we should `getAllReferencedChunks`  - this ensures that initialChunks or custom splitChunks can be tracked by federation chunk handlers, this also allows splitChunks to work again on more than `splitChunks: async`

```js
    const allChunks = [...(this.chunk?.getAllReferencedChunks() || [])];
    for (const chunk of allChunks) {
```


The remoteEntry should not be wrapped in require.X as this will convert it to promise when remote global should return get,init

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: Jest throws SyntaxError after updating from 5.94.0 to 5.95.0
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

We have a repo with Jest tests. The Jest config needs to specify [`transformIgnorePatterns`](https://jestjs.io/docs/configuration#transformignorepatterns-arraystring) to allow some third-party node modules to be transpiled, otherwise running Jest results in "Jest encountered an unexpected token" errors. This was working fine with webpack v5.94.0; however, after updating to v5.95.0, running Jest now results in errors like this:

```
Test suite failed to run

    Jest encountered an unexpected token

    Jest failed to parse a file. This happens e.g. when your code or its dependencies use non-standard JavaScript syntax, or when Jest is not configured to support such syntax.

    Out of the box Jest supports Babel, which will be used to transform your files into valid JS based on your Babel configuration.

    By default "node_modules" folder is ignored by transformers.

    Here's what you can do:
     • If you are trying to use ECMAScript Modules, see https://jestjs.io/docs/ecmascript-modules for how to enable it.
     • If you are trying to use TypeScript, see https://jestjs.io/docs/getting-started#using-typescript
     • To have some of your "node_modules" files transformed, you can specify a custom "transformIgnorePatterns" in your config.
     • If you need a custom transformation specify a "transform" option in your config.
     • If you simply want to mock your non-JS modules (e.g. binary assets) you can stub them out with the "moduleNameMapper" config option.

    You'll find more details and examples of these config options in the docs:
    https://jestjs.io/docs/configuration
    For information about custom transformations, see:
    https://jestjs.io/docs/code-transformation

    Details:

    /Users/me/my-app/node_modules/decamelize-keys/index.js:2
    import mapObject from 'map-obj';
    ^^^^^^

    SyntaxError: Cannot use import statement outside a module

```

I can't see anything else that changed that could have caused this – no other dependencies have been updated in my `yarn.lock` and rolling back to v5.94.0 makes the issue go away again, so it definitely seems to be linked to the webpack update.

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

Jest tests that built on the previous minor version should continue to build successfully after updating to the latest minor version.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.95.0
Node.js version: 20.17.0
Operating System: macOS 14.4 (23E214)
Additional tools: N/A
author: andrewpye

title: Output chunks change when build stays the same
body: # Bug report The original issue was reported here https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/70754, but I have a strong suspicion that the issue is with webpack and not with nextjs. **What is the current behavior?** when none of the source files change, webpack creates new output chunks for some of the pages. https://www.diffchecker.com/OidQJCx0/ according to [the webpack docs](https://webpack.js.org/guides/caching/): > The [contenthash] substitution will add a unique hash based on the content of an asset. When the asset's content changes, [contenthash] will also change. if you compare the changed `page-[contenthash].js` chunks across builds, you'll notice that the contents are nearly identical, except for the webpack chunk IDs at the top of the file. the contents of the array are the same, it's just that the order can change across builds https://www.diffchecker.com/Zp1tnr7X/ **i.e. because the chunk IDs aren't sorted at the top of the chunk, webpack treats these as "new" chunks, even though the actual code hasn't changed.** **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** https://github.com/chriskrogh/build-manifest-test 1. clone the repo and run `npm i` 2. run the nextjs build (`npm run build`) and take note of the `app-build-manifest.json` 3. run the nextjs build again and compare the `app-build-manifest.json`s across the two builds * you may want to sort them by route if they're not in the same order across builds * you may also need to repeat step 3 if you observe identical chunk names. see above for why that is **What is the expected behavior?** * chunks should be the same when the build doesn't change **Proposed solution** * sort the webpack chunk ID array before writing to the file so that the array stays the same if the contents are the same I'm willing to open a PR if someone points me to the right place. **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.90.0 Node.js version: 20.12.0 Operating System: Platform: darwin Arch: arm64 Version: Darwin Kernel Version 23.6.0: Mon Jul 29 21:14:30 PDT 2024; root:xnu-10063.141.2~1/RELEASE_ARM64_T6000 Available memory (MB): 32768 Available CPU cores: 10
author: chriskrogh

title: Collision with global variables when optimization.avoidEntryIife = true
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
When an entry module contains a local override of `const Symbol = 'Symbol'` and the compilation contains the runtime module `makeNamespaceObject` (i.e. `__webpack_require__.r`), the scope analysis doesn't rename the shadowing `Symbol` variable and results in the global `Symbol` in the runtime module being replaced with the local variable.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

Have an entry module that shadows the global `Symbol`.
Require the `makeNamespaceObject` (`__webpack_require__.r`) runtime module.

**What is the expected behavior?**

Entry modules need to rename any globals that they shadow if `optmization.avoidEntryIife = true`.

**Other relevant information:**

Node.js version: 18.19.1
Operating System: Linux

title: TypeError: __webpack_require__.O is not a function
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**




**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

``` js
const path = require("path");
const HtmlWebpackPlugin = require("html-webpack-plugin");
const { ModuleFederationPlugin } = require("webpack").container;
const rules = [
	{
		test: /\.js$/,
		include: path.resolve(__dirname, "src"),
		use: {
			loader: "babel-loader",
			options: {
				presets: ["@babel/react"]
			}
		}
	}
];
const getConfig = (env = "development") => {
	env = "development";
	return [
		{
			name: "app",
			context: __dirname,
			mode: env,
			devtool: "source-map",
			entry: {
				app: "./src/index.js"
			},
			output: {
				filename: "[name].js",
				path: path.resolve(__dirname, "dist/aaa"),
				uniqueName: "module-federation-aaa"
			},
			module: { rules },
			optimization: {
				runtimeChunk: "single",
				splitChunks: {
					chunks: "all",
					cacheGroups: {
						efg:{
							// react
							test: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/]react[\\/]/,
							name: "npm.react",
							priority: 10
						}
					},
				},
			},
			plugins: [
				new ModuleFederationPlugin({
					remoteType: "script", 
					name: "mfeAAA",
					library: {
						type: "umd",
						name: "mfeAAA"
					},
					runtime: false,
					// List of remotes with URLs
					remotes: {
						"mfe-a": "mfeAAA@/mfeAAA.js"
					},
					exposes: {
						"./Component": "./src/D"
					},
				}),
				new HtmlWebpackPlugin({
					template: "./index.html"
				})
			],
		},
	]
};
module.exports = getConfig;

```



This is an example of using module federation for self-use.

This webpack configuration will output `app.js`, `runtime.js`, `mfeAAA.js`, and other unimportant JS files. The order they are included in `index.html` is `runtime.js`, `app.js`, `mfeAAA.js`.

The key points of this issue are:
- webpack has `runtime: 'single'`, and `ModuleFederationPlugin` has option `runtime: false`.
- Both `runtime.js` and `mfeAAA.js` internally register `webpackJsonpCallback`.
- When `app.js` loaded, there is a callback `__webpack_require__.O(0, ["npm.react","vendors......`,
```
__webpack_require__ => { // webpackRuntimeModules
var __webpack_exec__ = (moduleId) => (__webpack_require__(__webpack_require__.s = moduleId))
  __webpack_require__.O(0, ["npm.react","vendors-node_modules_pnpm_react-dom_18_3_1_react_18_3_1_node_modules_react-dom_index_js"], () => (__webpack_exec__("./src/index.js")));
var __webpack_exports__ = __webpack_require__.O();
}
```
- But here, the `__webpack_require__` is from inside `mfeAAA.js`, and it does not define `__webpack_require__.O



**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->



**Other relevant information:**

webpack version:  5.95.0
Node.js version:
Operating System:

**Why would use self module federation?**

In some micro-front-end scenarios，appA will be embedded in appB, and appB will use the module federation of appA

The above example is not realistic; module federation in Webpack will not have a configuration that uses itself. I'm referring to the situation where mfeAAA.js and runtime.js are loaded together at runtime

title: Potential ReDoS Vulnerability or Inefficient Regular Expression in Project: Need for Assessment and Mitigation
body:
Hello,

I am writing to report a potential Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) vulnerability or Inefficient Regular Expression in the project. When using specially crafted input strings in the context, it may lead to extremely high CPU usage, application freezing, or denial of service attacks.

**Location of Issue:**

The vulnerability is related to a regular expression used in the following validation file, which may result in significantly prolonged execution times under certain conditions.

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/001cab14692eb9a833c6b56709edbab547e291a1/lib/util/semver.js#L221

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/001cab14692eb9a833c6b56709edbab547e291a1/examples/build-http/webpack.lock.data/https_cdn.skypack.dev/aggregate-error_v4.0.0-rCH8s5R9g4kQQ807o58j_dist_es2020_mode_imports_optimized_aggregate-error_12f7879e59421c0b09bf.js#L25

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/001cab14692eb9a833c6b56709edbab547e291a1/examples/build-http/webpack.lock.data/https_cdn.esm.sh/v53_aggregate-error_4.0.0_es2015_aggregate-error_ff6bcc1ba33bf3b1810a.js#L2

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/001cab14692eb9a833c6b56709edbab547e291a1/lib/util/conventions.js#L54

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/001cab14692eb9a833c6b56709edbab547e291a1/lib/MultiCompiler.js#L165

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/001cab14692eb9a833c6b56709edbab547e291a1/lib/ProgressPlugin.js#L66

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/001cab14692eb9a833c6b56709edbab547e291a1/examples/template-common.js#L36

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/001cab14692eb9a833c6b56709edbab547e291a1/lib/util/semver.js#L204


**PoC Files and Comparisons:**

[PoC_1.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/17352850/PoC_1.zip)
[PoC_2.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/17352851/PoC_2.zip)
[PoC_3.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/17352844/PoC_3.zip)
[PoC_4.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/17352845/PoC_4.zip)
[PoC_5.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/17352846/PoC_5.zip)
[PoC_6.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/17352847/PoC_6.zip)
[PoC_7.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/17352848/PoC_7.zip)
[PoC_8.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/17352849/PoC_8.zip)




To evaluate the performance of this inefficient regular expression matching with varying input contents, the following commands can be executed within every PoC_i folder:

```bash
$ npm install # Install necessary dependencies for the minimal proof of concept environment.
$ time node poc.js # Run the script with maliciously constructed string and record the running time.
$ time node normal_string.js # Run the script with normal strings of same length and record the running time.
```

In the most severe case, on my machine, the maliciously crafted string took the following time, and caused CPU usage to reach 98% during program execution:

```
real    1m36.146s
user    1m36.103s
sys     0m0.020s
```

However, a normal string of the same length only took the following time:

```
real    0m0.230s
user    0m0.118s
sys     0m0.010s
```

This reveals a significant efficiency problem with the regular expression used in the program under certain conditions.

**Proposed Solution:**

A simple strategy could be to limit the length of the string being matched by the regular expression, thereby preventing excessive time consumption during regex matching. To completely avoid the issue, the pathological part of the regular expression that causes catastrophic backtracking should be modified.

**Background Information:**

Here are some real-world examples of issues caused by ReDoS vulnerabilities:

1. In 2019, Cloudflare experienced a service disruption lasting approximately 27 minutes due to a ReDoS vulnerability that allowed crafted input to overwhelm regex processing, resulting in significant performance degradation and temporary service outage (source: [Cloudflare Incident Report](https://blog.cloudflare.com/details-of-the-cloudflare-outage-on-july-2-2019/)).
2. Stack Overflow was affected by a ReDoS vulnerability in 2016, causing multiple instances of service degradation and temporary outages of up to 34 minutes during peak traffic periods due to inefficient regular expression patterns (source: [Stack Overflow Incident Report](http://stackstatus.net/post/147710624694/outage-postmortem-july-20-2016)).

Thank you for your attention to this matter. Your evaluation and response to this potential security concern would be greatly appreciated.

Best regards,

my webpack config:

title: Parameters on `SyncBailHook` type for `compiler.entryOption` seem incorrect
body:
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/abe931a19b1046ad934b31179d91ccac73c2052c/lib/Compiler.js#L217-L218

Going by webpack documentation https://webpack.js.org/contribute/writing-a-plugin/#synchronous-hooks

And also the code within tapable https://github.com/webpack/tapable/blob/master/lib/SyncBailHook.js#L14-L17

it seems like returning `undefined` or `void` should be the typical mode of any callback attached to this hook. but, the exported type forbids this by requiring a `boolean` return.

experimentially, the only way to tap this hook without breaking the compilation is to return `undefined`.
author: turbocrime

title: Webpack doesn't support "fs" methods like "readdirSync" on Node.js targets.
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

When bundling a Node.js application with Webpack, it fails to support `fs` methods like `readdirSync`, causing the application to break in production when attempting to read files from a folder dynamically.

For example, the following code works fine in development but breaks after bundling:

```javascript
import path from 'node:path';
import fs from 'node:fs';

const files = fs.readdirSync(path.join(import.meta.dirname, 'functions'));

files.forEach((file) => import(path.join(import.meta.dirname, 'functions', file)).then((module) => module.default()));
```

The error typically seen is related to Webpack failing to detect or bundle the folder or its files properly.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

1. Create a Node.js project with Webpack bundling.
2. Use `fs.readdirSync()` to read files dynamically from a folder.
3. Bundle the project using Webpack.
4. Run the bundled code in a production environment.

Here is a minimal reproduction:

```javascript
// index.js
import path from 'node:path';
import fs from 'node:fs';

const files = fs.readdirSync(path.join(import.meta.dirname, 'functions'));

files.forEach((file) => import(path.join(import.meta.dirname, 'functions', file)).then((module) => module.default()));
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

Webpack should be able to bundle and handle file system operations such as reading directories (`fs.readdirSync()`) without breaking the functionality in production builds. Ideally, the app should dynamically import files from a folder at runtime without requiring additional workarounds or hacks.

**Other relevant information:**

Webpack version: 5.95.0  
Node.js version: 22.10.0
Operating System: Windows 11  
Additional tools: N/A
author: amirfarzamnia

title: Allow public path to be a function
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

Allow the public path to be a function.

**What is the expected behavior?**

It should call the function everytime instead of ` + `.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

https://developer.chrome.com/docs/extensions/reference/manifest/web-accessible-resources

There is an option called "use_dynamic_url" in Chrome extension.

Normally, resources can be accessed via URLs like `chrome-extension://odhaijconicohjickedigmojjmeblalp/my-file.js`, but with this option on (this options improves security and privacy), it has to be `chrome.runtime.getURL('my-file.js')`, and the return URL becomes `chrome-extension://21f06455-6875-452b-8132-01268c50ba0e/my-file.js` where the UUID is random every time.

It will be easier to support this use case in a plugin (https://github.com/awesome-webextension/webpack-target-webextension/issues/47) if the public path can be a function.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Add a new webpack internal function:

```js
__webpack__require__.xxx = function (path) {
    if (typeof __webpack_require__.p == 'function') return __webpack_require__.p(path)
    return __webpack_require.p + path
}
```

And replace all direct use of `RuntimeGlobals.publicPath` to call this helper function.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

In some conditions, `webpack` does not output an async chunk for a module imported with `import()`, but merges it with split chunks.

1. `git clone https://github.com/cdoublev/webpack-issue`
2. `npm i`
3. `npm run build`

`./dist` will contain `common.js`, `main.js`, `runtime.js`.

If you remove at least one `1` (byte) in `src/pages/home.js` and retry `npm run build`, or if you revert the change from #18750 (included in `webpack@5.95.0`), `./dist` will also contain `pages/home.js`.

Hi team, I just bumped my webpack from 5.95.0 to the newly released 5.96.0 and this has resulted in increased build times from 2-3minutes to ~15minutes for my React SPA. I first noticed this in my automated deployment pipelines in Bitbucket, but the same thing is noticed on my local dev machine. 

I downgraded back to 5.95.0 and the builds returned to the usual times, so definitely something to do with 5.96.0. 

Happy to help with debugging this - please let me know how to setup my environment to provide you with required logs. 

webpack.config.ts:

```

  const publicUrlOrPath = getPublicUrlOrPath(process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development', undefined, process.env.PUBLIC_URL)

  //set whether are creating source maps with prod builds
  // eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-inferrable-types

  const prod = process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'

  return {
    mode: prod ? 'production' : 'development',
    cache: { type: 'filesystem' },
    infrastructureLogging: { level: 'info' },
    stats: 'normal',
    // eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-unnecessary-condition
    devtool: !prod ? (genSourceMaps ? 'source-map' : false) : false, //https://webpack.js.org/configuration/devtool/
    bail: false, //this needs to be set to false for dev builds otherwise dev-server will break every time an import can't be resolved
    resolve: {
      modules: [resolve(__dirname, 'node_modules'), resolve(__dirname, './src')]
    },
    entry: './src/index.tsx',
    output: {
      path: resolve(__dirname, 'build'),
      publicPath: publicUrlOrPath,
      filename: prod ? '[name].[contenthash:8].js' : undefined,
      chunkFilename: prod ? '[name].[contenthash:8].chunk.js' : undefined,

            !prod && require.resolve('style-loader'),

                  manifest[file.name] = file.path

                }, seed)

                return {

        : []),
```

title: @types/eslint-scope needs to be made a proper dependency in webpack@5.96
body:
# Bug report

Starting in webpack@5.96.0, the `types.d.ts` file in the package root includes the following import:

```typescript
import { Scope } from "eslint-scope";
```

`eslint-scope` itself is in webpack's `dependencies`, so there's no runtime problem. But `eslint-scope` doesn't ship its own types - those live in the `@types/eslint-scope` package. And _that_ package is one of webpack's `devDependencies`. Consequently, packages that depend on webpack and interact with it via TypeScript might witness compilation errors.

**What is the current behavior?**

Compilation error.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

```bash
$ npm init --yes
$ npm install @types/node webpack typescript
$ cat > tsconfig.json <<EOF
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "es2016",
    "module": "commonjs",
    "strict": true,
    "types": ["node"]
  }
}
EOF
$ echo "import { webpack } from 'webpack';" > index.ts
$ npx tsc
```

Result:

```
node_modules/webpack/types.d.ts:8:23 - error TS7016: Could not find a declaration file for module 'eslint-scope'. '[...]/node_modules/eslint-scope/lib/index.js' implicitly has an 'any' type.
  Try `npm i --save-dev @types/eslint-scope` if it exists or add a new declaration (.d.ts) file containing `declare module 'eslint-scope';`

8 import { Scope } from "eslint-scope";
                        ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~


Found 1 error in node_modules/webpack/types.d.ts:8
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

No compilation error. `@types/eslint-scope` should be added to webpack's `dependencies`.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.96.0
Node.js version: 22.11.0

Workarounds:
* Add `@types/eslint-scope` as a top-level dependency of your own package.
* Enable `skipLibCheck` in tsconfig.json.

title: Variable name conflict between bundled module and webpack internal variables when concatenateModules is enabled
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When concatenateModules is enabled in Webpack’s production build, some modules are inlined directly into the bundle. This causes variable names from the included module to conflict with Webpack’s internal variable names, specifically the i variable in this case. The resulting bundle outputs an error:

```javascript
var __webpack_export_target__ = exports;
for(var i in __webpack_exports__) __webpack_export_target__[i] = __webpack_exports__[i];
if(__webpack_exports__.__esModule) Object.defineProperty(__webpack_export_target__, "__esModule", { value: true });
/******/ })();
```

Error in bundled file:
```javascript
Cannot redeclare block-scoped variable 'i'.ts(2451)
(local var) i: string
```
This error arises from the i variable declared in an imported module (ruleset.js in this case), which conflicts with Webpack’s internal loop variable.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

1.	Enable concatenateModules in Webpack’s production build configuration.
2.	Include a module (e.g., ruleset.js from @aws-sdk/client-lambda) that declares a variable (i in this case) with the same name as an internal variable in Webpack’s output.
3.	Build the project with Webpack, then inspect the bundled file and observe the variable name collision.

Relevant code from ruleset.js:
```javascript
;// ./node_modules/@aws-sdk/client-lambda/dist-es/endpoint/ruleset.js
const s = "required", t = "fn", u = "argv", v = "ref";
const a = true, b = "isSet", c = "booleanEquals", d = "error", e = "endpoint", f = "tree", g = "PartitionResult", h = { [s]: false, "type": "String" }, i = { [s]: true, "default": false, "type": "Boolean" }, ...
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

Webpack should ensure that internal variables do not collide with variables in bundled modules. A recommended fix would be to prefix internal variables with a unique identifier, such as `__webpack_i__`, to prevent such conflicts.

**Other relevant information:**
- webpack version:  v5.96.1
- Node.js version: v22.11.0
- Operating System: MacOS
- Additional tools: N/A

title: when using `outputModule` library entry files may export additional names
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

The `ModuleChunkFormatPlugin` may create arbitrary file-level names when formatting an ESM chunk.

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/31bd6a2d26308c63dfda8d0744139e6211a02fd2/lib/esm/ModuleChunkFormatPlugin.js#L58-L74

In all cases this has the potential to cause some name shadowing issues because the names are common words.

When a chunk is a library entrypoint and also uses a separate runtime chunk, this can cause a `SyntaxError` due to name collision.

Export statements in the user's code attempting to export the names `id`, `ids`, or `modules` will collide with the formatter's names at the file level.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

## input files

```js
// webpack.config.js
import path from "node:path";
export default {
  experiments: { outputModule: true },
  entry: "./src/index.js",
  output: {
    library: { type: "module" },
    path: path.resolve(import.meta.dirname, "dist"),
  },
  optimization: {
    runtimeChunk: 'single', // any value other than `false`
    minimize: false, // for legibility. no effect
  },
};
```

```js
// src/index.js
export const id = "collision";
```

## output file

```js
// dist/main.mjs
export const id = 792; // this is the template's generated export of id
export const ids = [792];
export const modules = {

/***/ 173:
/***/ ((__unused_webpack___webpack_module__, __webpack_exports__, __webpack_require__) => {

/* harmony export */ __webpack_require__.d(__webpack_exports__, {
/* harmony export */   id: () => (/* binding */ id)
/* harmony export */ });
const id = "collision";

/***/ })

};
;

// load runtime
import __webpack_require__ from "./runtime.mjs";
var __webpack_exec__ = (moduleId) => (__webpack_require__(__webpack_require__.s = moduleId))
import * as __webpack_chunk_0__ from "./main.mjs";
__webpack_require__.C(__webpack_chunk_0__);
var __webpack_exports__ = __webpack_exec__(173);
var __webpack_exports__id = __webpack_exports__.id;
export { __webpack_exports__id as id }; // this duplicated export of `id` is a `SyntaxError`
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

- `ModuleChunkFormatPlugin` should not use names that are at high risk of collision
- Emitted library entry files should not export names unless requested by the developer

Additionally, it seems strange that the output file imports itself, but that doesn't seem to cause a problem.

The example given is a minimal reproduction. I encountered this while working on some complex codesplitting for a browser extension. Maybe the runtime chunk feature is not intended to be combined with the library feature, but i didn't see that suggested anywhere.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.96.1
Node.js version: 23
author: turbocrime

title: The ModuleFederationPlugin does not support _webpack_public_path__
body:

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

The ModuleFederationPlugin does not support __webpack_public_path__, and the generated remote-entry.js does not include __webpack_require__. p = __webpack_public_path__, but instead has a fixed __webpack_require__.p = publicPath
author: yangdan8

title: CSS Module Order Changes When Using `sideEffects: false` with Barrel Files
body:
#### Connected Issues
- #7094 (CSS Order Differs Between Development & Production Modes when Treeshaking)

#### Description
When using CSS Modules in a monorepo setup with barrel files (index.ts) and `sideEffects: false`, webpack produces an incorrect CSS ordering in the output bundle. This might be connected to the long-standing CSS ordering issues with tree-shaking

#### Key Findings
1. The issue is specific to webpack 5 - other bundlers (rspack, vite, parcel) handle this correctly (see links in the end)
2. The problem occurs when all these conditions are met:
   - Using barrel files (index.ts) for imports
   - Having `sideEffects: false` in package.json
   - Using CSS modules (probably also normal css)
   - Being in a monorepo setup
3. Setting `"sideEffects": ["*.css"]` does NOT fix the issue - it behaves the same as `"sideEffects": false`

After some debugging we traced it to the build chunk graph logic:

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/5e21745e98eb90a029e1f5374d4e4ac338fbe7c7/lib/buildChunkGraph.js#L683-L708


```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph "No Barrel ✅"
        A3["@applications/base/src/index.ts preOrder: 0, postOrder: 6"]
        B3["@libraries/teaser/src/teaser.ts preOrder: 1, postOrder: 5"]
        E3["@segments/carousel/src/buttons.ts preOrder: 2, postOrder: 2"]
        F3["@segments/carousel/src/button.module.css preOrder: 3, postOrder: 1"]
        G3["button.module.css|0|||}} preOrder: 4, postOrder: 0"]
        H3["@libraries/teaser/src/teaser.module.css preOrder: 5, postOrder: 4"]
        I3["teaser.module.css|0|||}} preOrder: 6, postOrder: 3"]
        
        A3 --> B3
        B3 --> E3
        E3 --> F3
        F3 --> G3
        B3 --> H3
        H3 --> I3
        style A3 fill:#0a0a4a,stroke:#333
        style F3 fill:#294b51,stroke:#333
        style G3 fill:#294b51,stroke:#333
        style H3 fill:#294b51,stroke:#333
        style I3 fill:#294b51,stroke:#333
    end


    subgraph "sideEffects: true ✅"
        A2["@applications/base/src/index.ts preOrder: 0, postOrder: 8"]
        B2["@libraries/teaser/src/index.ts preOrder: 1, postOrder: 7"]
        C2["@libraries/teaser/src/teaser.ts preOrder: 2, postOrder: 6"]
        D2["@segments/carousel/src/index.ts preOrder: 3, postOrder: 3"]
        E2["@segments/carousel/src/buttons.ts preOrder: 4, postOrder: 2"]
        F2["@segments/carousel/src/button.module.css preOrder: 5, postOrder: 1"]
        G2["button.module.css|0|||}} preOrder: 6, postOrder: 0"]
        H2["@libraries/teaser/src/teaser.module.css preOrder: 7, postOrder: 5"]
        I2["teaser.module.css|0|||}} preOrder: 8, postOrder: 4"]
        
        A2 --> B2
        B2 --> C2
        C2 --> D2
        D2 --> E2
        E2 --> F2
        F2 --> G2
        C2 --> H2
        H2 --> I2

        style A2 fill:#0a0a4a,stroke:#333
        style F2 fill:#294b51,stroke:#333
        style G2 fill:#294b51,stroke:#333
        style H2 fill:#294b51,stroke:#333
        style I2 fill:#294b51,stroke:#333
    end

    subgraph "sideEffects: false ❌"
        A1["@applications/base/src/index.ts preOrder: 0, postOrder: 6"]
        B1["@libraries/teaser/src/teaser.ts preOrder: 1, postOrder: 5"]
        C1["@libraries/teaser/src/teaser.module.css preOrder: 2, postOrder: 1"]
        D1["teaser.module.css|0|||}} preOrder: 3, postOrder: 0"]
        E1["@segments/carousel/src/buttons.ts preOrder: 4, postOrder: 4"]
        F1["@segments/carousel/src/button.module.css preOrder: 5, postOrder: 3"]
        G1["button.module.css|0|||}} preOrder: 6, postOrder: 2"]
        
        A1 --> B1
        B1 --> C1
        C1 --> D1
        B1 --> E1
        E1 --> F1
        F1 --> G1

        style A1 fill:#0a0a4a,stroke:#333
        style C1 fill:#294b51,stroke:#333
        style D1 fill:#294b51,stroke:#333
        style F1 fill:#294b51,stroke:#333
        style G1 fill:#294b51,stroke:#333
    end
```

#### Working Variations

There are two workarounds:

-  Set `"sideEffects": true` in package.json (unfortunately this will [break chunk splitting](https://gist.github.com/jantimon/10b87c762e77dc31818920b5a1604c3e))

OR

- Import directly from implementation file instead of barrel:
   ```ts
   // Instead of: import { CarouselButton } from '@segments/carousel';
   import { CarouselButton } from '@segments/carousel/buttons';
   ```

But I believe we should fix it in webpack because it works in all other bundlers I tried (rspack, vite, parcel)

## Reproduction
Full reproduction repository: https://github.com/jantimon/reproduction-webpack-css-order

The issue can be reproduced by:
1. Having this import structure:
```ts
// @libraries/teaser/src/teaser.ts
import { CarouselButton } from '@segments/carousel'; // via barrel file
import styles from './teaser.module.css';
```

2. Building with `sideEffects: false` produces incorrect CSS order ❌

The repository has dist files checked in so you can see the full [main.css](https://github.com/jantimon/reproduction-webpack-css-order/blob/main/%40applications/base/dist/main.css)

```css
.hDE5PT5V3QGAPX9o9iZl { ... }
.yqrxTjAG22vkATE1VjR9 { background-color: orange; }  /* ❌ Should be last */
.R_y25aX9lTSLQtlxA1c9 { ... }
```

3. Building with `"sideEffects": ["*.css"]` produces incorrect CSS order ❌

See also [main.css](https://github.com/jantimon/reproduction-webpack-css-order/blob/css-side-effects/%40applications/base/dist/main.css)

```css
.hDE5PT5V3QGAPX9o9iZl { ... }
.yqrxTjAG22vkATE1VjR9 { background-color: orange; }  /* ❌ Should be last */
.R_y25aX9lTSLQtlxA1c9 { ... }
```

4. Building with `sideEffects: true` produces correct order ✅

See also [main.css](https://github.com/jantimon/reproduction-webpack-css-order/blob/side-effects-true/@applications/base/dist/main.css)

```css
.R_y25aX9lTSLQtlxA1c9 { ... }
.hDE5PT5V3QGAPX9o9iZl { ... }
.yqrxTjAG22vkATE1VjR9 { background-color: orange; }
```

5. Building without a barrel file with `sideEffects: false` also produces correct order  ✅

See also [main.css](https://github.com/jantimon/reproduction-webpack-css-order/blob/no-barell/%40applications/base/dist/main.css)

```css
.R_y25aX9lTSLQtlxA1c9 { ... }
.hDE5PT5V3QGAPX9o9iZl { ... }
.yqrxTjAG22vkATE1VjR9 { background-color: orange; }
```



## Comparison with Other Bundlers

Each bundler repository branch contains the same code structure and dependencies, only changing the build configuration for the respective bundler:

- [css](https://github.com/jantimon/reproduction-webpack-css-order/blob/rspack/%40applications/base/dist/main.css) built by rspack ✅
- [css](https://github.com/jantimon/reproduction-webpack-css-order/blob/vite/%40applications/base/dist/index.css) built by vite ✅
- [css](https://github.com/jantimon/reproduction-webpack-css-order/blob/parcel/%40applications/base/dist/index.5ff2b6c6.css) by parcel ✅

## Environment
- Webpack 5.96.1
- Node.js >= 20
- pnpm 8.15.4

title: `ModernModuleLibraryPlugin` attempts undefined access when handling any module that is not `ConcatenatedModule`
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

`ModernModuleLibraryPlugin.renderStartup` assumes that every input module is `ConcatenatedModule`, which is not guaranteed. This will error compilation when any output file represents a single chunk and a single input module.

This is quite likely if there are any:
- shared chunks
- workers
- dynamic imports
- imports with top-level async

## What is the current behavior?

the meta `exportsFinalName` is only created when a module is `ConcatenatedModule`

the name is mentioned in only 2 files: https://github.com/search?q=repo%3Awebpack%2Fwebpack+exportsFinalName&type=code

so `renderStartup` performs assignment of a potentially undefined property to `definitions`

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/da41ad1845947139375fb557107fa8bd2f6f8f27/lib/library/ModernModuleLibraryPlugin.js#L88-L99

and later performs unsafe index access of `definitions` 

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/da41ad1845947139375fb557107fa8bd2f6f8f27/lib/library/ModernModuleLibraryPlugin.js#L124-L128

## What is the expected behavior?

The plugin should not fail to output single-chunk files.

title: Provide a way to configure an entry to use dynamic `async import` expressions without also using `import` and `export` statements
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

I would like to be able to use `async import` while forbidding `import` and `export` from appearing in some output entry files.

### What is the present behavior?

Configuring webpack to output files with `await import()` requires the `outputModule` experiment and `chunkFormat: "module"`, which assumes `import` and `export` are permitted.

So presently, even if every import from the original code is an async import, webpack will use static `import` statements for things like the runtime, and an (unnecessary?) self-referencing import in entry files.

And `export` statements are generated even when an output module contained no export statements in its original source, and the output module will never be imported by another script, such as when the output is an application entry responsible for instantiating the runtime and providing its own content to the runtime.

### What is the expected behavior?

1. Webpack should be configurable to format outputs with `await import()` expressions, while disabling creation of static `import` statements.

2. Webpack should avoid creating `export` statements in outputs that did not contain `export` statements in their original source.

3. Webpack should not create `export` statements for `__webpack_id__`, `__webpack_ids__`, or `__webpack_modules__` when outputting an initial chunk that will instantiate the runtime and provide its own content to the runtime.

### What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?

Chrome extension content scripts suppport use of `async import`, and may import esm modules, but they do not support use of static `import` or `export` statements. 

I'd like to share chunks between a browser extension background (supports all module syntax) and content scripts (supports only `await import`).

### How should this be implemented in your opinion?

#### environment feature flags

Possibly add fields to the webpack configuration option `environment` to identify environments that support import statements, dynamic import expressions, and export statements, instead of collecting the support less specifically under options `envrionment.module` `environment.dynamicImport`

#### chunkLoading member

Possibly, since `chunkLoading` is configurable per-entry by `EntryDescription`, add a new member of `ChunkLoadingType` such as `'async-import'` that will instruct the formatter to prefer import expressions over import statements.

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/3b2db9ba5013f90950d47c7a051e4abe323117f3/declarations/WebpackOptions.d.ts#L70-L75

Then, `ModuleChunkFormatPlugin` may format output according to this option when importing chunks, including the runtime chunk if necessary.

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/3b2db9ba5013f90950d47c7a051e4abe323117f3/lib/esm/ModuleChunkFormatPlugin.js#L133-L140

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/3b2db9ba5013f90950d47c7a051e4abe323117f3/lib/esm/ModuleChunkFormatPlugin.js#L161-L172

#### avoid emitting unnecessary export statements

additionally, when creating `__webpack_id__`, `__webpack_ids__`, `__webpack_modules__` etc it may not be necessary to export them, if webpack knows that this file is not imported elsewhere.

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/a24e4fdd6e83992c7b017cfabafa82da72a1e4ce/lib/esm/ModuleChunkFormatPlugin.js#L59-L67

### Are you willing to work on this yourself?

yes. please let me know if this is appreciated, or considered not useful

**What is the current behavior?**
All variables exported from an ESM that contains a top-level awaited dynamic import are undefined.

```js
if(isElectron){
   var { IPC } = await import('../../ENV/electron')
}

   IPC?.on('json',() => {...})
}
```

This case demonstrates that some logic depends on a dynamic module, and `something` is provided by ProvidePlugin, but it's `undefined` at runtime. This issue can be resolved by replacing `await import()` with `require()`, but this makes the ESM code impure.

title: Webpack Aliases: Update documentation with warning about '#' alias prefix
body:
Request: Update the Webpack documentation to include an explicit warning about using the '#' symbol as a prefix for aliases, specifically for Sass files; 

Potential locations for including warning: 
https://webpack.js.org/configuration/resolve/#resolvealias
https://webpack.js.org/loaders/sass-loader/#root

Took a few hours to track down my issue, but I was using '#' as a prefix for aliases and this was generating 'module already loaded' errors in Webpack compilation when I updated the Webpack configuration (sass-loader) to explicitly use the 'api: modern/modern-compiler' option, in preparation for Dart-Sass 2.0. The 'legacy' API generates warnings about future deprecation, with Dart-Sass 2.0, but it does not throw errors or hint at the underlying issue.

The issue has been discussed in these threads - '#' is parsed as a URL fragment and not a resource.
https://github.com/sass/sass/issues/3958
https://github.com/webpack-contrib/sass-loader/issues/1205
author: SStranks

I tried disabling a few parts of our config and wasn't able to pinpoint exactly what the issue was. I think we have a pretty standard config file with multiple entries, typescript, babel, scss, images, fonts, etc.

author: Trinovantes

title: Q: How do I externalize a module within a monorepo into its own chunk?
body:
I have a monorepo setup split something like this
```
- main
- plugins
```

Where main holds projects such as 
```
- core
- state
// and so on
```

While plugin holds different apps. Each app can be built on its own, those apps do not depend one on another, their only dependencies point to main (such as `main/core`, `main/state` and so on).
Now each plugin outputs an entry point, let's call it `entry.js` - the problem with this entry is that it appends the whole code for `main/core` for example.

What's the best way to tell webpack that `main/core` is sort of an external and it should go on its own chunk? 

I added `main/core` to the list of externals, all good, it's now no longer in the final bundle, but it complains it's missing at runtime (which makes sense so far). But then I tried (what I thought it'd be the remaining part) to add that as a chunk separation logic within `splitChunks`

```
core: {
          chunks: 'all',
          name: 'core.js',
          test: (module) => {
            // if I console.log here, it does go through this, but the resulting chunk is nowhere to be seen
            return /[\\/]main[\\/]core[\\/]/.test(module.resource);
          },
          priority: 50,
          enforce: true,
        },
```

But for some reason, it skips creating this chunk entirely - or at least it's not creating it under this name, but instead it adds it bundled with some other chunk - in this case, how can I reference that chunk? 

How do I tell webpack that the externalized module `main/core` is the bundle webpack created it? (although it's not under the name I wanted it to be)

Running out of ideas, I'd appreciate any links/samples that can help me substract this dependency from the bundle

reproduce:

title: output "Last few GCs,JavaScript heap out of heap" when build 20 thousands pages
body:
build machine: linux 8core 32G memory
vue-cli project

Project npm package:

**vue3: 3.2.37
webpack: 5.89.0
@vue/cli-service: 5.0.8
vue-loader：17.4.2
node: 16.10.0**

In our project,we have **twenty thousands vue pages** and **five thousands js modules**.These pages is dynamic imported to the <component > with :is attribute,that is dynamic component in vue,and we build these pages to independent async chunks in webpack.But now, the project was build failed due to "JavaScript heap out of heap".we have adopt some solutions,such as,

(1) add the node memory to 16G/24G through "cross-env NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=16384";
(2)upgrade node version to 20 and reinstall relevant package;
(3)using webpack build instead of using vue-cli-service build,in order to avoiding extra plugins to slowing the build;
(4)use the cache.cacheDirectory options in webpack5;
(5)use DLLPlugin and DLLReferencePlugin to build the node_modules and other common components

but the above solutions were all failed with the same reason.

**Unbelievable,when we decrease the pages number to about ten thousands,it can build success.**
In addition,our project was upgraded from vue2,in vue2,it was build success with the same scale pages and js in webpack5.

Anyone else has the webpack build experence with the huge pages,how should I do to solve this problem? thanks!

title: [Feature request] inline worker in Webpack5
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

Reference:
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/14066

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Does webpack team have any plan for supporting inline worker?

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

We're implementing a parallel web rendering tools/libs, which requires to start web workers ASAP.

Using the `Blob` object to start a series of webworkers will help us to cut down the FP and TTI time.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Support the inline-worker under webpack5 worker syntax.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
no

title: No way to totally prevent chunks splitting for async modules (eg `await import('./test')`)
body:
I am developing a UserScript file.

In my entry script `main.js`, it will run code from other modules on demand, for example `if (xxx) { await import('./test.js') }`.

All of the code in `main.js` and `test.js` should be bundled together in one final output file: `my-example.user.js`, due to limit of UserScript. Dynamically loading another js file on runtime will fail.

In webpack, I tried to set `optimization.splitChunks` to `false`, or to `{chunks: () => false}`, or to `{minSize: Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER}`. But all of them, no use, no luck.
Webpack just always force split the code in test.js into a separate output file.
author: ChipZhang

title: Inline Web Workers in Webpack
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please Feature Title
Support Inline Web Workers with Advanced Configuration in Webpack

**Purpose of the Feature**
The purpose of this feature is to enable Webpack to support inline web workers directly, providing developers with a streamlined way to create and manage workers without needing separate files. This feature would simplify the development process and improve application performance by reducing the overhead of fetching additional worker files.

**Why This Feature is Needed**
Performance Improvements:

Inline web workers eliminate the need for additional HTTP requests to fetch worker files, reducing load times.
By creating workers dynamically, developers can optimize First Paint (FP) and Time to Interactive (TTI) metrics, crucial for modern web applications.
Ease of Use:

Managing worker files separately can be cumbersome, especially in large projects. Inline workers simplify this process by embedding worker code directly within the main application code.
Real-Time Applications:

Applications like rendering engines, simulations, or data processing tools benefit significantly from the ability to create workers dynamically.
Modern Use Cases:

Features like dynamic imports and caching can enhance the usability of inline workers, making them more versatile for real-world scenarios.
**Proposed Design and Solution**
1. Webpack Worker Syntax Extension
Extend Webpack’s existing Worker syntax to accept inline worker code as a string or template literal.
Automatically handle the conversion of inline code into a Blob and generate a URL using URL.createObjectURL.
2. Example Implementation
Here’s how developers could use the proposed feature:

javascript
Copy code
const worker = new Worker(`
    onmessage = function (e) {
        postMessage(e.data * 2);
    };
`, { type: 'inline' });

worker.postMessage(10);
worker.onmessage = (e) => console.log(e.data); // Output: 20
3. **Advanced Configuration Options**
Caching: Allow caching of inline worker code for reuse across multiple instances.
Dynamic Imports: Enable support for dynamic imports within inline worker code.
Example:
javascript
Copy code
const worker = new Worker(`
    importScripts('https://example.com/utility.js');
    onmessage = function (e) {
        postMessage(processData(e.data));
    };
`, { type: 'inline' });
4. Webpack Configuration
Developers could enable this feature using a configuration option in their webpack.config.js file:

javascript
Copy code
module.exports = {
    mode: 'development',
    worker: {
        inline: true, // Enable inline worker support
        caching: true, // Enable caching for inline workers
    },
};
5. Handling Errors and Compatibility
Ensure that inline worker support gracefully falls back to traditional worker files if the environment doesn’t support Blob or URL.createObjectURL.
make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->


**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes
author: sneha4080

title: web worked inline in webpac
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

****#**# Feature request******
**Support Inline Web Workers with Advanced Configuration in Webpack**


<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Why This Feature is Needed

Performance Improvements:
Inline web workers eliminate the need for additional HTTP requests to fetch worker files, reducing load times.
By creating workers dynamically, developers can optimize First Paint (FP) and Time to Interactive (TTI) metrics, crucial for modern web applications.
Ease of Use:

Managing worker files separately can be cumbersome, especially in large projects. Inline workers simplify this process by embedding worker code directly within the main application code.
Real-Time Applications:

Applications like rendering engines, simulations, or data processing tools benefit significantly from the ability to create workers dynamically.
Modern Use Cases:

Features like dynamic imports and caching can enhance the usability of inline workers, making them more versatile for real-world scenarios.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

Simplified Development: Managing worker code within the main application code eliminates the need for separate worker files, making the development process more efficient and less complex.

Real-Time Application Support: Inline workers enable faster worker creation, which is essential for real-time applications like rendering engines or data processing tools.

Better Integration with Modern Features: Inline workers can be integrated with dynamic imports and caching, optimizing resource usage and reducing the need for repeated file fetching.

Enhanced Webpack Worker Syntax: Extending Webpack’s worker syntax to support inline workers would make Webpack more flexible and powerful for developers working on performance-critical project


**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
Extend Worker Syntax: Webpack should support inline worker code as a string or template literal, converting it into a Blob and generating a URL using URL.createObjectURL().

Example:

javascript
Copy code
const worker = new Worker(`
    onmessage = function (e) {
        postMessage(e.data * 2);
    };
`, { type: 'inline' });
Advanced Configuration: Allow caching and dynamic imports within inline workers. Developers can enable these features in webpack.config.js.

Example:

javascript
Copy code
module.exports = {
    worker: {
        inline: true, // Enable inline workers
        caching: true, // Enable caching
    },
};
Fallback for Compatibility: Ensure the feature gracefully falls back to traditional workers if Blob or URL.createObjectURL() is not supported.

Webpack Plugin: Extend or create a plugin to handle inline workers, wrap the code in a Blob, and inject it into the bundle.
**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes
author: sneha4080

title: Outdated eslint-scope 5.1.1 is specified in dependencies
body: # Bug report **What is the current behavior?** Our project uses a webpack package, ESLint, and some ESLint plugins. We are currently facing a dependency conflict for the **eslint-scope** package. ESLint core itself has a dependency on eslint-scope, and some of the ESLint plugins also have eslint-scope as a dependency. The current package.json of webpack specifies ESLint and its plugins as **devDependencies**. However, **eslint-scope** and **@types/eslint-scope** packages are located in **dependencies**: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/package.json So webpack is using ESLint of version ^9.12.0 and eslint-scope of version 5.1.1. At the same time, ESLint itself has a dependency on eslint-scope. ESLint is using eslint-scope of version ^8.2.0 which is not compatible with version 5.1.1: https://github.com/eslint/eslint/blob/main/package.json Note, eslint-scope of 5.1.1 was released over 4 years ago: https://www.npmjs.com/package/eslint-scope/v/5.1.1 And the current latest is 8.2.0: https://www.npmjs.com/package/eslint-scope/v/8.2.0 If we run webpack with eslint-scope 5.1.1, we get no errors. But if we force the 8.2.0 eslint-scope version, we get the following error when running webpack: ``` [webpack-cli] Error [ERR_PACKAGE_PATH_NOT_EXPORTED]: Package subpath './lib/referencer' is not defined by "exports" in \node_modules\eslint-scope\package.json at exportsNotFound (node:internal/modules/esm/resolve:296:10) at packageExportsResolve (node:internal/modules/esm/resolve:643:9) at resolveExports (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:639:36) at Module._findPath (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:747:31) at Module._resolveFilename (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1234:27) at Module._load (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1074:27) at TracingChannel.traceSync (node:diagnostics_channel:315:14) at wrapModuleLoad (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:217:24) at Module.require (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1339:12) at Module.patchedRequire [as require] (\node_modules\diagnostic-channel\dist\src\patchRequire.js:14:46) { code: 'ERR_PACKAGE_PATH_NOT_EXPORTED' } error Command failed with exit code 2. ``` 1. Is there a reason why webpack requires eslint-scope, specifically version 5.1.1? 2. Should eslint-scope be moved to devDependencies similar to the rest of the ESLint packages? Having an outdated eslint-scope of 5.1.1 breaks some of the ESLint plugins we use that rely on the latest ESLint version. **What is the expected behavior?** 1. eslint-scope should be located in devDependencies together with the rest of the eslint packages. 2. eslint-scope should be removed from the list of dependencies at all since it is automatically included in the eslint core itself and comes together with the eslint. 3. If the above is not possible, perhaps at least update it to the latest supported since the 5.1.1 version is quite old and not compatible with the latest ESLint. **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.94.0, latest 5.97.1 (same happens in both versions) Node.js version: 22 Operating System: windows Additional tools: ESLint: 9.12.0

title: Runtime executes twice with `runtimeChunk: 'single'`
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Runtime executes twice.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

```js
        optimization: {
            runtimeChunk: 'single',
            splitChunks: {
                chunks: 'all',
                name: function (module, chunks, cacheGroupKey) {
                    // Third-party module.
                    if (cacheGroupKey === 'defaultVendors') {
                        return `~m~${module.context.replace(/.*\/node_modules\/([^/]+).*/, '$1')}`;
                    }
                }
            },

        plugins: [
            new ChunksWebpackPlugin(),
        ],
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

Including the contents of `foo-scripts.html` should be the correct way to load the chunks for `foo` entrypoint. The chunk collections typically always starts with `<script defer src="{{ static }}/c/runtime.js"></script>`, but this is actually problematic because the runtime also seems to be included in the entrypoint chunk, _despite_ the fact that `runtimeChunk: 'single',` has been specified. Removing `runtime.js` from the script stack causes the entrypoint to only be run once. However, the runtime should not be included in the entrypoint when `runtimeChunk: 'single'` is specified (ergo this is a bug in webpack, not the `ChunksWebpackPlugin`).

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.97.1
Node.js version: v22.12.0
Operating System: WSL
Additional tools: ???
author: Bilge

title: `chunkLoading: false` in entry descriptor has no effect
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Based on my understanding, setting `chunkLoading: false` in an [entry descriptor](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/entry-context/#entry-descriptor) should bundle all chunks into that entry point, without any chunk loading required. However, that is not the case; it still adds chunks to that entry point.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

```js
        entry: {
            foo: {
                import: [
                    './ts/foo.ts',

                    './css/foo.less',
                    './css/bar.less',
                ],
                chunkLoading: false,
                asyncChunks: false,
                runtime: false,
            },
            ...
        }

        plugins: [
            new ChunksWebpackPlugin({
                generateChunksFiles: false,
                generateChunksManifest: true,
            }),
        ],

        optimization: {
            runtimeChunk: 'single',
            splitChunks: {
                chunks: 'all',
                minSizeReduction: 1024 * 64,
                maxInitialSize: 1024 * 512,
                name: function (module, chunks, cacheGroupKey) {
                    if (cacheGroupKey === 'defaultVendors') {
                        const packageName = module.context.replace(/.*\/node_modules\/([^/]+).*/, '$1');

                        return `m/${packageName}`;
                    }
                },
            },
        },
```

I am using [ChunksWebpackPlugin](https://github.com/yoriiis/chunks-webpack-plugin) to generate a manifest that makes it clear which chunks are included in each entry point. In my case, I end up with something like the following,

```js
  "foo": {
    "styles": [
      "foo.css"
    ],
    "scripts": [
      "m/svelte.js",
      "foo.js"
    ]
  },
```

We can see it included the `svelte.js` chunk (separately) instead of copying it into `foo.js` as directed by `chunkLoading`.

**What is the expected behavior?**

If `chunkLoading: false` is specified for an entry point, everything should be packed into that entry point such that it runs entirely independently, without the need to load any additional chunks.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.97.1
Node.js version: v22.12.0
Operating System: WSL
Additional tools:
author: Bilge

title: [harmony import] Failed to add property to namespace when the module value is a function
body:
# Bug report **What is the current behavior?** ``` target: node12.20 format: commonjs externalsPresets: { node: false, }, resolve: { fallback:{ stream: require.resove("stream-browserify") } } ``` ```ts // source code import * as Stream from 'stream' const { Writable } = Stream new Writable() ``` In my case, it was processed like: ```ts // EXTERNAL MODULE: ./node_modules/@ali/stream-browserify/index.js var stream_browserify = __webpack_require__(45367); // stream_browserify is function Stream() { // EE.call(this); // } var stream_browserify_namespaceObject = /*#__PURE__*/__webpack_require__.t(stream_browserify, 2); const { Writable } = stream_browserify_namespaceObject; new Writable() // Error ! Writeable is undefined, cause it should be access from `stream_browserify_namespaceObject.default` ``` I notice there're two webpack runtime helper code `__webpack_require__.n` and `__webpack_require__.t`:
the helper function source code

```js /* webpack/runtime/compat get default export */ /******/ (() => { /******/ // getDefaultExport function for compatibility with non-harmony modules /******/ __webpack_require__.n = (module) => { /******/ var getter = module && module.__esModule ? /******/ () => (module['default']) : /******/ () => (module); /******/ __webpack_require__.d(getter, { a: getter }); /******/ return getter; /******/ }; /******/ })(); /* webpack/runtime/create fake namespace object */ /******/ (() => { /******/ var getProto = Object.getPrototypeOf ? (obj) => (Object.getPrototypeOf(obj)) : (obj) => (obj.__proto__); /******/ var leafPrototypes; /******/ // create a fake namespace object /******/ // mode & 1: value is a module id, require it /******/ // mode & 2: merge all properties of value into the ns /******/ // mode & 4: return value when already ns object /******/ // mode & 16: return value when it's Promise-like /******/ // mode & 8|1: behave like require /******/ __webpack_require__.t = function(value, mode) { /******/ if(mode & 1) value = this(value); /******/ if(mode & 8) return value; /******/ if(typeof value === 'object' && value) { /******/ if((mode & 4) && value.__esModule) return value; /******/ if((mode & 16) && typeof value.then === 'function') return value; /******/ } /******/ var ns = Object.create(null); /******/ __webpack_require__.r(ns); /******/ var def = {}; /******/ leafPrototypes = leafPrototypes || [null, getProto({}), getProto([]), getProto(getProto)]; /******/ for(var current = mode & 2 && value; typeof current == 'object' && !~leafPrototypes.indexOf(current); current = getProto(current)) { /******/ Object.getOwnPropertyNames(current).forEach((key) => (def[key] = () => (value[key]))); /******/ } /******/ def['default'] = () => (value); /******/ __webpack_require__.d(ns, def); console.log('enter'); /******/ return ns; /******/ }; /******/ })(); ```

because `stream_browserify` is a function here, it can't match the condition(`typeof value === 'object'`) in `__webpack_require__.t`, so the `stream_browserify_namespaceObject` only has the default property. Is this a bad case in `__webpack_require__.t`? And what's the difference between `t` and `n`, it seems both of them handle namespace import **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** I try to reproduce it in a simply webpack project, but it use `.n`, so it works fine there, I haven't figured out how to create the same output **What is the expected behavior?** get full property of namespace object **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.8.22 Node.js version: 20.16.0 Operating System: Mac OS Additional tools:
