title: [bug] Non-deterministic ordering of modules in bundle output when using deterministic module IDs on different platforms
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Currently, some dependencies are bundled differently depending on the platform that the bundle operation is being performed on. In the attached minimal repro, the `jszip` package is bundled on both Linux and Windows machines. The bundle output contains the exact same content, _except_ for one specific module is being included in the bundle at a different point depending on the platform. Interestingly, the module IDs remain the same (as would be expected with deterministic module IDs), it's just the order of entry in the output bundle.

Example diff:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/79c3c3d0-753a-435a-8499-8feca0a8c36d)

Note that changing the ordering of the module entries ensures that there is no diff between the two produced bundles.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

[jszip-bundle-repro.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/18459584/jszip-bundle-repro.zip)

Using the included repro:
- `npm i`
- `npm run build` and observe the outputs on Linux
- Perform same operation on Windows
- Compare the two generated files

For convenience, I've included the bundle output in the repro zip for both Linux and Windows.

**What is the expected behavior?**

Module ordering in bundle output should be deterministic on both platforms when using deterministic module IDs

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.95.0
Node.js version: 18.19.1
Operating System: Linux/Windows
Additional tools: jszip

body:

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

I encountered a problem. When I used webpack 4.47.0 to develop an application, `webpack` replaced the `core-js` method in development mode, resulting in an error. The error message is as follows:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1af1c2b1-552e-425d-8e78-37e4272d6c56)

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/421cdc3c-d91b-437f-a389-187cbd01d1d9)

This error is caused by the fact that the modified code of `webpack` needs to use the global Object, but `core-js` declares var Object inside the function, overwriting the global Object.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->

<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

 I think `var Object` in a function is not a good practice, but solving that problem by constraining all third-party libraries is obviously hard to achieve.

So I hope `webpack` can give a suitable solution.

title: You may need an additional plugin to handle "node:" URIs
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Inability to use `unstorage fsDriver` or `unstorage fsDriverLite` due to `You may need an additional plugin to handle "node:" URIs.` 

After doing research: 
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/71041521/unhandledschemeerror-what-is-this-nodebuffer-error-and-how-do-i-solve-it
- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/13290

And attempting the solution presented:
```js
webpackConfig.plugins.push( new webpack.NormalModuleReplacementPlugin(/^node:/, (resource) => { resource.request = resource.request.replace(/^node:/, '') }) )
```

The problem goes from this:
```
 ⨯ node:fs
Module build failed: UnhandledSchemeError: Reading from "node:fs" is not handled by plugins (Unhandled scheme).
Webpack supports "data:" and "file:" URIs by default.
You may need an additional plugin to handle "node:" URIs.
Import trace for requested module:
node:fs
../../node_modules/unstorage/drivers/fs-lite.mjs
./lib/store/credentials.ts
./auth.ts
```

To that:
```
 ⨯ ../../node_modules/unstorage/drivers/fs-lite.mjs:1:1
Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'
Did you mean './fs'?
Requests that should resolve in the current directory need to start with './'.        
Requests that start with a name are treated as module requests and resolve within module directories (node_modules).
If changing the source code is not an option there is also a resolve options called 'preferRelative' which tries to resolve these kind of requests in the current directory too.
> 1 | import { existsSync, promises as fsp } from "node:fs";
    | ^
  2 | import { resolve, join } from "node:path";
  3 | import { createError, createRequiredError, defineDriver } from "./utils/index.mjs";
  4 | import {

https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/module-not-found

Import trace for requested module:
./lib/store/credentials.ts
./auth.ts
./middleware.ts
```

So I am out of ideas at this point.

Here is my `next.config.ts`
```js
import type { NextConfig } from "next";

export default {
  i18n: {
    defaultLocale: "en",
    locales: ["en", "fr"],
  },
  reactStrictMode: true,
  sassOptions: {
    silenceDeprecations: ["legacy-js-api"],
  },
  experimental: {
    optimizePackageImports: ["@mantine/core", "@mantine/hooks"],
  },
  webpack(
    config,
    { buildId, dev, isServer, defaultLoaders, nextRuntime, webpack },
  ) {
    // https://react-svgr.com/docs/next/
    {
      const fileLoaderRule = config.module.rules.find((rule: any) =>
        rule.test?.test?.(".svg"),
      );

      config.module.rules.push(
        // Reapply the existing rule, but only for svg imports ending in ?url
        {
          ...fileLoaderRule,
          test: /\.svg$/i,
          resourceQuery: /url/, // *.svg?url
        },
        // Convert all other *.svg imports to React components
        {
          test: /\.svg$/i,
          issuer: fileLoaderRule.issuer,
          resourceQuery: { not: [...fileLoaderRule.resourceQuery.not, /url/] }, // exclude if *.svg?url
          use: [
            {
              loader: "@svgr/webpack",
              options: {
                svgoConfig: {
                  plugins: [
                    {
                      name: "preset-default",
                      params: {
                        overrides: {
                          removeViewBox: false,
                        },
                      },
                    },
                  ],
                },
              },
            },
          ],
        },
      );

      // Modify the file loader rule to ignore *.svg, since we have it handled now.
      fileLoaderRule.exclude = /\.svg$/i;
    }

    config.plugins.push(
      new webpack.NormalModuleReplacementPlugin(/^node:/, (resource: any) => {
        resource.request = resource.request.replace(/^node:/, "");
      }),
    );

    return config;
  },
} satisfies NextConfig;
```

title: Import/export of module without explicit references to symbols disables export mangling for module
body:
# Bug report

_*****EDIT:**  I originally wrote that this issue was due to "barrel files" but as I was creating the repro case, I realized that isn't true.  Pure barrel files seem to work.  I've refined the issue description below._

**What is the current behavior?**

If there is a module that imports/exports another module without explicitly referencing any of the exports of that module, webpack disables export mangling for the module.

In a large code base, this creates a significant code bloat because all of the references everywhere to the exports of the modules will use the full name instead of a mangled one or two characters.  This really adds up.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

A simple repro case for this can be found here, with a description in the README: https://github.com/scameron/webpack-issue-mangling

**What is the expected behavior?**

Ideally, the export mangling would continue to be enabled even if I have modules that do not explicitly reference the exports but just "pass-through" the imports as subsequent exports.  I can't think of any reason why this case would need to disable export mangling, but maybe I'm missing something.

I took a quick look at the code, but I'm not sure this is correct.  Here's what I found though:

In the `FlagDependencyUsagePlugin`, where it analysis dependencies between modules and determines whether or not export mangling should be used or not used, it has a check to see if the `usedExports` array is empty, meaning that the module being processed does explicitly use any of the exports of the dependency module being considered.  If this is true, it will call `SetUsedInUnknownWay` on the export info, which internally sets `canMangleUse` to false.

For reference:

* The `FlagDependencyUsagePlugin`code that checks for empty "used exports" during dependency processing is here: [FlagDependencyUsagePlugin.processReferencedModule](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/FlagDependencyUsagePlugin.js#L92)
* The `ExportInfo` code that sets `canMangleUse` to false is here: [ExportsInfo.setUsedInUnknownWay](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/ExportsInfo.js#L369)

title: `--env-file` for webpack CLI
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

NodeJS recently supported new `--env-file` flag: https://nodejs.org/en/learn/command-line/how-to-read-environment-variables-from-nodejs. I believe similar API could be a great addition to webpack CLI.

Currently, it's possible to achieve the same behavior using one of the following approaches: 
- `node --env-file .env node_modules/webpack-cli/bin/cli.js` - exposes internal structure of webpack cli package
- `NODE_ENV=production PUBLIC_PATH=http://locahost:8080 ... npx webpack` - will pollute the scripts section when many env variables are used
- use https://www.npmjs.com/package/dotenv on build time. This was the "common" approach for Node apps, but it seems that more and more projects use the native way of handling env files now.

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

`npx webpack --env-file=.env.webpack` would make env variables accessible on build time (in webpack configuration)

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

It would be great to avoid using extra packages for handling env files, especially when Node already supports that.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

N/A

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**

N/A

title: Importing both JS and assets in the same entry breaks the runtime
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When the runtime is enabled, a single entry with both JS and non-JS imports will fail to load.

The runtime fails with the following error when trying to locate the module:

```
Uncaught TypeError: __webpack_modules__[moduleId] is not a function
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

Simply importing both JS and assets in a single entry will break Webpack runtime:

```js
module.exports = {
    mode: 'production',
    entry: {
        app: {
            import: ['./app.js', './image.png'],
        },
    },
}
```

It makes very little sense when using Webpack, but was still found in the wild : https://github.com/Mailu/Mailu/blob/6b470ac4038f5981e2106e8f0cfc8acdbe9825b0/core/admin/assets/webpack.config.js#L12.

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

I would say the behavior is expected: since 5.96.0, Webpack does not generate JS anymore for static assets, which makes sense. This was introduced in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/commit/420d0d0eed79b0c300f18466a47ed7bd5f4ff14b and already discussed in separate issues here: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/18928.

Of course we could fix the runtime to not try to load modules for static assets that do not have any JS generated, which is already fine if the entry has *none*. I do not think it is necessary though, and I would rather make it clear in the documentation that this is not a supported use-case: static assets should either be referenced in JS *or* imported in their own entry.

My recommended fix would be a mention in the docs.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: > 5.96.0
Node.js version: v23.4.0
Operating System: any
Additional tools: -

body:

# Bug report

<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->

**What is the current behavior?**
webpack treats relative context required files as modules when it match the `module` configuration , which is inconsistent with import statement

```javascript
// file structure
// ./src/mods
//      ./1.js
//      ./2.js
// index.js

// configuration
resolve.modules: ['node_modules', path.resolve(__dirname, 'src')],

// index.js
const req = require.context('./mods', true, /\.js$/); // both 1.js and 2.js are modules
import mod1Relative from './mods/1.js'; // not a module
import mod1Alias from 'mods/2.js'; // module
```

**how I declar the 'module'**:
use rawModule hook
```javascript
resolver.getHook('before-rawModule').tapAsync('runtime-external-resolve', (req, ctx, cb) => {
      console.log('----isModule: ', req.request);
      return cb();
  });
```

title: Lift the restriction of 'Top-level-await' for CommonJS entry module
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

Currently webpack raises `Module parse failed: Top-level-await is only supported in EcmaScript Modules` on entry module.  
But entry module doesn't have to be a module at all -- it can be any code sequence.  
Looking at the generated code, make the entry IIFE async is sufficient to enable top-level-await in this use case.  

However, I don't know what if what is the implications/semantics for other use cases.

**What is the expected behavior?**
Do not raise 'Module parse failed: Top-level-await is only supported in EcmaScript Modules' on entry module.  
(At least the pure CommonJS modules case is fine.)

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
Userscript extensions, like Tampermonkey or Violentmonkey, support top-level-await.  
And because early hook/interception is common for userscripts, async modules could be unwanted.
A common combo will be CommonJS + async top-level entrance.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
- Add an option to explicitly config the mode of entry module, including a mode 'no-module' (just a code sequence);
- If type is 'no-module':
  * Make the entry IIFE async;
  * Ensure the sync behavior of the entry file is honored: await cannot be injected before or during entry module.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: Asset in child compilation does not exist after upgrading to webpack 5.96.0
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

When using the Angular CLI with the webpack builder and with "direct template loading" disabled, component templates end up blank.

I have created a cut-down version of what the Angular CLI is doing, so it's not a problem with the Angular CLI.

During the compilation, a "resource" can be loaded. This is typically a HTML file that is a template for a component. The resource is loaded via a child compilation. When getting the asset from the child compilation, in 5.95.0 there is exactly one asset - the one being requested. In 5.96.0 and above, there are no assets.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

First, clone this repository.
```bash
git clone https://github.com/reduckted/repro-webpack-template.git
cd repro-webpack-template
```

Switch to the `standalone596` branch (this uses `webpack@5.96.0`, but the bug is also present in 5.98.0), install dependencies and then build.
```bash
git checkout standalone596
npm i
npm run build
```

The output shows that the template was _not_ loaded:
```
asset main.js 159 bytes [compared for emit] (name: main)
./src/index.js 22 bytes [built] [code generated]

LOG from reproduction
<i> template: ''

webpack 5.96.0 compiled successfully in 108 ms
```


**What is the expected behavior?**

To see the expected behavior, switch to the `standalone595` branch (this uses `webpack@5.95.0`), install dependencies and then build.
```bash
git checkout standalone595
npm i
npm run build
```

The output shows that the template was loaded:
```
asset main.js 159 bytes [compared for emit] (name: main)
asset ./src/template.html 18 bytes [compared for emit]
./src/index.js 22 bytes [built] [code generated]

LOG from reproduction
<i> template: 'I am a template!
<i> '

webpack 5.95.0 compiled successfully in 113 ms
```

I think I was able to track down the commit where this started to break. The last good commit was 5ad5c12ffe5e7b41ea97390870d5509a37f29bf4. 

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.96.0 and above.
Node.js version: 22.13.1
Operating System: Windows 10
Additional tools: Angular CLI v19.1.6
author: reduckted

title: DLL with chunks have its module init code replaced by reference to dll (loaded by other chunks in main)
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

I use DLL to share code among many separate page bundles.

This vendor DLL and page bundles have chunks (created because the explicit use of `import()`)

When a dynamically-linked module is referenced from a chunk of page bundle but the module have not yet been instantiated. Loading the chunk would fail with an error.

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

I believe loading the chunk causes the module instantiation code (which is referencing the DLL) to replace the actual implementation in the vendor bundle (with used to contain the actual implementation) causing the error.

Code: https://github.com/pengjunhsu-houzz/webpack-chunked-dll-repro

You can run `npm install && npm run build` and then use `python3 -m http.server` in `dist/`

or, deployed on: https://pengjunhsu-houzz.github.io/webpack-chunked-dll-repro/

If the dll-lib is instantiated before the chunk loads (as in the case in this PR: https://github.com/pengjunhsu-houzz/webpack-chunked-dll-repro/pull/1) the error does not happen.

It is also discovered that deterministic module ids also avoid the bug: https://github.com/pengjunhsu-houzz/webpack-chunked-dll-repro/pull/2 (but makes dev builds difficult to debug)

Yet another way to resolve it for this specific case is to disable async chunks in vendor, so there's no jsonp callback in vendor bundle: https://github.com/pengjunhsu-houzz/webpack-chunked-dll-repro/pull/3

**What is the expected behavior?**

DLL modules need not to be instantiated ahead of time for lazily loaded chunks to import correctly.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.98.0 (latest as of writing, also fails on 5.97.1)
Node.js version:  v23.1.0
Operating System: macOS, Linux (GitHub runner)
Additional tools:
author: pengjunhsu-houzz

title: Any way to bypass the push method ovverride in JsonpChunkLoadingRuntimeModule?

I'm encountering an issue with [the following line](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/de2199c7536af2fa756f9261a192a0515b156905/lib/web/JsonpChunkLoadingRuntimeModule.js#L465) in Webpack:
```
chunkLoadingGlobal.push = webpackJsonpCallback.bind(null, chunkLoadingGlobal.push.bind(chunkLoadingGlobal));
```

This fails in my environment because I enforce restrictions on built-in Array methods, making them non-configurable and non-writable to prevent modifications which also applies to instances...

Is there a way to bypass this override?

body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->

Having trouble with using import() function and module federation in worker.


**What is the current behavior?**
If there is a import of module federation source in a worker file or some code import by a worker file, it gonna fail in runtime. 

`/* webpack/runtime/load script */` try to use jsonp to download code in worker and result in an error like 'Uncaught ReferenceError: document is not defined'.

The config `dynamicImportInWorker: true` also not work in this case.    

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Code like this

```
// common.js
import CodeB from 'moduleFederation/LargeB'

export heaveB = () => {
    CodeB()
}
```

```
// a.worker-inner.js
import { heavyB } from './common'

onmessage(() => {
   heavyB() 
})
```


```
// a.worker.js
import('./a.worker-inner.js')
```

```
// entry.js
import { heavyB } from './common'

if (
   window.offscreenCanvas // in my case, or maybe other condition
) {
   const worker = new Worker(new URL('./a.worker.js'), import.meta.url)
   worker.postMessage('to the heavy stuffs for me')
}else{
   heavyB()
} 
```

title: Webpack tries to emit failed asset to invalid path after compilation error
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

If an asset module fails to compile, Webpack tries to concatenate the absolute path of the asset's source file to the absolute path of the base output directory, resulting in an invalid file path on Windows (which causes `mkdir` to fail). I believe this would cause a duplication of the output folder structure on a Linux/Unix machine, but I don't have a way to test that.

For example, I have a SCSS file in my test project with a compilation error. The result of running Webpack is the following:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5f4c5c82-caa2-4b9e-a0f4-01113a495ecc)

As you can see, it takes the base output folder (`C:\Development\Sandbox\WebpackAssetErrorReproduction\dist`) and tries to append the absolute path basename (directory name) of the source file that caused the error (`C:\Development\Sandbox\WebpackAssetErrorReproduction\src\styles`), resulting in an invalid Windows path: `C:\Development\Sandbox\WebpackAssetErrorReproduction\dist\C:\Development\Sandbox\WebpackAssetErrorReproduction\src\styles`.

I predict that on Linux/Unix, if the root of the source code was at, e.g., `/home/development/WebpackAssetErrorReproduction`, it would most likely try to create `/home/development/WebpackAssetErrorReproduction/dist/home/development/WebpackAssetErrorReproduction/src/styles`.

I spent a bit of time tracing this problem through the Webpack source code enough that I am _fairly_ confident that it's a Webpack issue and not a loader/plugin issue. The invalid filename comes from [lines 209-211 in AssetModulePlugin](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/v5.98.0/lib/asset/AssetModulesPlugin.js#L209-L211), which call `module.nameForCondition()` in the event that `errored` is `true`. In my case, `nameForCondition()` was returning `"C:\Development\Sandbox\WebpackAssetErrorReproduction\src\styles\homepage.scss"`:

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/f1bdec5cc70236083e45b665831d5d79d6485db7/lib/asset/AssetModulesPlugin.js#L205-L218

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I have created a (somewhat) minimal reproduction sample. This is a stripped-down copy of the Webpack workspace that we use at my company, with the essential parts of our configuration arranged exactly as they are for our real workspace.

1. Check out https://github.com/ArcanoxDragon/webpack-asset-path-bug-repo
2. Install packages using Yarn: `yarn install` (`corepack` is required to utilize Yarn 4.x)
3. Run Webpack via the Yarn script: `yarn build` (the script invokes `tsx` to load the TypeScript configuration)
4. Observe the error from the screenshot above
5. Fix the SCSS error:
    - In `src/styles/homepage.scss`, change the first line so that it reads `@use "partial" as *;` instead of just `@use "partial";`
6. Run Webpack again via the same Yarn script
7. Observe that there are no errors and the compilation succeeds. Also observe that the CSS asset is correctly emitted to `./dist/css/homepage.css`.

Something to note is that the behavior is different on Webpack 5.96.0. A completely different error is emitted that seems to be related to calling `toString` on `undefined` while serializing the cache...I'm not sure it's related at all. The `mkdir` issue happens on 5.97.0 and 5.98.0.

**What is the expected behavior?**

I am not 100% sure - it seems like the fact that Webpack is trying to emit content for a failed asset in the first place is incorrect behavior. Aside from that, however, if it _should_ try and emit such a file, it should not be concatenating an absolute path onto the base path output path. It should utilize the same target file path logic as if the asset compiles successfully, which should include calling the `filename` generator if configured.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: `5.98.0`
Node.js version: `22.13.1`
Operating System: `Windows 11 24H2 x64`
Additional tools: TypeScript, sass-embedded, sass-loader, swc

title: crossOriginLoading: 'anonymous' has different behavior between JsonP and prefetch, causing cache misses
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
This has been reported a few times, but I don't think a correct resolution has been reached

When `crossOriginLoading: 'anonymous'` is set, there is differing behavior in applying the `crossOrigin="anonymous"` attribute to JSONP tags and prefetch tags
In the JSONP fetch routine, there is the following check
```
if (script.src.indexOf(window.location.origin + '/') !== 0) {
    script.crossOrigin = "anonymous";
}
```

In the prefetch routine, there is no equivalent check
```
link.crossOrigin = "anonymous"
```

In our application, the end result of this discrepancy is as follows:

1. We serve the same bundled scripts both in same-origin and cross-origin contexts, so we set the `crossOriginLoading: 'anonymous'` even for scripts that will be served in same-origin.
2. In the same-origin context, the page loads and prefetch requests are made with `crossorigin=anonymous`. Because this attribute is set, the prefetch request from the browser includes the `Origin` request header.
3. Our CDN (AWS Cloudfront) sees that the request has the `Origin` header, so it responds with `Vary: Origin`
4. Later, we need to hydrate a chunk that has been prefetched. Because we are in a same-origin context, the Webpack runtime does not add `crossorigin=anonymous` to the `<script>` tag. As a result, the `Origin` request header is not included
5. Because `Vary: Origin` was sent in step 3, and because this new request does not have the `Origin` header, the browser treats the hydration request as a miss, and refetches the chunk. This makes prefetching useless.

To validate this theory, I wrote a hack plugin to remove this same origin check from the runtime. With this, the prefetch cache is correctly used, because the JsonP requests include the `Origin` header

_**What is the purpose/goal of the same origin check, before adding the attribute?**_ Can we add an option to force the inclusion of the `crossorigin` attribute?

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

1. Set `{output: {crossOriginLoading: 'anonymous'}}`
2. See the discrepancy in the generated runtime.js

**What is the expected behavior?**
I would like to see either:
* [Preferred] Skip same origin check, or provide an option to force the addition of `crossorigin="anonymous"`
* OR: Prefetch requests also perform a same origin check


<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.91.0
Node.js version: 22 LTS
Operating System: Mac latest
Additional tools:

title: Deprecation Warning: HookMap#tap is deprecated, but JavascriptParser.unittest.js still uses it.
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
While running all the tests, I came across this warning:

```
DeprecationWarning: HookMap#tap(key,…) is deprecated. Use HookMap#for(key).tap(…) instead. at /webpack/test/JavascriptParser.unittest.js:325:25
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
You can easily reproduce this by running

```
yarn jest JavascriptParser.unittest.js
```

Run the code, and webpack throws a deprecation warning.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
I believe it is a very simple fix, in the previous code:

at line 325
```
testParser.hooks.new.tap("xyz", "JavascriptParserTest", expr => {
				if (!testParser.state.xyz) testParser.state.xyz = [];
				testParser.state.xyz.push(testParser.parseString(expr.arguments[0]));
				return true;
			}
```

We can implement the for hook as:

```
testParser.hooks.new.for("xyz").tap("JavascriptParserTest", expr => {
				if (!testParser.state.xyz) testParser.state.xyz = [];
				testParser.state.xyz.push(testParser.parseString(expr.arguments[0]));
				return true;
			}
```

Now, when we run 
```
yarn jest JavascriptParser.unittest.js
```
The deprecation warning no longer occurs.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**

title: Add a JSON generator option to avoid `JSON.parse`
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

When a `.json` module is sufficiently large, webpack leverages `JSON.parse` to enhance performance.

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/7168389ac65dcc6bfbffa524e003acf7911da71a/lib/json/JsonGenerator.js#L176-L181

But `JSON.parse` is not faster in all cases (see the motivation below).

**What is the expected behavior?**

A new option to allow generate plain object literal instead of `JSON.parse` for `.json` modules.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

In [Lynx](https://lynxjs.org/), we are using compiled bytecodes on the main-thread. So using object literal is faster and smaller than `JSON.parse`.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

It would be great if an option like `module.generator.json.avoidJSONParse` could be added to avoid generating `JSON.parse`

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: type: 'asset/source' in Webpack v5.96.0 Causing CSS Files Not to Be Packaged
body:
### Issue with `type: 'asset/source'` in Webpack v5.96.0 Causing CSS Files Not to Be Packaged

**My project is an Angular project with a custom-written Webpack configuration.**

---

#### Problem Description
When using Webpack version 5.95.0, I used the following configuration to handle CSS files, and the CSS files were correctly packaged during the build process, with styles displaying properly on the page. However, after upgrading Webpack to v5.96.0, the CSS files are no longer packaged during the build process, causing the page styles to fail.

---

#### mjs Configuration Code:
```javascript
{
    test: /\.css$/,
    type: 'asset/source',
    include: [
        helpers.root('_COMMON', 'archive', 'webcomponent'),
        new RegExp('OPF\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep + 'src\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep)
    ]
}
```

---

#### header Component:
```typescript
@Component({
    selector: "header",
    templateUrl: "./header.component.html",
    styleUrls: ["./header.css"]
})
```

---

#### header.css:
```css
.sample {
    background-color: #ccc;
}
```

---

#### Debugging Findings
After debugging, I found that the `[webpack\lib\asset\AssetSourceGenerator.js]` file in Webpack v5.96.0 has been modified. In v5.95.0, it directly returns `[return TYPES]`. The new logic in v5.96.0 causes `originModule` to be `null`, which prevents the CSS files from being correctly processed.

If I comment out `type: 'asset/source'` and replace it with `use: ['raw-loader']`, the CSS files are correctly packaged and the styles display properly:

---

#### Alternative Configuration:
```javascript
{
    test: /\.css$/,
    include: [
        helpers.root('_COMMON', 'archive', 'webcomponent'),
        new RegExp('OPF\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep + 'src\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep)
    ],
    use: ['raw-loader']
}
```

---

#### Environment Information
- Webpack Version: v5.96.0
- Node.js Version: 20.18.2
- Operating System: Windows 11 22H2

---

#### Question
What additional configuration is needed when using `type: 'asset/source'` to ensure that CSS files are correctly packaged?

title: module federation: if a dependent chunk fails on first import, subsequent imports incorrectly return success
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

Using https://github.com/module-federation/core/blob/main/packages/utilities/src/utils/importRemote.ts, and the import fails with "Loading chunk xxx failed" where xxx is a dependent chunk (listed as a dependency in "consumes"); a subsequent call to `importRemote` with the same module will return success without trying to reload the missing chunk.

Stepping through the code, it appears that this is because where a chunk fails to load, the entry in `startedInstallModules` is still set to true, and so it isn't later reloaded.

I tested with reseting the value in `startedInstallModules` to false. In this case, the chunk is loaded, but then I ran into another problem. In "bootstrap", it finds a "cached module" and returns it even though its "loaded" flag is false. Turning on `strictModuleErrorHandling` doesn't solve it since it just throws the previous exception. But turning on `strictModuleExceptionHandling` does fix it since it causes the module to be initialized again.

**What is the expected behavior?**

There should be a way to reload a module (and dependencies) that previously failed to completely load.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.98.0
Node.js version: 18.20.7
Operating System: Windows 11
Additional tools:
author: mbest

title: Introduce typescript/auto Mode for Faster TypeScript Transpilation in Webpack
body:
# Feature request

## What is the expected behavior?  
Introduce a new TypeScript mode (`typescript/auto`) in Webpack that can leverage the new Go-based TypeScript compiler (Corsa) or Node.js built-in TypeScript transpilation for faster builds. This would serve as a lightweight alternative to `ts-loader`, focusing purely on transpilation without type-checking.  

## What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?  
- **Faster Compilation:** The Corsa compiler promises a **10x speed boost**, and using Node.js for transpilation is already a popular, lightweight alternative.  
- **Better Developer Experience:** This can speed up **HMR and incremental builds** significantly.  
- **Smoother Transition:** Developers can opt into this mode without replacing their entire toolchain.  

## How should this be implemented in your opinion?  
Introduce a `typescript/auto` mode that dynamically selects the best available transpiler:  
- **Use Corsa (if stable and available).**  
- **Fallback to Node.js TypeScript transpilation** (like `ts-node`, but integrated).  
- **Skip type-checking in Webpack builds** but allow separate type-checking with `fork-ts-checker-webpack-plugin`.  
- **Ensure compatibility** with Webpack’s caching and module resolution.  

## Are you willing to work on this yourself?  
Yes, I’m interested in exploring the feasibility of this feature and contributing to discussions and development.

title: Asset module binary files are stored in webpack cache
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Currently asset modules are stored in webpack filesystem cache, seemingly multiple times per asset, increasing the cache size significantly.

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Asset modules are cached in cache packs, seems both by the asset parser and then when dumping the compilation generated assets. In our large production build, webpack cache takes ~17GB.

We added the following patch in IdleFileCachePlugin.js and the cache size dropped to 1.8GB and cached build times improved.

```
diff --git a/lib/cache/IdleFileCachePlugin.js b/lib/cache/IdleFileCachePlugin.js
index 3ac59121bafafbdafae3a7dbe0db04242fb41352..fa67d0c0d64935dd4d53e650945a083521d4d748 100644
--- a/lib/cache/IdleFileCachePlugin.js
+++ b/lib/cache/IdleFileCachePlugin.js
@@ -57,6 +57,23 @@ class IdleFileCachePlugin {
 		compiler.cache.hooks.store.tap(
 			{ name: "IdleFileCachePlugin", stage: Cache.STAGE_DISK },
 			(identifier, etag, data) => {
+				if (identifier.includes("|asset/")) {
+					// avoid storing big binary blobs in cache
+					// we also skip anything directly related to them (like generated code) since that is invalidated every time because the file itself isn't in cache. 
+					return;
+				}
+				if (identifier.startsWith("Compilation/assets|")) {
+					// this skips storing generated complete assets in cache, since the asset resources are not cached, these files get invalidated anyway
+					return;
+				}
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Enable filesystem cache, configure asset/resource modules.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Asset module binary data is not stored in cache. Perhaps add a size limit to allow caching (just an example) 2KB assets, but nothing bigger. Reading the asset from disk directly would be as fast as reading it from the cache.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.97.1
Node.js version: 23.8.0
Operating System: Win11
Additional tools:
author: Knagis

title: 'test' option for SourceMapDevToolPlugin does not filter by extension (as documented)
body: # Bug report **What is the current behavior?** The SourceMapDevToolPlugin's `test` and `include` are functionally identical. They are documented as follows: > `test` (string RegExp [string, RegExp]): Include source maps for modules based on their extension (defaults to .js, .mjs, and .css). > > `include` (string RegExp [string, RegExp]): Include source maps for module paths that match the given value. These two options both use the exact same `ModuleFilenameHelpers.matchObject ` helper function (as of commit ???): ```js ModuleFilenameHelpers.matchObject = (obj, str) => { if (obj.test && !ModuleFilenameHelpers.matchPart(str, obj.test)) { return false; } if (obj.include && !ModuleFilenameHelpers.matchPart(str, obj.include)) { return false; } if (obj.exclude && ModuleFilenameHelpers.matchPart(str, obj.exclude)) { return false; } return true; }; ``` This function delegates to `matchPart`: ```js const matchPart = (str, test) => { if (!test) return true; if (Array.isArray(test)) { return test.some(test => matchPart(str, test)); } if (typeof test === "string") { return str.startsWith(test); } return test.test(str); }; ``` When either of these settings uses a string, they match against the start of the filename, not the end (as `test` documents, it should match on extension). **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** The following plugin config gives me a sourcemap for the generated `main.js`: ```js SourceMapDevToolPlugin({ test: "main.js", // ✅ Works! }), ``` However, if I want to filter so only `.js` files get a sourcemap, the following config _doesn't_ work: ```js // Doesn't work - no sourcemaps for js files SourceMapDevToolPlugin({ test: ".js", }), ``` Neither does this: ```js // Doesn't work - no sourcemaps for js files SourceMapDevToolPlugin({ test: [".js"], }), ``` A regular expression does work but makes for more complicated config: ```js // Doesn't work - no sourcemaps for js files SourceMapDevToolPlugin({ test: /\.js$/ }), ``` **What is the expected behavior?** I expect that I can use the `test` plugin option to filter which files get a sourcemap by providing it a file extension or array of extensions (ie. `test: '.js'`). The documentation even lists what the default is as `.js .mjs .css`). **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.98.0 Node.js version: 20.18.3 Operating System: macOS Sequoia 15.3.2 Additional tools:

title: support ignoreList option in SourceMapDevToolPlugin
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
`ignoreList` is part of sourcemap [spec](https://tc39.es/ecma426/#json-ignoreList) and supported in [chrome devtools](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/devtools/x-google-ignore-list), even it can be implemented in user side like next.js's [implementation](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/src/build/webpack/plugins/devtools-ignore-list-plugin.ts) but it will introduce extra performance cost especially in Rspack, if it's implemented in sourceMapDevtoolPlugin, rspack can also provide same functionality with best performance.
<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
support `ignoreList` option in sourcemapDevtoolPlugin
**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
follow sourcemap spec and provide same api for rspack & webpack with performant implementation
**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
nearly same as next's implementation but no need to introduce extra processAssets hook call
**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: ReferenceError: process is not defined on v5.99.0
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

After updating webpack from 5.98.0 to 5.99.0, my Vue 3 application throws a runtime error:
ReferenceError: process is not defined.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/07dbaab0-4b47-4620-bcb6-59db4d592790)

This error occurs in the browser environment after the build.

**Steps to Reproduce**

Use webpack 5.98.0 with a Vue 3 project that relies on process (e.g., for environment variables like process.env.NODE_ENV).

Update webpack to 5.99.0.

Run the production build (webpack build --mode production or similar).

Serve the built application and observe the runtime error in the browser console.

**What is the expected behavior?**

The process should be available in the browser environment after the build without errors, as it was in webpack 5.98.0.

**Other relevant information:**

webpack version: 5.99.0 (works in 5.98.0)
Node.js version: v22.13.0
Operating System: Windows 10
Additional Tools: Vue 3, relevant loaders/plugins (e.g., vue-loader).

title: TS2305: Module '"lib"' has no exported member 'MyClass'
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

I'm developing a library with UMD export, but when I try to import it into another project, I get the error "TS2305: Module '"../../1_Library/dist/lib"' has no exported member 'MyClass'."

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

Minimum reproduction repo: [https://github.com/theberrigan/lib-import-bug](https://github.com/theberrigan/lib-import-bug)

1. Create new library project.
Content of ```src/lib.ts```:
```ts
class MyClass {}

export {
    MyClass
};
```
Important part of ```webpack.config.js```:
```js
{
    entry: './src/lib.ts',
    output: {
        path: path.resolve(__dirname, 'dist'),
        filename: 'lib.js',
        globalObject: 'globalThis',
        library: {
            type: 'umd',
            name: 'MyLib',          // <-- NOTE
            umdNamedDefine: false,  // <-- NOTE
        },
    },
    // ...
}
```
2. Build it: ```npm run build```
3. Create a new project that will import our library.
Content of ```src/main.ts```:
```ts
import { MyClass } from '../../1_Library/dist/lib';

console.log('-->', MyClass);
```
4. Run webpack dev server: ```npm start```
5. Webpack will show error:
```
TS2305: Module '"../../1_Library/dist/lib"' has no exported member 'MyClass'
```
6. Open in browser ```http://127.0.0.1:12013/```
7. Open console and you will see this:
```
--> class MyClass {}
[webpack-dev-server] Errors while compiling. Reload prevented.
index.js:603 [webpack-dev-server] ERROR in ./src/main.ts (C:\Projects\JSProjects\ImportBug\2_Consumer\src\main.ts) 1:9-16
[tsl] ERROR in C:\Projects\JSProjects\ImportBug\2_Consumer\src\main.ts(1,10)
      TS2305: Module '"../../1_Library/dist/lib"' has no exported member 'MyClass'.
```
This means that ```MyClass``` was imported correctly, but Typescript is not happy.
8. Ok, then try import library in different way.
Content of ```src/main.ts```:
```ts
// MyLib is the library name specified in webpack.config.js of the 1_Library project
import { MyLib } from '../../1_Library/dist/lib';

console.log('-->', MyLib);
```
9. Project will be compiled without error, but in browser you will see:
```
--> undefined
```
**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

This import should work without errors:
```ts
import { MyClass } from '../../1_Library/dist/lib';
```
This import should not work at all:
```ts
import { MyLib } from '../../1_Library/dist/lib';
```

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.99.5
Node.js version: 23.8.0
Operating System: Windows 10 (10.0.19042.746)
Additional tools: webpack-cli 6.0.1, webpack-dev-server 5.2.1, typescript 5.8.3, ts-loader 9.5.2

body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->

# Bug report

<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/19431

**What is the current behavior?**

This is a minimal example to showcase a problem with webpack5 and es modules async chunk loader.

Here's the reproduction codebase: https://stackblitz.com/edit/webpack-webpack-js-org-kxd532jl?file=README.md

It seems like `publicPath` is not being properly respected when loading chunks.

To test this out:

1. npm run build
2. npm start

`npm start` will run two servers:

1. the main distribution folder with the html file pointing to the main entry output `main.js`
2. the public folder containing all the chunks generated (port 9010)

If you try to access the application, if everything goes well you should see the number `1` on the page.
This won't happen in this case as webpack will try to access the chunk without properly following `publicPatch` (publicPath is pointing to `http://localhost:9010/`)

title: [CSS] HMR becomes unrecoverable after postcss-loader error
body:
### Bug report

#### Actual Behavior

When enabling `experiments.css` and using `postcss-loader`; If you make an edit to a CSS file that results in an error (e.g. invalid CSS), webpack hot module replacement no longer detects further changes in that file - even if you correct or undo the edit (CTRL-Z).
You need to restart webpack for it to start detecting the file again.
This is only in the specific scenario of a _breaking_ change in CSS. Any valid change continues to be detected by hot module replacement.
I am not sure if this is a duplicate of https://github.com/webpack-contrib/postcss-loader/issues/700, since they are not using `experiments.css` there.

#### Expected Behavior

HMR should continue detecting updates to the previously edited file. If the error is corrected, it should recompile and emit the successful file.

#### How Do We Reproduce?

Minimal repro: https://github.com/sergey-v9/webpack-postcss-loader-hmr-issue.

Run `npm start`;
Edit file `src/styles.css` - add any letter in the end of file;
Save edits;
You got the error in a console:
```
<e> TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'exports')
<e>     at W:\tmp\webpack-postcss-loader-hmr-issue\node_modules\webpack\lib\css\CssModulesPlugin.js:390:35     
<e>     at Hook.eval [as call] (eval at create (W:\tmp\webpack-postcss-loader-hmr-issue\node_modules\tapable\lib\HookCodeFactory.js:19:10), <anonymous>:12:16)
<e>     at W:\tmp\webpack-postcss-loader-hmr-issue\node_modules\webpack\lib\javascript\JavascriptModulesPlugin.js:603:32
<e>     at tryRunOrWebpackError (W:\tmp\webpack-postcss-loader-hmr-issue\node_modules\webpack\lib\HookWebpackError.js:81:7)
<e>     at JavascriptModulesPlugin.renderModule (W:\tmp\webpack-postcss-loader-hmr-issue\node_modules\webpack\lib\javascript\JavascriptModulesPlugin.js:601:36)
<e>     at W:\tmp\webpack-postcss-loader-hmr-issue\node_modules\webpack\lib\javascript\JavascriptModulesPlugin.js:728:10
<e>     at W:\tmp\webpack-postcss-loader-hmr-issue\node_modules\webpack\lib\Template.js:301:12
<e>     at Array.map (<anonymous>)
<e>     at Template.renderChunkModules (W:\tmp\webpack-postcss-loader-hmr-issue\node_modules\webpack\lib\Template.js:299:30)
<e>     at JavascriptModulesPlugin.renderChunk (W:\tmp\webpack-postcss-loader-hmr-issue\node_modules\webpack\lib\javascript\JavascriptModulesPlugin.js:727:13)
```

The only way to fix HMR now is to restart webpack.

#### Side notes

This behaviour was introduced in v5.97.0. 

v5.96.1 does not fail in such unrecoverable way:
```
css ./src/styles.css 1 bytes [built] [code generated] [1 error]

ERROR in css ./src/styles.css
Module build failed (from ./node_modules/postcss-loader/dist/cjs.js):

SyntaxError

(12:1) W:\tmp\webpack-postcss-loader-hmr-issue\src\styles.css Unknown word d

  10 |   text-align: center;
  11 | }
> 12 | d
     | ^
  13 |
 @ ./src/index.js 1:0-22

webpack 5.96.1 compiled with 1 error in 18 ms

```

---

```
> npx webpack info
  System:
    OS: Windows 11 10.0.26100
    CPU: (20) x64 Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-14600K
    Memory: 14.09 GB / 47.77 GB
  Binaries:
    Node: 23.11.0 - C:\Program Files\nodejs\node.EXE
    Yarn: 4.8.1 - C:\Program Files\nodejs\yarn.CMD
    npm: 10.9.2 - C:\Program Files\nodejs\npm.CMD
    pnpm: 10.8.1 - C:\Program Files\nodejs\pnpm.CMD
  Browsers:
    Edge: Chromium (134.0.3124.93)
    Internet Explorer: 11.0.26100.1882
  Packages:
    html-webpack-plugin: ^5.6.3 => 5.6.3
    postcss-loader: ^8.1.1 => 8.1.1
    webpack: 5.99.6 => 5.99.6
    webpack-cli: ^6.0.1 => 6.0.1
    webpack-dev-server: ^5.2.1 => 5.2.1
```

title: Main chunk points to the wrong runtime chunk when using Module Federation
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When running the `watch` command, at some point the `runtime-app` chunk referenced in the `app` chunk is different from the one referenced in the `asset-manifest.json`. 

Since we load this code in a PHP app, we use the manifest to load the chunks dynamically. 

Having the manifest reference a different chunk than the one referenced in the `app` chunk means that the browser loads two different `runtime-app` chunks, one requested by the manifest, and the other by the `app` chunk.

Since the second one is the oldest, the end result is that the `watch` command is not doing its job, because from a certain moment, I'm not able to see the changes I make to the code reflected in the browser.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

Here is a GitHub repository with the code needed to reproduce the issue: https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/.

How to reproduce:
1. Clone the repository
2. Run `npm install` to install the dependencies
3. Run `npm run watch` to start watching for changes
4. Open the `asset-manifest.json` file in the public folder, and open the `app` chunk that is referenced in the `entry-points` field. Now look for the `runtime-app` chunk in the `app` chunk and check that it's the same that is referenced in the `asset-manifest.json` file: it should be the same (with the same hash)
5. Make a change to the code in `app.js` and save it
6. The issue is still not there
7. Now make a change to the `design-area.js` file and save it
8. The issue should be there now: the `runtime-app` chunk in the `asset-manifest.json` file and in the `app` chunk should have different hashes

The issue happens only when using the `watch` command, and not when using the `build` command (which is not implemented in this repo).

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

I would expect that the `runtime-app` chunk referenced in the `asset-manifest.json` file and in the `app` chunk are always the same, so that I can use the manifest to load the chunks dynamically without having to worry about which one is loaded.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.99.5
Node.js version: 18.17.1
Operating System: macOS Sequoia 15.3.2
Additional tools:

title: regression: missing named exports for asset/inline with output.module in Webpack 5.99.0+
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->

# Bug report


<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Starting from Webpack 5.99.0, when using `output.module: true` with `experiments.outputModule` and handling assets via `type: 'asset/inline'`, the generated output no longer includes named exports for those assets.

In Webpack 5.98.0, the bundle correctly emitted export statements for inlined assets. After upgrading, these exports are missing, causing undefined when importing those assets from the output module.

This appears to be a regression introduced in 5.99.0.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

https://github.com/altinokdarici/webpack-internal-image-issue

**What is the expected behavior?**

Webpack should generate named exports for the inlined asset as it did in 5.98.0:

```js
var __webpack_exports__logo = __webpack_exports__.logo;
export { __webpack_exports__logo as logo };
```

title: Dynamic virtual modules and rebuilds
body: Hello! First of all, I'll describe my case study. I hope issues is suitable for this problem. I need to create dynamic virtual files, for example for a case like file-system routing. In the case of file-system routing, the mechanism looks like this: - import virtual file with list of routes - `import routes from 'my-virtual-routes-list'` is added to the application code - the content of the `my-virtual-routes-list` module is generated at build time by reading the contents of the `routes` folder - When changes are made to the `routes` folder, I want to rebuild the contents of `my-virtual-routes-list` and start rebuilding the application I have tried two mechanisms: - custom webpack loader - `virtual-webpack-modules` library - https://github.com/sysgears/webpack-virtual-modules In both cases I've encountered an extra (or few) rebuild of the project after initial compilation, and the problem is related to tracking changes to build dependencies, in both cases I use `loaderContext.addDependency` or `loaderContext.addContextDependency`. It seems like I'm trying to do a not very complex and uncommon thing, but I'm going about it the wrong way, and would be very grateful for tips or recipes for solving such cases. ### Custom loader Loader is simple, for `'./file-system-routes'` import it will read routes from file system, from `routes` directory. Source code - https://github.com/SuperOleg39/webpack-loader-rebuild/blob/main/loaders/file-system-routes.js When I add `routes` folder to build dependencies with `addContextDependency`, after some research I've noticed repeated builds when `routes` folder does not exist at the moment of compilation start, but looks like there can be other cases. Also, with custom loader I need to create `file-system-routes.js` file before compilation, or encounter "Module is not found" error, so I decided to try another way and switched to virtual modules. ### Virtual modules With default `virtual-webpack-modules` plugin usage I have a lot of problems with virtual modules paths (need to be relative to compilation context, didn't work when the import of the virtual module was in third-party packages), and decided to try implement Vite virtual module covention with `virtual:` protocol - https://vite.dev/guide/api-plugin.html#virtual-modules-convention Initially I have a failed attempt to patch module resolving for `virtual:*` imports, and find another way in `FileUriPlugin` plugin source code. So how I want this to work: - In application code import virtual module - `import { routes } from 'virtual:myscope/file-system-routes'` - the content of the `virtual:myscope/file-system-routes` module is generated at build time by reading the contents of the `routes` folder - When changes are made to the `routes` folder, I want to rebuild the contents of `virtual:myscope/file-system-routes` and start rebuilding the application `virtual:` protocol support is simple, read virtual module from file system, because it was already added there by `webpack-virtual-modules` plugin - https://github.com/SuperOleg39/webpack-virtual-modules-rebuild/blob/main/plugins/virtual-protocol-plugin.js For `routes` folder changes, I create plugin with `chokidar`, where virtual module content is updated after changes - https://github.com/SuperOleg39/webpack-virtual-modules-rebuild/blob/main/plugins/file-system-pages-watch-plugin.js And main problem here is rebuilds after compilation, because virtual module `virtual:myscope/file-system-routes` is added to build dependencies with `loaderContext.addDependency(filePath)`: Looks like watchpack marked `virtual:myscope` as initial-missing directory.
author: SuperOleg39

title: [TerserWebpackPlugin] Inconsistent order of params/variables in identical builds on arm/amd
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

We build our app on both arm and amd architectures for k8s, and we build each separately so that native bindings can be resolved properly. I've been tracking down a ChunkError due to differently named chunks and I've narrowed it down to the Terser plugin somehow coming up with two different sets of minified variables for the output `webpack-` bundle...

## AMD
```js
var e,t,n,r,o,u,i,f,c,d={}
```
## ARM
```js
var e,t,n,r,o,u,i,c,f,d={}
```

As you can see, the positions of the `f` and `c` are swapped. Further down in the file we can see that it properly uses each variable -- so in the AMD build we might see `f++` but in the ARM build its `c++` so its still using the correct variables in the correct places, it just switched up the order of the minified names for some reason.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

It's quite difficult to replicate as its flaky -- for most files, the order is consistent, but occasionally the `webpack-` bundle has the issue. I'll try to get a working example soon.

title: Invalid hook call in React only when using Webpack rather than the Vite bundler
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When serving my Webpack + TypeScript + React project I'm getting:

```plain
Invalid hook call. Hooks can only be called inside of the body of a function component. This could happen for one of the following reasons:
1. You might have mismatching versions of React and the renderer (such as React DOM)
2. You might be breaking the Rules of Hooks
3. You might have more than one copy of React in the same app
  (reported line: Container.tsx:17)

Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'useContext')
    at push.../node_modules/react/cjs/react.development.js.exports.useContext (react.development.js:1168:1)
    at Container (Container.tsx:17:29)
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

- Project:  [webpackbug.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19922620/webpackbug.zip)
  - To run: `npm i && npm run dev`
- Webpack wrapper CLI: https://github.com/hydroperx/webpack
- [Webpack configuration](https://github.com/hydroperx/webpack/blob/master/src/processes/BuildProcess.ts#L140)

The error indicates that I am using React wrongly, but I confirmed I am using component top-level hooks only and the React and react-dom packages are not duplicating.

[Full information here on why I'm doing it right.](https://stackoverflow.com/q/79585274/26380963)

- To update, I am satisfied with using Vite as I figured out how to handle browser support differently in `@hydroperx/fluent`.

My only workaround is to simply use Vite instead of Webpack.

**What is the expected behavior?**

Display a webpage with user interface elements such as a heading title "Metro demo", and several other components such as context menu, buttons, icons, live tiles and others, with no console or page error.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.99.6
Node.js version: 22.14.0
Operating System: Windows 11
Additional tools: N/A

title: Update webpack from 5.97.1 to 5.98.0 causes Cypress tests stop running with component testing
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Cypress component tests fail to run when the `webpack` dependency is updated from `5.97.1` to version `5.98.0` or any later version.  The issue does not appear to affect end-to-end (e2e) tests in other projects, suggesting it is specific to component testing.

**What is the current behavior?**

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

1. Acess the codesandbox and fork the repo: [https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/cypress-cucumber-3y4jwl](https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/cypress-cucumber-3y4jwl)
2. Open the terminal
3. Run `yarn` to install dependencies
4. Run `yarn cy:run` to execute the tests.  The tests should pass at this stage
5. Open the `package.json` file
6. Change the `webpack` version from `5.97.1` to `5.98.0`
7. In the terminal, run `yarn` again to update the dependencies
8. Run `yarn cy:run` in the terminal

Observe that the Cypress component tests now fail.

Version 5.97.1:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/830722a3-df75-4278-8432-42de07b0f6cd)

Version 5.98.0 (and all above)
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/167beb7f-43e0-435f-a907-26e23e08efcd)
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ae592c6e-d967-4438-81e0-650edefbe315)

Full error:
```sh
  1) An uncaught error was detected outside of a test:
     TypeError: The following error originated from your test code, not from Cypress.

  > Cannot read properties of null (reading 'replace')

When Cypress detects uncaught errors originating from your test code it will automatically fail the current test.

Cypress could not associate this error to any specific test.

We dynamically generated a new test to display this failure.
      at maybeRetrievePositionFromSourceMap (http://localhost:8080/__cypress/src/vendors-node_modules_badeball_cypress-cucumber-preprocessor_dist_entrypoint-browser_js-node_m-d01cc3.js:2416:39)
      at Registry.defineStep (http://localhost:8080/__cypress/src/vendors-node_modules_badeball_cypress-cucumber-preprocessor_dist_entrypoint-browser_js-node_m-d01cc3.js:2868:75)
      at defineStep (http://localhost:8080/__cypress/src/vendors-node_modules_badeball_cypress-cucumber-preprocessor_dist_entrypoint-browser_js-node_m-d01cc3.js:1594:35)
      at ./cypress/integration/common/when.step.tsx (webpack://storybook-webpack-react/./cypress/integration/common/when.step.tsx:7:0)
      at __webpack_require__ (http://localhost:8080/__cypress/src/main.js:2709:42)
      at ./cypress/integration/features/check-tree.feature (webpack://storybook-webpack-react/./cypress/integration/features/check-tree.feature:5:4)
      at __webpack_require__ (http://localhost:8080/__cypress/src/main.js:2709:42)
      at __webpack_require__.t (http://localhost:8080/__cypress/src/main.js:2757:38)
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

The Cypress component tests should pass regardless of the webpack version (5.97.1, 5.98.0, or later).  The update to webpack should not cause the tests to fail.

**Other relevant information:**

I've opened this issue on the preprocessor github, and this was the response I got:

*This regression is due to https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/19184 (I bisected their changelog between v5.97.1 and v5.98.0). The new source maps resulting from said change can't (appears to) be resolved using SourceMapConsumer from source-map. Unfortunately however, I wouldn't be able to tell you why this is. I recommend that try opening up a ticket in their (webpack's) tracker.*

webpack version:  5.97.1 | 5.98.0
Node.js version: 20.12.0
Operating System: Ubuntu 22.04
Additional tools: Preprocessor: @badeball/cypress-cucumber-preprocessor (v 22.0.1)
