title: Google Summer of Code 2025 Proposal
body:
Personal Information

  • Full Name: Lakshit Goyal

  • Email: lakshitgoyal111@gmail.com

  • GitHub Profile: https://github.com/lakshitgoyal

  • University: Maharaja Agrasen Institute of Technology, GGSIPU

  • Year of Study: 1st Year

  • Time Zone: IST (GMT +5:30)

  • Working Hours per Week: 20–25 hours

Project Title

Unified Webpack Dev Tools

Synopsis

Webpack currently offers multiple tools for development purposes: webpack-dev-server, webpack-hot-middleware, and webpack-dev-middleware. However, these tools often overlap in functionality and introduce inconsistencies and complexity in setup. This project aims to unify these tools into a clean, extensible monorepo structure.

The core goal is to extract hot module replacement (HMR) and client logic into a new package (webpack-hmr-middleware), deprecate webpack-hot-middleware, and refactor webpack-dev-server into a monorepo that includes webpack-dev-server, webpack-dev-middleware, and the newly created HMR middleware. The result will be a simpler and more powerful development toolchain for the Webpack ecosystem.

Benefits to the Community

  • Simplified Developer Experience: Developers will interact with a unified API and configuration, reducing cognitive load.

  • Better Maintenance: By isolating functionality, the Webpack team and contributors can iterate faster and with more confidence.

  • Modern Architecture: A monorepo setup will enable better version control, testing, and dependency sharing.

  • Deprecation Cleanup: Eliminating legacy tools like webpack-hot-middleware paves the way for a cleaner codebase and documentation.

Deliverables

  1. New Middleware Package: webpack-hmr-middleware containing extracted HMR and client logic.

  2. Migration: Move applicable logic and options from webpack-hot-middleware into the new middleware.

  3. Deprecation PR: Initiate and document the deprecation of webpack-hot-middleware.

  4. Monorepo Setup: Transform webpack-dev-server into a monorepo housing:

    • webpack-dev-server

    • webpack-dev-middleware

    • webpack-hmr-middleware

  5. Documentation: Provide updated docs and migration guides for users.

  6. Testing: Ensure full test coverage across all packages.

Timeline

Period | Task Description -- | -- Community Bonding (May) | Set up development environment, read existing codebases, talk with mentors, explore current dev tool integration. Week 1–2 (June) | Extract hot/client logic from webpack-dev-server into the new webpack-hmr-middleware. Set up initial structure. Week 3–4 | Move core logic and options from webpack-hot-middleware. Ensure feature parity. Week 5 | Integration testing with webpack-dev-server and webpack-dev-middleware. Midterm Evaluation | Completed webpack-hmr-middleware with migration done. Working integration. Week 6–7 | Transform webpack-dev-server into a monorepo using tools like pnpm, npm workspaces, or lerna. Week 8 | Move and link the three packages into the monorepo. Clean up structure. Week 9 | Final tests and cleanup. Deprecation PR for webpack-hot-middleware. Week 10 | Documentation for new tools and migration guide. Final Evaluation | Final polish, mentor reviews, submission of project and blog.

Technical Approach

1. Codebase Familiarization

  • Analyze the code for webpack-dev-server, webpack-dev-middleware, and webpack-hot-middleware.

  • Understand how HMR and client logic are distributed.

2. Middleware Creation

  • Build a new package: webpack-hmr-middleware that contains all logic related to:

    • Hot Module Replacement.

    • WebSocket client logic.

    • Middleware configuration and event handling.

3. Code Migration

  • Move hot reload features from webpack-hot-middleware to the new middleware.

  • Ensure backward compatibility (where necessary) using fallbacks or migration utilities.

4. Monorepo Setup

  • Use npm workspaces or pnpm to turn webpack-dev-server into a monorepo.

  • Ensure seamless linkage of the three packages.

5. Testing and Validation

  • Write integration tests.

  • Validate that existing projects using these tools continue to work.

  • Benchmark performance improvements or parity.

6. Documentation

  • Provide API documentation and integration examples.

  • Write a migration guide for users moving from webpack-hot-middleware.

About Me

I am Lakshit Goyal, a first-year Computer Science student at MAIT, GGSIPU. I have a strong foundation in JavaScript, HTML, CSS, and Node.js, and I am passionate about web performance and tooling. I’ve actively explored developer tools and am enthusiastic about simplifying build systems for broader adoption.

Being new to open source, I am excited about the opportunity to contribute to a widely-used tool like Webpack and learn from experienced mentors. I’m eager to build, test, and improve code that will be used by thousands of developers around the world.

Why This Project?

I love clean developer tooling. When I first learned about Webpack’s development workflow, I was impressed but also slightly confused by the number of dev tools involved. I want to contribute to making this process clearer and more powerful for others. This project is the perfect opportunity for me to grow as a developer while directly improving an essential tool used across the web ecosystem.

Contributions So Far

I am currently exploring the repositories listed for this project and plan to make meaningful contributions before the coding period begins. I aim to submit PRs or engage with relevant issues in the coming weeks.

Final Note

I'm highly motivated to contribute to Webpack and grow as a developer through GSoC. I appreciate the opportunity to collaborate with experienced mentors and work on tools that impact developers globally.

Thank you for considering my proposal!
author: lakshitgoyal

title: Type error with latest @types/node (both v20 and v22)
body:
# Bug report

When using the latest @types/node in conjunction with webpack, I get the following:
```
node_modules/webpack/types.d.ts:15875:15 - error TS2417: Class static side 'typeof WebpackError' incorrectly extends base class static side '{ readonly prototype: Error; captureStackTrace(targetObject: object, constructorOpt?: Function | undefined): void; prepareStackTrace(err: Error, stackTraces: CallSite[]): any; stackTraceLimit: number; }'.
  Types of property 'prepareStackTrace' are incompatible.
    Type '((err: Error, stackTraces: CallSite[]) => any) | undefined' is not assignable to type '(err: Error, stackTraces: CallSite[]) => any'.
      Type 'undefined' is not assignable to type '(err: Error, stackTraces: CallSite[]) => any'.

15875 declare class WebpackError extends Error {
                    ~~~~~~~~~~~~


Found 1 error in node_modules/webpack/types.d.ts:15875
```

**What is the current behavior?**

Fails type checking.

**What is the expected behavior?**

Passes type checking.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.99.7
Node.js version: Irrelevant
Operating System: Irrelevant

Steps to reproduce:
```
mkdir webpack-type-error
cd webpack-type-error
npm init -y
npm i typescript webpack @types/node
npx tsc --init --module nodenext --moduleResolution nodenext --skipLibCheck false
echo "import * as webpack from 'webpack'; console.log(webpack);" > index.ts
npx tsc --noEmit
```

Fix:
change:
`static prepareStackTrace?: (err: Error, stackTraces: NodeJS.CallSite[]) => any;`
to:
`static prepareStackTrace: (err: Error, stackTraces: NodeJS.CallSite[]) => any;`
author: AviVahl

title: Custom CSS classes in .vue components are overridden by Quasar defaults since Webpack 5.99.0
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**
Since Webpack version 5.99.0, custom CSS classes defined inside .vue components are being overridden by the default styles of Quasar Framework components (e.g., q-btn, q-card).
This behavior did not occur in version 5.98.0 and is visually breaking the layout of my application.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Create a .vue component and define some custom CSS styles.

Use a Quasar component inside it.

Build/run the project with Webpack 5.99.0 or higher.

Notice that your custom styles are no longer applied correctly—they're overridden by Quasar's default styles.

Note: It's not every component that it's been overridden

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0f4bbf95-43e8-4c16-86e5-5e98af5735d4)

title: SyntaxError when outputting a module in production mode while using webpack-dev-server (5.99.0 regression)
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

When using `webpack-dev-server` to serve a bundle that uses a package in the `node_modules` and that is built using `mode: "production"` and `output.module: true`, the served module contains a syntax error.

In other words, from my investigation, the prerequisites are as follows:

- Have some `node_modules` imported in the source code;
- Have `mode` set to `"production"`;
- Have `output.module` set to `true` (and indirectly have `experiments.outModule` set to `true`);
- Use `webpack-dev-server` to serve the bundle.

The bug still occurs if Hot Module Replacement and live reload is/are disabled in `webpack-dev-server`.

The bug has been tested in 5.99.0 through 5.99.8 and did not occur in 5.98.0.

This seems to be related to the refactor that was done in 5.99.0. It seems to be somewhat related to https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/19451, and loosely related to https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/19394.

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

A syntax error is thrown because of the use of a variable not defined. That variable has the name of the entry point file name. It seems like the entry point is tree shaken and only the Webpack runtime chunk is left.

Here is some pseudo code of the input:
```
// src/index.js
import value from "@kevinpastor/value";

export default value;
```

and the pseudo code of its output:
```
// index.mjs

// Runtime chunk (possibly only from webpack-dev-server)...

export { src as default };
```

Notice how the `src` variable has not definition; this is not an oversight.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

It was a bit harder to create a reproduction setup since this is not happening during normal build and only occurs during what we call "release preview", but anyway:

https://github.com/kevinpastor/webpack-5.99.0-regression

Reproduction:

- Run `npm install`.
- Run `npm start`.
- Manually open the `index.html` file in your browser.
- Open the browser dev tools console.
- Observe the error message: `Uncaught SyntaxError: Export 'src' is not defined in module`.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

The output bundle should include the built source code.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**

Node.js version: 20.18.0
Operating System: Windows 11, under WSL

title: Unsupported import.meta methods to not replaced since v5.99
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->

# Bug report

Unsupported import.meta methods to not replaced since v5.99.

## Minimal example

- Source code:

```js
function main() {
    if (!import.meta.webpackHot) {
        return;
    }
    if (import.meta.webpackHot.status() !== 'idle') {
        console.log('idle');
    }
}

main();
```

- webpack.config.mjs:

```js
export default {
  mode: "development",
  devtool: false,
};
```

- Output (5.97):

```js
/******/ (() => { // webpackBootstrap
/*!**********************!*\
  !*** ./src/index.js ***!
  \**********************/
function main() {
    if (true) {
        return;
    }
    if (/* unsupported import.meta.webpackHot */ undefined.status() !== 'idle') {
        console.log('idle');
    }
}

main();

/******/ })()
;
```

- Output (5.99.8):

```js
/******/ (() => { // webpackBootstrap
/*!**********************!*\
  !*** ./src/index.js ***!
  \**********************/
function main() {
    if (true) {
        return;
    }
    if (import.meta.webpackHot.status() !== 'idle') {
        console.log('idle');
    }
}

main();

/******/ })()
;
```

This results in a "Uncaught SyntaxError: Cannot use 'import.meta' outside a module" runtime error

title: De-structuring namespace import with default value throws "SyntaxError: Unexpected token"
body:
# Bug report

An invalid syntax is generated when an [esm namespace import](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Statements/import#:~:text=module.js%22%3B-,Namespace%20import,-The%20following%20code) is de-structured and a [default value](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Destructuring#default_value) is provided.  This causes an exception from acorn thus:
```
SyntaxError: Unexpected token (2:22)
    at pp$4.raise (file:///home/projects/github-vbzovyki/node_modules/acorn/dist/acorn.js#cjs:3656:15)
    at pp$9.unexpected (file:///home/projects/github-vbzovyki/node_modules/acorn/dist/acorn.js#cjs:776:10)
    at pp$5.parsePropertyValue (file:///home/projects/github-vbzovyki/node_modules/acorn/dist/acorn.js#cjs:3366:19)
    at pp$5.parseProperty (file:///home/projects/github-vbzovyki/node_modules/acorn/dist/acorn.js#cjs:3310:10)
    at pp$5.parseObj (file:///home/projects/github-vbzovyki/node_modules/acorn/dist/acorn.js#cjs:3265:23)
    at pp$7.parseBindingAtom (file:///home/projects/github-vbzovyki/node_modules/acorn/dist/acorn.js#cjs:2120:21)
    at pp$8.parseVarId (file:///home/projects/github-vbzovyki/node_modules/acorn/dist/acorn.js#cjs:1346:20)
    at pp$8.parseVar (file:///home/projects/github-vbzovyki/node_modules/acorn/dist/acorn.js#cjs:1329:12)
    at pp$8.parseVarStatement (file:///home/projects/github-vbzovyki/node_modules/acorn/dist/acorn.js#cjs:1195:10)
    at pp$8.parseStatement (file:///home/projects/github-vbzovyki/node_modules/acorn/dist/acorn.js#cjs:931:19)
```

See [stackblitz reproduction](https://stackblitz.com/edit/github-vbzovyki)

I believe it's due to [the `JSON.stringify` in the `HarmonyImportSpecifierDependency`](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/dec18718be5dfba28f067fb3827dd620a1f33667/lib/dependencies/HarmonyImportSpecifierDependency.js#L390C5-L390C51) which is wrapping a `newName` in quote characters, thus producing an invalid de-structuring:

```js
import * as namespace from "./some-module.js";
({ /* name */ "newName" = <default value> } = namespace);
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

Expected would be valid de-structuring syntax:

```js
import * as namespace from "./some-module.js";
({ /* name */ newName = <default value> } = namespace);
```

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.99.8
Node.js version: v20.19.1
Operating System: Ubuntu 20.04.0 LTS
Additional tools: https://stackblitz.com/
author: TomStrepsil

title: Feature Request for Webpack Issue #14800: Enhanced Destructuring Support
body:
Summary: 
This feature request aims to enhance Webpack's support for destructuring across various contexts, particularly in plugins like EnvironmentPlugin, DefinePlugin, and ImportMetaPlugin. The goal is to enable seamless tree shaking and efficient code bundling when using destructuring with dynamic imports, namespace objects, and JSON files.

Motivation: Currently, Webpack's support for destructuring in certain plugins is limited, leading to suboptimal tree shaking and larger bundle sizes. Modern JavaScript features like destructuring have become essential for writing clean, concise, and maintainable code. Extending destructuring support will:

Improve tree shaking efficiency.

Reduce bundle sizes by eliminating unused code paths.

Enhance compatibility with modern JavaScript practices.

Simplify developer workflows when using Webpack.


Scope: This feature would include:

1. Parser-Level Improvements:

Extend the core parser to handle destructuring more effectively in module scopes.

Ensure plugins like EnvironmentPlugin and DefinePlugin can recognize destructured variables without breaking tree shaking.



2. Plugin-Specific Enhancements:

Update plugins like ImportMetaPlugin to support partial destructuring without introducing unnecessary code paths.

Improve support for destructuring namespace imports and JSON files.



3. Testing and Validation:

Add comprehensive test cases for various destructuring patterns across supported plugins.

Ensure backward compatibility with existing projects.




Expected Outcome:

Reduced bundle sizes for projects using destructuring.

Improved performance due to more efficient tree shaking.

Better developer experience when using Webpack for modern JavaScript applications.
author: Shivshankarpradhan

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

With `experiments.lazyCompilation` enabled, `import()`ing an asset module will fail with the following error message:

```
hook.js:608 TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'call')
    at __webpack_require__ (bootstrap:22:1)
    at fn (hot module replacement:61:1)
    at __webpack_require__.t (create fake namespace object:10:1)
```

<details>
  <summary>Relevant code from __webpack_require__</summary>

```js
	// Execute the module function
	var execOptions = { id: moduleId, module: module, factory: __webpack_modules__[moduleId], require: __webpack_require__ };
	__webpack_require__.i.forEach(function(handler) { handler(execOptions); });
	module = execOptions.module;
	execOptions.factory.call(module.exports, module, module.exports, execOptions.require);
//.                         ^^^^
```
</details>

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Reproduction repository: https://github.com/uhyo/webpack-bug-repro

Revelant settings from `webpack.config.js`:

```js
  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.png$/,
        type: 'asset/resource',
      },
    ],
  },
  experiments: {
    lazyCompilation: {
      entries: false,
      imports: true,
    }
  }
```

Relevant code:

```js
import(`./assets/illust.png`).then((module) => {
  console.log('Image loaded:', module.default);
  const img = document.createElement('img');
  img.src = module.default;
  document.body.appendChild(img);
}).catch((error) => {
  console.error('Error loading image:');
  console.error(error);
});
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

`import()`ing an asset module should succeed with `lazyCompilation` enabled.

title: Broken import of esm modules
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

PR #19434 broke how chunks are loaded when they have a directory segment in the `output.filename`. The tests in the PR don't sufficiently test that the output is correct. This breaks esm imports in webpack 5.99.6 and above. This problem was correctly identified in #19495 but dismissed, claiming that the `/./` in the final path is not an issue.

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

The concatenated url is incorrect. It includes both the `publicPath`, which is absolute to the server, and the relative path based on the current chunk file. The combination is nonsensical unless the current chunk is placed in the root directory.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

The issue is reproducible in this simple test case: https://github.com/mariusGundersen/webpack-module-publicPath-issue. 


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

The path to the chunk should not depend on the current chunk location, since it uses the `publicPath`, which makes the lookup incorrect. The `rootOutputDir` should only be used when `publicPath` is `auto`.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.99.8
Node.js version: 22.6.0
Operating System: Windows and Linux

title: Incorrect handling of createRequire and require
body:
# Bug report

Context: My projects use NextJs (webpack behind the scene for bundling) and Prisma (database ORM), and Prisma uses their code generator to generate the client code by project.

**What is the current behavior?**

Webpack couldn't bundle the TypeScript code generated by Prisma properly, specifically line 80-83 of [code](https://github.com/CHC383/Next.js-Prisma-TS-Generator/blob/main/src/generated/prisma/internal/class.ts#L80-L83) in `<generated_output>/internal/class.ts`, and cause the error below

```
TypeError: t is not a function
    at Object.getQueryCompilerWasmModule (.next/server/app/page.js:1:29120)
```

The current generated bundle code is
```js
r=t("file:///xxx/webpack-require-debug/src/generated/prisma/internal/class.ts").resolve("@prisma/client/runtime/query_compiler_bg.postgresql.wasm")
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

- Minimum reproduction with Webpack (v5.99.9) only: https://github.com/CHC383/webpack-require-debug
    - `pnpm i`
    - `pnpm webpack`
    - Inspect the output `dist/bundle.js` and search for `class.ts`, and find the part as the current behavior above. The error seems to be a runtime error, so not sure how to trigger it during the webpack bundling, for the original reproduction please check the next example.
- Minimum reproduction with the NextJs stack (Webpack v5.98.0): https://github.com/CHC383/Next.js-Prisma-TS-Generator
    - `pnpm i`
    - `pnpm build`
    - The error message `TypeError: t is not a function` is shown in the console output

**What is the expected behavior?**

Please correct me if I am wrong, the expected code might be something like
```js
n=require.resolve("@prisma/client/runtime/query_compiler_bg.postgresql.wasm");
```

**Questions**
1. Can and should this use case handle by Webpack?
2. Is their an alternative of the code to achieve the same effect but more bundler friendly?
3. Is their a workaround (configure/override in webpack config) to handle the current code, such as `module.rules` with `string-replace-loader` like https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/4175#issuecomment-450746682

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  v5.99.9
Node.js version: v22.16.0
Operating System: MacOS
Additional tools:

**Note:**
- Related issues: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/14228 and https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/16724
- I have also cross posted on Prisma (https://github.com/prisma/prisma/issues/27343) and NextJs (https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/80257), but after some investigation I realized the root cause is on Webpack
author: CHC383

title: CodeMirror 6 Package Deduplication Issue in Webpack
body:
### Summary
When integrating CodeMirror 6 in applications bundled with webpack, multiple instances of critical CodeMirror packages (@codemirror/state and @codemirror/view) are created, causing broken functionality, incompatible state objects, and rendering problems. This requires manual deduplication configuration in both bundlers, which should ideally be handled automatically.

**Environment**
Webpack: v5.58.0
Webpack CLI: v4.9.2
Webpack Dev Server: v4.15.2
@codemirror/state: v6.5.2
@codemirror/view: v6.36.4
React: v18.2.0
Node.js: (latest stable)
OS: macOS

**Description**
CodeMirror 6 is modular and consists of several packages that work together. When integrated into a project using webpack or vite, the bundlers fail to properly deduplicate the CodeMirror packages, leading to multiple instances of the same package being loaded. This causes critical issues with the editor functionality.

_The two most affected packages are:_

**@codemirror/state:** Manages the editor state and transactions
**@codemirror/view:** Handles the DOM representation of the editor

**Without proper deduplication, the following issues occur:**

 - Multiple instances of CodeMirror packages leading to broken functionality
 - Errors related to incompatible state objects
 - Rendering problems in the code editor

### Steps to Reproduce
 - Create a new React application using webpack or vite
 - Install CodeMirror 6 packages: @codemirror/state, @codemirror/view, and other required CodeMirror packages
 - Create a component that uses CodeMirror
 - Import this component in another part of your application
 - Run the application and observe the console errors or broken functionality

### Current Workarounds

// In webpack.config.js/cjs
```
resolve: {
    // Dedupe packages to avoid duplicate instances
    alias: {
        "@codemirror/state": path.resolve(__dirname, "node_modules/@codemirror/state"),
        "@codemirror/view": path.resolve(__dirname, "node_modules/@codemirror/view"),
    },
},
```

### Expected Behavior
The bundlers should automatically deduplicate these packages without requiring manual configuration. This is especially important for packages like CodeMirror that rely on singleton instances of certain modules to function correctly.

### Actual Behavior
Without the manual deduplication configuration, multiple instances of CodeMirror packages are created, causing the editor to malfunction with errors related to incompatible state objects and rendering issues.

### Possible Root Cause
The issue may be related to how webpack handle module resolution and deduplication, particularly for packages that require singleton instances or are mark as sideEffect false. The problem might be exacerbated by the modular nature of CodeMirror 6, where different parts of an application might import different CodeMirror packages that internally depend on @codemirror/state and @codemirror/view. I think that is somehow related to agresive tree shaking that cause some parts of codemirror package are created more than once and than some inner check for instanceof are not working. Without alias rule i was observe that state object create was called 2 times from 2 different places in final bundle, but with allias is also called 2times but from 1 place now.

### Additional info
We use in project packages that internally use codemirror multiple times, but always its same version of this package.

title: Syntactically invalid bundle generated for commonjs-static when using ts-loader
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When using `commonjs-static` as the library type along with `ts-loader`, webpack may output a bundle that is syntactically invalid. In particular, if the keyword `this` is present in the source code at the module level (as it may be in e.g. code transpiled from TypeScript, or in polyfill code), webpack ends up generating a `for` loop which is missing a closing brace:

```js
/******/        var __webpack_exports__ = {};
/******/        __webpack_modules__["./src/index.ts"](0, __webpack_exports__);
/******/        for(var __webpack_i__ in __webpack_exports__) {
/******/          exports[__webpack_i__] = __webpack_exports__[__webpack_i__];
/******/        
/******/        Object.defineProperty(exports, "__esModule", { value: true });
```

```
> npx webpack build
asset main.js 1.67 KiB [emitted] (name: main)
./src/index.ts 194 bytes [built] [code generated]
webpack 5.99.8 compiled successfully in 686 ms
> node dist/main.js 
./dist/main.js:36
/******/ })()
          ^

SyntaxError: Unexpected token ')'
    at internalCompileFunction (node:internal/vm:73:18)
    at wrapSafe (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1176:20)
    at Module._compile (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1218:27)
    at Module._extensions..js (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1308:10)
    at Module.load (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1117:32)
    at Module._load (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:958:12)
    at Function.executeUserEntryPoint [as runMain] (node:internal/modules/run_main:81:12)
    at node:internal/main/run_main_module:23:47

Node.js v18.16.0
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

* Create a project from the files in this gist (put `index.ts` in `src`): https://gist.github.com/arvidfm/350eb8bdb328b0e70fec2eeea240a6df
* Run `npx webpack build`
* Run `node dist/main.js`

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

`node dist/main.js` should execute successfully, without raising a `SyntaxError`.

**Other relevant information:**
The issue can be found on these lines: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/a11302288d2a8851ff89405a122c1d04709574ea/lib/library/AssignLibraryPlugin.js#L339-L349

In particular, if `hasProvided` is `false`, the closing brace for the `for` loop is not emitted.

```
> npx webpack --version

  System:
    OS: Linux 6.11 Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS 24.04.2 LTS (Noble Numbat)
    CPU: (20) x64 12th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-12800H
    Memory: 13.76 GB / 30.98 GB
  Binaries:
    Node: 18.16.0 - ~/.nix-profile/bin/node
    npm: 9.5.1 - ~/.nix-profile/bin/npm
  Packages:
    ts-loader: 9.5.2 => 9.5.2 
    webpack: 5.99.8 => 5.99.8 
    webpack-cli: 6.0.1 => 6.0.1
```

title: Should delete unused ignored module (`RawModule`)
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**


When using side effect import on an ignored module (or `RawModule`), the module would always exist in `__webpack_modules__` with empty content.

```js
/******/ 	var __webpack_modules__ = ({

/***/ 914:
/***/ (() => {

/* (ignored) */

/***/ })

/* harmony import */ var foo__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(914);
```

A typical use case for an ignored module is importing a module intended solely for debugging purposes.

> For a real-world case, see: https://github.com/lynx-family/lynx-stack/pull/1250
> ```js
> import '@lynx-js/react/debug'
> ```

Imports to such ignored modules are expected not to contribute to an increase in bundle size in production.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

https://github.com/colinaaa-reproductions/webpack-repro-tree-shaking-raw-module

title: `Float16Array` type not available in Node v22
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

Since https://github.com/webpack/webpack/releases/tag/v5.100.0 (and in particular since [#19622 (`e9cc0fe`)](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/19622/commits/e9cc0fe7e68f56d42798a818defbdef47348e6e7)), the type `Float16Array` is referenced in webpack's types. This type is not available in Node v22 (see also https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Float16Array#browser_compatibility), so a TypeScript type check fails.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Install `typescript` v5.8.3, `@types/node` v22.16.2 and `webpack` v5.100.0. Run `tsc`:

```
node_modules/webpack/types.d.ts(266,4): error TS2304: Cannot find name 'Float16Array'.
node_modules/webpack/types.d.ts(17334,6): error TS2304: Cannot find name 'Float16Array'.
node_modules/webpack/types.d.ts(17354,6): error TS2304: Cannot find name 'Float16Array'.
```


**What is the expected behavior?**

No type errors. Node v22 should still be supported because it is the current LTS version: https://nodejs.org/en/about/previous-releases#release-schedule

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.100.0
Node.js version: 22.16.2

title: Top level await leaves imported module wrapped in a Promise on HMR
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

A module with a top-level await is left wrapped in a Promise.

Steps to reproduce:

1. Clone the repo: https://github.com/faergeek/webpack-top-level-await-hmr
2. Install dependencies: `npm install`
3. Start watch mode: `npm start`
4. Edit `src/add.js` file.
5. Observe a console log showing `{ add: undefined, addNs: Promise(...) }`.
6. Comment out line 1 in `src/add.js`, uncomment line 2. This results in an update bubbling up to `src/index.js`, which might be a separate issue as well.
7. Edit `src/add.js` again. This time log shows `{ add: [Function: add], addNs: Object [Module] { .... } }`, which is a normal behavior.

**What is the expected behavior?**

A module should not be left wrapped in a Promise. Parent module should see a result of a Promise resolution instead.

**Other relevant information:**

Node.js version: v24.4.1
Operating System: Arch Linux
Additional tools: webpack-cli

title: siblings not specified correctly in chunk（stats json）
body:
Here is the setting for webpack
``` javascript
cacheGroups: {
        lib: {
          test: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/]/,
          priority: -10,
          name: 'lib_haha',
          enforce: true,
          reuseExistingChunk: true,
        },
        commonAsync: {
          name: 'commonAsync_haha',
          chunks: 'async',
          minChunks: 2,
          priority: -20,
          enforce: true, 
},
        commonInitial: {
          name: 'commonInitial_haha',
          chunks: 'initial',
          minChunks: 2,
          priority: -15,
          enforce: true,          // reuseExistingChunk: true,
        },
      },
```
Here is the js file
``` javascript
btn.onclick = () => {
  import('./async1' /* webpackChunkName: "foo" */
  ).then(e => {
    console.log("async1", e)
    e.async1()
    import('./async2' /* webpackChunkName: "bar" */
    ).then(e => {
      e.async2()
      console.log(e)
      console.log("index", dayjs().format(), hello)
    })
  })
}
```
We can see above that 'foo' and 'bar' should be async chunks, And there are some common parts in 'foo' and 'bar' which will be separated as another chunk named commonAsync_haha due to the cacheGroups config.

However in the stats json
``` json
{

    "id": "bar",
    "siblings": ["commonAsync_haha"],
    "parents": ["commonInitial_haha", "index", "lib_haha", "runtime"],
    "children": [],
    },
    {
      "rendered": true,
      "initial": false,
      "entry": false,
      "recorded": false,
      "reason": "split chunk (cache group: commonAsync) (name: commonAsync_haha)",
      "size": 85,
      "sizes": { "javascript": 85 },
      "names": ["commonAsync_haha"],
      "idHints": ["commonAsync"],
      "runtime": ["runtime"],
      "files": ["commonAsync_haha.js"],
      "auxiliaryFiles": [],
      "hash": "0dfd0fa2b5b03aaf5b88",
      "childrenByOrder": {},

title: Using `.length` on iterables (target ES5)
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When using 'es5' target, Webpack treats iterables like arrays but looping over them using `.length` property when this doesn't exist on iterables. It will convert the following:

```js
        for (const [walletId, subscriptions] of subscriptionUpdates.entries()) {
          input.props.dispatch({
            type: 'CURRENCY_ENGINE_UPDATE_CHANGE_SERVICE_SUBSCRIPTIONS',
            payload: {
              walletId,
              subscriptions
            }
          })
        }
```

into

```js
                      for (O = 0, A = I.entries(); O < A.length; O++)
                        (_ = s(A[O], 2)),
                          (T = _[0]),
                          (P = _[1]),
                          e.props.dispatch({
                            type:
                              'CURRENCY_ENGINE_UPDATE_CHANGE_SERVICE_SUBSCRIPTIONS',
                            payload: { walletId: T, subscriptions: P }
                          })
```

Notice `A.length` is not value for `A` which is an iterable returned by `I.entries()`.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

1. Clone https://github.com/edgeapp/edge-core-js
2. Checkout version 2.33.0 (tag v2.33.0)
3. Run `yarn prepare` to build the package
4. Run `npm pack` to bundle
5. Run `tar -xvf edge-core-js-2.33.0.tgz` to unarchive the package
6. Run `cd package/android/src/main/assets/`
7. Run `npx prettier edge-core.js -w` to format the minified file
8. View line 32833 of edge-core.js for the transpilation

**What is the expected behavior?**

The transpilation should use `Array.from()` to convert iterables to arrays before using it in a for loop, or it should use another looping mechanism that doesn't rely on `.length`.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.89.0 (fails on 5.101.0 also)
Node.js version: 20.x
Operating System: macOS 15.5 (24F74)
Additional tools:

title: Webpack introduces "unreachable code after return statement" warning in Firefox
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Webpack replaces an unreachable `break` statement with an unreachable `{}` statement. This causes a warning in Firefox (https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Errors/Stmt_after_return).

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

1. Create a file `source.js` with the following content:
    ```js
    function test_switch(value) {
        switch (value) {
            case 1:
                return;
                break;
        }
    }
    ```
2. Build the file. Using development mode, since production mode uses stronger dead code elimination. Using no devtool, so the output is easy to inspect.
    ```sh
    webpack --mode development --devtool false --entry ./source.js
    ```
3. Output in `dist/main.js`, note that the `break` has been replaced by `{}`:
    ```js
    /******/ (() => { // webpackBootstrap
    /*!*******************!*\
      !*** ./source.js ***!
      \*******************/
    function test_switch(value) {
        switch (value) {
            case 1:
                return;
                // removed by dead control flow
    {}
        }
    }
    
    /******/ })()
    ;
    ```

Note that the same occurs for unreachable `break` statements after a `return` in a `while` loop, and possibly other places too.

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

I expect the unreachable `break` statement to removed altogether, and not be replaced by another unreachable statement (though I am sure there were reasons for this).


**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.101.2
Node.js version: 22.16.0
Operating System: Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS
Additional tools: webpack-cli@6.0.1

---

Note: You might call this a bug in Firefox, since the `{}` is technically a block statement here and not an empty object expression. But I suppose it's specifically a warning for when you meant to type `return {}` and accidentally inserted a newline. So I would not expect them to consider this a bug on their end.

**What is the current behavior?**
Upgrade from webpack 5.100.0 to 5.101.2 fails because of a typescript error when adding the webpack compiler to webpack-dev-middleware: `'Compiler | null' is not assignable to parameter of type 'Compiler | MultiCompiler'`. 

I could not find any reports on this issue. Is this wanted and expected behaviour moving forwards? And what would be the best practice way of fixing it? I can of course add a null check in my code to fix this, but is that what you would recommend? 


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Webpack-dev-middleware is configured [according to the docs](https://webpack.js.org/guides/development/#using-webpack-dev-middleware).

This is a simplified version of our `server.ts` with the relevant config.
``` 
import webpack from 'webpack';
import webpackDevMiddleware from 'webpack-dev-middleware';
import webpackDevConfig from './webpack-config';

const compiler = webpack(webpackDevConfig); // Returns type Compiler | null

const middleware = webpackDevMiddleware(compiler, { 
    publicPath: webpackDevConfig.output.publicPath,
    writeToDisk: true,
});
// webpackDevMiddleware expects compiler to be of type 'Compiler | MultiCompiler', this fails

[…]
```

**What is the expected behavior?**
I expect the typescript types of `webpack` and `webpack-dev-middleware` to be compatible. Especially since we have a fairly standard setup of webpack-dev-middleware. I could simply add a null check to make typescript happy, but is that the solution you recommend moving forwards?

```
const compiler = webpack(webpackDevConfig);

// A possible solution:
if (!compiler) {

}

const middleware = webpackDevMiddleware(compiler, {

title: Incorrect resolution of default import for library.type = "system"
body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/orgs/webpack/discussions/19922
Originally posted by **AlekseyMalakhov** September 20, 2025 Currently I’m migrating my project from **Webpack 4** to **Webpack 5**. Project uses SystemJS modules. In **Webpack 4** when you bundle a library (e. g. React) as SystemJS module, default import of resulting module will **return a whole object** (React in my case). In **Webpack 5** when you bundle a library (e. g. React) as SystemJS module, default import of resulting module will **return undefined**, instead of the whole object. I created a small, reproducible example. [Here is a link](https://github.com/AlekseyMalakhov/webpack5_bug) Repo contains 2 folders - webpack4 and webpack5 with (almost) identical code. The goal is to show the difference in behavior between Webpack 4 and 5. How to start: 1. Run `npm install` in both folders (webpack4 and webpack5) 2. Run `npm run serve` in both folders 3. In your browser open 2 URLs: [http://localhost:9004](http://localhost:9004) - corresponds to Webpack4 and [http://localhost:9005](http://localhost:9005) - corresponds to Webpack5 4. Open devtools and check Console for both tabs. Console should display `console.logs` from `src/app.js` file. You will see the following difference: [http://localhost:9004](http://localhost:9004) - displays data for **both default and named import** (React and useEffect). [http://localhost:9005](http://localhost:9004) - displays data **only for named import** (useEffect). React is undefined. If we check the resulted `main.js` files created by both webpacks we will see the that **Webpack 5** has the following expression: But the problem is that `e` **does not have a `default`** property. That’s why React is undefined. **Webpack 4** does not have this string and works correctly - for default import it returns the whole React module. Any suggestions how to fix it in **Webpack 5**?
author: alexander-akait

title: On import './filename.css'  add './filename.css' to CSSStyleSheet
body:
### Feature Proposal

It would be useful for the style-loader when it encounters
```
import './filename.css'
```
to add './filename.css' to the DOM CSSStyleSheet, perhaps in the href attribute. 

### Feature Use Case

Use case is so that the appropriate CSSStyleSheet can be added to shadowDOM css. Right now this is accomplished by checking all main DOM CSSStyleSheets for the selectors in './filename.css' but this is error prone. It would be better to just search CSSStyleSheets for the matching 'href'.

### Additional Context

_No response_

title: webpack output is not treated as a module (`import.meta`)
body:
### Bug Description

Goal:  get import.meta to be "ignored" correctly by webpack.

Some context: in Bitfocus Companion, with its current webpack settings,
   `import.meta.url` (in @sentry/node-core/build/esm/sdk/esmLoader.js) is converted to a hardcoded string containing the full dev environment path  -- since the goal is distribute the code, this is an error, and since the "problem" is in a third-party package, I can't simply change their code. Also note that the code works correctly before packaging.
    
Setting `module.parser.javascript.importMeta: false` in webpackconfig fixes that problem in the code (i.e. import.meta is left in the code) but it causes other errors including a variation on this simplified one described below.)

No matter what I have tried, so far, one thing or another breaks... Here's one example:



### Link to Minimal Reproduction and step to  reproduce

Please see code at: https://github.com/arikorn/wptest


To run (assuming corepack is enabled?, etc.):
~~~
yarn
# test that the code works:
node FixImports.mts  

# now pack and test the packed code:
npx webpack
node dist/main.mjs
~~~


### Expected Behavior

both unpacked and packaged code should run to completion.

### Actual Behavior

Unpacked code is fine but with packed code, running it results in the following error:

~~~bash
$ node dist/main.mjs
<anonymous_script>:20
        const testPath = path__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_1___default().join(import.meta.dirname, 'node_modules/bufferutil')
                                                                                 ^^^^

SyntaxError: Cannot use 'import.meta' outside a module
~~~

Note that the "entrypoint" filename, the package.json, and the packaged filename all indicate that it's a module.

I have tried numerous variations in the config file while keeping `module.parser.javascript.importMeta: false`  but with no effect. In the bitfocus repo I've also tried things like turning importMeta off and setting `module.parser.javascript.url: 'relative'` but that both causes other errors and does *not* substitute a relative URL for `import.meta.url` (i.e. it's still a full path)

Incidentally, in the Bitfocus repo version of this, if I substitute `__dirname` for `import.meta.dirname` node complains that `__dirname` is not defined in the packaged code! I.e. the code errors whether you assume it's a module or not!  (In this simplified version, as long as `node.__dirname` is set to true, this code-fix works. Though of course it's technically wrong to do so, and breaks the unpackaged version.

### Environment

```shell
System:
    OS: Linux 6.6 Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS 24.04.3 LTS (Noble Numbat)
    CPU: (6) x64 Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-9400 CPU @ 2.90GHz
    Memory: 5.08 GB / 7.68 GB
  Binaries:
    Node: 22.21.0 - /run/user/1000/fnm_multishells/2456183_1762289908966/bin/node
    Yarn: 4.10.3 - /mnt/c/Program Files/nodejs/yarn
    npm: 10.9.4 - /run/user/1000/fnm_multishells/2456183_1762289908966/bin/npm
  Packages:
    webpack: ^5.102.1 => 5.102.1 
    webpack-cli: ^6.0.1 => 6.0.1
```

### Is this a regression?

None

### Last Working Version

_No response_

### Additional Context

As mentioned above, this is a very simplified version of what I've been encountering with Bitfocus Companion. If you're able and willing to evaluate that more complex setup too -- i.e., if fixing this doesn't help? -- I could provide instructions. That repo is much more finicky, as mentioned above, and even setting `type: "module"` in dist/package.json causes the packaged code to fail ("require is not defined" somewhere). Just to be clear, the same setting in the base dev folder works just fine, it's only when applied to the packaged code folder that it causes a problem.)

title: NodeEnvironmentPlugin does not purge filesystem for watch-runs
body:
I'm seeing an issue with Webpack where it cannot find a module from `node_modules` after an `npm install` while running in watch mode. While it seems appropriate that this can temporarily occur while performing the `npm install`, it seems Webpack does not recover from this and still considers the module 'not found' during subsequent compilations, even after the `npm install` has completely finished. 

I can try to create a reproduction case for this issue, but I'm still trying to pinpoint what exactly goes wrong and how Webpack is supposed to work in such cases. My suspicion goes to the input filesystem caching performed by https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/102e1a4d81d487568721039e6a588e8420a30b50/lib/node/NodeEnvironmentPlugin.js#L53. There is a `beforeRun` hook to purge the cached filesystem in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/102e1a4d81d487568721039e6a588e8420a30b50/lib/node/NodeEnvironmentPlugin.js#L60-L68, but it seems this hook is never run in watch mode (either using `--watch` or `webpack-dev-server`), probably because those do not trigger the `beforeRun` hook(?). 

Adding a similar listener on the `watchRun` hook seems to solve my issue. Is my assumption correct that such a hook listener for watch mode not being present is a bug?

title: Incorrect debug ID is produced when using `devtool: "hidden-source-map-debugids"`
body:
### Bug Description

When I either set a `devtool` config value to `hidden-source-map-debugids` or use a `webpack.SourceMapDevToolPlugin` with a configuration matching `hidden-source-map-debugids`, the debug ID appended to the generated `.js` file is corrupted – it looks like `//# debugId=<hash>false` instead of `//debugId=<hash>`.

### Link to Minimal Reproduction and step to  reproduce

https://github.com/in-doge-we-trust/webpack-debugids-append-issue-repro

Case #1 (`webpack.SourceMapDevToolPlugin` is used manually) steps:
1. Run `npm install`
2. Run `npm run build`

Case #2 (`devtool` set to `hidden-source-map-debugids`) steps:
1. Run `npm run clear` (optional)
2. Run `npm run build`

### Expected Behavior

`debugId` value in the source-map file matches the value in `//# debugId=<hash>` comment appended to the generated `.js`.

### Actual Behavior

`debugId` value in the source-map file is correct, while `//# debugId=<hash>` comment has `false` appended to the end and looks like this – `//# debugId=<hash>false`.

### Environment

```shell
Binaries:
    Node: 22.18.0
    npm: 10.9.3
Packages:
    webpack: 5.102.1 => 5.102.1
    webpack-cli: 6.0.1 => 6.0.1
```

### Is this a regression?

No

### Last Working Version

_No response_

### Additional Context

As a temporary workaround, developers can force the `append` to be a new line character:
```js
// webpack.config.js
const config = {
  // --snip--

  plugins: [
    new webpack.SourceMapDevToolPlugin({
      filename: "[file].map",

      debugIds: true,
      append: '\n', // force append value

      module: true,
      columns: true,
    }),
  ],
};

export default config;
```

In that case, `append` doesn't corrupt the injected `debugId`, so it looks correct.

title: Unused function in ESM code winds up incorrectly referencing a global
body:
### Bug Description

An unused function in ESM code winds up being incorrectly transformed, referencing a global variable rather than the imported variable.

This causes an unrelated variable in the entry module that happens to have the same name to be renamed.

### Link to Minimal Reproduction and step to  reproduce

Reproduction: [test.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23684113/test.zip)

1. Unzip the reproduction project.
2. Run `npm install && npm exec webpack`.
3. Examine `dist/main.js`.

### Expected Behavior

If function `unused()` is not tree-shaken out, it looks something like this:
```js
function unused() {
    return (0,_i18n_js__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__.__)( "wtf" );
}
```
Also, in the entry module, the `const __` retains that name.

### Actual Behavior

Function `unused()` is present, but incorrectly appears like this:
```js
function unused() {
    return __( "wtf" );
}
```
Also the `const __` has been renamed to `const src_`, presumably to avoid interfering with that global variable reference.

### Environment

```shell
System:
    OS: Linux 6.16 Debian GNU/Linux forky/sid
    CPU: (16) x64 AMD Ryzen 7 7840U w/ Radeon  780M Graphics
    Memory: 22.74 GB / 30.66 GB
  Binaries:
    Node: 22.21.0 - /usr/bin/node
    Yarn: 1.22.22 - /usr/local/bin/yarn
    npm: 10.9.4 - /usr/bin/npm
    pnpm: 10.23.0 - /usr/local/bin/pnpm
  Browsers:
    Firefox: 145.0.1
    Firefox Developer Edition: 145.0.1
  Packages:
    webpack: 5.103.0 => 5.103.0 
    webpack-cli: ^6.0.1 => 6.0.1
```

### Is this a regression?

None

### Last Working Version

_No response_

### Additional Context

v5.92.0 introduced the "`const __` has been renamed to `const src_`" part, presumably due #18772. Turning off `optimization.avoidEntryIife` works around that part.

Versions as far back as v5.61.0 generate the incorrect code in the `unused` function. I couldn't check earlier versions due to errors like "error:0308010C:digital envelope routines::unsupported".

author: ShenHongFei

title: [Feature] Add visual build progress indicator with estimated completion time
body:
### Feature Proposal

## Problem Statement

When building large projects with Webpack, developers often face long build times (5-30+ minutes) with minimal feedback about progress. The current output shows:

- Module count updates
- Percentage completion (sometimes)
- But no indication of:
  - Estimated time remaining
  - Which phase is taking the longest
  - Visual progress indication
  - Historical build time comparison

This leads to:
- Developers context-switching during builds
- Inability to identify performance regressions
- Unclear whether a build is stuck or just slow
- Difficulty optimizing build configuration

## Proposed Solution

Add an enhanced progress plugin that provides:

### 1. Visual Progress Bar
```
Building [████████████░░░░░░░░] 65% | ETA: 2m 15s
┌─ Modules: 3,247/5,012 (65%)
├─ Current: optimizing chunk assets
├─ Duration: 8m 42s
└─ Average build: 13m 20s (baseline)
```

### 2. Phase Breakdown
Show time spent in each phase:
- Module resolution: 2m 15s
- Module building: 4m 30s 
- Chunk optimization: 1m 57s
- Asset optimization: Currently running...

### 3. Historical Context
- Compare current build against last N builds
- Highlight phases that are significantly slower
- Show trend over time

### 4. Configuration Options

```javascript
module.exports = {
  plugins: [
    new webpack.ProgressPlugin({
      visualProgress: true,
      estimateTime: true,
      showPhases: true,
      compareBaseline: true,
      baselineBuilds: 10,
      profile: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development'
    })
  ]
};
```

## Benefits

1. **Better Developer Experience**
   - Know when to take a coffee break vs stay focused
   - Understand if changes impacted build performance
   - Identify optimization opportunities

2. **CI/CD Insights**
   - Track build time regressions in pipelines
   - Identify slow phases for optimization
   - Historical data for capacity planning

3. **Debugging Aid**
   - Quickly identify if build is stuck vs slow
   - See which loaders/plugins are bottlenecks
   - Compare development vs production builds

## Implementation Considerations

- Should work with existing `ProgressPlugin`
- Minimal performance overhead (< 1%)
- Optional features (can be disabled)
- Works in both terminal and CI environments
- Compatible with webpack 5.x

## Related Work

- Vite shows build progress with ETA
- Next.js has visual progress indicators
- Turbopack provides detailed timing information

This would bring Webpack's developer experience closer to modern build tools while maintaining its flexibility and power.

## Willing to Contribute

I'd be happy to work on a PR for this if the maintainers are interested. This could potentially be implemented as an enhanced version of the existing ProgressPlugin or as a separate community plugin.

### Feature Use Case

**As a developer working on a large enterprise application:**

I want to see detailed build progress with time estimates so that I can:
- Better manage my time during long builds (know if I have time for coffee or need to stay focused)
- Identify when builds are slower than usual and investigate why
- Track performance impact of configuration changes
- Report accurate build time requirements to my team and management

**As a CI/CD engineer:**

I want historical build metrics so that I can:
- Set appropriate timeouts for build pipelines
- Identify performance regressions before they impact the team
- Justify infrastructure upgrades with data
- Optimize build configurations based on phase-level metrics

**Example Workflow:**
```bash
$ npm run build

Building [████████████░░░░░░░░] 65% | ETA: 2m 15s
┌─ Phase: Optimizing chunk assets
├─ Modules: 3,247/5,012 processed
├─ Duration: 8m 42s
└─ Baseline: 13m 20s avg (23% faster!) ✨

[Developer sees build is faster than normal, confirming recent optimizations worked]
```

### Additional Context

_No response_

title: Copy anchor link to clipboard when clicked in blog pages
body:
### Feature Proposal

What happened:
At the moment, when clicking an anchor link on the official Webpack blog (for example:
https://webpack.js.org/blog/2020-12-08-roadmap-2021/#what-happened-so-far),
the browser simply navigates to that anchor. It does not copy the link (including the #slug) to the clipboard for easy sharing.
This makes it harder for users to quickly share deep links to specific sections — even though the link is visible in the URL bar, manually copying it every time is inconvenient.

📌 What I expected:
Clicking an anchor link should copy the full URL including the hash/fragment (e.g., #what-happened-so-far) to the clipboard automatically, so it can be easily shared. This improves usability when navigating blog posts or documentation.

### Feature Use Case

- Users often want to share or reference specific sections of a blog post.
- Automatically copying the permalink on click improves shareability and user experience.
- Modern documentation/blog platforms (e.g., GitHub, VuePress) often provide visual affordances for sharing anchor links.

### Additional Context

Example behavior:
Click on “What happened so far” → clipboard now contains
https://webpack.js.org/blog/2020-12-08-roadmap-2021/#what-happened-so-far

⚠️ This should not break normal navigation if the user wants to scroll to the anchor — both behaviors should work nicely together.

title: Webpack doesn't unbox externals when libraryTarget is amd and package.json type field is explicitly "module"
body:
### Bug Description

Given:
 1. a project that explicitly specifies `"type": "module"` in `package.json`;
 2. a source code in ESM format that does import from external library `import defaultExport, { namedExport } from 'external-dependency'`;
 3. webpack build with `output.libraryTarget = 'amd'` and externals section.

With this combination webpack will produce code that won't work in some cases because it doesn't unwrap "default" export. It also means that if external dependency is also built with webpack it is guaranteed that resulting combination won't work, because webpack produces amd module that returns ESM-like object.

If "type" field is unset, produced output is correct and there is no issue.

For me it looks like a bug, because when I'm writing code in ESM format I'm assuming that (by default) module that I'm importing is ESM too or behaves like ESM. And conversion to amd format should preserve this semantics for externals without depending on type field of package being built.

### Link to Minimal Reproduction and step to  reproduce

Below are 3 files constituting the project being built
and produced output + profiles for builds with type module set and without it: [output.tar.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24938930/output.tar.gz)

package.json
```json
{
  "name": "main",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": {
    "build": "webpack"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "webpack": "^5.104.1",
    "webpack-cli": "^6.0.1"
  }
 }
```

index.js
```javascript
import defaultExport, { namedExport } from 'external-dependency'

console.log(defaultExport, namedExport)

export default defaultExport
export { namedExport }
```

webpack.config.js
```javascript
export default {
  entry: "./index.js",
  mode: "none",
  output: {
    libraryTarget: "amd",
  },
  externals: {
    "external-dependency": "amd external-dependency",
  },
}
```

### Expected Behavior

Produced output unpacks default export

### Actual Behavior

Produced output doesn't unpack default export

### Environment

```shell
System:
  OS: macOS 26.2
  CPU: (12) arm64 Apple M2 Max
  Memory: 455.16 MB / 64.00 GB
Binaries:
  Node: 25.5.0 - /opt/homebrew/bin/node
  Yarn: 1.22.22 - /opt/homebrew/bin/yarn
  npm: 11.8.0 - /opt/homebrew/bin/npm
  pnpm: 10.28.2 - /opt/homebrew/bin/pnpm
Browsers:
  Chrome: 144.0.7559.98
  Edge: 144.0.3719.92
  Firefox: 146.0.1
  Safari: 26.2
Packages:
  webpack: ^5.104.1 => 5.104.1
  webpack-cli: ^6.0.1 => 6.0.1
```

### Is this a regression?

None

### Last Working Version

_No response_

### Additional Context

I assume that amd is not the only problematic library target. CommonJS output should have similar issues.

body:
### Feature Proposal Add code snippets and helpful suggestions to webpack error messages to make debugging easier. When an error occurs, show: - The actual code around the error (3-5 lines) - A visual indicator pointing to the exact error location - Suggestions on how to fix the error - Links to relevant documentation **Current behavior:** ``` ERROR in ./src/index.js:15:8 Module parse failed: Unexpected token (15:8) You may need an appropriate loader to handle this file type. ``` **Proposed behavior:** ``` ERROR in ./src/index.js:15:8 13 | import { Component } from 'react'; 14 | 15 | const App = () => { ^ | 16 | return
Hello
; 17 | }; Module parse failed: Unexpected token (15:8) You may need an appropriate loader to handle this file type. 💡 Suggestion: Add babel-loader to process JSX files module: { rules: [{ test: /\.jsx?$/, use: 'babel-loader' }] } 📖 Learn more: https://webpack.js.org/loaders/babel-loader/ ``` ### Feature Use Case **Problem:** When developers get webpack errors, they only see file path and line number. They have to: - Open the file manually - Find the error line - Research how to fix it - This wastes 15-30 minutes per error **Solution:** Show code context directly in the error message so developers can: - See the problematic code immediately - Understand what's wrong - Get suggestions on how to fix it - Copy configuration examples **Real-world scenarios:** 1. **New developer joins a project:** - Runs build, gets parse error - Sees code snippet and suggestion - Fixes it in 2 minutes instead of 20 minutes 2. **Large codebase with 500+ files:** - Error in `./src/utils/helpers/validation.js:142:8` - Don't need to open file - code visible in error - Saves navigation time 3. **Common loader errors:** - Missing CSS loader? See suggestion with config - Missing babel-loader? See suggestion with config - No more searching documentation **Impact:** - Saves 10-25 minutes per error - Better onboarding for new developers - Fewer support questions - Makes webpack more user-friendly ### Additional Context ### Current Error Examples **Example 1: Module Parse Error** ``` ERROR in ./src/components/App.jsx:12:5 Module parse failed: Unexpected token (12:5) You may need an appropriate loader to handle this file type. ``` **Problem:** No code shown, no suggestion about which loader to use. **Example 2: Missing Module** ``` ERROR in ./src/index.js:3:0 Module not found: Error: Can't resolve './utils/helper' in '/project/src' ``` **Problem:** No code snippet showing the import, no suggestions about common fixes. **Example 3: CSS Loader Error** ``` ERROR in ./src/styles.css:1:0 Module parse failed: Unexpected token (1:0) You may need an appropriate loader to handle this file type. ``` **Problem:** No suggestion about css-loader, no configuration example. ### Comparison with Other Tools **TypeScript shows:** ``` src/index.ts:15:8 - error TS2304: Cannot find name 'Component'. 15 const App: Component = () => { ^ ``` **ESLint shows:** ``` ./src/index.js 15:8 error 'Component' is not defined no-undef 13 | import { Component } from 'react'; 14 | > 15 | const App: Component = () => { | ^ 16 | return
Hello
; 17 | }; ``` **Webpack currently shows:** ``` ERROR in ./src/index.js:15:8 Module parse failed: Unexpected token (15:8) ``` **Observation:** Other modern tools show code context, webpack doesn't. ### Workarounds (Current Solutions) 1. **Use external plugins:** - `friendly-errors-webpack-plugin` (community plugin) - `error-overlay-webpack-plugin` - **Problem:** Requires extra dependencies, not built-in 2. **Manual investigation:** - Open file in editor - Find error line - Search online for solutions - **Problem:** Time-consuming, requires webpack knowledge ### Implementation Notes - **Performance:** Minimal impact (files already in memory during compilation) - **Security:** Respect `stats.errorDetails` setting, allow disabling via config - **Priority errors:** Start with most common (parse errors, missing modules, loader errors) - **Backward compatible:** Fully additive, no breaking changes ### Benefits **For developers:** - Faster debugging - Better onboarding - Less frustration **For maintainers:** - Fewer support questions - Better issue reports - Improved webpack reputation **For ecosystem:** - Lower barrier to entry - Better developer experience - Aligns with modern tooling standards
author: asadjan4611

title: Bug / UX Enhancement - Circular Dependency Error Messages
body:
### Bug Description

When webpack encounters a circular dependency, the error message is too generic and doesn't provide enough information to help developers debug the issue. The current error only states "There is a circular build dependency" without showing:
- Which modules are involved in the cycle
- The dependency chain showing how modules depend on each other
- File paths and line numbers where dependencies are imported
- Suggestions on how to break the cycle

This makes debugging circular dependencies extremely difficult, especially in large codebases with hundreds of modules.

**Current error message:**
```
There is a circular build dependency, which makes it impossible to create this module
```

**Problem:** Developers have no idea which files are causing the circular dependency and must manually trace through imports, which can take hours in large projects.

## 2. Link to Minimal Reproduction and Steps to Reproduce

### Minimal Reproduction Repository

Create a simple project with the following structure:

**File structure:**
```
project/
├── src/
│   ├── index.js
│   ├── moduleA.js
│   └── moduleB.js
└── webpack.config.js
```

**src/index.js:**
```javascript
import { funcA } from './moduleA';
console.log('App started');
```

**src/moduleA.js:**
```javascript
import { funcB } from './moduleB';

export function funcA() {
  return 'Function A';
}
```

**src/moduleB.js:**
```javascript
import { funcA } from './moduleA';

export function funcB() {
  return 'Function B';
}
```

**webpack.config.js:**
```javascript
module.exports = {
  mode: 'development',
  entry: './src/index.js',
  output: {
    filename: 'bundle.js',
    path: __dirname + '/dist'
  }
};
```

### Link to Minimal Reproduction and step to  reproduce


1. Create the files above in a new directory
2. Run `npm init -y` to initialize package.json
3. Install webpack: `npm install --save-dev webpack`
4. Run webpack: `npx webpack`
5. Observe the error message

**Expected result:** Error message shows circular dependency chain
**Actual result:** Generic error message without dependency chain

### Alternative: Large Project Scenario

If testing in a large project (1000+ modules):

1. Create a circular dependency between any two modules
2. Run webpack build
3. Observe the generic error message
4. Try to find the circular dependency manually (very time-consuming)

**Project structure example:**
```
Large project with:
- 500+ source files
- Multiple entry points
- Complex dependency graph
- Circular dependency somewhere in the chain
```

### Expected Behavior


The error message should display:

1. **Full dependency chain** showing the circular path
   - Example: `moduleA.js → moduleB.js → moduleA.js`

2. **File paths with line numbers** for each dependency
   - Show where each import occurs

3. **Visual representation** of the cycle
   - Clear indication of the circular path

4. **Actionable suggestions** for breaking the cycle
   - Common solutions like extracting shared code
   - Using dynamic imports
   - Refactoring suggestions

**Expected error output:**
```
ERROR in Circular dependency detected

Circular dependency chain:
  ./src/moduleA.js (line 1)
    → ./src/moduleB.js (line 1)
      → ./src/moduleA.js (line 1)

To fix this circular dependency:
  - Extract shared code from moduleA.js and moduleB.js to a separate module
  - Use dynamic imports: import('./moduleB').then(...)
  - Consider refactoring the module structure to remove the dependency cycle
```


### Actual Behavior


**Current error output:**
```
ERROR in ./src/moduleA.js

There is a circular build dependency, which makes it impossible to create this module
```

**Problems with current behavior:**
- ❌ No indication of which other modules are involved
- ❌ No dependency chain shown
- ❌ No file paths or line numbers for other modules in the cycle
- ❌ No suggestions on how to fix it
- ❌ Developer must manually trace through all imports to find the cycle

**Impact:**
- In small projects: 10-15 minutes to find and fix
- In large projects: 30 minutes to several hours
- Frustrating developer experience
- Wastes significant development time


### Environment

```shell
**Manual Environment Information:**

System:
  OS: Windows 10 10.0.19045
Binaries:
  Node: 22.16.0
  npm: [your npm version]
Packages:
  webpack: 5.104.1


**Webpack version:** 5.104.1

**Node version:** 22.16.0

**Operating System:** Windows 10

**Package Manager:** npm / yarn (specify which one you use)
```

### Is this a regression?

None

### Last Working Version

_No response_

### Additional Context


### Current Implementation Details

**Code Location:**
- Error class: `lib/errors/BuildCycleError.js`
- Detection logic: `lib/Compilation.js` (around line 2348)
- Error formatting: `lib/formatLocation.js`

**Current BuildCycleError implementation:**
```javascript
class BuildCycleError extends WebpackError {
  constructor(module) {
    super("There is a circular build dependency, which makes it impossible to create this module");
    this.name = "BuildCycleError";
    this.module = module;
  }
}
```

**Problem:** Only stores the module that triggered the error, not the full dependency chain.

### Real-World Impact Examples

**Example 1: Small Project (10-20 files)**
- Developer gets circular dependency error
- Must manually check each file's imports
- Time to fix: 10-15 minutes
- **With enhanced error:** 2-3 minutes (immediate visibility)

**Example 2: Medium Project (100-200 files)**
- Circular dependency in utility modules
- Error shows only one file
- Developer traces through 15+ files to find cycle
- Time to fix: 30-45 minutes
- **With enhanced error:** 5 minutes (full chain visible)

**Example 3: Large Project (500+ files)**
- Circular dependency in component library
- Multiple entry points
- Complex dependency graph
- Time to fix: 1-3 hours (or more)
- **With enhanced error:** 10-15 minutes (clear path shown)

### Comparison with Other Tools

**TypeScript:**
- Shows full error chain
- Provides file paths and line numbers
- Example: "Circular reference detected: A.ts -> B.ts -> A.ts"

**ESLint:**
- Shows dependency relationships
- Clear error messages with context

**Webpack (Current):**
- Generic message only
- No chain information
- No actionable guidance

### Workarounds (Current Solutions)

**Workaround 1: Manual Tracing**
- Open each file mentioned in error
- Check all imports
- Follow the chain manually
- **Problem:** Extremely time-consuming, error-prone

**Workaround 2: Use External Tools**
- Install `madge` to detect circular dependencies: `npx madge --circular src/`
- Use `dependency-cruiser` for dependency analysis
- **Problem:** Requires additional tools, not integrated with webpack

**Workaround 3: Add Custom Plugin**
- Create custom plugin to track dependencies
- Log dependency chains during build
- **Problem:** Complex, requires deep webpack knowledge

### Proposed Solution Details

**Enhancement approach:**
1. Track dependency chain during module resolution
2. Store full chain in BuildCycleError
3. Format chain with file paths and line numbers
4. Add suggestions based on common patterns

**Implementation considerations:**
- Use existing `formatLocation` utility for location formatting
- Leverage module graph information already available
- Add chain tracking to circular dependency detection logic
- Format output similar to other webpack errors

**Example enhanced error format:**
```
ERROR in Circular dependency detected

Circular dependency chain:
  ./src/components/Button.js (line 5: import Icon from './Icon')
    → ./src/components/Icon.js (line 12: import { Button } from './Button')
      → ./src/components/Button.js (line 5)

To fix this circular dependency:
  💡 Extract shared code to a separate module (e.g., ./src/components/shared.js)
  💡 Use dynamic imports: const Icon = () => import('./Icon')
  💡 Refactor component structure to remove mutual dependencies
```

### Benefits

**For Developers:**
- ✅ Saves 10 minutes to several hours per circular dependency
- ✅ Faster debugging and resolution
- ✅ Better understanding of code structure
- ✅ Reduced frustration

**For Teams:**
- ✅ Faster onboarding (new developers can fix issues quickly)
- ✅ Less time spent on debugging
- ✅ Better code quality (easier to identify architectural issues)

**For Webpack:**
- ✅ Improved developer experience
- ✅ Fewer support questions
- ✅ Better reputation as developer-friendly tool
- ✅ Aligns with modern tooling standards

### Related Issues

- Similar error message improvements in webpack discussions
- Community requests for better debugging information
- Part of broader effort to improve webpack DX (Developer Experience)

### Technical Notes

- **Non-breaking change:** Fully additive, doesn't affect existing functionality
- **Performance impact:** Minimal (dependency chain already tracked during resolution)
- **Backward compatible:** Existing error handling remains unchanged
- **Implementation complexity:** Medium (requires tracking and formatting logic)

### Testing Scenarios

**Test cases to consider:**
1. Simple 2-module cycle (A → B → A)
2. Complex multi-module cycle (A → B → C → D → A)
3. Cycle with nested dependencies
4. Cycle in different entry points
5. Cycle with dynamic imports
6. Cycle in node_modules (if applicable)

---

## Additional Notes

- This affects all developers using webpack
- More impactful in large codebases
- Aligns with webpack's goal of better developer experience
- Non-breaking change (additive enhancement)
- Would complement other error message improvements
